name: ivy
annotation: FUNCTION: Strong inhibitor of lysozyme C.
notes: Inhibitor of vertebrate lysozyme

name: curA
annotation: FUNCTION: Catalyzes the metal-independent reduction of curcumin to dihydrocurcumin (DHC) as an intermediate product, followed by further reduction to tetrahydrocurcumin (THC) as an end product. It also acts on 3-octene-2-one, 3-hepten-2-one, resveratrol, and trans-2-octenal. {ECO:0000269|PubMed:21467222}.
notes: NADPH-dependent curcumin reductase (EC 1.3.1.n3) (NADPH-dependent curcumin/dihydrocurcumin reductase)

name: yacG
annotation: FUNCTION: Inhibits all the catalytic activities of DNA gyrase by preventing its interaction with DNA. Acts by binding directly to the C-terminal domain of GyrB, which probably disrupts DNA binding by the gyrase. {ECO:0000255|HAMAP-Rule:MF_00649, ECO:0000269|PubMed:18586829}.
notes: DNA gyrase inhibitor YacG

name: sbmC
annotation: FUNCTION: Inhibits the supercoiling activity of DNA gyrase. Acts by inhibiting DNA gyrase at an early step, prior to (or at the step of) binding of DNA by the gyrase. It protects cells against toxins that target DNA gyrase, by inhibiting activity of these toxins and reducing the formation of lethal double-strand breaks in the cell. Protects cells against the natural plasmid-encoded toxins microcin B17 (MccB17) and CcdB, and synthetic quinolones. Can also protect cells against alkylating agents that act independently of DNA gyrase, suggesting a more general role in protecting cells against DNA damage. {ECO:0000269|PubMed:11850398, ECO:0000269|PubMed:13680098, ECO:0000269|PubMed:8709849, ECO:0000269|PubMed:9442027}.
notes: DNA gyrase inhibitor

name: lit
annotation: FUNCTION: Interacts with a short DNA sequence about one-quarter of the way into the major capsid protein gene 23 of T4; general translation inhibition occurs when this late gene of the virus is expressed. {ECO:0000269|PubMed:2452152}.
subcellular_location: SUBCELLULAR LOCATION: Cell membrane {ECO:0000305}; Multi-pass membrane protein {ECO:0000305}.
notes: Cell death peptidase (Bacteriophage T4 late gene expression-blocking protein) (GpLit)

name: intD
annotation: FUNCTION: Integrase from the cryptic lambdoid prophage DLP12. Integrase is necessary for integration of the phage into the host genome by site-specific recombination. In conjunction with excisionase, integrase is also necessary for excision of the prophage from the host genome.
notes: Prophage integrase IntD (Prophage DLP12 integrase) (Prophage QSR' integrase)

name: intR
annotation: FUNCTION: Integrase is necessary for integration of the phage into the host genome by site-specific recombination. In conjunction with excisionase, integrase is also necessary for excision of the prophage from the host genome (By similarity). {ECO:0000250}.
notes: Prophage integrase IntR (Putative lambdoid prophage Rac integrase)

name: intQ
annotation: FUNCTION: Integrase is necessary for integration of the phage into the host genome by site-specific recombination. In conjunction with excisionase, integrase is also necessary for excision of the prophage from the host genome (By similarity). {ECO:0000250}.
notes: Putative defective protein IntQ (Putative lambdoid prophage Qin defective integrase)

name: chondroitin-[core protein]
annotation: Chondroitin is a naturally occurring heteropolysaccharide belonging to the family of glycosaminoglycans. It is a nonsulfated derivative of Chondroitin-sulfates, consisting of a β-D-glucuronyl-(1,3)-N-acetyl-β-D-galactosamine disaccharide repeating unit connected through a β(1,4) glycosidic linkages. Chondroitin is widely used as a therapeutic material for the prevention or alleviation of symptoms of diseases such as rheumatoid arthritis, and in food supplements.

The acyl chain of plant ceramides contains alpha-hydroxylated very long chain fatty acids. name: a siderophore
annotation: Although iron is extremely abundant on Earth, its solubility is very low at physiological pH in aerobic environments. While iron(II) is souble, it is rapidly oxidized in the presence of oxygen into insoluble ferric oxyhydroxides. In addition, iron is often complexed with organic molecules. The concentration of free iron in body fluids is estimated to be about 10-18 M . The most common iron uptake mechanism used by microorganisms are ferrisiderophore acquisition systems. Siderophores are small organic compounds with a very high affinity for iron. Once secreted into the environment, siderophores efficiently solubilize and chelate iron(III). The iron-siderophore complexes (ferrisiderophores) are imported back into the cell by specific transport systems, crossing both the outer and inner membranes . Once in the cytoplasm (and in some cases, in the periplasm), iron is released from siderophores by hydrolysis, proton-assisted dissociation of the complex, or reduction of the metal center . Siderophores are very common in pathogenic bacteria, since iron in the host organism is usually tightly bound to proteins, resulting in an extremely low concentration of free iron in the blood. More than 500 different siderophores have now been identified. The most common siderophores use hydroxamate and/or catechol functions, attached to either linear or cyclic scaffolds, to form a hexadentate structure. Siderophores with lower denticity (tetradentate, tridentate, and bidentate) are also produced, but have a much lower affinity toward iron(III). In general, all siderophores possess a higher affinity for iron(III) than for iron(II) . Most siderophores belong to one of three major groups: the Catecholate-siderophores "catechols", Hydroxamate-siderophores "hydroxamates" and Thiazoline-sideroph

name: μ-carrageenan
annotation: Carrageenans Carrageenans and Carrageenoses carrageenoses are sulfated polysaccharides that are extracted from sea weeds belonging to the TAX-2763 (red algae), mainly from TAX-2769, and species from the TAX-31444 and TAX-33664 genera. They are laid out in the cell wall at a high density and can constitute up to 50% of the algae dry mass. The basic units of Carrageenans carrageenans are repeats of alternating α-1,3-linked D-galactose and β-1,4-linked D-galactose units, which are often substituted by sulfate. Carrageenans are classified according to the number and the position of sulfated ester . mu-Carrageenan is sulfated at position 4 of the β-1,4 linked D-galactose units and position 6 of the α-1,3-linked galactose units. In Carrageenoses carrageenoses the α-1,3-linked galactose units are modified by having a 3,6-anhydro bridge. These bridges, which form a double helix during aggregation, are responsible for the gelation that is typical to these compounds. Despite this distinction, carrageenoses are often referred to as carrageenans. The three most industrially exploited carrageenans, namely, kappa-Carrageenan, iota-Carrageenan and Lambda-Carrageenan, are distinguished by the presence of one, two or three ester-sulfate groups per repeating disaccharide unit respectively.

name: a glucuronoarabinoxylan oligosaccharide
annotation: Glucuronoxylans Glucuronoxylans are linear polymers of β-D-xylopyranosyl units linked by (1→4) glycosidic bonds, with many of the xylose units substituted with 2, 3 or 2,3-linked galacturonate residues. Many of the D-glucuronosyl units are methylated at position 4. Glucuronoarabinoxylans, typically found in softwood, have the same xylan backbone as in Glucuronoxylans glucuronoxylans , but about one of ten β-D-xylopyranosyl resudue is substituted by α-L-arabinofuranosyl at position 3. Unlike glucuronoxylans, glucuronoarabinoxylans are not acetylated .

name: an [E1 NEDD8-activating enzyme]-L-cysteine
annotation: NEDD8-activating enzymes, also known as E1 enzymes, catalyze the first step in the neddylation process. The E1 enzyme, which is a herodimeric complex, binds ATP-Mg2+ and NEDD8 and catalyzes C-terminal acyl adenylation of NEDD8. A catalytic L-cysteine residue of the E1 enzyme then attacks the NEDD8-AMP complex through acyl substitution, creating a thioester bond and an AMP leaving group. This active form of NEDD8, bound to the E1 enzyme, is transferred to NEDD8-carrier-proteins-E2 through a transthioesterification reaction, in which an E2 catalytic L-cysteine attacks the backside of the E1-NEDD8 complex.

An analysis of the form of Amadori products at room temperature has found that they exist at approximately 61% β-pyranose, 6% α-pyranose, 15% β-furanose, 16% α-furanose, and 2% keto forms .

name: a ganglioside
annotation: Gangliosides "Gangliosides" are molecules composed of Glycosphingolipids (Ceramides and Oligosaccharides) with one or more sialic acids (usually N-ACETYLNEURAMINATE) linked to the sugar chain. They are components of the cell plasma membrane that modulates cell signal transduction events, and appear to concentrate in lipid rafts. The name ganglioside is derived from the ganglion cells of the brain, from which the first gangliosides were isolated by the German scientist Ernst Klenk in 1942 . Indeed, gangliosides dominate the composition of the glycocalyces of most of the cells of the vertebrate nervous system. Although gangliosides with various glycan structures are found in every vertebrate tissue, they are markedly more abundant in the nervous system, where each cell has on average about 10 million cell surface gangliosides . Variations in the sialoglycan and lipid components of the gangliosides result in hundreds of distinct ganglioside structures with a variety of functions that ranges from regulating receptor tyrosine kinases to directing cell-cell communication, cell-substrate interaction, adhesion, and protein targeting . Mammalian brain gangliosides, however, are dominated by only four types of gangliosides - CPD-7188 "ganglioside GM1a, Ganglioside-GD1a "ganglioside GD1a", Ganglioside-GD1b "ganglioside GD1b" and Ganglioside-GT1b "ganglioside GT1b" . The ceramide lipid components of brain gangliosides are dominated by a single fatty acid (STEARIC_ACID) attached to one of two sphingoid bases, SPHINGOSINE and CPD-19684 . While many non-human mammals synthesize gangliosides that contain two types of sialic acids - N-ACETYLNEURAMINATE and CPD-273, mammalian brain gangliosides almost exclusively carry the former. Like other glycosphingolipids, gangliosides are biosynthe

name: a cyanocobamide
annotation: Cobamides "A cobamide" is a corrinoid molecule based on the structure of CPD-20843, a cobalt-corrinoid hexaamide that is formed from COBINAMIDE by the attachment of an α-D-ribofuranose 3-phosphate residue at the 2 position of the aminopropanol chain. Biologically active forms of cobamides contain an upper (β) and a lower (α) axial ligands. The most common upper axial ligand are either a methyl group or a 5'-deoxyadenosyl group, although some other forms exist. More variability is found with the lower axial ligand, where more that 16 different forms have been described. Some procedure for cobamide purification involve the replacement of the upper ligand with a cyano group. A common example is CPD-315, also known as vitamin B12. Cyanocobamides do not occur in living organisms, and are only produced artificially. This class includes all cobamides that have a cyano group as their upper axial ligand.

name: cycloamylose
annotation: Cycloamyloses are large cyclic α-1,4 linked glucans that contain dozens to hundreds of glucose units. They are similar to Cyclodextrins cyclodextrins, but those are much smaller, typically containing 6, 7 or 8 glucose units. Cycloamyloses are formed from high molecular weight Alpha-Amyloses by the action of EC-2.4.1.25.

name: a pectate
annotation: Pectin "Pectin" is a structural heteropolysaccharide consisting of a complex set of polysaccharides that is present in most primary cell walls and is particularly abundant in the non-woody parts of terrestrial plants. It s involved in crosslinking cellulose and hemicellulose fibers, which provides rigidity to the cell wall. The characteristic structure of pectin is a linear chain of α-(1-4)-linked D-GALACTURONATE that forms the pectin-backbone, 1-4-alpha-D-galacturonosyl. Different components of pectin have different substitutions and additions. A common type is Rhamnogalacturonans-I "rhamnogalacturonan I" (RG-I), which makes up 20-35% of pectin. In RG-I some of the D-GALACTURONATE units in the backbone are replaced by (1-2)-linked L-rhamnose, to which side chains of various neutral sugars branch off, mostly D-Galactose, L-ARABINOSE and CPD-25028. Another common type is CPD-14581 "rhamnogalacturonan II" (RG-II), which makes up about 10% of pectin. RG-II is based on a backbone of 8-9 galacturonate units, which are decorated by four side chains consisting of 12 different types of sugars in more than 20 different linkages. RG-II usually forms dimeric structures via the formation of crosslinks by a 1:2 borate diol ester between apiosyl residues in one of the side chains. Other types of pectin include chains of apiogalacturonan, homogalacturonan, and xylogalacturonan. There is currently no consensus as to how the pectic polysaccharides are linked to each other, or to other polymers, in the wall. However, it is thought that the different peptides are covalently linked to each other via their backbones, forming a large linear structure with many side branches . In nature, around 80% of the carboxyl groups of the galacturonate units are esterified with METOH. Some plants, inclu

name: elemental selenium
annotation: Selenium is an essential trace element that is crucial for the normal development of bacteria and animals. High concentrations of selenium are however toxic, largely because transporters and enzymes involved in sulfur and sulfo-compound metabolism can often erroneously substitute Elemental-selenium for sulfur and can misincorporate seleno-amino acids into proteins . In humans the safety threshold between acute selenium poisoning (>400 μg/day) and selenium deficiency (<40 μg/day) is very narrow. The natural forms of selenium include SELENATE [Se(VI)], SELENITE [Se(IV)], Elemental-selenium [Se(0)], and SE-2 [Se(-II)]. Selenite is the most toxic, while elemental selenium is insoluble and is generally considered to be non-toxic . However, selenium rarely occurs in its elemental state in nature. Elemental selenium can be formed by certain organisms, usually by reduction of the oxidized forms SELENATE and SELENITE. Some bacterial species are known to form selenium nanoparticles that consist of elemental selenium coated by a mixture of proteins, lipids, and carbohydrates . Elemental selenium occurs in nature in two main allotropic forms. The most stable allotrope is a polymeric form that appears as a dense purplish-gray solid. It forms polyselenides in the presence of strong reducing agents. The second major allotrope is red selenium which, similar to Elemental-Sulfur "elemental sulfur", forms individual molecules of eight-membered rings (although rings composed of more or less than 8 selenium atoms have been reported). Red selenium converts to the gray polymeric allotrope upon heating.

name: an oxyvinylglycine
annotation: Oxyvinylglycines are non-proteinogenic amino acids that are produced by certain bacteria. They irreversibly inhibit enzymes that require PYRIDOXAL_PHOSPHATE as a co-factor and thus have multiple targets in bacteria, animals, and plants . One such plant target is the ethylene biosynthesis enzyme EC-4.4.1.14, which is inhibited by CPD-13353 (AVG) and by CPD-20103 . AVG is commercially available under the name of ReTain and is widely used for the regulation of fruit set in orchard crops. Another member of the oxyvinylglycines, CPD-20100 (FVG), is a natural herbicide that blocks the germination of a large variety of grassy weed species .

name: a quinolone
annotation: A quinolone is a compound that contains the Quinolines quinoline structure modifed by an oxo group on the pyridine ring. The first quinolone compound to be discovered was CPD-25979, identified in 1962 as an impurity in the chemical manufacturing of the antimalarial agent CPD-23333. That compound lead to the development of the antibiotic CPD-21025 "nalidixic acid" . Since then more than 10,000 quinolone derivatives have been patented or published. Early on the quinolones were found to have antibiotic activity. They directly inhibit DNA synthesis by binding to the polymerase-DNA complex. Quinolones also stabilize DNA strand breaks created by DNA gyrase and topoisomerase IV, and ternary complexes of drug, enzyme, and DNA block progress of the replication fork. Fluoroquinolones are WHO-approved second-line drugs to treat tuberculosis. Quinolones were later found to exhibit a variety of biological activities, including antimalarial, antiviral and antitumor activities . Many compounds demonstrate anticancer activity through DNA intercalation. Most of these drugs are currently used in the treatment of human malignancies targeting topoisomerase (types II) enzymes . The quinolones stabilize covalent enzyme—cleaved DNA complexes that are intermediates in the catalytic cycle of the enzyme . Quinolones also have antiviral activities. They were shown to protect cells from HIV-mediated cytotoxicity . Some of the compounds target the viral protein integrase .

annotation: Carboxyphthiodiolenones are intermediates in Phthiocerols phthiocerol biosynthesis.

name: a saponin
annotation: Saponins are a class of secondary metabolites that have been grouped together phenomenologically by the foaming they produce when shaken in aqueous solutions, and structurally by their composition of one or more hydrophilic glycoside moieties combined with a lipophilic triterpene derivative. They are particularly abundant in various plant species, but are also found in marine organisms. The aglycones (glycoside-free portion) of the saponins are termed sapogenins. They can be any one of a wide variety of polycyclic organic structures originating from the serial addition of ten-carbon terpene units to compose a C30 triterpene skeleton. The number and length of saccharide chains attached to the sapogenin can vary greatly.

annotation: Bacteriochlorophyll f is very similar to CPD-9094 "bacteriochlorophyll e", but lacks the C20 methyl group found in the latter. It is not observed naturally , but is produced in mutants of CPD-9094 "bacteriochlorophyll e"-producing bacteria that lack EC-2.1.1.333 .

name: a glucosylceramide(40:1)-3
annotation: Glucosyl ceramides are a type of sphingolipid. Each member of this class has a long carbon chain attached at position R1 to form a long chain base and a long carbon chain is attached at position R2 to form a fatty amide. Either or both of these chains may contain one or more double bonds and may have hydroxyl groups attached. Based on the notation GlcCer(m:n)-p, each compound within this class has a total of 40 carbons (m), one double bond (n), and three hydroxyl groups (p) present in the sphingoid base and the fatty amide. However, the length, hydroxylation position, double bond position, and double bond orientation of the groups attached at R1 and R2 will differ between individual members of this group of compounds.

annotation: Thiophenes are molecules that contain at least one CPD-25910 ring.

name: a corphin
annotation: Corphins are hexahydroporphinoid ligands that combine structural elements of both the Porphyrins porphyrins and the Corrins corrins. They contain the carbon-nitrogen skeleton of porphyrins and a chromophore system similar to that occurring in corrins. Pyrrocorphins are corphin tautomers in which one of the four five-membered rings is in pyrrolic form .

name: a penam
annotation: This compound class stands for Lactams lactams in which the amide bond is contained within a four-membered ring, which includes the amide nitrogen and the carbonyl carbon. β-Lactam compounds imitate the naturally occurring D-ALA-D-ALA substrate for the group of enzymes known as penicillin binding proteins (PBPs), which cross-link the peptidoglycan in bacterial cell wall, and thus act as powerful antibiotics . Naturally occurring Beta-Lactams "β-lactam antibiotics" are classified into several groups according to their chemical structure: In Monolactams monolactams the β-lactam ring is not fused to another ring. This class includes compounds such as the Nocardicins nocardicins and the Monobactams monobactams . In Penams penams the β-lactam ring is fused to a saturated five atom ring (a 4:5 structure). Penams contain a sulfur atom at position 1 of the five atom ring (for example, the PENICILLIN penicillins) . Related classes include the Carbapenams carbapenams, which contain a carbon atom in that position , and the Clavams oxopenams (also known as clavams), which contain an oxygen atom at that position (e.g. CPD-9261) . Penems Penems are molecues similar to penams in which the five atom ring is unsaturated at positions 2-3. Similarly, penems contain a sulfur atom at position 1 of the five atom ring, while Carbapenems carbapenems contains a carbon atom in that position . In Cephams cephams and Cephems cephems the β-lactam ring is fused to a six-atom ring (a 4:6 strcuture) that contains a sulfur atom at position 1. In cephams the ring is saturated, while cephems have an unsaturated bond at positions 3-4 . 1-Oxocephams/1-oxacephems conain an oxygen atom at position 1 .

name: a bacteriochlorophyll f

name: gellan
annotation: Gellan is a water-soluble polysaccharide produced by the bacterium TAX-179878. The repeating unit of the polymer is the tetrasaccharide [β-D-Glc(1→4)-βD-GlcA(1→4)-β-D-Glc(1→4)-α-L-Rha(1→3)]. Gellan is primarily used in microbiological cultures as a gelling agent, alternative to agar. It is also used as a food additive, as a thickener, emulsifier, and stabilizer.

name: an α-pinene
annotation: Pinene is a bicyclic monoterpene found in the resins of many conifers (including pine, from which its name is derived), as well as in non-coniferous plants. Two main forms exist - α-pinene and β-pinene.

name: a rhamnolipid
annotation: This class contains glycosides composed of one or two rhamnose moieties joined in an O-glycosidic linkage to a non-glycero lipid moiety. The rhamnose moieties are linked to each other through an α-1,2-glycosidic linkage. The lipid moiety is mainly two (sometimes one or three) β-hydroxy fatty acid chains (represented here in part by R1 and R2) linked to each other through an ester bond between the β-hydroxyl group of the chain distal to the glycosidic bond with the carboxyl group of the chain proximal to the glycosidic bond. The carboxyl group of the distal chain is usually free, but in a few cases it is esterified with a short alkyl group. The fatty acid chains may be saturated, mono-unsaturated, or poly-unsaturated and the chain length may vary from C8 to C16. In rare cases the rhamnose 2-hydroxyl group distal to the glycosidic bond may be acylated with a long chain alkenoate .

name: a hydrolyzable tannin
annotation: A hydrolyzable tannin is a type of tannin that, upon heating with hydrochloric or sulfuric acids, yields CPD-183 or CPD-17769. At the center of a hydrolyzable tannin molecule there is a carbohydrate core (usually D-glucose, although sometimes it is a cyclitol like QUINATE or SHIKIMATE). The hydroxyl groups of the carbohydrate core are partially or totally esterified with phenolic groups such as CPD-183 in Gallotanins gallotannins or CPD-17769 in Ellagitannins ellagitannins.

name: an efrapeptin
annotation: Efrapeptins are peptides produced by fungi in the genus TAX-29909 that have antifungal, insecticidal, and mitochondrial ATPase inhibitory activities .

name: a mycolipanoate
aliases: a mycolipanic acid|a mycothianoic acid|a mycothianoate|a (2S,4S,6S)-2,4,6-trimethyl-very-long-chain fatty acid
annotation: A unique feature of mycobacterial cell wall lipids is the presence of very long chain fatty acids with multiple methyl branches toward the carboxyl end of the carbon chain. Many types of such acids exist, including Mycosanoic-Acids "mycosanoic acids", Mycolipanoic-Acids "mycolipanoic acids", Mycolipanolic-Acids "mycolipanolic acids", Mycolipenic-Acids "mycolipenic acids", Mycolipodienoic-Acids "mycolipodienoic acids", Phthioceranic-Acids "phthioceranic acids", and Mycocerosic-Acids "mycocerosic acids". Mycolipanoic acids (also named mycothianoic acids) are 2,4,6-trimethylated very-long-chain alkanoic acids . They are formed by EC-2.3.1.252. 3-Hydroxylated versions of these acids are known as Mycolipanolic-Acids "mycolipanolic acids" . They are produced by a different enzyme .

name: a TAG(18:3/36:4)
annotation: This class of triacylglycerols contains three acyl chains formed by the esterification of glycerol with three fatty acids. The names of triacylglycerol compound classes provide information about the three acyl chains of each member of the class, using the nomenclature TAG(a:b/y:z) where "a" represents the number of carbons found in one acyl chain and "b" represents the number of desaturations present in chain "a" and where "y" represents the total number of carbons found in the other two acyl chains and "z" represents the total number of desaturations present in the two chains included in "y." Therefore, in this TAG(18:3/36:4) class of compounds, one acyl chain has 18 carbons and three double bonds. The other two acyl chains have a total of 36 carbons and four double bonds. The attachment sites of the acyl chains at the R1 (sn-1), R2 (sn-2), or R3 (sn-3) position along the glyceryl backbone may vary among class members and cannot be inferred from their order in the compound class name. Additionally, the location and orientation of the double bonds in the unsaturated acyl chain(s) may vary among members of the class and are not specified by this notation.

name: a phthiodiolone B
annotation: Phthiodiolones Phthiodiolones are intermediates in Phthiocerols phthiocerol biosynthesis. They contain an oxo group where phthiocerols contain a methoxyl group. Two types of phthiodiolone exist. In Phthiodiolone-A "phthiodiolone A" the oxo and the methyl groups are attached at positions 3 and 4, respectively, and the hydroxyl groups are at positions 9 and 11. In Phthiodiolone-B "phthiodiolone B" a methyl group is missing from the beginning of the molecule, and thus the oxo and methyl groups are attached at positions 2 and 3, respectively, and the hydroxyl groups are at positions 8 and 10.

name: [dermatan]-6-O-sulfo-N-acetylgalactosamine
annotation: Dermatan-Sulfate "Dermatan sulfate" is a glycosaminoglycan found mostly in skin but also in blood vessels, the heart valves, tendons, and the lungs. Dermatan sulfate is composed of repeating disaccharide units composed of a hexose sugar and a hexuronic acid, many of which sulfated. The most common disaccharide unit in dermatan sulfate is composed of CPD-12515 and CPD-12557 (many are sulfated) linked by β(1 -> 3) linkages. This compound stand for CPD-12557 sulfated at the 6' position within the context of Dermatan-Sulfate.

name: a carvone
annotation: There are two enantiomers of carvone. CPD-1089 "(-)-Carvone" smells like spearmint, while CPD-10037 smells like caraway seeds.

name: a methyl-esterified homogalacturonan
annotation: Around 80% of the carboxyl groups of the galacturonate units in the 1-4-alpha-D-galacturonosyl homogalaturonan component of natural Pectin pectin are esterified with METOH.

name: a (4E,8E,10E)-sphinga-4,8,10-trienine ceramide
annotation: Ceramides Ceramides are found in high concentrations within the cell membrane and are one of the component lipids that make up Sphingomyelins sphingomyelin, one of the major lipids in the lipid bilayer. This type of ceramide with a sphingoid base with three double bonds has been found in the marine diatom TAX-35128 , as well as in the spermatozoa of the starfish TAX-7602 . A related compound that contains a methyl branch was reported from a squid neuron sphingomylein .

name: a rhamnogalacturonan type I
annotation: Rhamnogalacturonans-I "Rhamnogalacturonan I" (RG-I) is a polysaccharide that represents about 20-35% of Pectin pectin. It contains a backbone consisting of repeating α-(1,2)-L-rhamnose-α-(1,4)-D-GALACTURONATE disaccharide units. Between 20 and 80% of the Rha residues are substituted with side chains of various oligosaccharides such as arabinans, galactans, and arabinogalactans. The number and type of side chains are highly variable depending on the species as well as the cell type and developmental stage. The side chains can consist of individual, linear, or branched CPD-12045 (α-L-Araf) and GALACTOSE (β-D-Galp) residues, including α-1,5-linked L-arabinan with 2- and 3-linked arabinose or arabinan branching, β-1,4-linked D-galactans with degrees of polymerization (DP) of up to 47 , β-1,4-linked D-galactans with 3-linked L-arabinose or arabinan branching, and β-1,3-linked D-galactan with β-6-linked galactan or arabinogalactan branching (reviewed in references . The side chains may also contain α-L-Fucp, β-D-GlcpA, and 4-O-Me β-D-GlcpA residues . The biosynthetic pathway of RG-I is not yet characterized, though some of the enzymes involved have been identified. They include the galacturonosyltransferase GAUT11, the hexosyltransferase of unknown function MUCI70 , and the rhamnosyltransferases RRT1-RRT4 .

name: an oleanonic-acid-type saponin ginsenoside
annotation: Ginsenosides Ginsenosides or panaxosides are a class of steroid glycosides and triterpene saponins found exclusively in the plant genus TAX-4053 (ginseng). They are the principal components responsible for the pharmaceutical activities of ginseng. Ginsenosides are composed of an aglycone and sugar moieties. Based on the structure of their aglycones, ginsenosides have been categorized into three broad types; Protopanaxadiol-type-ginsenosides "protopanaxadiol-type ginsenosides" (PPD), which contain hydroxyl groups at positions 3 and 20, Protopanaxatriol-type-ginsenosides "protopanaxatriol-type ginsenosides" (PPT), which contain hydroxyl groups at positions 3, 6 and 20, and Oleanonic-acid-type-ginsenosides "oleanonic-acid-type saponins". Although ginsenosides only have three kinds of aglycones, more than 60 kinds of ginsenosides have been identified by now due to the variability in the type of sugar moieties, their number, and the carbon positions they link to. The biological activity of ginsenoside increases with the decrease of the number of the sugar moieties. All of the ginsenosides found in large quantities in wild ginseng (known as major ginsenosides) are heavily glycosylated, while the more active less-glycosylated ginsenosides are found in much smalller quantities (and are commonly referred to as the minor ginsenosides). Following oral intake of ginseng, the large low-solubility and poor-permeability major ginsenosides cannot be absorbed by the human body directly , and are deglycosylated into smaller forms in the gastrointestinal tract by intestinal bacteria and/or digestive enzymes . Glycosidase enzymes that can convert the major ginsenosides to minor ginsenosides are not common and are of commercial interest.

name: a galactogen
annotation: Galactogen is a branched-chain homopolymer of galactose. Galactogen has been found in the albumen glands of several molluscan genera, as well as in cattle, carp, rabbit and man .

name: a [glycerolipid]-α-linolenate
aliases: a [glycerolipid]-γ-linolenic acid|9Z|a [glycerolipid]-(9Z|a [glycerolipid]-(6|9|15Z)-octadeca-9|a [glycerolipid]-6Z|15-trienoate|12Z|15)-linolenate|12Z-octadecatrienoate|12)-linolenate|12Z-octadecatrienoic acid|15Z-octadecatrienoic acid|a [glycerolipid]-9Z|12)-linolenoic acid|12|a [glycerolipid]-γ-linolenate|15)-linolenic acid|a [glycerolipid]-(9
annotation: This class describes a CPD-8117 group that is incorporated within a lipid molecule CPD-8117 (GLA) is a polyunsaturated (18:3) ω-6 fatty acid (the 6 refers to the position of the first double bond from the methyl end of the fatty acid). It is synthesized from LINOLEIC_ACID by Δ6 desaturase enzymes. Unlike its precursor, GLA is not an essential fatty acid for animals, since almost all animals possess a Δ6 desaturase enzyme. GLA is of major importance in animal cells, being the precursor for the synthesis of several Δ6-desaturated fatty acids, such as ARACHIDONIC_ACID and 5Z8Z11Z14Z17Z-EICOSAPENTAENOATE (see PWY-7592). Δ6-desaturated fatty acids have roles in the maintenance of membrane structure and function, in the regulation of cholesterol synthesis and transport, in the prevention of water loss from the skin, and as precursors of eicosanoids, including prostaglandins and leucotrienes . Like other fatty acids, CPD-8117 is rarely found in its free form. It is usually found either as GAMMA-LINOLENOYL-COA or Gamma-linolenoyl-groups "incorporated into a lipid".

name: an arabinoglucuronoxylan
annotation: Glucuronoarabinoxylans Glucuronoarabinoxylans (GAX) and Arabinoglucuronoxylans arabinoglucuronoxylans (AGX) are components of Hemicelluloses that have a similar structure - a β(1→4)-D-xylopyranose backbone with single 4-O-methylglucuronate and α-L-arabinofuranose residues attached at position 2 and 3, respectively. However, Glucuronoarabinoxylans Glucuronoarabinoxylans consist of Arabinoxylan backbone that contains about ten times more α-L-arabinofuranose side chains than glucuronate ones, while Arabinoglucuronoxylans arabinoglucuronoxylans are Glucuronoxylans with a small proportion of the xylopyranose residues of the backbone disubstituted by two α-L-arabinofuranose residues. Another difference is that Glucuronoarabinoxylans glucuronoarabinoxylans contain some xylopyranose units that are doubly substituted by both 4-O-methylglucuronate and α-L-arabinofuranose . Some of the arabinofuranose side chains in AGX are further substitued by either a β-D-xylopyranose attached by a (1→2) linkage. This sugar moiety has been found usually esterified by ferulate at position 5 of the arabinofuranose unit and occur as a widespread component of grass cell walls . The AGX type polymers occur in appreciable quantity in coniferous species, although it is usually not the dominant hemicellulosic component (an exception is the hemicellulose of the tropic conifer TAX-1030197 ). They are also the dominant hemicelluloses in the cell walls of lignified supporting tissues of grasses and cereals.

name: a mineralocorticoid
annotation: Mineralocorticoids are a class of steroid hormones that regulate water and electrolyte metabolism. The primary endogenous mineralocorticoid is aldosterone. The name glucocorticoid is derived from an early observations that these hormones are involved in the retention of the mineral sodium.

name: a tunicamycin
annotation: CPD-5242 Tunicamycin is a mixture of nucleoside antibiotics targeting bacterial teichoic acid and peptidoglycan biosynthesis at the steps of EC-2.7.8.33, and EC-2.7.8.13, respectively. Tunicamycins cannot be used clinically since they are also acive against the eukaryotic enzyme EC-2.7.8.15, which is involved in an early step of protein N-glycosylation. The compounds bind specifically to the active site of the enzymes, blocking their activity and terminating the biosynthesis of the polymers at the first commited step . The first tunicamycin compound was originally isolated in 1957 under the name mycospocidin (it was eventually shown to be a tunicamycin ). A few years later more tunicamycin compounds were isolated from cultures of TAX-1079983, this time under the name tunicamycin, and initially described as anti-viral agents, although they were subsequently shown to be also active against yeasts, fungi and Gram-positive bacteria . Tunicamycins were subsequently isolated from TAX-1901 and TAX-1969, in which they accumulate to much higher concentrations. The process for tunicamycin preparation was patented 1980 with TAX-1079985 . All tunicamycins contain URACIL, N-acetyl-D-glucosamine, a unique 11-carbon 2-aminodialdose called CPD-19359, and a fatty acid linked to the amino group of the tunicamine. The various members of the tunicamycin group, all of which appear to have antimicrobial activity, differ only in the structure of their fatty acyl chain .

name: a pterin
annotation: Pterins Pterins are heterocyclic compounds composed of fused CPD-26401 and PYRIMIDINE-RING rings (a CPD-15307 ring system), with an amino group in the 2-position and an oxo group in the 4-position of the pyrimidine ring.

name: an L-arabinan
annotation: L-arabinan is a common hemicellulosic component of the cell wall of higher plants. It is a branched polysaccharide with a backbone of α-(1,5)-linked arabinofuranosyl residues with α-(1,2)- and α-(1,3)-linked arabinofuranosyl side chains and is involved in covalent cross-lining of cell wall polysaccharides. It is usually associated with pectic substrates, and pectins with attached arabinans can be isolated from many fruits and vegetables such as apples, sugar beet, apricots, carrots, cabbage, onion and pears.

name: β-D-glucosyluronate-[heparan sulfate]
annotation: This compounds describes an CPD-12521 residue located at the non-reducing end of Heparan-Sulfate.

name: D-galactofuranose
annotation: This compound stands for a mixture of CPD0-2486 and CPD0-2485. In most cases, each of these compounds mutarotates quickly to form a mixture of the two, and thus it is the general rule to use this non-specific form in reactions unless it is specifically known that an enzyme can accept only one of the forms.

name: a debranched α-limit dextrin
aliases: a debranched glycogen phosphorylase-limit dextrin|a debranched glycogen phosphorylase-limited dextrin
annotation: CPD0-971 "A limit dextrin" is the name given to the highly-branched core of the polysaccharide fragments remaining at the end (limit) of exhaustive hydrolysis of CPD-7043 amylopectin or Glycogens glycogen by α-1,4-glucan hydrolase enzymes such as GLYCOPHOSPHORYL-CPLX and MALDEXPHOSPHORYL-CPLX. These enzymes shorten the chains, but are not able to hydrolyze the α-1,6 bonds at branch points, forming Truncated-Limit-Dextrins. Following the action of the YPR184W-MONOMER, which completely removes the branching chains, CPD0-1027 is formed.

name: a para-quinone
annotation: A quinone in which the two oxo groups of the quinone are located para to each other on the 6-membered quinonoid ring.

name: an oligosaccharide with β-L-arabinopyranose at the non-reducing end
display_name: an oligosaccharide with β-L-arabinopyranose at the non-reducing end

name: albendazole S-oxide
annotation: ALBENDAZOLE Albendazole and CPD-20524 are benzimidazole compounds used as broad-spectrum anthelmintics against gastrointestinal nematodes and the larval stages of cestodes . Both compounds are oxidized by EC-1.14.14.73, and EC-1.14.13.32, to an S-oxide, which is the form responsible for their systemic biological activity .

name: an H antigen
annotation: The H-antigen "H antigens" are fucosylated oligosaccharides that decorate proteins on the surface of red blood cells in people with O blood type. In people with A or B blood types an additional monosaccharide is attached to one of the β-D-galactosyl residues of the H antigen (CPD-12557 in A-antigen "A antigens" and GALACTOSE in B-antigen "B antigens"). These fucosylated structures are found not only on blood cells but also on many other cell types (mainly epithehal), and it has been suggested that they should be called histo-blood group antigens. The majority of the A, B and H antigens are linked to the ends of long polylactosamine chains attached mainly to band 3 protein (encoded by SLC4A1), the anion exchange protein of the red blood cells. A minor fraction of the antigens is attached to a ceramide moiety, forming neutral glycosphingolipids. Several H antigens exist, differing in their core disaccharide . The different types are: Type-1-H-Antigen "Type 1": αFuc-(1,2)-βGal-(1→3)-βGlcNac-(1→R) Type-2-H-Antigen "Type 2": αFuc-(1,2)-βGal-(1→4)-βGlcNac-(1→R) Type-3-H-Antigen "Type 3": αFuc-(1,2)-βGal-(1→3)-αGalNac-(1→R) Type-4-H-Antigen "Type 4": αFuc-(1,2)-βGal-(1→3)-βGalNac-(1→R) Type-5-H-Antigen "Type 5": αFuc-(1,2)-βGal-(1→3)-βGal-(1→R) Type-6-H-Antigen "Type 6": αFuc-(1,2)-βGal-(1→4)-βGlc-(1→R) Most of the H antigens found in the cell are either type 1 or type 2, followed by type 3 and 4. Type 1 is found in secretions and in the serum, while type 2 is found exclusively on the surface of red blood cells. Type 6 molecules have been described in exocrine secretions. It is not clear if type 5 H antigens exist, although the type 5 precursor has been characterized .

name: an acetal
annotation: An acetal is an organooxygen compound having the structure RR'C(OR'')(OR''') (where R'', R'' are not H). Mixed acetals have different R'' and R''' groups.

name: a levan
annotation: Levan is a non-structural polysaccharide present in several microorganisms and a few plant species. It consists almost solely of fructosyl residues linked via β-2,6 linkages, and is packed into nano-sized spherical forms, giving it a very low intrinsic viscosity. Levan is a main ingredient in natto, a traditional Japanese food. Plants synthesize levan in the vacuole by the concerted action of various fructosyltransferases such as EC-2.4.1.10. When acting alone, this enzyme synthesizes linear levan chains from sucrose via the trisaccharide intermediate 6-KESTOSE (it can also use 1-KESTOTRIOSE and NEOKESTOSE as the initial fructose acceptors). Plant fructans generally have a low degree of polymerization (DP <10 to 100). The occurrence of levansucrase is widespread in Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria. Bacterial levans reach much higher molecular masses and contain up to 10,000 fructosyl units. Species that belong to the TAX-1301, TAX-1243, and TAX-1578 genera also contain EC-2.4.1.9, which forms β-(2,1) fructosyl-fructose linkages. When expressed together with levansucrase, that enzyme creates branching points that connect multiple β-(2,6) linked levan chains, forming a branched polymer known as Graminans graminan .

name: an azaarene
annotation: Azaarenes are aromatic compound that contains one or more nitrogen atoms within the aromatic ring.

name: a glucan
annotation: A glucan molecule is a polysaccharide of D-glucose monomers linked by glycosidic bonds.

name: dermatan sulfate disaccharide
annotation: This is the most common repeating disaccharide unit found in Dermatan-Sulfate, with both CPD-12515 and CPD-12557 in their sulfated form.

name: a chitosan fragment
annotation: Chitosan is a linear polysaccharide composed of randomly distributed β-(1-4)-linked D-glucosamine and N-acetyl-D-glucosamine units. Chitosan is found in the cell walls of some phytopathogenic fungi. The natural occuring chitosan has a degree of acetylation between 20-30%, with the acetyl residues grouped into clusters. Chitosan is mostly produced commercially by deacetylation of CHITIN , which is the structural element in the exoskeleton of crustaceans. The most popular technique for obtaining chitosan is alkaline deacetylation of chitin: this involves boiling chitin in concentrated alkali (50% w/v) for several hours. This process also yields chitosan with a degree of acetylation between 20-30%. However, in such a chitosan, unlike the naturally occurring chitosan, the acetyl groups are uniformly distributed along the polymer chain. Completely de-acetylated forms of chitosan do not exist naturally, but can be synthesized by special chemical treatment.

name: a proteoglycan
annotation: Proteoglycans are glycoproteins in which the carbohydrate units are Glycosaminoglycans glycosaminoglycans (GAG). Most glycosaminoglycans are attached via a specific tetrasaccharide known as the GlcA-Gal-Gal-Xyl-Proteins "glycosaminoglycan-protein linkage region".

name: a B antigen
aliases: a histo-blood group substance B|an α-D-galactosyl-(1->3)-[α-L-fucosyl-(1->2)]-β-D-galactosyl-R|a B histo-blood group antigen
annotation: The B antigen is an oligosaccharide that decorates proteins on the surface of red blood cells in people with B blood type. It is generated from H-antigen, which is found in people with O blood type, by the attachement of an GALACTOSE residue by a 1,3 linkage to one of the β-D-galactosyl residues of the H antigen. People with AB blood type contain both A-antigen "A antigens" and B-antigen "B antigens". The majority of the A, B and H antigens are linked to the ends of long polylactosamine chains attached mainly to band 3 protein (encoded by SLC4A1), the anion exchange protein of the red blood cells. A minor fraction of the antigens is attached to a ceramide moiety, forming neutral glycosphingolipids.

name: a phenol
annotation: PHENOL Phenol is an organic compound consisting of a phenyl group (-C6H5) bonded to a hydroxyl group. The word phenol is also used to refer to any compound that contains a six-membered aromatic ring, bonded directly to a hydroxyl group (-OH). Thus, Phenols phenols are a class of organic compounds of which the PHENOL "phenol compound" is the simplest member. The R group in this structure contains a six-membered aromatic ring.

name: D-mannuronate
annotation: Mannuronate Mannuronate is a reducing acidic sugar that exists in solution in multiple configurations. It can form a ring structure, as shown for CPD-14490 and CPD-15205. The open-chain structure shown here, which is the structure of CPD-21250, is provided only as a schematic illustration of chirality. Consult the appropriate class instances for information on specific ring structures and their anomeric configuration.

name: a bilin
annotation: Bilins (also known as bilanes or bile pigments) are pigments formed in many organisms as a metabolic product of certain porphyrins. They consist of linear arrangements of four pyrrole rings (tetrapyrroles). Bilin (also called bilichrome) received its name since it was first identified as a bile pigment of mammals. However, billins are found in many organisms, including lower vertebrates, invertebrates, green plants, some algae and cyanobacteria. Bilins can range in color from red, orange, yellow or brown to blue or green.

name: a [hypoxia inducible factor (HIF) α subunit]-L-asparagine
family_key: sm:name:a hypoxia inducible factor hif subunit l asparagine

name: a fortimicin
annotation: Fortimicin is the collective name for a family of aminoglycoside antibiotics produced by TAX-1880 . The main ingredient is CPD-27388, also known as astromicin.

name: a trans-methoxy-C60-meroacyl-adenylate
family_key: sm:name:a trans methoxy c60 meroacyl adenylate

name: a 2-O-acetylated rhamnogalacturonan I
annotation: Rhamnogalacturonans-I "Rhamnogalacturonan I " is a polysaccharide in the Pectin pectin family, consisting of repeating α-(1,2)-L-rhamnose-α-(1,4)-D-GALACTURONATE disaccharide units. About half of the Rha residues are substituted with side chains of various neutral oligosaccharides such as arabinans, galactans, and arabinogalactans. Most pectic substances are furthermore esterified with acetyl or methyl groups at some of the galacturonate residues in the backbone.

name: a galactofuranosemannan core (Aspergillus)
annotation: Mannans are polymers composed of MANNOSE residues. Pure mannans are rarely found, and most contain α(1,6)-linked galactopyranosyl side chains. However, in certain filamentous fungi galactofuranose (Galf) is found in galactomannan instead of galactopyranose. The galactomannan attached to N-glycans in TAX-746128 is composed of a linear mannan core comprising CPD-20116 "α-(1-2)-linked mannotetraose" repeating units attached to each other via α-(1-6) linkages and decorated by side chains of β-(1-5)-galactofuranose units, which are linked to the C2 position of the non-reducing mannose residue in each mannotetraose unit . The length of the side chains depends on growth conditions. Cells grown in YPD medium produced galactomannan with side chain consisting an average of 3-4 units of galactofuranose , while cells grown in YNB medium incorporated on average 10 Galf units that may contain one β-(1,6) linkage midchain . It should be noted that a different form of galactomannan containing galactofuranose resides is found in O-glycans produced by this organism, in which the mannan core is much shorter, consisting of only one to two mannosyl residues .

name: an anthracene
annotation: This class describes compounds containing an CPD-21675 skeleton.

name: a mix-linked (1→3/1→4)-β-D-xylan
annotation: 13-14-Mixed-Xylan "Mix-linked (1→3/1→4)-β-D-xylan" is a polysaccharide composed of both β-1,3- and β-1,4-linked BETA-D-XYLOSE residues. This polysaccharide has been isolated from red algae such as the edible red seaweed TAX-2822 and members of the genus TAX-2794 (which gave it the name rhodymenan). Chemical analyses of xylans from TAX-2822 and TAX-1633773 indicated a 1:4 ratio of 1,3-linkages to 1,4 linkages . Xylan from TAX-2837323 has a ratio of 1:2 .

name: a 4,6-diamino-5-formamidopyrimidine in DNA
annotation: One of two major products resulting from the oxidation of adenine in DNA

name: an N-acylethanolamine
annotation: N-Acylethanolamines N-acylethanolamines are a class of endogenous bioactive lipid molecules formed by the linkage of an acyl group to the nitrogen atom of ETHANOL-AMINE. These compounds exert a variety of biological actions through their interactions with various receptors. One of the N-acylethanolamines, CPD-7598, has received much attention because it is an endocanabinoid (an endogenous ligand of the cannabinoid receptors CB1 and CB2). The other N-acylethanolamines do not bind to cannabinoid receptors.

name: a glucuronoxylan oligosaccharide
annotation: Glucuronoxylans Glucuronoxylans are linear polymers of β-D-xylopyranosyl units linked by (1→4) glycosidic bonds, with many of the xylose units substituted with (1→2)- and/or (1→3)-linked glucuronate residues. Many of the D-glucuronosyl units are methylated at position 4. Angiosperm (hardwood) glucuronoxylans also have a high rate of substitution (70-80%) by acetyl groups, at position 2 and/or 3 of the β-D-xylopyranosyl, conferring on the xylan its partial solubility in water. Glucuronoxylan oligosaccharides are formed from glucuronoxylan by the action of glucuronoxylanase enzymes (3.2.1.136-RXN "EC 3.2.1.136")

name: a glyceollin
annotation: Glyceollins are the main phytoalexins found in soybean (Glycine max). Consistent with most of the isoflavonoid-derived phytoalexins, glyceollins are induced in response to plant pathogens and related stress .

name: a gramicidin
annotation: The general term Gramicidin usually refers to a heterogeneous mixture of three antibiotic compounds, CPD-10415, CPD-10416, and CPD-10417, in a ratio of 80, 5 and 15%, respectively. All of these compounds are linear pentadecapeptides produced by TAX-1393, and the only difference among them is the amino acid at position 11, which is tryptophan, phenylalanine, or tyrosine, respectively. This heterogeneous mixture is often referred to as gramicidn D, and is one of the three constituents of the consumer antibiotic Neosporin. These linear compounds are different from CPD-10290, which is a cyclic pentadecapeptide (see PWY-6003).
notes: audit_collides_with=molecule.ecocyc.Gramicidin

name: a (hydroxy)phthioceranyl-CoA
annotation: This class stands for the acyl-CoA form of either Phthioceranic-Acids "phthioceranic acids" or Hydroxyphthioceranic-Acids "hydroxyphthioceranic acids". In heptamethylated phthioceranic acids, R1,R2,R3 = H. In octamethylated phthioceranic acids, R1,R3 = H, R2=CH3. In heptamethylated hydroxyphthioceranic acids, R2,R3 = H, R1=OH. In octamethylated hydroxyphthioceranic acids, R1= H, R2=CH3, and R3=OH.

name: an icosanoid
annotation: Icosanoids (often spelled eicosanoids) are signaling molecules generated by oxidation of 20-carbon fatty acids. Icosanoids participate in many cellular processes including promoting or inhibiting inflammation; responding to allergy, fever and other immune system challenges; regulating normal childbirth; contributing to the perception of pain; regulating cell growth; controlling blood pressure; and modulating the regional flow of blood to tissues. There are multiple subfamilies of icosanoids, the main of which are the Prostaglandins prostaglandins, Thromboxanes thromboxanes, Leukotrienes leukotrienes, Lipoxins lipoxins, Resolvins resolvins, and Eoxins eoxins. Each family can have up to 4 series of compounds, which are derived from the ω-3 fatty acid 5Z8Z11Z14Z17Z-EICOSAPENTAENOATE), the ω-6 fatty acids ARACHIDONIC_ACID [(5Z,8Z,11Z,14Z)-icosatetraenoate] and CPD-8120 [(8Z,11Z,14Z)-icosatrienoate], and the ω-9 fatty acid CPD-24529, respectively.

name: a [glycerolipid]-densipolate
annotation: CPD-12192 Densipolate is an unusual hydroxylated fatty acid characterized from TAX-98013 seed oil, of which it is the major contituent . Like other fatty acids, CPD-12192 is rarely found in its free form. It is usually found either as CPD-15365 or CPD-17397 "incorporated into a lipid".

name: pullulan
annotation: Pullulan is a polysaccharide polymer consisting of MALTOTRIOSE units connected to each other by an α-1,6 glycosidic bond. Pullulan is produced from starch by the fungus TAX-5580.

name: a medium-chain alkane
annotation: Alkanes are saturated acyclic hydrocarbons. They consist only of hydrogen and saturated carbon atoms and are bonded exclusively by single bonds. They could be either branched or unbranched. Linear alkanes are often classified by the length of their acyl moiety. Short-Chain-Alkanes "Short-chain alkanes" contain less than 6- carbons. Medium-Chain-Alkanes "Medium-chain alkanes" contain 6-12 carbons. Long-Chain-Alkanes "Long-chain alkanes" contain 13-22 carbons. Very-Long-Chain-Alkanes "Very-long-chain alkanes" contain 23 carbons or longer.

name: a pyran
annotation: A pyran is a six-membered heterocyclic, non-aromatic ring, consisting of five carbon atoms and one oxygen atom and containing two double bonds. There are two isomers of pyran that differ by the location of the double bonds - CPD-15489 and CPD-15490.

name: an azasugar
annotation: An azasugar is a nitrogen derivatives of a sugar in which a PIPERIDINE piperidine structure replaces the CPD-23528 structure.

name: a mycolipenoate
aliases: a mycolipenic acid|a mycothienoic acid|a mycothienoate
annotation: A unique feature of mycobacterial cell wall lipids is the presence of very long chain fatty acids with multiple methyl branches toward the carboxyl end of the carbon chain. Many types of such acids exist, including Mycosanoic-Acids "mycosanoic acids", Mycolipanoic-Acids "mycolipanoic acids", Mycolipanolic-Acids "mycolipanolic acids", Mycolipenic-Acids "mycolipenic acids", Mycolipodienoic-Acids "mycolipodienoic acids", Phthioceranic-Acids "phthioceranic acids", and Mycocerosic-Acids "mycocerosic acids". Mycolipenoic acids are 2,4,6-trimethylated 2-alkenoic very-long-chain acids. They typically contain 22-28 carbons and are found only in virulent strains . They were found to be inhibitory to leukocyte migration and leukotoxic.

name: a monosaccharide
annotation: Monosaccharides are polyhydroxy aldehydes or polyhydroxy ketones with three or more carbon atoms. The generic term monosaccharide denotes a single unit, without glycosidic connection to other such units. Monosaccharides with an aldehydic carbonyl group are called aldoses, while those with a ketonic carbonyl carbonyl group are called ketoses. In addition to aldoses and ketose, the term 'monosaccharide' also includes additional forms, such as dialdoses, alduloses, and diketoses, as well as deoxy and amino monosaccharides, and their derivatives, provided that the parent compound has a (potential) carbonyl group.

name: α-carrageenose
annotation: Carrageenans Carrageenans and Carrageenoses carrageenoses are sulfated polysaccharides that are extracted from sea weeds belonging to the TAX-2763 (red algae), mainly from TAX-2769, and species from the TAX-31444 and TAX-33664 genera. They are laid out in the cell wall at a high density and can constitute up to 50% of the algae dry mass. The basic units of Carrageenans carrageenans are repeats of alternating α-1,3-linked D-galactose and β-1,4-linked D-galactose units, which are often substituted by sulfate. Carrageenans are classified according to the number and the position of sulfated ester . In Carrageenoses carrageenoses the α-1,3-linke galactose units are modified by having a 3,6-anhydro bridge. These bridges, which form a double helix during aggregation, are responsible for the gelation that is typical to these compounds. Despite this distinction, carrageenoses are often referred to as carrageenans. The three most industrially exploited carrageenans, namely, kappa-Carrageenan, iota-Carrageenan and Lambda-Carrageenan, are distinguished by the presence of one, two or three ester-sulfate groups per repeating disaccharide unit respectively. theta-Carrageenan is sulfated at position 2 of both the β-1,4-linked D-galactose units and the α-1,3-linked 3,6-anhydrogalactose units.

name: a protopanaxadiol-type ginsenoside with two glucosyl residues at position 3
annotation: Ginsenosides Ginsenosides or panaxosides are a class of steroid glycosides and triterpene saponins found exclusively in the plant genus TAX-4053 (ginseng). They are the principal components responsible for the pharmaceutical activities of ginseng. Based on the structure of their aglycones, ginsenosides have been categorized into three broad types; Protopanaxadiol-type-ginsenosides "protopanaxadiol-type ginsenosides" (PPD, containing hydroxyl groups at positions 3 and 20), Protopanaxatriol-type-ginsenosides "protopanaxatriol-type ginsenosides" (PPT, containing hydroxyl groups at positions 3, 6 and 20), and Oleanonic-acid-type-ginsenosides "oleanonic-acid-type saponins".

name: a phenolphthiotriol A
annotation: Phenolphthiotriol Phenolphthiotriols are intermediates in Phenolphthiocerols phenolphthiocerol biosynthesis. Phenolphthiocerol glycolipids have been found in many Mycobacterium species, including TAX-1769, TAX-1768, TAX-1765, TAX-1781, and TAX-78331 . Two types of phenolphthiotriol exist. In Phenolphthiotriol-A "phenolphthiotriol A" the first hydroxyl and the methyl groups are attached at positions 3 and 4, respectively, and the other hydroxyl groups are at positions 9 and 11. In Phenolphthiotriol-B "phenolphthiotriol B" a methyl group is missing from the beginning of the molecule, and thus the first hydroxyl and the methyl groups are attached at positions 2 and 3, respectively, and the other hydroxyl groups are at positions 8 and 10.

name: a (2'R)-2'-hydroxy-phytoceramide
annotation: Alpha-hydroxyphytoceramides "An α hydroxyphytoceramide" is Phytoceramides in which the fatty acid moiety is hydroxylated. The hydroxylation usually occurs at the carbon closest to the amide bond, and is not visible in the structure shown here. Alpha-hydroxyphytoceramides "α Hydroxyphytoceramides" are produced by yeast and plants. These compounds are also known as "plant ceramide (V)".

name: an adenine-type cytokinin
annotation: Cytokinins are a class of plant hormones that promote cell division, or cytokinesis, in plant roots and shoots. While their main role is to control cell growth and differentiation, they also affect apical dominance, axillary bud growth, and leaf senescence. The cytokinins are structurally diverse, and have been divided into two main types: Adenine-Cytokinins "adenine-type cytokinins" and Phenylurea-Cytokinins "phenylurea-type cytokinins". All the natural cytokinins fall into the first type - phenylurea-type cytokinins are synthetic. The adenine cytokinins are classified further by the configuration of their N6-side chain as isoprenoid or aromatic cytokinins. The most prevalent cytokinins are by far those with an unsaturated isoprenoid side chain, and in particular those with a trans-hydroxylated N6-side chain, such as CPD-4210 and its derivatives. Most of the adenine-type cytokinins are modified by glucosylation of the adenine ring at the 3-, the 7-, or 9-position and conjugation of alanine at the 9-position of zeatin, forming lupinic acid. With the exception of the 3-glucosides, the N-glucosyl and alanyl conjugates generally exhibit no or low activity. The most important modifications are those affecting the N6-side chain, where even small substitutions have pronounced effects on cytokinin activity. The most common modifications of the side chain include reduction (e.g. trans-zeatin to dihydrozeatin), conjugation to O-glycosides (O-glucosides and O-xylosides), and side chain cleavage by cytokinin oxidases .

name: a bornane monoterpenoid
annotation: Bornane monoterpenoids are compounds based on the CPD-27361 skeleton.

name: a coelenteramide
annotation: OPLOPHORUS-LUCIFERIN Coelenterazine and its analogues are the luciferase substrate (=luciferin) in the light-emitting process of bioluminescent coelenterates as well as various, distantly related, types of organisms such as squids, shrimps, fishes, and some chaetognaths . It is also found in many nonluminous organisms . In bioluminescent systems, OPLOPHORUS-LUCIFERIN coelenterazine is bound by either Coelenterazine-binding-proteins "coelenterazine-binding protein" or by Aequorins "an aequorin" protein, depending on the species. In the first system, upon binding of Ca2+ ions the coelenterazine-binding protein releases coelenterazine, which is then oxidized and decarboxylated by the luciferase enzyme, resulting in CPD-20235. In the second system, aequorin binds and oxidizes coelenterazine, retainning it in the form of bound CPD-20222. Upon binding of Ca2+ ions, the enzyme catalyzes the decarboxylation and formation of CPD-20235. In vitro the excited coelenteramide relaxes to the ground state by emission of blue light. However, in vivo the excitation energy is transferred to GFP by Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) . Coelenterazine is a derivative of imidazopyrazinone. Its biosynthetic origin is not known. However, it has been shown that the hydromedusa TAX-6100 is not able to synthesize it, and is dependent on a dietary supply of this luciferin for bioluminescence . The only report of de novo biosynthesis of coelenterazine is from the bioluminescent shrimp TAX-576189 .

name: a xyloglucan
annotation: Xyloglucan Xyloglucan is the major component of the Hemicelluloses in cell walls of dicotyledon type I and non-graminaceous monocotyledon plants. It also exists in some seeds of trees growing in the tropical zone. Xyloglucan interacts with cellulose microfibrils via hydrogen bonds to form a structural network that is assumed to play a key role in cell wall integrity. Cross-linking between the xyloglucan chains is a major factor in the stability of the resulting three-dimensional matrix . Xyloglucan has a backbone of β-1,4-linked glucose residues, most of which are substituted in C-6 by α-D-xylosyl residues . In most plants some of the xylosyl residues are further substituted by (1,2)-β-D-galactosyl residues, and some of the galactosyl residues are capped by (1,2)-α-L-fucose . Other types of xyloglucans, for example those found in the Poaeceae and Solanaceae, feature xylose units that bear α-L-arabinosyl instead of β-D-galactosyl residues. A common terminology uses G for basic non-substituted glucosyl units within a xylosylglycan, X for glucose residues substituted by α-D-xylose, L for glucose residues substituted by α-D-xylose and β-D-galactose, and F for glucose residues substituted by α-D-xylose, β-D-galactose and α-L-fucose . Fucosyl residues are typically absent from seed xyloglucans but present on the xyloglucans in the vegatative portions of the same plant . Most xylosylglucans consist of repeating heptasaccharide Xyloglucans-Xylose XXXG units. The average molecular weight of xyloglucan is more than 50,000 . A common form of xyloglucan that is often used in enzyme assays is produced from tamarind (TAX-58860) seeds.

name: an anthocyanidin
annotation: Anthocyanidins are common plant pigments derived from a CPD-15025 ion. The anthocyanidins are sugar-free, their glycosylated forms are known as Anthocyanins anthocyanins.

name: a glucocorticoid
annotation: Glucocorticoids are a class of steroid hormones that regulate a variety of physiological processes, in particular control of the concentration of glucose in blood. They bind to the glucocorticoid receptor (GR), which is present in almost every vertebrate animal cell. The name glucocorticoid is derived from the role in the regulation of glucose metabolism, the synthesis in the adrenal cortex, and the steroidal structure.

name: melanin
annotation: Melanin is a broad class of compounds derived from TYR and found in plants, animals, and protists. Melanins are excellent photoprotectants due to their photochemical properties. They absorbs UV-radiation and transforms the energy into heat with minimal production of free radicals. Common forms of animal melanins include eumelanin (the most abundant melanin in humans), pheomelanin (responsible for red hair) and neuromelanin (found in the brain). Melanins are often used for defense against different types of stresss.

name: a linoleate-derived metabolite
annotation: The oxidation of the polyunsaturated fatty acids LINOLEIC_ACID, CPD-8120, ARACHIDONIC_ACID, LINOLENIC_ACID, 5Z8Z11Z14Z17Z-EICOSAPENTAENOATE icosapentaenoate (EPA) and CPD-10244 docosahexaenoate (DHA) results a wide array of lipid metabolites, including (but not limited to) Prostaglandins prostaglandins, Thromboxanes thromboxanes, Leukotrienes leukotrienes, Lipoxins lipoxins, Resolvins resolvins, and Eoxins eoxins. The best-studied of these are the compounds derived from C20 acids, which are known as Icosanoids icosanoids. Many of these compounds act as signaling molecules that play prominent roles in many physiological processes. In mammals they participate in the regulation of many key biological processes in the cardiovascular, renal, reproductive and other systems, with a particular important role in inflammation.

name: a 5-hydroxymethylcytosine in DNA
annotation: This compound class stands for generic and unspecified DNA 5-hydroxymethylcytosines. Phage T4 DNA differs from typical DNA molecules in that it contains no CYTOSINE. Instead, in contains the modified base CPD-15531 which pairs with GUANINE. The hydroxy group is often glucosylated, as in Alpha-Glycosylated-Hydroxymethyl-DNAs "α-glucosyl-hydroxymethylcytosine", Beta-Glycosylated-Hydroxymethylated-DNAs "β-glucosyl-hydroxymethylcytosine" and Gluc-Glycosyl-Hydroxymethylcytosine-DNAs "β-glucosyl-(1→4)-β-glucosyl-hydroxymethylcytosine", so that T4 DNA is typically covered with sugar chains.

name: an inositol
annotation: Inositols Inositol is the generic name for 1,2,3,4,5,6-cyclohexanehexol. Epimerization of the six hydroxyl groups results in nine stereoisomers. Among the isomers, MYO-INOSITOL is the most abundant in nature and it is an essential compound in eukaryotic cell membranes as a phosphatidylinositol moiety, which plays an important role in signal transduction. Some other forms, such as CPD-8050, are also found in nature, although they are less abundant. Nomenclature of cyclitols can be quite confusing. Two alternative naming schemes are possible for inositol compounds since carbon-1 could be assigned to either of two enantiotopic carbons. The two methods result in 1D-stereochemistry and 1L-stereochemistry based names. If carbon-1 is placed at the top of the molecule, L-numbering proceeds clockwise, while D-numbering proceeds counter-clockwise. Both the 1D or 1L designations can be employed currently. However, the symbol Ins implies myo-inositol with D- numbering. Additional information on the nomenclature of myo-inositol compounds can be found in the 1973 document Nomenclature of cyclitols by the Nomenclature Committee of the International Union of Biochemistry (NC-IUB), and the 1988 publication Numbering of atoms in myo-inositol, published by the same body, whose aim was to relax some of the rules specified in the 1973 publication. To aid in following the transformations in inositol compounds in pathways, the structures of inositol stereoisomers in MetaCyc have been formatted to show carbon 1 at the top of the ring, with numbering proceeding clockwise. An exception is the structure of MYO-INOSITOL, which is presented with carbon 3 at the top and counter-clockwise numbering. The reason is that carbon 3 of MYO-INOSITOL (which is based on L-numbering) matches the carbon-1 of 1D

annotation: Carrageenans Carrageenans and Carrageenoses carrageenoses are sulfated polysaccharides that are extracted from sea weeds belonging to the TAX-2763 (red algae), mainly from TAX-2769, and species from the TAX-31444 and TAX-33664 genera. They are laid out in the cell wall at a high density and can constitute up to 50% of the algae dry mass. The basic units of Carrageenans carrageenans are repeats of alternating α-1,3-linked D-galactose and β-1,4-linked D-galactose units, which are often substituted by sulfate. Carrageenans are classified according to the number and the position of sulfated ester . In Carrageenoses carrageenoses the α-1,3-linked galactose units are modified by having a 3,6-anhydro bridge. These bridges, which form a double helix during aggregation, are responsible for the gelation that is typical to these compounds. Despite this distinction, carrageenoses are often referred to as carrageenans. The three most industrially exploited carrageenans, namely, kappa-Carrageenan, iota-Carrageenan and Lambda-Carrageenan, are distinguished by the presence of one, two or three ester-sulfate groups per repeating disaccharide unit respectively.

name: cypemycin
annotation: Cypemycin is a modified peptide antibiotic produced by TAX-932694. It has potent activity against mouse leukemia cells . The peptide is classified as a member of the lantibiotic family of peptides mostly due to the presence of dehydrobutyrine (Dhb) and S-[(Z)-2-aminovinyl]-D-cysteine (Avi-Cys) residues. Six distinct posttranslational modifications participate in cypemycin biosynthesis, altering 9 of the 22 amino acids found in the mature peptide, including four threonine residues that are dehydrated to form Dhb, one cysteine that is dethiolated to form dehydroalanine, a C-terminal Cys that is oxidatively decarboxylated to form an enethiolate, two isoleucine residues that are isomerized to form L-allo-Isoleucine, and two N-methylations of the N-terminal alanine .

name: a Good's buffer substance
annotation: Good's buffer substances are compounds that are suitable to serve as buffers in experimental biological systems. Named after Dr. Norman Good .

name: an apiogalacturonan
annotation: Apiogalacturonans "Apiogalacturonan" is a substituted 1-4-alpha-D-galacturonosyl "homogalacturonan" in which the α-1,4-linked D-GALACTURONATE backbone is substituted at position O-3 with CPD-13046. The homogalacturonan backbone is partially methylesterified at the C-6 carboxyl, and may be O-acetylated at positions O-2 or O-3.

annotation: Like other fatty acids, DIHYDROSTERCULIC-ACID is rarely found in its free form. It is usually found Lipid-dihydrosterculate "incorporated into a lipid".

name: an auxin
annotation: Auxins are a class of plant hormones, and were the first of the major plant hormones to be discovered. The distribution of auxins within the plant is a key factor that influences growth, reaction to the environment, and organ development. The four naturally occurring auxins are INDOLE_ACETATE_AUXIN (IAA), CPD-14644, PHENYLACETATE and CPD-10507; IAA is by far the most important auxin and generates the majority of auxin effects in intact plants

name: an aryl sulfate
annotation: The R group in this structure represents a six-membered aromatic ring.

name: a TAG(18:2/36:0)
annotation: This class of triacylglycerols contains three acyl chains formed by the esterification of glycerol with three fatty acids. The names of triacylglycerol compound classes provide information about the three acyl chains of each member of the class, using the nomenclature TAG(a:b/y:z) where "a" represents the number of carbons found in one acyl chain and "b" represents the number of desaturations present in chain "a" and where "y" represents the total number of carbons found in the other two acyl chains and "z" represents the total number of desaturations present in the two chains included in "y." Therefore, in this TAG(18:2/36:0) class of compounds, one acyl chain has 18 carbons and two double bonds. The other two acyl chains have a total of 36 carbons and no double bonds. The attachment sites of the acyl chains at the R1 (sn-1), R2 (sn-2), or R3 (sn-3) position along the glyceryl backbone may vary among class members and cannot be inferred from their order in the compound class name. Additionally, the location and orientation of the double bonds in the unsaturated acyl chain(s) may vary among members of the class and are not specified by this notation.

name: an amidine
annotation: Amidine compounds are derivatives of carboxylic acids (R-COOH) in which the hydroxy group is replaced by an amino or a modified amino group and the oxo group is replaced by R=N. The most common amidines in organic chemistry are Carboxamidine carboxamidines (RC(=NR)NR2).

name: a flavanone glycoside
annotation: A member of the class of flavanones having one or more glycosyl residues attached at unspecified positions.

name: a viologen
annotation: Viologens are organic compounds with the formula (C5H4NR)2n+. In some viologens, the pyridyl groups are further modified. Viologens are named as such because they produce violet color upon reduction.

name: an urushiol
annotation: Urushiol Urushiols are a class of compounds found in the sap of plants of the family TAX-4011, especially members of the TAX-43852 genus. (e.g., poison oak, poison ivy, poison sumac). The urushiols consist of a catechol substituted with an alkyl chain that has 15 or 17 carbon atoms that may be saturated or unsaturated. Most plants have a mixture of several compounds, and the exact composition varies depending on the plant souce Urushiols cause an allergic skin rash on contact, with the likelihood and severity of allergic reaction dependent on the degree of unsaturation of the alkyl chain. Urushiol is an oleoresin. After injury to the plant it leaks to the surface where it contacts oxygen. The oxidation and polymerization of urushiol in the tree's sap in the presence of moisture allows it to form a hard lacquer, which is used to produce traditional Chinese and Japanese lacquerware. The name comes from kiurushi, the Japanese name for the lacquer produced in East Asia from the sap of TAX-4013 (the Lacquer Tree).

name: a diphenyl ether
annotation: Diphenyl ethers are aromatic ethers in which an oxygen is attached to two phenyl substituents.

name: a leucoanthocyanidin
annotation: a leucoanthocyanidin is Flavanols with hydroxy groups at positions 3 and 4. Further oxygenation may be present at other ring positions.

name: a purine ribonucleoside
annotation: Nucleosides are glycosylamines consisting of Bases bound to a CPD0-1110 ribose or 2-DEOXYRIBOSE sugar via a β-glycosidic linkage. The nucleosides are called Ribonucleosides ribonucleosides when the sugar is CPD0-1110 ribose, and Deoxy-Ribonucleosides deoxyribonucleosides when it is 2-DEOXYRIBOSE. Common Ribonucleosides ribonucleosides include CYTIDINE, URIDINE, ADENOSINE, GUANOSINE and INOSINE. Examples of Deoxy-Ribonucleosides deoxyribonucleosides include THYMIDINE and DEOXYADENOSINE. Purines are heterocyclic aromatic compounds that contain a PYRIMIDINE-RING ring fused to an IMIDAZOLE-RING ring. Two of the ribonucleosides in nucleic acids, ADENOSINE and GUANOSINE, are purines.

name: an aldose
annotation: An aldose is a monosaccharide that contains only one aldehyde group per molecule, located at a terminal carbon. It has the chemical formula H[CH(OH)]nC(=O)H, where n is equal or greater than 2. The definition of aldose also includes the intramolecular hemiacetals of such compounds. Aldoses can isomerize into Ketoses ketoses.

name: chondroitin sulfate A
annotation: Chondroitin-sulfates "Chondroitin sulfate" is a sulfated Glycosaminoglycans "glycosaminoglycan" composed of alternating units of CPD-12557 and CPD-12521. It is usually found attached to proteins as part of a proteoglycan. The saccharide units can be sulfated in variable positions and quantities - CPD-12557 can be sulfated at positions 4 and 6 to yield CPD-12516, CPD-12565 or CPD-12526, while CPD-12521 can be sulfated at position 2 to form CPD-12527. The following names have been used to define different sulfation states: Chondroitin-Sulfate-A "Chondroitin sulfate A" contains CPD-12521 and CPD-12516 Chondroitin-Sulfate-C "Chondroitin sulfate C" contains CPD-12521 and CPD-12565 Chondroitin-Sulfate-D "Chondroitin sulfate D" contains CPD-12527 and CPD-12565 Chondroitin-Sulfate-E "Chondroitin sulfate E" contains CPD-12521 and CPD-12526 The term "chondroitin sulfate B" has been used in the past to refer to Dermatan-Sulfate, but is not used any more.

name: a quinoline
annotation: The quinolines are aromatic heterocyclic compounds that contain a BENZENE ring ortho fused to carbons 2 and 3 of a PYRIDINE ring, as in QUINOLINE.

name: a pyoverdine
annotation: Pyoverdines are a group of structurally related siderophores produced by fluorescent Pseudomonas species, in which they provide the primary iron uptake system. Even though the structures of more than 50 different pyoverdines from different strains and species of Pseudomonas have been determined, they all possess a similar structure composed of three parts: a conserved fluorescent dihydroxyquinoline chromophore, an acyl side chain (either dicarboxylic acid or amide) bound to the amino group of the chromophore (R1 in the structure below), and a variable peptide chain linked by an amide group bound to the C1 (rarely C3) carboxyl group of the chromophore (R2 in the structure below) .

name: a β-D-glucan with a C3-substituted glucose
annotation: This compound class stands for a β-D-glucan that contains an internal glucose residue with a substitution at C-3, at the non-reducing end of a glycosyl linkage. The linkage between the glucose residues may be either β(1,3) or β(1,4) linkage (the illustarion here depicts the former).
