name: S. cerevisiae mannan (simplified)
annotation: The mannan found in the cell walls of TAX-4932 is composed of a linear chain of α-(1,6)-D-mannan decorated by side chains and attached to ASN residues of proteins by a linkage unit consisting of two β-(1,4)-N-acetylglucosamine residues. The composition of the side chains varies, but largely consists of α-(1,3)-mannosyl-α-(1,2)-mannosyl-α-(1,2)-mannosyl chains. The side chain can be shorter, and can be branched as well. For the sake of simplicity, the structure shown here shows only a single side chain. In the actual polymer almost all of the residues of the backbone are decorated with side chains.

name: a phthiocerol A
annotation: Phthiocerols Phthiocerol-based lipids and glycolipids have been found in many species of TAX-1762, including TAX-1773, TAX-1765, TAX-33894, TAX-1781, TAX-1768, TAX-1777, TAX-1769 and TAX-1809 . Phthiocerols comprise a linear carbohydrate molecule to which one methoxyl group, one methyl group, and two secondary hydroxyl groups are attached. Two types of phthiocerols have been described. In Phthiocerol-A "phthiocerol A" the methoxyl and methyl groups are attached at positions 3 and 4, respectively, and the hydroxyl groups are at positions 9 and 11. In Phthiocerol-B "phthiocerol B" a methyl group is missing from the beginning of the molecule, and thus the methoxyl and methyl groups are attached at positions 2 and 3, respectively, and the hydroxyl groups are at positions 8 and 10. The two hydroxyl groups serve as attachment points for fatty acids, in a manner analogous to the much more ubiquitous sn-glycerol. The phthiocerol molecules have a range of 28 to 34 carbon atoms in the main chain, indicating a promiscuity of the enzymes involved in their biosynthesis. Longer molecules have additional carbon atoms added at the ω end of the molecule. In most organisms the two hydroxyl groups are esterified by Mycocerosic-Acids "mycocerosic acids". In TAX-1781 and TAX-1809, however, they are esterified by Phthioceranic-Acids "phthioceranic acids" .

name: idose
annotation: Idose is not found in nature, though its uronic acids, D-Iduronate and to a lesser extent L-Iduronate, are common. L-idose is the C-5 epimer of D-Glucose.

name: a steroid hormone
annotation: Steroid hormones are steroids that act as hormones. Steroid hormones are divided into five groups based on the receptors to which they bind: Glucocorticoids glucocorticoids, Mineralocorticoids mineralocorticoids, Androgens androgens, Steroidal-Estrogens "steroidal estrogens", and Progestogens progestogens. Certain vitamin D derivatives (e.g. CALCITRIOL) are sometimes considered a sixth steroid hormone group. The natural steroid hormones of mammals are generally synthesized from cholesterol in the gonads and adrenal glands.

name: a monolignol
annotation: Monolignols, also called lignols, are the source materials for biosynthesis of both lignans and lignin.

annotation: Bacillosamine is a rare amino sugar that was discovered in TAX-1402. It serves as a link glycan in bacterial glycoproteins. Note that bacillosamine is a reducing sugar that exists in solution in multiple configurations. The open-chain structure shown here is provided only as a schematic illustration of chirality. name: a TAG(18:2/34:0)
annotation: This class of triacylglycerols contains three acyl chains formed by the esterification of glycerol with three fatty acids. The names of triacylglycerol compound classes provide information about the three acyl chains of each member of the class, using the nomenclature TAG(a:b/y:z) where "a" represents the number of carbons found in one acyl chain and "b" represents the number of desaturations present in chain "a" and where "y" represents the total number of carbons found in the other two acyl chains and "z" represents the total number of desaturations present in the two chains included in "y." Therefore, in this TAG(18:2/34:0) class of compounds, one acyl chain has 18 carbons and two double bonds. The other two acyl chains have a total of 34 carbons and no double bonds. The attachment sites of the acyl chains at the R1 (sn-1), R2 (sn-2), or R3 (sn-3) position along the glyceryl backbone may vary among class members and cannot be inferred from their order in the compound class name. Additionally, the location and orientation of the double bonds in the unsaturated acyl chain(s) may vary among members of the class and are not specified by this notation.

name: an A vitamin
annotation: Vitamin A is a group of related compounds that include Retinols retinol, Retinals retinal, Retinoates retinoate, and several provitamin A carotenoids, among which CPD1F-129 is the most important. Vitamin A has multiple functions: it is important for growth and development, for the maintenance of the immune system and for vision. Vitamin A is needed by the retina of the eye in the form of retinal, which combines with protein Opsins opsin to form the light-absorbing molecule rhodopsin (see PWY-6861). Vitamin A, in the form of retinoic acid, is also an important hormone-like growth factor for epithelial and other cells. In foods of animal origin, the major form of vitamin A are Retinyl-Esters esters, primarily CHOCOLA_A, which are converted to retinol in the small intestine.

name: a cerebroside
annotation: Cerebrosides consist of Ceramides with a single sugar residue at the 1-hydroxyl moiety. The sugar residue can be either glucose or galactose, and the two major types are thus named Glucosyl-ceramides glucocerebrosides and D-galactosylceramide galactocerebrosides. Galactocerebrosides are typically found in neural tissue, while glucocerebrosides are found in other tissues.

name: a protopanaxatriol-type ginsenoside with a single glucosyl at position 6
annotation: Ginsenosides Ginsenosides or panaxosides are a class of steroid glycosides and triterpene saponins found exclusively in the plant genus TAX-4053 (ginseng). They are the principal components responsible for the pharmaceutical activities of ginseng. Based on the structure of their aglycones, ginsenosides have been categorized into three broad types; Protopanaxadiol-type-ginsenosides "protopanaxadiol-type ginsenosides" (PPD, containing hydroxyl groups at positions 3 and 20), Protopanaxatriol-type-ginsenosides "protopanaxatriol-type ginsenosides" (PPT, containing hydroxyl groups at positions 3, 6 and 20), and Oleanonic-acid-type-ginsenosides "oleanonic-acid-type saponins".

name: a chlorin
annotation: A chlorin is a large heterocyclic aromatic ring consisting of 3 pyrroles and one reduced pyrrole, coupled through 4 methine linkages. Unlike Porphyrins, a chlorin is largely aromatic, but not aromatic through the entire circumference of the ring. Chlorophylls are Mg-containing chlorin derivatives.

name: an oligodeoxyribonucleotide
annotation: An oligodeoxyribonucleotide is a short nucleic acid polymer with 50 or fewer deoxyribonucleotides. Oligodeoxyribonucleotide can be either single- or double-stranded.

name: a medium-chain fatty aldehyde
annotation: Fatty-Aldehydes "Fatty aldehydes" are aldehydes that are formed by the reduction of the carboxylic acid group of Fatty-Acids fatty acids. Many enzymes that act on fatty acids, as well as the corresponding Fatty-Acyl-CoAs "fatty acyl-CoAs", Fatty-Alcohols alcohols, and Fatty-Aldehydes aldehydes, have evolved to recognize substrates with a limited range of chain lengths. To classify these enzymes, it is helpful to divide the substrates into smaller groups based on their chain length. The following subgroups have been defined: Short-Chain-Aldehydes "Short-chain fatty aldehydes" are derived from Short-chain-fatty-acids "fatty acids that have 3-5 carbons in the longest chain". Medium-Chain-Aldehydes "Medium-chain fatty aldehydes" are derived from Medium-chain-fatty-acids "fatty acids that have 6-12 carbons in the longest chain". Long-Chain-Aldehydes "Long-chain fatty aldehydes" are derived from Long-Chain-Fatty-Acids "fatty acids that have 13-22 carbons in the longest chain". Very-Long-Chain-Aldehydes "Very-long-chain fatty aldehydes" are derived from Very-long-chain-fatty-acids "fatty acids that have 23-27 carbons in the longest chain". Ultra-Long-Chain-Aldehydes "Ultra-long-chain fatty aldehydes" are derived from Ultra-long-chain-fatty-acids "fatty acids that have more than 27 carbons in the longest chain".

name: a bacteriochlorophyll e
annotation: Green sulfur bacteria (TAX-191412) and green flimentous bacteria (TAX-1106) contain chlorosomes - large organelles that are believed to be the most efficient light-harvesting antennae in nature . Green sulfur bacteria can grow at light intensities that are too low to support growth of any other phototroph, up to a million times lower light than normal sunlight . Chlorosomes contain about 1,500 bacteriochlorophyll molecules per reaction center. They are filled with 10-30 rod-shaped elements which are oriented parallel to the long axis of the chlorosomes and have a diameter of 10 nm. Each rod consists of a tubular bilayer formed by self-organization of the antenna bacteriochlorophyll molecules. Unlike all other known photosynthetic antenna, in which the chlorophyll molecules are bound to proteins, chlorosomes do not contain accessory proteins. The chlorosomal bacteriochlorophyll pigments are able to self-aggregate due to their unique chemical characteristics. The bacteriochlorophylls found in chlorosomes are either CPD-9092 "bacteriochlorophyll c", CPD-9093 "bacteriochlorophyll d" or CPD-9094 "bacteriochlorophyll e", depending on the species. They all possess a hydroxyl group at the C-31 position that allows them to chelate the Mg atom of a neighboring molecule and to interact with the C-13-oxo carbonyl group of another neighboring pigment molecule, affording the self-aggregation . In addition, all of these bacteriochlorophylls lack C-132-methylcarboxyl side chains, which are found in all other (bacterio)chlorophylls but would interfere with the ability to form aggregates . Despite of their names, CPD-9092 "bacteriochlorophyll c", CPD-9093 "bacteriochlorophyll d" and CPD-9094 "bacteriochlorophyll e" are actually chlorins rather than bacteriochlorins. All three are usually

name: cyanophycin
annotation: Cyanophycin Cyanophycin is an amino acid polymer that is found in most cyanobacteria. It is a high-molecular weight copolymer, composed of an L-ASPARTATE backbone and ARG side groups, with typical size of 25-100 kDa. The arginine moieties are linked to the β-carboxyl group of each aspartate by their α-amino group. Cyanophycin is largely insoluble under physiological conditions and is accumulated in the form of granules in the cytoplasm during phosphate or sulfur starvation, generally in the early and mid-stationary phase . The polymer serves as a temporary nitrogen reserve, which accumulates during the transition from the exponential- to the stationary-growth phase and disappears when balanced growth resumes. ARG contains 4 nitrogen atoms per molecule, the highest N:C ratio of any amino acid. Even though cyanophycin is considered largely exclusive for cyanobacteria, it is also found in a few heterotrophic bacteria such as TAX-62977 .

name: a prostanoid
annotation: The term prostanoid is used to describe a subclass of Icosanoids icosanoids that includes the Prostaglandins prostaglandins and the Thromboxanes thromboxanes. These lipid compounds are derived from the ω-6 essential fatty acid ARACHIDONIC_ACID. Every prostanoid contains 20 carbon atoms with an internal carbon ring structure.

name: a sialyl-Lewis x epitope
aliases: an sLex antigen|αNeuNAc-(2→3)-βGal-(1→4)-[αFuc-(1→3]-βGlcNAc-[protein]|a sialyl Lewis x antigen|a histo-blood group sialyl Lewis x antigen
annotation: The sialyl Lewis-X (SLeX) antigen is expressed on glycolipids and glycoproteins on the cell surfaces. In adults, SLeX antigen is expressed principally on the surfaces of granulocytic cells. It plays an important role during early steps of inflammatory response as a ligand of the three known selectins - E-selectin (also known as endothelial leucocyte adhesion molecules, ELAM-1), P-Selectin, and L-selectin, resulting in the capture of leucocytes from the plasma and their attachment to blood endothelial cells . In the case of L-selectin, the antigen needs to be sulfated first, forming 6-Sulfo-sLex-Antigen . The antigen is expressed in high frequency in tumor tissue where it plays a role in adhesion between tumor cells and blood endothelial cells during tumor metastases . The SLeX antigen is synthesized from B-Gal-14-NacGlc-R by the action of two enzymes, EC-2.4.3.6 and EC-2.4.1.152 .

name: a mature peptidoglycan (Selenomonas ruminantium)
annotation: The peptidoglycan of TAX-971, a strictly anaerobic bacterium, contains CADAVERINE, which is linked covalently to the α nitrogen of the D-GLT residue . The CADAVERINE is necessary for the normal growth of the bacterium. Inactivation of EC-4.1.1.18, which produces the cadaverine from LYS, results in abnormal cell structure and cell lysis .

name: a lanthipeptide
annotation: Lanthipeptides are a family of RiPPs "ribosomally synthesized and post-translationally modified peptides" (RiPPs) that is characterized by the presence of multiple CPD-3736 (Lan) and CPD-25467 (MeLan) residues. These residues are formed during the cyclization of these compounds, which is catalyzed by lanthipeptide synthases. These enzymes catalyze dehydration of Ser/Thr residues, followed by intramolecular addition of the thiol group of a Cys residue to the dehydro amino acids, which results in the formation of the thioether crosslinks of (methyl)lanthionine . Members of the lanthipeptides that have antimicrobial activity are known as lantibiotics (note that lanthipeptides used to be called lantipeptides, but their name was revised to reflect the presence of lanthionine groups ). Lantibiotic peptides have high affinity for their various targets, which is typically a small molecule . For example, CPD-12301 binds with high affinity to the bacterial cell wall precursor NAcMur-Peptide-NAcGlc-Undecaprenols "lipid II" , and CPD-27028 specifically recognizes L-1-PHOSPHATIDYL-ETHANOLAMINE "phosphatidyl ethanolamine" . Five classes of lanthipeptides have been defined, based on the type of lanthipeptide synthase involved. The biosynthesis of class I lantipeptides involves separate Ser/Thr dehydratases (LanB) and cyclases (LanC), while formation of (methyl)lanthionine in class II compounds is carried out by a single bifunctional enzyme carrying both a dehydratase and a cyclase domain (LanM) . Both class III and class IV involve single three-domain enzymes consisting of an N-terminal lyase, a central kinase and a C-terminal cyclase domains. The difference between these two classes is in the cyclase domain. While class IV enzymes (LanL) have a cyclase domain similar to LanC (which c

name: laminaran
annotation: Laminaran (also known as laminarin) is a storage glucan produced by brown algae. It is a highly water soluble, branched polysaccharide based on a linear β-(1→3)-linked glucose-based chain with an average degree of polymerization (DP) of 20-30 glucose units and β-(1→6)-linked side chains consisting of one or more glucose residues. The ratio of β(1→3) to β(1→6) linkages is 3:1. Measurements conducted in 2019 along transects in the Arctic, Atlantic, and Pacific oceans revealed a median concentration of 26 ± 17% laminarin within the particulate organic carbon pool .

name: a naphthalene
annotation: Naphthalenes are any benzenoid aromatic compound that has a skeleton composed of two ortho-fused benzene rings.

name: a pyrone
annotation: Pyrones (also called pyranones) are a class of heterocyclic chemical compounds. They contain a six-membered heterocyclic, non-aromatic ring consisting of five carbon atoms and one oxygen atom and containing two double bonds and a ketone functional group. Most pyones belong to either of two isomers based on the CPD-11706 (2-pyrone) or CPD-26550 (4-pyrone) structure. The 2-pyrone structure common in nature as part of the Coumarins "coumarin" ring system, while the 4-pyrone structure is found in some natural chemical compounds such as CPD-26551, CPD-15508 and KOJIC-ACID. 3-Pyrone structures exist, but are far less common.

name: a coelenterazine
annotation: OPLOPHORUS-LUCIFERIN Coelenterazine and its analogues are the luciferase substrate (=luciferin) in the light-emitting process of bioluminescent coelenterates as well as various, distantly related, types of organisms such as squids, shrimps, fishes, and some chaetognaths . It is also found in many nonluminous organisms . In bioluminescent systems, OPLOPHORUS-LUCIFERIN coelenterazine is bound by either Coelenterazine-binding-proteins "coelenterazine-binding protein" or by Aequorins "an aequorin" protein, depending on the species. In the first system, upon binding of Ca2+ ions the coelenterazine-binding protein releases coelenterazine, which is then oxidized and decarboxylated by the luciferase enzyme, resulting in CPD-20235. In the second system, aequorin binds and oxidizes coelenterazine, retainning it in the form of bound CPD-20222. Upon binding of Ca2+ ions, the enzyme catalyzes the decarboxylation and formation of CPD-20235. In vitro the excited coelenteramide relaxes to the ground state by emission of blue light. However, in vivo the excitation energy is transferred to GFP by Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) . Coelenterazine is a derivative of imidazopyrazinone. Its biosynthetic origin is not known. However, it has been shown that the hydromedusa TAX-6100 is not able to synthesize it, and is dependent on a dietary supply of this luciferin for bioluminescence . The only report of de novo biosynthesis of coelenterazine is from the bioluminescent shrimp TAX-576189 .

name: PGL-1
annotation: CPD-18578 Phenol-phthiocerol-based glycolipids, known as PGLs, have been found in TAX-1769, TAX-1768, TAX-1765, TAX-1781, and TAX-78331 . In the major PGL of TAX-1769, known as PGL-1, the phenolphthiocerol moiety is attached to the trisaccharide 3,6-di-O-methyl-β-D-glucopyranosyl-(1→4)-2,3-di-O-methyl-α-L-rhamnopyranosyl-(1→3)-3-O-methyl-α-L-rhamnopyranose and the two secondary alcohol functions are esterified by Mycocerosic-Acids "mycocerosic acids" . PGL-1 inhibits the proliferation of T lymphocytes after stimulation with concanavalin A, seems to be associated with resistance to intracellular killing by macrophages, and promotes phagocytosis of TAX-1769 by macrophages and Schwann cells through binding to complement component C3 and laminin-2, respectively.

name: a penicillin
annotation: Penicillins are medically important Beta-Lactams "β-lactam" antibiotics biosynthesized as end products by certain filamentous fungi, including TAX-5076 and TAX-162425. Β-Lactam antibiotics inhibit the formation of peptidoglycan cross-links in the bacterial cell wall by the irreversible binding of their four-membered β-lactam ring to the so-called Penicillin-Binding-Proteins "penicillin binding proteins" (PBPs). The PBPs are proteins that contain a D,D-peptidase domain, most commonly used for hydrolyzing the D-ALA-D-ALA bond found in the pentapetide moiety of peptidoglycans. Since the β-lactam moiety of PENICILLIN "penicillins" is similar to the D-Ala-D-Ala structure, these enzymes bind penicillin irreversibly and become inactive. Several groups of enzymes belong to this family - carboxypeptidases, transpeptidases, and endopeptidases. The carboxypeptidase remove the terminal D-ALANINE from the pentapeptide, the transpeptidase form crosslinks between the pentapeptides of adjacent glycan threads, and the endopeptidases hydrolyze these crosslinks. This irrevesible binding prevents the enzyme from catalyzing the formation of these cross-links, resulting in a defective cell wall and cell death. Penicillin antibiotics were the first drugs that were effective against many previously serious diseases, such as syphilis, and infections caused by staphylococci and streptococci. They are still widely used today, although more and more types of bacteria have acquired resistance.

name: a sialyl-Lewis a epitope
aliases: an sLea antigen|αNeuNAc-(2→3)-βGal-(1→3)-[αFuc-(1→4]-βGlcNAc-[protein]|a histo-blood group sialyl Lewis a epitope
annotation: The sLea-Antigen "sialyl-Lewis a epitope" (SLea) was discovered in 1979 with the use of a monoclonal antibody . While the antigen is believed to play an important role during embryogenesis , in adults only low amounts of the antigen are present on normal epithelial cells . When the selectin family of adhesion molecules was described, it was found that SLea, along with the sLex-Antigen "sialyl-Lewis x antigen", is a ligand for E-selectin. Higher expression of the SLea antigen in adults is strongly associated with cancer. It was found in high amounts on the surface of human pancreatic, colon and gastric cancer cell lines and in human adenocarcinomas of the colon, stomach, pancreas and gall-bladder . The expression of the antigen has been linked to tumor progression. A gradual increase in SLea was found in colon and rectum during neoplastic transformation and progression , and its presence was restricted to tumorigenic and invasive human urothelial cell lines . The antigen is responsible for adhesion of colon, pancreas, urinary bladder and gastric cancer cells to endothelium via its interaction with selectin E . The expression level of the SLea antigen is used as a prognostic factor for colorectal carcinoma, and is associated with high incidence of recurrence and the length of survival time .

name: an avermectin
annotation: The avermectins are a family of 16-membered macrocyclic lactone derivatives with potent anthelmintic and insecticidal properties produced by TAX-33903. The compounds are glycosylated with two molecules of CPD-13948 . Eight different avermectins are produced, consisting of four pairs of homologue compounds, with a major (a-component) and minor (b-component) components that are produced in ratios of 80:20 to 90:10. The a compounds and b compounds differ by having a butyl or a propyl side chain, respectively. Other differences regard the presence or absence of a double bond in one of the ketide rings and a methyl group in one of the CPD-13948 sugars . The avermectins block the transmission of electrical activity in invertebrate nerve and muscle cells mostly by enhancing the effects of glutamate at the glutamate-gated chloride channel that is specific to protostome invertebrates. This results in an influx of chloride ions into the cells, leading to hyperpolarisation and subsequent paralysis of invertebrate neuromuscular systems . Half of the 2015 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine was awarded to William C. Campbell and Satoshi Omura for discovering avermectin. Several derivatives of avermectins have been developed, including CPD-25537, CPD-25538, CPD-22128, CPD-25539, and CPD-25437.

name: a polysialic acid
annotation: Polysialic acids are polymers of N-ACETYLNEURAMINATE "sialic acid" and its derivatives. The degree of polymerization is 8 to 200 Sia residues. Polysialic acid is an unusual posttranslational modification that occurs on the neural cell adhesion molecules (NCAMs). In the synapse, the strong negative charge of the polysialic acid prevents NCAM cross-linking of cells.

annotation: This class of triacylglycerols contains three acyl chains formed by the esterification of glycerol with three fatty acids. Therefore, in this TAG(18:3/36:1) class of compounds, one acyl chain has 18 carbons and three double bonds. The other two acyl chains have a total of 36 carbons and one double bond. The attachment sites of the acyl chains at the R1 (sn-1), R2 (sn-2), or R3 (sn-3) position along the glyceryl backbone may vary among class members and cannot be inferred from their order in the compound class name. name: a glyco-amino acid
annotation: The glycation reaction, also known as the Maillard reaction, as first identified by the French chemist L. Maillard, is ubiquitous in nature. The reaction involves the formation of Schiff's bases between reducing sugars (such as glucose) and biological amines such as an amino acid, which then rearrange to form stable ketoamine products, or amadori compounds.

name: an oxime
annotation: An oxime is one in a class of chemical compounds with the general formula (R1)(R2)C=NOH, where R1 is an organic side chain and R2 is either hydrogen, forming Aldoxime, or another organic group, forming Ketoximes. Oximes are usually generated by the reaction of HYDROXYLAMINE and Aldehydes aldehydes or LONG-CHAIN-KETONE ketones. The term oxime is derived from a condensation of the words oxygen and Imides imide.

name: chondroitin
annotation: Chondroitin is a naturally occurring heteropolysaccharide belonging to the family of glycosaminoglycans. It is a nonsulfated derivative of Chondroitin-sulfates, consisting of a β-D-glucuronyl-(1,3)-N-acetyl-β-D-galactosamine disaccharide repeating unit connected through a β(1,4) glycosidic linkages. Chondroitin is widely used as a therapeutic material for the prevention or alleviation of symptoms of diseases such as rheumatoid arthritis, and in food supplements.

name: a chemically-produced chitosan
annotation: Chitosan Chitosan is a linear polysaccharide composed of randomly distributed β-(1-4)-linked D-glucosamine and N-acetyl-D-glucosamine units. Chitosan is found in the cell walls of some phytopathogenic fungi. The natural occuring chitosan has a degree of acetylation between 20-30%, with the acetyl residues grouped into clusters. Chitosan is mostly produced commercially by deacetylation of CHITIN , which is the structural element in the exoskeleton of crustaceans. The most popular technique for obtaining chitosan is alkaline deacetylation of chitin: this involves boiling chitin in concentrated alkali (50% w/v) for several hours. This process also yields chitosan with a degree of acetylation between 20-30%. However, in such a chitosan, unlike the naturally occurring chitosan, the acetyl groups are uniformly distributed along the polymer chain. Completely de-acetylated forms of chitosan do not exist naturally, but can be synthesized by special chemical treatment.

name: a xylan
annotation: Xylan is a general name given for polysaccharides composed of xylose residues. It is the principal type of Hemicelluloses in plant biomass representing the second most abundant polysaccharide in the biosphere after CELLULOSE . The most common form of xylan is 1-4-beta-Xylan "(1→4)-β-D-xylan", which is found in the cell walls of plants and some algae. Pure 1-4-beta-Xylan "(1→4)-β-D-xylan" is uncommon. In most cases it is acetylated and/or decorated with additional sugars, in the form of Arabinoxylan arabinoxylan, Glucuronoxylans glucuronoxylan, Glucuronoarabinoxylans glucuronoarabinoxylan or Arabinoglucuronoxylans arabinoglucuronoxylan. The secondary hydroxyl groups on xylose carbons 2 and 3 can be substituted by a number of residues, the type and degree of substitution depending on xylan's botanical source. Arabinose and glucuronic acid are linked to the backbone by glycosidic bonds. Other substituents form ester bonds: acetate groups are bound directly to the backbone, while cinnamoyl (feruloyl and coumaroyl) residues are linked to the side chain arabinoses . Due to its heterogeneous structure, the biodegradation of xylan requires the concerted action of a complex set of enzymes . These enzymes are mainly extracellular and are secreted by numerous fungi and bacteria . The endoxylanases and β xylosidases hydrolyze the main chain; the former generating short chain oligosaccharides and the latter acting on these products to liberate xylose. The side chains are hydrolyzed by glycanases, α-glucuronidases, acetyl xylan esterases and feruloyl esterases . 13-BETA-D-XYLANS "(1->3)-β-D-xylan" is found only in cell walls of some red and green algae.

name: a neurotransmitter
annotation: Neurotransmitters are endogenous compounds that are used to transmit information across the synapse between a neuron and another cell.

name: a xanthophyll
annotation: Xanthophylls are a subclass of carotenoids. While carotenes are purely hydrocarbons with no oxygen, xanthophylls contain oxygen atoms in the form of hydroxyl groups and/or as epoxids.

name: D-rhamnopyranose
annotation: This compound stands for a mixture of CPD-13829 and CPD-25021. In most cases, each of these compounds mutarotates quickly to form a mixture of the two, and thus it is the general rule to use this non-specific form in reactions unless it is specifically known that an enzyme can accept only one of the forms.

name: chondroitin sulfate D
annotation: Chondroitin-sulfates "Chondroitin sulfate" is a sulfated Glycosaminoglycans "glycosaminoglycan" composed of alternating units of CPD-12557 and CPD-12521. It is usually found attached to proteins as part of a proteoglycan. The saccharide units can be sulfated in variable positions and quantities - CPD-12557 can be sulfated at positions 4 and 6 to yield CPD-12516, CPD-12565 or CPD-12526, while CPD-12521 can be sulfated at position 2 to form CPD-12527. The following names have been used to define different sulfation states: Chondroitin-Sulfate-A "Chondroitin sulfate A" contains CPD-12521 and CPD-12516 Chondroitin-Sulfate-C "Chondroitin sulfate C" contains CPD-12521 and CPD-12565 Chondroitin-Sulfate-D "Chondroitin sulfate D" contains CPD-12527 and CPD-12565 Chondroitin-Sulfate-E "Chondroitin sulfate E" contains CPD-12521 and CPD-12526 The term "chondroitin sulfate B" has been used in the past to refer to Dermatan-Sulfate, but is not used any more.

name: 11-cis-3-hydroxyretinal
annotation: 3-Hydroxy-11-cis-retinal is a retinoid used as the visual pigment chromophore in some insects . When the receptor absorbs light photons the retinal undergoes a conformation change to All-trans-3-hydroxyretinal . Most of the insect visual pigments use the (3R)-3-hydroxyretinal enantiomer as the visual pigment chromophore. However, members of the higher Diptera, the TAX-480117, have been shown to synthesize and use the (3S)-3-hydroxyretinal enantiomer as chromophore .

name: a ketal
annotation: A ketal is a acetal of formula R2C(OR)2 (R is not H), derived from a ketone by replacement of the oxo group by two hydrocarbyloxy groups.

name: a [glycerolipid]-10,13-epoxy-11-methyl-octadecadienoate
annotation: This furan-containing fatty acid has been reported from the bacterium TAX-1063 . Like other fatty acids, CPD-18205 is rarely found in its free form. It is usually found as either an acyl-[acp] form or epoxy-11-methyl-octadecadienoate-lipid "incorporated into a lipid".

name: a folate
annotation: Folate (or folic acid) is an inactive synthetic form of THF-GLU-N tetrahydrofolate (also known as vitamin B9). Once ingested, it is converted to the active form, which is essential for nucleotide biosynthesis and homocysteine remethylation.

name: a carotenoid
annotation: carotenoids Carotenoids are isoprenoid pigments in the yellow to red color range. They have conjugated double bond chains of varying length with varying substituents, which determines their light absorption spectrum. They are produced by photosynthetic organisms and some non-photosynthetic fungi and bacteria. Animals, including humans, cannot synthesize carotenoids, although they are an essential source of retinoids and vitamin A. Carotenoids have diverse functions that include participation in photosynthetic light-harvesting complexes, serving as antioxidants, virulence factors, and modulation of membrane function. In photosynthetic organisms carotenoids provide protection against photooxidative stress through energy-dissipation of excess light absorbed by the antenna pigments as well as light-harvest. The prominent CPD1F-119, as well as CPD1F-130, are involved in the reduction of cataract and macular degeneration; others are exploited as coloring agents in flowers and fruits to attract pollinators (and in industry as food colorant) and agents of seed dispersal. Carotenoids serve as precursors for the plant growth hormone Abscisic-Acid and Vitamin-A (for a succinct review of the applications of carotenoids, see ). In addition, they may be cleaved to Apocarotenoids apocarotenoids, whose functions include serving as a cofactor for light-sensing proteins, acting as hormones, or acting as antifungals. Carotenoids have been the subject of metabolic pathway engineering studies because microbial carotenoid production is environmentally preferable to chemical synthesis. Over 700 carotenoids have been identified. The two main structural classes of carotenoids are the Carotenes carotenes and the Xanthophylls xanthophylls, which are composed of hydrocarbons and oxygenated hydroca

name: a TAG(18:3/32:2)
annotation: This class of triacylglycerols contains three acyl chains formed by the esterification of glycerol with three fatty acids. The names of triacylglycerol compound classes provide information about the three acyl chains of each member of the class, using the nomenclature TAG(a:b/y:z) where "a" represents the number of carbons found in one acyl chain and "b" represents the number of desaturations present in chain "a" and where "y" represents the total number of carbons found in the other two acyl chains and "z" represents the total number of desaturations present in the two chains included in "y." Therefore, in this TAG(18:3/32:2) class of compounds, one acyl chain has 18 carbons and three double bonds. The other two acyl chains have a total of 32 carbons and two double bonds. The attachment sites of the acyl chains at the R1 (sn-1), R2 (sn-2), or R3 (sn-3) position along the glyceryl backbone may vary among class members and cannot be inferred from their order in the compound class name. Additionally, the location and orientation of the double bonds in the unsaturated acyl chain(s) may vary among members of the class and are not specified by this notation.

name: [rhamnogalacturonan I oligosaccharide]-β-D-galacturonate
annotation: Rhamnogalacturonans-I "Rhamnogalacturonan I " is a polysaccharide in the Pectin pectin family, consisting of repeating α-(1,2)-L-rhamnose-α-(1,4)-D-GALACTURONATE disaccharide units. About half of the Rha residues are substituted with side chains of various neutral oligosaccharides such as arabinans, galactans, and arabinogalactans. This compound describes a CPD-12524 residue at the reducing end of rhamnogalacturonan I. Even though the residues of D-GALACTURONATE within the rhamnogalacturonan polymer are in β configuration, the enzyme that cleaves the polymer to produce oligosaccharides with D-GALACTURONATE at the reducing end (rhamnogalacturonan hydrolase) causes inversion of the anomeric configuration, resulting in a terminal CPD-12524 residue.

name: a strigolactone
annotation: Strigolactones are plant hormones that stimulate the branching and growth of symbiotic arbuscular mycorrhizal fungi, but inhibit plant shoot branching. They promote the probability of establishment of a symbiotic association between the plant and the fungi. The first strigolactone (CPD-14655) was described from TAX-3635 (cotton) infected by the root parasitic weeds TAX-4169 (witchweed) (hence the name). It has been shown that the seeds of the parasites germinated only if they were exposed to stimulant molecules present in the root exudate of the host. Although the nature of strigol was not understood at the time, it was observed that it was active at hormonal levels . Discovery of additional strigolactones from other plants followed, such as CPD-14659 from TAX-4558 , CPD-14660 from TAX-3917 and CPD-14657 from TAX-57577 (red clover) . The real purpose of strigolactones, which was understood 40 years after the discovery of strigol, is to promote the formation of symbiotic associations known as arbuscular mycorrhiza, which consist of close interaction between the plants roots and TAX-214504 fungi . Arbuscular mycorrhiza, which are found in more than 80% of higher plant species, are one of the most ancient symbioses of land plants. The fungi acquire and deliver nutrients to the plants, which in return provides the fungi with carbohydrates produced by photosynthesis . The lactones induce extensive hyphal branching in germinating spores of the fungi, which increases the chance of contact with the root and subsequently infecting it. The branching phenomenon has been known for more than 30 years before the identity of the chemical signal that promotes it has been traced to strigolactone . Since strigolactones are very labile and active at concentrations as low as picomolar, the

name: a ginsenoside
annotation: Ginsenosides Ginsenosides or panaxosides are a class of steroid glycosides and triterpene saponins found exclusively in the plant genus TAX-4053 (ginseng). They are the principal components responsible for the pharmaceutical activities of ginseng. Ginsenosides are composed of an aglycone and sugar moieties. Based on the structure of their aglycones, ginsenosides have been categorized into three broad types; Protopanaxadiol-type-ginsenosides "protopanaxadiol-type ginsenosides" (PPD), which contain hydroxyl groups at positions 3 and 20, Protopanaxatriol-type-ginsenosides "protopanaxatriol-type ginsenosides" (PPT), which contain hydroxyl groups at positions 3, 6 and 20, and Oleanonic-acid-type-ginsenosides "oleanonic-acid-type saponins". Although ginsenosides only have three kinds of aglycones, more than 60 kinds of ginsenosides have been identified by now due to the variability in the type of sugar moieties, their number, and the carbon positions they link to. The biological activity of ginsenoside increases with the decrease of the number of the sugar moieties. All of the ginsenosides found in large quantities in wild ginseng (known as major ginsenosides) are heavily glycosylated, while the more active less-glycosylated ginsenosides are found in much smalller quantities (and are commonly referred to as the minor ginsenosides). Following oral intake of ginseng, the large low-solubility and poor-permeability major ginsenosides cannot be absorbed by the human body directly , and are deglycosylated into smaller forms in the gastrointestinal tract by intestinal bacteria and/or digestive enzymes . Glycosidase enzymes that can convert the major ginsenosides to minor ginsenosides are not common and are of commercial interest.

name: vernolate
annotation: Vernolates Vernolate (vernolic acid) is a fatty acid with an 18-carbon chain that contains a cis double bond at position 9 and an epoxy group at positions 12-13. Like other fatty acids, Vernolates is rarely found in its free form. It is usually found Glycerolipid-vernolate "incorporated into a lipid". When studied in planrts, the compound is usually referred to as "vernolic acid". When studied in mammals, it is referred to as "isoleukotoxin".

name: a post transition metal cation
annotation: Post transition metals, also known as the poor metals, is a group of metals. They normally have higher electronegativities and melting points than the transition metals.

name: a rotenone
annotation: Rotenoids are advanced isoflavonoids that have the specific 4-ring heterocyclic coret . The rotenones are members of the rotenoids that consist of a 6a,12a-dihydrochromeno[3,4-b]chromen-12(6H)-one skeleton, including substituted products.

name: an aldonate
annotation: Aldonates are carbohydrate acids formed by oxidizing the aldehyde functional group of an aldose to a carboxylic acid functional group.

name: a very long-chain alkane
annotation: Alkanes are saturated acyclic hydrocarbons. They consist only of hydrogen and saturated carbon atoms and are bonded exclusively by single bonds. They could be either branched or unbranched. Linear alkanes are often classified by the length of their acyl moiety. Short-Chain-Alkanes "Short-chain alkanes" contain less than 6- carbons. Medium-Chain-Alkanes "Medium-chain alkanes" contain 6-12 carbons. Long-Chain-Alkanes "Long-chain alkanes" contain 13-22 carbons. Very-Long-Chain-Alkanes "Very-long-chain alkanes" contain 23 carbons or longer.

name: a 7-deazapurine
annotation: Pyrrolopyrimidine nucleosides analogs, collectively referred to as deazapurines, are an important class of structurally diverse compounds found in a wide variety of biological niches .

name: a glucosinolate
annotation: Glucosinolates "Glucosinolates" are secondary metabolites found in 16 plant families, including agriculturally important crop plants of the TAX-3700 such cabbage, mustard, oilseed rape, and broccoli, as well as the model plant TAX-3702, and are responsible for the typical sharp taste and odor of these plants. The glucosinolates are substituted β-thioglucoside N-hydroxysulfates that are formed from any one of eight amino acids, namely, L-ALPHA-ALANINE, VAL, LEU, ILE, PHE, MET, TYR and TRP. Over 115 naturally occurring glucosinolates have been identified. Much of the diversity amongst glucosinolates arises from the addition of different sized alkyl groups to the side chain of the amino acids, principally valine, phenylalanine and methionine . They are grouped into aliphatic, aromatic, and indolic types, depending on whether they are derived from methionine, phenylalanine and tyrosine, or tryptophan. Indolylmethyl glucosinolates are found mainly in leaves. For a review, see . Glucosinolates are stored in the vacuole and come into contact with the enzyme EC-3.2.1.147 (myrosinase) upon breakage of the cell. Myrosinase de-glucosylates the glucosinolates, resulting in formation of nitriles, isothiocyanates and thiocyanates. The precise product that is formed depends on the glucosinolate and whether an epithiospecifier protein is present. The breakdown products give the typical sharp taste and odor to many of the members of brassicaceae that are eaten, such as raddish and mustard. Arabidopsis has been shown to accumulate at least 23 different glucosinolates in both seeds and leaves.

name: a peptide antibiotic
annotation: This compound class stands for generic and unspecified peptide antibiotics.

name: D-fucose
annotation: Fucose is a hexose deoxy sugar. It received its name from the brown algae genus TAX-3011, from which it was first decribed. The alga was named because of its dark redish color, as one of the meanings of the Latin word fucus is "red paint". Algae of the TAX-3009 order produce fucose-rich polysaccahrides known as Fucoidans fucoidans. However, fucose is a common sugar, found in N-linked glycans on the mammalian, insect and plant cell surface, in many bacterial glycans, and in other biomolecules. Most of the fucose found in biology is in the L-fucoses "L conformation", and the use of D-Fucoses is rather rare (for example in some Cardenolide-Glycosides "cardenolide glycosides"). Note that fucose is a reducing sugar that exists in solution in multiple configurations. The open-chain structure shown here is provided only as a schematic illustration of chirality. Consult the appropriate class instances for information on specific ring structures and their anomeric configuration.

name: a glycosylinositolphosphoceramide(46:2)-2
annotation: In this class of glycosylinositol phosphoceramides, a long carbon chain is attached at position R1 to form a fatty amide and a long carbon chain is attached at position R2 to form a long chain base. Either or both of these chains may contain one or more double bonds and may have hydroxyl groups attached. Based on the notation GIPC(m:n)-p, each compound within this class has a total of 46 carbons (m), two double bonds (n), and two hydroxyl groups (p) present in the sphingoid base and the fatty amide. However, the length, hydroxylation position, double bond position, and double bond orientation of the groups attached at R2 and R3 will differ between individual members of this group of compounds.

name: a short-chain fatty alcohol
annotation: Fatty alcohols are aliphatic Alcohols alcohols consisting of a chain of 3 or more carbon atoms. They may be saturated or unsaturated and may be branched or unbranched. Many enzymes that act on Fatty-Acids "fatty acids", as well as the corresponding Fatty-Acyl-CoAs "fatty acyl-CoAs", Fatty-Alcohols alcohols, and Fatty-Aldehydes aldehydes, have evolved to recognize substrates with a limited range of chain lengths. To classify these enzymes, it is helpful to divide the substrates into smaller groups based on their chain length. The following subgroups have been defined: Short-Chain-Alcohols "Short-chain alcohols" have 3-5 carbons in the longest chain. Medium-Chain-Alcohols "Medium-chain alcohols" have 6-12 carbons in the longest chain. Long-chain-alcohols "Long-chain alcohols" have 13-22 carbons in the longest chain. Very-Long-Chain-Alcohols "Very-long-chain alcohols" have 23-27 carbons in the longest chain. Ultra-Long-Chain-Alcohols "Ultra-long-chain alcohols" have more than 27 carbons in the longest chain.

name: a meroterpenoid
annotation: Meroterpenoids are secondary metabolites that are produced in part from a terpenoid co-substrate by mixed biosynthetic pathways. They are hybrid compounds that combine terpenoid scaffolds with polyketides, alkaloids, phenols, or amino acids. They are widely produced by bacteria, algae, plants, and animals . The term meroterpenoid was first used by in 1986 to describe natural products bearing a mixed terpenoid biogenesis. The prefix mero- is derived from the Greek word "merus", which means "fragment or part" . Many meroterpenoids demonstrate biological activities of medical importance, including antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, anti-atherosclerotic, anti-adipogenic, anti-diabetic, anti-carcinogenic, and neuroprotective activities.

name: a medium-chain fatty alcohol
annotation: Fatty alcohols are aliphatic Alcohols alcohols consisting of a chain of 3 or more carbon atoms. They may be saturated or unsaturated and may be branched or unbranched. Many enzymes that act on Fatty-Acids "fatty acids", as well as the corresponding Fatty-Acyl-CoAs "fatty acyl-CoAs", Fatty-Alcohols alcohols, and Fatty-Aldehydes aldehydes, have evolved to recognize substrates with a limited range of chain lengths. name: a bleomycin
annotation: Bleomycin is a glycopeptide antibiotic produced by the bacterium TAX-29309. Bleomycin refers to a family of structurally related compounds. When used as an anticancer agent, the chemotherapeutical forms are primarily CPD-13136 and CPD-13137.

name: a gentamicin
annotation: Gentamicins are a family of Aminoglycoside-Antibiotics "aminoglycoside antibiotics" produced by Gram-positive bacteria of the TAX-1873 genus. They were discovered in 1963 by a group of scientists at the Schering Corporation . Gentamicins are particularly active against Gram-negative bacteria, and are bactericidal. They act by binding the 30S subunit of the bacterial ribosome, interrupting protein synthesis. Despite their high antibacterial activity, intravenous use is limited by their ototoxicity and nephrotoxicity. However, they are used commonly as a topical treatment to skin infections. The most common form of gentamycin is the GENTAMICIN-C "C complex", which consists of a mixture of CPD-14213, CPD-14216, CPD-14211, CPD-14212 and CPD-15231. While the name can be written as both gentamicin and gentamycin, the former is more common, to emphasize the biological origin of the antibiotics.

Alpha-hydroxydihydroceramides "α Hydroxydihydroceramides" are also known as "plant ceramide (II)".

name: acharan sulfate
annotation: Acharan sulfate is a member of the Glycosaminoglycans "glycosaminoglycan" family discovered in the giant African snail TAX-2315439 . It consists of a repeating disaccharide unit composed of -[→4)-N-acetyl-α-D-glucosamine-(1→4)-2-sulfo-α-L-idopyranosyluronate-(1→]-, making it structurally related to HEPARIN and Heparan-Sulfate . In the snail acharan sulfate is produced by internal granules and is mainly secreted into the outer surface of the body as a mucous material . Acharan sulfate has been shown to present several biological activities in vitro, including inhibition of angiogenesis due to inhibition of vascular endothelial growth factor and the decrease of mitogenic activity of fibroblast growth factor .

name: curdlan
annotation: Curdlan is a linear high-molecular-weight 1,3-β-D-glucan exopolysaccharide polymer produced by by soil bacteria of the family Rhizobiaceae . Curdlan has numerous applications as a gelling agent in the food, construction, and pharmaceutical industries and has been approved as a food additive by the U. S. Food and Drug Administration

The basic repeating disaccharide unit consists of GALACTOSE and N-ACETYL-BETA-D-GLUCOSAMINE, either of which can be sulfated at carbon position 6. aliases: an i histo-blood group antigen|4-N-acetyl-D-glucosaminyl-[glycoprotein]|4)-N-acetyl-β-D-glucosyl-(1|3-β-D-galactosyl-1|4-[β-D-galactosyl-1|6)]-N-acetyl-β-D-glucosaminyl-(1|3)-β-D-galactosyl-(1|a β-D-Gal-(1→4)-β-D-GlcNAc-(1→3)-β-D-Gal-(1→4)-β-D-GlcNAc-R|an N-acetyl-β-D-galactosyl-(1|an I histo-blood group antigen|4)-N-acetyl-D-glucosaminyl-[glycoprotein]|4)-N-acetyl-β-D-glucosaminyl-(1|a β-D-Gal-(1→4)-β-D-GlcNAc-(1→3)-[β-D-Gal-(11→4)-β-D-GlcNAc-(1->6)]-β-D-Gal-(1→4)-β-D-GlcNAc-R|4)-N-acetyl-β-D-glucosaminyl-R|4-N-acetyl-β-D-glucosyl-1|an I antigen|4)-[β-D-galactosyl-(1|a β-D-galactosyl-(1|a β-D-galactosyl-(1→4)-N-acetyl-β-D-glucosaminyl-(1→3)-β-D-galactosyl-(1→4)-N-acetyl-β-D-glucosaminyl-R|an N-acetyl-β-D-galactosyl-1|6]-N-acetyl-β-D-glucosaminyl-1
annotation: The Lower-case-i-Antigens "i antigen" is found on membrane proteins in red blood cells and many other cell types as well as various body secretions of developing fetuses and newborn infants , while the Upper-case-I-antigens "I antigen" is found in all adults . The i antigen undergoes gradual conversion to the I antigen in newborn infants, a process that completes in about 18 months. Both antigens are based entirely on Type 2 (BETA-D-GALACTOSYL-ETCETERA-GLUCOSAMINE "N-acetyllactosamine") chains. The basic i structure is an unbranched polylactosamine comprising at least two N - acetyllactosamine units connected by a β(1→3) linkage . The crucial step in the transition from the i antigen to the I antigen is catalyzed by EC-2.4.1.150, also known as the I-branching enzyme, which introduces the branching by adding a GlcNAc residue by a β(1→6) linkage to the galactose residue to which other residues are already attached by a 1,3 linkage . Note that the structure shown here is the core structure that defines I antigenicity. Most i antigens are further glycosylated. (Reviewed in ).

name: a TAG(18:2/34:1)
annotation: This class of triacylglycerols contains three acyl chains formed by the esterification of glycerol with three fatty acids. The names of triacylglycerol compound classes provide information about the three acyl chains of each member of the class, using the nomenclature TAG(a:b/y:z) where "a" represents the number of carbons found in one acyl chain and "b" represents the number of desaturations present in chain "a" and where "y" represents the total number of carbons found in the other two acyl chains and "z" represents the total number of desaturations present in the two chains included in "y." Therefore, in this TAG(18:2/34:1) class of compounds, one acyl chain has 18 carbons and two double bonds. The other two acyl chains have a total of 34 carbons and one double bond. The attachment sites of the acyl chains at the R1 (sn-1), R2 (sn-2), or R3 (sn-3) position along the glyceryl backbone may vary among class members and cannot be inferred from their order in the compound class name. Additionally, the location and orientation of the double bonds in the unsaturated acyl chain(s) may vary among members of the class and are not specified by this notation.

name: a lignan
annotation: Lignans are dimers formed from β-β' linkages between monolignols (COUMARYL-ALCOHOL, CONIFERYL-ALCOHOL and SINAPYL-ALCOHOL), which in turn are formed from the hydroxycinnamic acids COUMARATE, FERULIC-ACID and SINAPATE, respectively . They are found in plants and many algae, where they function as defense molecules against herbivores and microorganisms .

annotation: Isolichenin Isolichenin is a complex glucan characterized from from Iceland Moss (TAX-78064). It consists solely of D-glucose residues united by α(1-3)- and β(1-4)-glucosidic linkages which are present in the relative proportion of 3:2. The molecule appears to be linear, with an average chain length of 42-44 glucose residues .

name: a 6-sulfo-sialyl Lewis x epitope
aliases: an sLex antigen|αNeuNAc-(2→3)-βGal-(1→4)-[αFuc-(1→3]-6-sulfo-βGlcNAc-[protein]|a 6-sulfo sialyl Lewis x antigen
annotation: The sialyl Lewis-X (SLeX) antigen is expressed on glycolipids and glycoproteins on the cell surfaces. In adults, SLeX antigen is expressed principally on the surfaces of granulocytic cells. It plays an important role during early steps of inflammatory response as a ligand of the three known selectins - E-selectin (also known as endothelial leucocyte adhesion molecules, ELAM-1), P-Selectin, and L-selectin, resulting in the capture of leucocytes from the plasma and their attachment to blood endothelial cells . The antigen is expressed in high frequency in tumor tissue where it plays a role in adhesion between tumor cells and blood endothelial cells during tumor metastases . The SLeX antigen is synthesized from B-Gal-14-NacGlc-R by the action of two enzymes, EC-2.4.3.6 and EC-2.4.1.152 .

name: a fatty alcohol
annotation: Fatty alcohols are aliphatic Alcohols alcohols consisting of a chain of 3 or more carbon atoms. They may be saturated or unsaturated and may be branched or unbranched. Many enzymes that act on Fatty-Acids "fatty acids", as well as the corresponding Fatty-Acyl-CoAs "fatty acyl-CoAs", Fatty-Alcohols alcohols, and Fatty-Aldehydes aldehydes, have evolved to recognize substrates with a limited range of chain lengths. To classify these enzymes, it is helpful to divide the substrates into smaller groups based on their chain length. The following subgroups have been defined: Short-Chain-Alcohols "Short-chain alcohols" have 3-5 carbons in the longest chain. Medium-Chain-Alcohols "Medium-chain alcohols" have 6-12 carbons in the longest chain. Long-chain-alcohols "Long-chain alcohols" have 13-22 carbons in the longest chain. Very-Long-Chain-Alcohols "Very-long-chain alcohols" have 23-27 carbons in the longest chain. Ultra-Long-Chain-Alcohols "Ultra-long-chain alcohols" have more than 27 carbons in the longest chain.

name: an aminoglycoside antibiotic
annotation: The aminoglycosides are a class of clinical antibiotics that interact with bacterial ribosomal RNA and inhibit protein synthesis. The majority of the aminoglycosides consist of a unique aminocyclitol CPD-10151 core, which is decorated with various aminosugars, leading to diverse structural molecules,including Kanamycins kanamycin, CPD-14154 tobramycin, Gentamycins gentamycin, CPD-14143 ribostamycin, CPD-14140 butirosin, Neomycins neomycin, CPD-14177, Lividomycins lividomycin, CPD-14157 apramycin, and HYGROMYCIN-B. Other groups of aminoglycosides contain cyclitols either derived from either MYO-INOSITOL or from D-SEDOHEPTULOSE-7-P.

name: a ω-3 fatty acid
annotation: Omega 3 fatty acids (popularly referred to as ω-3 fatty acids or n-3 fatty acids) are fats commonly found in marine and plant oils. They are polyunsaturated fatty acids with a double bond (C=C) starting after the third carbon atom from the end of the carbon chain. The fatty acids have two endsthe acid (COOH) end and the methyl (CH3) end. The location of the first double bond is counted from the methyl end, which is also known as the omega (ω) end or the n end.

annotation: Compounds in this class are ceramides formed from a saturated 18-carbon fatty acid (octadecanoate) and the long chain base (LCB) sphinga-(4,8)-dienine. They have a molecular formula of C36H69NO3. In plants, there is commonly a mixture of ceramides with the double bond at position 8 in the LCB in cis and trans configurations, whereas the double bond at the 4 position is generally in the trans configuration .

name: a fucoidan
annotation: Fucoidan is a generic term used to describe sulfated polysaccharides (MW: average 20,000) found in various species of brown algae such as kombu (edible kelp from mostly the family TAX-33636), limu moui (TAX-86906), bladder wrack (TAX-49266), wakame (TAX-74381), mozuku (TAX-309737), and hijiki (TAX-590727). Fucoidan was first reported in 1913 under the name fucoidin , but the name was changed in 1959 to conform with polysaccharide nomenclature . Fucoidan structure can be divided into two groups depending on their sources: one group includes TAX-33637 species that have their central chains composed by (1→3)-linked α-L-fucopyranose residues ; a second group includes fucoidan isolated from TAX-52968 and TAX-3011 species that have their central chains composed of repeating (1→3) and (1→4) linked α-L-fucopyranose residues . Besides fucose and sulfate, the presence of monosaccharide residues such as mannose, galactose, glucose, xylose and uronic acids were also identified . Several fucose-containing polymers with less than 90% fucosyl residues have also been reported from brown algae and some of them were given names: ascophyllan or xylofucoglycuronan, isolated from TAX-52969, is based on a backbone of mannuronate with branches of 3-O-D-xylosyl-L-fucose-4-sulfate ; sargassan or glycuronofucoglycan, also isolated from TAX-52969, is based on linear chains of D-galactose with branches of L-fucose-3-sulfate or occasionally uronate . Other, generic names in use are F-fucoidan, which consists of mostly fucose , U-fucoidan, which contains approximately 20% of glucuronic acid, and G-fucoidan, which contains a substantial amount of galactose . Fucoidan has been reported to possess anti-tumoral , anti-coagulant , anti-viral and anti-inflamatory activities . It is used mainly in the cosm

name: a furanocoumarin
annotation: The Furanocoumarins furanocoumarins are one of the main groups of coumarins. Their synthesis starts with the coupling of CPD-4211 to the coumarin CPD-8186, leading to formation of a skeleton that contains a furan ring fused with coumarin. Furanocoumarins are minor constituents in plants belonging to TAX-4037, TAX-3803, TAX-3487, and TAX-23513 families. They are natural toxins that protect the plants from insects, herbivory, and microbes. Production is triggered by several types of stresses, including fungal infections and stresses such as wounding or acid fog . Furanocoumarins are also toxic to humans. Direct contact with the sap from some plants containing furanocoumarins (such as TAX-380077) causes inflammation, burns and irritating sensitivity to sunlight . Some naturally occurring methoxy derivatives of furanocoumarins have been applied clinically, coupled with exposure to sunlight, to cure dermatological diseases for more than 2000 years. Linear furanocoumarins such as CPD-13042 and 5-METHOXYFURANOCOUMARIN are used in the clinic in combination with UV radiation to treat several diseases, such as mycosis fungoides, vitiligo and psoriasis. Several clinical trials are ongoing to evaluate furanocoumarins potential to treat additional conditions, such as graft versus host disease, Crohn's disease, and several cancer types . A well documented toxicity of furanocoumarins is related to their inhibition of cytochrome P450 (CYP) enzymes . An example is the interaction of furanocoumarins produced by grapefruit with HS08544-MONOMER (CYP3A4), an enzyme that is involved in the bioinactivation of about 50% of all drugs currently used, resulting in enhanced systemic drug concentration through impaired drug metabolism, and leading to recommendation to avoid grapefruit while taking

name: a cutin monomer
annotation: There are two major monomer families of cutin, the C16 and C18 families. The C16 family consists mainly of 16-hydroxy palmitic acid and 9,16 or 10,16-dihydroxypalmitic acid. The C18 family consists mainly of 18-hydroxyoleic acid, 9,10-epoxy-18-hydroxystearic acid, and 9,10,18-trihydroxystearate.

name: D-iduronate
annotation: L-Iduronate is a reducing acidic sugar that can form a ring structure, as shown for CPD-12515. CPD-12515 is the major uronic acid component of the glycosaminoglycans Dermatan-Sulfate and HEPARIN. It is also present in Heparan-Sulfate, but in a minor amount relative to its carbon-5 epimer GLUCURONATE. CPD-12515 is often modified by the addition of an O-sulfate group at carbon position 2 to form CPD-12518. The open-chain structure shown here is provided only as a schematic illustration of chirality. Consult the appropriate class instances for information on specific ring structures and their anomeric configuration.

name: an enamine
annotation: An enamine is an amine with the structure RNR'R'', where R has a double bond adjacent to the amine nitrogen.

name: a rhodoquinol
annotation: Rhodoquinone (RQ) is a modified ubiquinone, where the methoxyl group in position 3 is replaced by an amino group. The compound was originally isolated from TAX-1085 , where is it used for the reduction of fumarate to succinate under anaerobic conditions. The rhodoquinone/rhodoquinol standard redox potential is substantially lower than that of ubiquinone/ubiquinol/UQH2 (-63 vs. +110 mV). The lower redox potential drives the succinate dehydrogenase complex (complex II) in reverse to function as a fumarate reductase. Rhodoquinone is not limited to prokaryotes. It was soon discovered that some eukaryotic parasites such as the nematodes TAX-6253 and TAX-6252 , as well as parasitic helminths also contain rhodoquinone, which is essential for fumarate reduction under anaerobic conditions . Later it was shown that rhodoquinone is present in all eukaryotes that reduce fumarate during anoxia, including freshwater snails, mussels, lugworms, and oysters . More recently, rhodoquinone has been detected in mitochondria purified from certain mouse and human tissues .

name: NADPHX
annotation: NADPHX is a hydrated form of NADPH, where the hydroxyl group is attached at position 6 of the nicotinamide moiety. NADHX and NADPHX are inhibitors of several dehydrogenases and it is thus important for cells to eliminate them .

name: an aldopyranose
annotation: Aldoses "An aldose" is a monosaccharide containing one aldehyde group per molecule and having a chemical formula of the form CnH2n-1On where n>=3. Aldoses can form cyclical structures by a reaction between the hydroxyl group on carbon 5 with the aldehyde at carbon 1, forming a a six-membered ring consisting of five carbon atoms and one oxygen atom. These cyclical molecules are referred to as aldopyranoses, since the ring is reminiscent of Pyrans pyran (although with no double bonds).

name: a deep rough form lipopolysaccharide
annotation: Deep-rough TAX-562 mutants lack the heptose region of the inner core oligosaccharide in their lipopolysaccharides.

name: D-mannofuranose
annotation: This compound stands for a mixture of CPD-25010 and CPD-25011. In most cases, each of these compounds mutarotates quickly to form a mixture of the two, and thus it is the general rule to use this non-specific form in reactions unless it is specifically known that an enzyme can accept only one of the forms.

name: a β bitter acid
annotation: The ripe cones of the hop plant (TAX-3486) are covered with glandular hairs containing a mixture of essential oil and bitter acids. These compounds are important not only for the beer brewing industry but also for their antimicrobial, antifungal and antifeedant activities . The bitter acids consist of the so-called α- and β-acids. The former group is of particular importance for the brewing industry as they are transformed into iso-α-acids such as CPD-7116 and CPD-7115 during the brewing process, conferring the bitter taste of beer .

name: a cobinamide
annotation: CPD-24496 "A cobinamide" is a cobalt-corrinoid hexaamide. The key structural feature of cobinamides is a corrin ring with a cobalt ion in its center. Four of the six coordination sites of the cobalt ion are provided by the corrin ring. In many cobinamides a fifth coordination site is provided by Cobamide-Upper-Ligands "an upper ligand" located above the ring plane. Unlike in Cobamides cobamides, cobinamides lack Cobamide-Lower-Ligands "a lower ligand". The three common upper ligands are an 5'-deoxyadenosyl group, as in ADENOSYLCOBINAMIDE, a methyl group, as in CPD-24497, and a cyano group, as in CPD-22314.

name: a vaccenate
annotation: CPD-17257 trans-Vaccenate is a naturally occurring trans-fatty acid produced by mammals. The name is derived from the Latin vacca (cow) since it is the main trans fatty acid in cow's milk. It is also the predominant trans fatty acid in human milk. CPD-17257 trans-Vaccenate was discovered in 1928 in animal fats and butter . It is formed with a conjugated isomer of linoleate known as CPD-15704 rumenic acid" by biohydrogenation of dietary polyunsaturated fatty acids in the rumen . Another isomer, CPD-9247, is produced from CPD-9245 by bacteria and plants. It is one of only two unsaturated fatty acids in TAX-562 (see PWY-5973). Some plant species such as TAX-48545 and TAX-73871 (Doxantha unguis-cati L.) produce relatively high levels of CPD-9245 and CPD-9247 in their seed oils . The plant pathway is likely identical to the bacterial one (in ).

name: a para-substituted 2-methoxyphenol
annotation: Monolignols, also called lignols, are the source materials for biosynthesis of both lignans and lignin.

name: a Lewis y epitope
aliases: a histo-blood group Ley antigen|AH6 antigen
annotation: The Lewis y epitope (Ley) is a difucosylated oligosaccharide derived from B-Gal-14-NacGlc-R "type 2 precursor disaccharides" carried by glycoconjugates on the cell surface. Ley is expressed predominately during embryogenesis, and expression on adult tissue is restricted to granulocytes and epithelial surfaces , where it acts as a ligand for lectin-like proteins . However, Ley is overexpressed in the majority of carcinomas, including breast, ovary, pancreas, prostate, colon, and non-small cell lung cancers, and has been correlated to the pathological staging and prognosis . In these cases, Ley occurs either at the plasma membrane as a glycolipid or linked to surface receptors . Ley was shown to play an important role in promoting cell proliferation, tumorigenecity and adhesion , and several clinical trials were conducted using Ley monoclonal antibodies for targeted immunotherapy of tumors . The human pathogenic bacterium TAX-210 is known to express the Ley antigen in its lipopolysaccharide, mimicking human cell surface glycoconjugates .

name: pinene
annotation: Pinene is a bicyclic monoterpene found in the resins of many conifers (including pine, from which its name is derived), as well as in non-coniferous plants. Two main forms exist - α-pinene and β-pinene.

name: a buffer
annotation: Buffers are chemical compounds that, in solution (typically aqueous), resist change in pH upon addition of small amounts of acid or base.

name: a bacteriochlorophyll a
annotation: Bacteriochlorophylls are photosynthetic light harvesting pigments found in photosynthetic bacteria. There are many different types of bacteriochlorophylls, including Bacteriochlorophyll-a bacteriochlorophyll a, CPD-9091, CPD-9092, CPD-9093, CPD-9094 and CPD-9095 , with vaying structures . In addition, while most of the bacteriochlorophylls contain a MG+2 ion, there are varieties that contain a ZN+2 ion . Most of the bacteria that synthesize bacteriochlorophyll are anoxygenic species that belong to the proteobacteria. They are also known as purple bacteria, due to the color of the pigment. These species photosynthesize only under anaerobic conditions. However, bacteriochlorophylls are also present in some groups of aerobic bacteria, such as members of the Acidiphilium genus . For a complete list of aerobic bacteria that contain bacteriochlorophyll, see . Much of the knowledge about bacteriochlorophyll biosynthesis comes from the organisms TAX-1061 and TAX-1063, which produce mainly CPD-9067, the most common form of the bacteriochlorophylls. Bacteriochlorophyll-a "Bacteriochlorophyll a" is the most common type of bacteriochlorophyll, and is found in light-harvesting antenna systems and reaction center complexes of most anoxygenic photosynthetic bacteria, except for some purple bacteria that produce giving CPD-9091 and heliobacteria that produce CPD-9095. Different forms of bacteriochloropyll a have been reported. The green sulfur bacterium TAX-1097 synthesizes two types - one with an esterified PHYTOL side chain (CPD-9067) and one with a CPD-18893 side chain (CPD-18892). Some TAX-1218 spp. produce CPD-18894 .

name: ibuprofen
annotation: Ibuprofen is nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) used for pain relief, fever reduction, and against swelling. Like other NSAID drugs (e.g. CPD-524 and CPD-7669), ibuprofen works by inhibiting the enzyme EC-1.14.99.1 (also known as cyclooxygenase or COX) that converts ARACHIDONIC_ACID to PROSTAGLANDIN-H2, a precursor to other prostaglandins that mediate pain, inflammation, and fever. In addition, PROSTAGLANDIN-H2 is also a precursor of ALPHA11-ALPHA-EPOXY-15-HYDROXYTHROMBA, which stimulates platelet aggregation. The inhibition of the COX1 variant of the COX cyclooxygenase also reduces the formation of blood clots. Ibuprofen inhibits both isoforms of cyclooxygenase, HS01815 (COX1) and HS01115 (COX2). Ibuprofen was derived from PROPIONATE during the 1960s. Originally marketed as Brufen, ibuprofen is available under a variety of popular trademarks, including Motrin, Nurofen, Advil, Nuprin and many others. The active form of ibuprofen is CPD-15516. The drug is synthesized as a racemic mixture, but an isomerase encoded by the HS01416 gene converts the inactive CPD-15517 "(R)-form" in vivo to the active form via a coenzyme A derivative .

name: lacto-N-biose I
annotation: The Lacto-N-biose I disaccharide, also known as type 1 chain, is the precursor of a number of important carbohydrate epitopes in human body, such as the Lewis-a-epitope "Lewis A", Antigen-Leb "Lewis B" and sLea-Antigen "sialyl Lewis A" (SLea) epitopes. These epitopes are involved in a variety of biological processes including pathogen adhesion, fertilization, and tumor metastasis. This disaccharide is also present in other important oligosaccharides, such as CPD-16473 (LNT). LNT and its fucosylated and/or sialylated derivatives are among some of the major components in human milk oligosaccharides. Some of them share similar structures with the glycan ligands recognized by human pathogens and are proposed to protect infants against these pathogens via competitive inhibition of the binding between the pathogens and the epithelial ligands .

name: a hydroxylamino group-containing compound
annotation: This compound class stands for generic and unspecified compounds with a hydroxylamino group.

name: a retinoid
annotation: Retinoids are a group of compounds that are related to vitamin A, including all-trans forms such as CPD-13524, RETINAL and RETINOATE, cis forms such as CPD-882, CPD-881, CPD-13549 and CPD-13548, and Retinyl-Esters "retinyl esters" such as CHOCOLA_A. Retinoids have many important and diverse functions throughout the body including roles in vision, regulation of cell proliferation and differentiation, growth of bone tissue, immune function, and activation of tumor suppressor genes. Structurally, all retinoids possess a β-ionone ring and a polyunsaturated side chain, with either an alcohol, aldehyde, a carboxylic acid group or an ester group. The side chain is composed of four isoprenoid units, with a series of conjugated double bonds which may exist in trans- or cis-configuration.

name: a salinosporamide
annotation: The marine bacterium TAX-168695 produces a family of potent 20S-proteasome inhibitors. Originally three members of the family were isolated - CPD-12674, CPD-12685, and CPD-27917 . Later additional compounds - CPD-12686 through CPD-27921 and CPD-27922 . These related compounds possess a unique bicyclic structural element, composed of a tightly fused Gamma-Lactams "γ-lactam" and Beta-lactones "β-lactone" rings. The salinosporamides bind irreversibly to the proteosome with great affinity, and CPD-12674 is used as an anticancer agent. It entered phase I human clinical trials for the treatment of multiple myeloma only three years after its discovery .

name: a depsipeptide
annotation: A depsipeptide is a peptide in which one or more of the amide (-CONHR-) bonds are replaced by ester (COOR) bonds. The structure shown is an example of a pentadepsipeptide.

name: a pyranoside
annotation: A pyranoside is Aldopyranoses in which the anomeric OH at C1 has been converted into an OR group.

name: a glucuronoarabinoxylan
annotation: Glucuronoarabinoxylans Glucuronoarabinoxylans (GAX) and Arabinoglucuronoxylans arabinoglucuronoxylans (AGX) are components of Hemicelluloses that have a similar structure - a β(1→4)-D-xylopyranose backbone with single 4-O-methylglucuronate and α-L-arabinofuranose residues attached at position 2 and 3, respectively. However, GAX-type polymers consist of Arabinoxylan backbone that contains about ten times more α-L-arabinofuranose side chains than glucuronate ones, while AGX-type polymers are Glucuronoxylans with a small proportion of the xylopyranose residues of the backbone disubstituted by two α-L-arabinofuranose residues . Another difference is that GAX contain some xylopyranose units that are doubly substituted by both 4-O-methylglucuronate and α-L-arabinofuranose . Unlike glucuronoxylans, glucuronoarabinoxylans are not acetylated . Some of the arabinofuranose side chains in GAX are further substitued by either a β-D-xylopyranose attached by a (1→2) linkage or by a second arabinofuranose attached by a (1→3) linkage. GAX-type polymers are typically located in the non-endospermic tissues of cereal grains such as in wheat, corn, and rice bran . GAX fractions that were isolated from wheat bran were feruloylated .

name: a protein-bound uremic retention solute
annotation: Uremic retention solutes are toxic compounds that accumulate in blood and tissues of patients with uremia (chronic renal failure). They may be free or protein-bound. The common protein-bound solutes include CPD-108 (p-cresol), CPD-16817, CPD-425, CPD-16818 (CMPF), and HOMO-CYS . Protein-bound compounds pose challenges to treatment since their removal by conventional hemodialysis is low.
family_key: sm:name:a protein bound uremic retention solute

name: paramylon
annotation: Paramylon is a storage glucan produced by Euglenoids, discovered in 1850 . It is a high-molecular-weight (between 100 and 500 kDa) linear polysaccharide made up of glucose units connected by β(1->3) linkages . It contains no more than 700 polymerized glucose molecules and exhibits a high degree of crystallinity (about 90 %) in its natural state . Paramylon molecules are arranged as an intermolecular triple helix forming microfibrils, which in turn make up fibres. Rectangular and wedge-shaped segments consisting of these fibres are arranged to form granules of different shapes throughout the cytoplasm, called paramylon bodies. The granules are surrounded by a biomembrane and show an unusual high degree of crystallinity. The paramylon bodies are often visible as colorless or white particles in light microscopy . Grown under optimal conditions, TAX-3039 can accumulate paramylon to approximately 80% of its dry weight . Under anaerobic conditions paramylon is promptly degraded and converted to Wax-Esters "wax esters" composed of saturated fatty acids and alcohols with chain lengths of 10-18. The major constituents are CPD-7836 and CPD-7875 . This phenomenon, which is named PWY-5885 "wax ester fermentation", provides the cells with ATP. The wax esters, which are insoluble in water, accumulate in the cells, and are converted back to paramylon when aerobic conditions return .

annotation: A heteroatomic molecular entity is a molecular entity consisting of two or more chemical elements.

name: Rhizobium leguminosarum bv. trifolii exopolysaccharide repeat unit
annotation: In nodule bacteria, the production of Exopolysaccharides exopolysacchrides (EPS) is necessary for the nodulation of legumes such as Medicago, Pisum, Trifolium, Leucaena, and Vicia spp., which form indeterminate-type nodules. TAX-384 synthesizes an EPS that is necessary for establishing nitrogen-fixing symbiosis with Pisum sativum, Vicia faba, and Vicia sativa. This EPS is a polymer of a repeating unit consisting of a backbone of two glucose and two glucuronic acid residues, and a side chain that is biovar-specific. The repeat unit is modified by two pyruvyl and one acetyl substituents, and in some strain also by one nonstoichiometric hydroxybutanoyl group. The repeat units are linked together by an α(1→4) linkage. The structure shown here is from TAX-386 .
family_key: sm:name:rhizobium leguminosarum bv trifolii exopolysaccharide repeat unit
