name: a thia fatty acid
aliases: sulfur-containing fatty acid|sulfur-containing fatty acids|thia fatty acids
annotation: Thia fatty acids are fatty acids in which at least one of the chain methylene groups is replaced by sulfur.

name: an organophosphonate
annotation: Organophosphonates are organic molecules containing a direct carbon—phosphorus (C—P) bond in the form of C-PO(OH)2 or C-PO(OR)2 (where R can be either an alkyl or an aryl group). Phosphonates are quite common among different organisms, including both prokaryotes and eukaryotes. They occur primarily as components of phosphonolipids (CPD-1106 is a common phosphonate constituent of phospholipids, analogous to PHOSPHORYL-ETHANOLAMINE). In addition, phosphonates are constituents of polysaccharides, glycoproteins, glycolipids, and several antibiotics. While phosphonates are chemically sturdy compounds, they can be degraded by environmental microorganisms.

name: a nortropane alkaloid
annotation: Nortropane alkaloids are alklaoids dreived from PSEUDOTROPINE. They differ in the stereochemical configuration from the Tropane-Alkaloids "tropane alkaloids", which are derived from TROPINE.

name: a phenolphthiodiolenone A
annotation: Phenolphthiodiolenone Phenolphthiodiolenones are intermediates in Phenolphthiocerols phenolphthiocerol biosynthesis. Phenolphthiocerol glycolipids have been found in many Mycobacterium species, including TAX-1769, TAX-1768, TAX-1765, TAX-1781, and TAX-78331 . Two types of phenolphthiodiolenone exist. In Phenolphthiodiolenone-A "phenolphthiodiolenone A" the oxo and the methyl groups are attached at positions 3 and 4, respectively, and the hydroxyl groups are at positions 9 and 11. In Phenolphthiodiolenone-B "phenolphthiodiolenone B" a methyl group is missing from the beginning of the molecule, and thus the oxo and methyl groups are attached at positions 2 and 3, respectively, and the hydroxyl groups are at positions 8 and 10.

name: a β-D-glucosyl-N-acylsphinganine
annotation: "a glucosylceramide (I)" refers to Glucosyl-ceramides glucosylceramides whose long chain base is CPD-13612 sphinganine.

name: a flavonol
annotation: Favonols are secondary metabolites present in a wide variety of fruits and vegetables. They are based on a backbone of Flavones with a hydroxyl group attached to the C3 carbon.

name: a fatty aldehyde
annotation: Fatty-Aldehydes "Fatty aldehydes" are aldehydes that are formed by the reduction of the carboxylic acid group of Fatty-Acids fatty acids. Many enzymes that act on fatty acids, as well as the corresponding Fatty-Acyl-CoAs "fatty acyl-CoAs", Fatty-Alcohols alcohols, and Fatty-Aldehydes aldehydes, have evolved to recognize substrates with a limited range of chain lengths. To classify these enzymes, it is helpful to divide the substrates into smaller groups based on their chain length. The following subgroups have been defined: Short-Chain-Aldehydes "Short-chain fatty aldehydes" are derived from Short-chain-fatty-acids "fatty acids that have 3-5 carbons in the longest chain". Medium-Chain-Aldehydes "Medium-chain fatty aldehydes" are derived from Medium-chain-fatty-acids "fatty acids that have 6-12 carbons in the longest chain". Long-Chain-Aldehydes "Long-chain fatty aldehydes" are derived from Long-Chain-Fatty-Acids "fatty acids that have 13-22 carbons in the longest chain". Very-Long-Chain-Aldehydes "Very-long-chain fatty aldehydes" are derived from Very-long-chain-fatty-acids "fatty acids that have 23-27 carbons in the longest chain". Ultra-Long-Chain-Aldehydes "Ultra-long-chain fatty aldehydes" are derived from Ultra-long-chain-fatty-acids "fatty acids that have more than 27 carbons in the longest chain".

name: L-fructopyranose
annotation: This compound stands for a mixture of CPD-10727 and CPD-10728. In most cases, each of these compounds mutarotates quickly to form a mixture of the two, and thus it is the general rule to use this non-specific form in reactions unless it is specifically known that an enzyme can accept only one of the forms.

name: a cobamide upper ligand
annotation: Cobamides "A cobamide" is a corrinoid molecule based on the structure of CPD-20843, a cobalt-corrinoid hexaamide that is formed from COBINAMIDE by the attachment of an α-D-ribofuranose 3-phosphate residue at the 2 position of the aminopropanol chain. Cobamides serve as cofactors for a number of important enzymes. A key structural feature of cobinamides is a corrin ring with a cobalt ion in its center. Four of the six coordination sites of the cobalt ion are provided by the corrin ring, while the other two are provided by Cobamide-Upper-Ligands upper (β) and Cobamide-Lower-Ligands lower (α) ligands, which are located above and below the ring plane, respectively. The best known cobamide, Cobalamins cobalamin, contains DIMETHYLBENZIMIDAZOLE as the lower ligand and either a methyl group or 5'-deoxyadenosyl group as the upper ligand.

name: a TAG(18:3/40:0)
annotation: This class of triacylglycerols contains three acyl chains formed by the esterification of glycerol with three fatty acids. The names of triacylglycerol compound classes provide information about the three acyl chains of each member of the class, using the nomenclature TAG(a:b/y:z) where "a" represents the number of carbons found in one acyl chain and "b" represents the number of desaturations present in chain "a" and where "y" represents the total number of carbons found in the other two acyl chains and "z" represents the total number of desaturations present in the two chains included in "y." Therefore, in this TAG(18:3/40:0) class of compounds, one acyl chain has 18 carbons and three double bonds. The other two acyl chains have a total of 40 carbons and zero double bonds. The attachment sites of the acyl chains at the R1 (sn-1), R2 (sn-2), or R3 (sn-3) position along the glyceryl backbone may vary among class members and cannot be inferred from their order in the compound class name. Additionally, the location and orientation of the double bonds in the unsaturated acyl chain(s) may vary among members of the class and are not specified by this notation.

name: a [mitogen-activated protein kinase] L-tyrosine phosphate
annotation: Mitogen-Activated-Protein-Kinase "Mitogen-activated protein kinases" (MAP kinase or MAPK) are eukaryotic protein kinases that are specific to the amino acids serine, threonine, and tyrosine. They are found in all eukaryotes including unicellular eukaryotes, fungi, plants, and animals. MAP kinases are involved in directing cellular responses to a diverse array of stimuli, such as mitogens, osmotic stress, heat shock and proinflammatory cytokines. They regulate cell functions including proliferation, gene expression, differentiation, mitosis, cell survival, and apoptosis . MAP kinases are catalytically inactive in their base form. In order to be active, they need to be phosphorylated. Most of the MAP kinases belong to the "classical MAP kinases" family and need to be phosphorylated on two amino acids, a threonine and a tyrosine, which are found in a TxY motif at the activation loop. Phosphorylation of the tyrosine precedes phosphorylation of the threonine. Some MAP kinases are atypical and require only a single residue in their activation loops to be phosphorylated.
family_key: sm:name:a mitogen activated protein kinase l tyrosine phosphate

name: a type 2 histo-blood group antigen precursor disaccharide
annotation: The histo-blood group antigen precursor disaccharides are found at the terminal end of oligosaccharide chains attached to proteins or sphingolipids, and serve as the actual substrates for various glycosyltransferases that terminate the chain elongation process, forming the ABH or Lewis antigens. Five types of precursor disaccharides are known to carry ABH activity - type 1 through type 4 and type 6. Type 1 precursors are widely expressed on endodermally-derived tissues such as lining epithelia and glandular epithelia. Type 2 precursors are found mainly on ecto- or mesodermally-derived tissues, including erythrocytes . Type 3 and 4 precursors are found on ecto- or endodermally-derived tissues, including the salivary glands and kidneys . Since ABH antigens are present not only on erythrocytes, but also on various organs such as the salivary glands and kidneys , they are called histo-blood group ABH antigens. Six types of precursor disaccharides have been described: beta-Gal-13-beta-GlcNac-R: β-D-galactosyl-(1→3)-N-acetyl-β-D-glucosaminyl-R B-Gal-14-NacGlc-R: β-D-galactosyl-(1→4)-N-acetyl-β-D-glucosaminyl-R beta-Gal-13-alpha-GalNac-R: β-D-galactosyl-(1→3)-N-acetyl-α-D-galactosaminyl-R beta-Gal-13-beta-GalNac-R: β-D-galactosyl-(1→3)-N-acetyl-β-D-galactosaminyl-R beta-Gal-13-beta-Gal-R: β-D-galactosyl-(1,3)-β-D-galactosyl-R beta-Gal-14-beta-Glc-R: β-D-galactosyl-(1,4)-β-D-glucosyl-R

name: a sesterterpenoid
annotation: Terpenoids Terpenoids make up a very large family of natural products containing more than 50,000 structurally diverse compounds. They are categorized by the number of C5 isoprene units as follows: Hemiterpenes Hemiterpenoids (one C5 unit), Monoterpenes monoterpenoids (C10; two C5 units), Sesquiterpenes sesquiterpenoids (C15; three C5 units), Diterpenes diterpenoids (C20; four C5 units), Sesterterpenes sesterterpenoids (C25; five C5 units), Triterpenes triterpenoids (C30; six C5 units), C35-Terpenes "sesquarterpenoids" (C35; seven C5 units), and Tetraterpenes tetraterpenoids (C40; eight C5 units).

name: chondroitin sulfate E
annotation: Chondroitin-sulfates "Chondroitin sulfate" is a sulfated Glycosaminoglycans "glycosaminoglycan" composed of alternating units of CPD-12557 and CPD-12521. It is usually found attached to proteins as part of a proteoglycan. The saccharide units can be sulfated in variable positions and quantities - CPD-12557 can be sulfated at positions 4 and 6 to yield CPD-12516, CPD-12565 or CPD-12526, while CPD-12521 can be sulfated at position 2 to form CPD-12527. The following names have been used to define different sulfation states: Chondroitin-Sulfate-A "Chondroitin sulfate A" contains CPD-12521 and CPD-12516 Chondroitin-Sulfate-C "Chondroitin sulfate C" contains CPD-12521 and CPD-12565 Chondroitin-Sulfate-D "Chondroitin sulfate D" contains CPD-12527 and CPD-12565 Chondroitin-Sulfate-E "Chondroitin sulfate E" contains CPD-12521 and CPD-12526 The term "chondroitin sulfate B" has been used in the past to refer to Dermatan-Sulfate, but is not used any more.

name: a reduced factor 420
aliases: a reduced cofactor F420|a reduced coenzyme F420
annotation: One of the distinguishing features of methanogenic bacteria is their blue-green fluorescence when irradiated with UV light. This fluorescence is due to F420, a flavin-related redox-active compound that is used as a cosubstrate by many enzymes in these organisms. While it is well-known for its role in methanogenesis, factor 420 has also been found in various eubacteria of the actinobacteria class, such as Streptomyces, Rhodococcus, Nocardioides, and Mycobacterium, where it assists in the synthesis of antibiotics like tetracycline and lincomycin. In addition, a modified form of the factor is also used by cyanobacteria in the repair of DNA damage. Factor 420 (F420), named for its intense absorbance at 420 nm, was originally isolated by Cheeseman et al , and later identified by Eirich et al as the N-(N-L-lactyl-γ-L-glutamyl)-L-glutamic acid phosphodiester of 7,8-didemethyl-8-hydroxy-5-deazariboflavin 5'-phosphate . The PWY-8112 "F420 biosynthetic pathway" produces the intermediate CPD-17242 "F420-0", which contains no glutamate side chains. Glutamates are then added by several enzymes. F420 molecules isolated from different organisms differ in the number of the glutamate molecules in the side chains, as well as the nature of the bond (which could be either α or γ) .

annotation: Phenolphthiodiolenone Phenolphthiodiolenones are intermediates in Phenolphthiocerols phenolphthiocerol biosynthesis. Phenolphthiocerol glycolipids have been found in many Mycobacterium species, including TAX-1769, TAX-1768, TAX-1765, TAX-1781, and TAX-78331 . Two types of phenolphthiodiolenone exist. In Phenolphthiodiolenone-A "phenolphthiodiolenone A" the oxo and the methyl groups are attached at positions 3 and 4, respectively, and the hydroxyl groups are at positions 9 and 11. In Phenolphthiodiolenone-B "phenolphthiodiolenone B" a methyl group is missing from the beginning of the molecule, and thus the oxo and methyl groups are attached at positions 2 and 3, respectively, and the hydroxyl groups are at positions 8 and 10.

name: a diterpenoid
annotation: Terpenoids Terpenoids make up a very large family of natural products containing more than 50,000 structurally diverse compounds. They are categorized by the number of C5 isoprene units as follows: Hemiterpenes Hemiterpenoids (one C5 unit), Monoterpenes monoterpenoids (C10; two C5 units), Sesquiterpenes sesquiterpenoids (C15; three C5 units), Diterpenes diterpenoids (C20; four C5 units), Sesterterpenes sesterterpenoids (C25; five C5 units), Triterpenes triterpenoids (C30; six C5 units), C35-Terpenes "sesquarterpenoids" (C35; seven C5 units), and Tetraterpenes tetraterpenoids (C40; eight C5 units). Diterpenes Diterpenoids (also referred to as diterpenes) are widely distributed in plants, marine invertebrates, and microorganisms. They are produced from the common isoprene precursor GERANYLGERANYL-PP. The diverse carbon skeletons of diterpenes are formed through carbocation-mediated cyclization cascades catalyzed by diterpene cyclases. Diterpene cyclases are categorized into two classes, class I and class II, on the basis of their reaction mechanisms and catalytic domains.

name: a [mycoredoxin]-L-cysteine
annotation: Mycoredoxin is a mycothiol-dependent reductase identified in Mycobacteria that couples the reducing power of CPD1G-2 to redox active proteins . Mycoredoxin acts as an oxidoreductase exclusively linked to the mycothiol electron transfer pathway and can reduce S-mycothiolated mixed disulfides. It contains a thioredoxin fold and a mycothiol-binding site. Mycothiol is covalently attached to a nucleophilic cysteine, forming Mycothiol-mycoredoxin-mixed-disulfides .

name: a type I plant arabinogalactan
annotation: Arabinogalactans (AGs) are widely spread throughout the plant kingdom. Many edible and inedible plants are rich sources of these polysaccharides. AGs are associated with the pectin cell-wall component by physical bonds, and some of them are covalently linked to the complex pectin molecule as neutral side chains . AGs have been classified into two general forms, named Plant-Arabinogalactans-I "type I" and Plant-Arabinogalactans-II "type II". AGs of type I have a linear β-(1→4)-D-Galp backbone, while those of type II have a β-(1→3)-D-Galp backbone. 20-40% of the type I AGs consist of short chains of α-L-Araf residues, mostly (1→5)-linked at position 3. It is commonly found in pectins from citrus, apple and potato. Type I AG has been isolated from the skin of TAX-371859 (prickly pear) fruits .

name: a 24-epibrassinolide sulfate
annotation: Sulfated brassinosteroids, including 24-epibrassinolide-sulfate can be produced in vitro, but the single sulfate group present on these compounds may be added at one of several different positions , represented by "R" groups on this compound. Brassinosteroid sulfates have not been detected in vivo in plants to date, but, there is some indirect evidence for their existence .

name: an A antigen
aliases: an A histo-blood group antigen|a histo-blood group substance A|an N-acetyl-α-D-galactosaminyl-(1,3)-[α-L-fucosyl-(1,2)]-β-D-galactosyl-R|a [glycoprotein]-N-acetyl-α-D-galactosaminyl-(1,3)-[α-L-fucosyl-(1,2)]-D-galactose
annotation: The A antigen is an oligosaccharide that decorates proteins on the surface of red blood cells in people with A blood type. It is generated from H-antigen, which is found in people with O blood type, by the attachement of an CPD-12557 residue by a 1,3 linkage to one of the β-D-galactosyl residues of the H antigen. People with AB blood type contain both A-antigen "A antigens" and B-antigen "B antigens". The majority of the A, B and H antigens are linked to the ends of long polylactosamine chains attached mainly to band 3 protein (encoded by SLC4A1), the anion exchange protein of the red blood cells. A minor fraction of the antigens is attached to a ceramide moiety, forming neutral glycosphingolipids.

name: a fucan
annotation: Fucan is a generic term used to define a family of L-fucoses-containing sulfated polysaccharides with extremely variable molecular weights that are produced by brown algae (TAX-2870) and marine vertebrates . It has been recommended to use this term to refer to polysaccharide based mainly on sulfated L-fucose, with less than 10% other monosaccharides . In marine invertebrates, sulfated fucans have been found in the egg jelly coat of sea urchins and in the body wall of sea cucumber . In brown algae the fucan is present in the cell walls and can account for more than 40% of the dry weight of isolated cell walls . The fucans of brown algae are often called Fucoidans fucoidans. The structures of fucans vary among species and sometimes among different parts of the organism . Fucan structures can be divided into three main groups: one group, found in TAX-33637 species of brown algae and several species of sea urchines, has a central chain composed of (1→3)-linked α-L-fucopyranose residues ; a second group, found so far only in the sea urchins TAX-7640 and TAX-7671, has a central chain composed of (1→4)-linked α-L-fucopyranose residues ; the third group, which includes fucoidan isolated from TAX-52968 and TAX-3011 species of brown algae, has a central chain composed of repeating (1→3) and (1→4) linked α-L-fucopyranose residues . Besides fucose and sulfate, the presence of monosaccharide residues such as mannose, galactose, glucose, xylose and uronic acids were also identified . Several fucose-containing polymers with less than 90% fucosyl residues have also been reported from brown algae and some of them were given names: ascophyllan or xylofucoglycuronan, isolated from TAX-52969, is based on a backbone of mannuronate with branches of 3-O-D-xylosyl-L-fucose-4-sulfate ; sargassa

name: an isoprenoid
annotation: The nomenclature of isoprenoids and their derivatives is somewhat confusing. Here are some guidelines for understanding these names: Isoprenoids are composed of repeating isoprene units. The units can be attached in either a trans conformation (the two carbons are on opposite sides of the double bond) or a cis conformation (the two carbons are on the same side of the double bond). It is common to use E and Z instead of trans and cis , respectively. The numbering of carbons in the molecule starts from the carbon attached to the hydroxyl or phosphate group. The isoprene unit furthest from the C1 carbon is called the ω unit. Unlike all other isoprene units, the ω unit is not a stereo center since the last carbon is attached to two identical methyl groups. There are two widely used naming systems, and unfortunately they refer to the isoprene units in opposite order. One method describes the molecule starting with the unit with the lowest carbon number (ie the one furthest from the ω unit), naming each of the stereo-active carbons by its number, followed by its stereo designation. For example, CPD-11977 "this molecule" is referred to as 2,6-cis,10-trans-tetraprenyl diphosphate or (2Z,6Z,10E)-tetraprenyl diphosphate. According to the other method, the molecule is described by the stereoconfiguration of the molecule starting with the ω unit. For example CPD-11977 is referred to as ω,E,Z,Z tetraprenyl diphosphate. On top of this, many of the shorter molecules have trivial names that are commonly used. Some of these names are specific for a particular stereoisomer and some are used for all stereoisomer. For example, GERANYLGERANYL-PP "geranylgeranyl diphosphate" is commonly used for the all-trans isomer of the molecule with four isoprene units, while farnesyl diphosphate is used

name: an XLXG xylogulcan
aliases: a xyloglucan with galactose side chain at position 2
annotation: Xyloglucan Xyloglucan is the major hemicellulose in dicotyledon type I cell walls. The cross-linking between xyloglucan chains is a major factor in the stability of the three-dimensional matrix between adjacent cellulose microfibrils . Xyloglucan has a backbone of β-1,4-linked cellotetraose units, which are substituted in C-6 by α-D-xylosyl residues at two or three of the first three glucosyl residues, but not in the position closest to the reducing end . Some of the xylosyl residues are subsequently further substituted by (1,2)-β-D-galactosyl residues at the second and/or third xylose residue and by (1,2)-α-L-fucose at the galactosyl unit of the third xylose residue . A common terminology uses G for basic non-substituted glucosyl units within a xylosylglycan, X for glucose residues substituted by α-D-xylose, L for glucose residues substituted by α-D-xylose and β-D-galactose, and F for glucose residues substituted by α-D-xylose, β-D-galactose and α-L-fucose . Most xylosylglucans consist of repeating heptasaccharide Xyloglucans-Xylose XXXG units.

name: a β-lactam
annotation: This compound class stands for Lactams lactams in which the amide bond is contained within a four-membered ring, which includes the amide nitrogen and the carbonyl carbon. β-Lactam compounds imitate the naturally occurring D-ALA-D-ALA substrate for the group of enzymes known as penicillin binding proteins (PBPs), which cross-link the peptidoglycan in bacterial cell wall, and thus act as powerful antibiotics . Naturally occurring Beta-Lactams "β-lactam antibiotics" are classified into several groups according to their chemical structure: In Monolactams monolactams the β-lactam ring is not fused to another ring. This class includes compounds such as the Nocardicins nocardicins and the Monobactams monobactams . In Penams penams the β-lactam ring is fused to a saturated five atom ring (a 4:5 structure). Penams contain a sulfur atom at position 1 of the five atom ring (for example, the PENICILLIN penicillins) . Related classes include the Carbapenams carbapenams, which contain a carbon atom in that position , and the Clavams oxopenams (also known as clavams), which contain an oxygen atom at that position (e.g. CPD-9261) . Penems Penems are molecues similar to penams in which the five atom ring is unsaturated at positions 2-3. Similarly, penems contain a sulfur atom at position 1 of the five atom ring, while Carbapenems carbapenems contains a carbon atom in that position . In Cephams cephams and Cephems cephems the β-lactam ring is fused to a six-atom ring (a 4:6 strcuture) that contains a sulfur atom at position 1. In cephams the ring is saturated, while cephems have an unsaturated bond at positions 3-4 . 1-Oxocephams/1-oxacephems conain an oxygen atom at position 1 .

name: a TAG(18:3/40:1)
annotation: This class of triacylglycerols contains three acyl chains formed by the esterification of glycerol with three fatty acids. The names of triacylglycerol compound classes provide information about the three acyl chains of each member of the class, using the nomenclature TAG(a:b/y:z) where "a" represents the number of carbons found in one acyl chain and "b" represents the number of desaturations present in chain "a" and where "y" represents the total number of carbons found in the other two acyl chains and "z" represents the total number of desaturations present in the two chains included in "y." Therefore, in this TAG(18:3/40:1) class of compounds, one acyl chain has 18 carbons and three double bonds. The other two acyl chains have a total of 40 carbons and one double bond. The attachment sites of the acyl chains at the R1 (sn-1), R2 (sn-2), or R3 (sn-3) position along the glyceryl backbone may vary among class members and cannot be inferred from their order in the compound class name. name: a di-rhamnolipid
annotation: This class contains glycosides composed of a di-rhamnose moiety joined in an O-glycosidic linkage to a lipid moiety. The rhamnose moieties are linked to each other through an α-1,2-glycosidic linkage. The lipid moiety is mainly two (sometimes one or three) β-hydroxy fatty acid chains (represented here in part by R1 and R2) linked to each other through an ester bond between the β-hydroxyl group of the chain distal to the glycosidic bond with the carboxyl group of the chain proximal to the glycosidic bond. The carboxyl group of the distal chain is usually free, but in a few cases it is esterified with a short alkyl group. The fatty acid chains may be saturated, mono-unsaturated, or poly-unsaturated and the chain length may vary from C8 to C16. In rare cases the rhamnose 2-hydroxyl group distal to the glycosidic bond may be acylated with a long chain alkenoate. . Also see Mono-Rhamnolipids and 3-3-HAAs.

annotation: This compound is an intermediate in the biosynthesis of the CPD-13839. In nodule bacteria, the production of Exopolysaccharides exopolysacchrides (EPS) is necessary for the nodulation of legumes such as Medicago, Pisum, Trifolium, Leucaena, and Vicia spp., which form indeterminate-type nodules. TAX-1041145 synthesizes an acidic EPS, which is necessary to establish nitrogen-fixing symbiosis with Pisum sativum, Vicia faba, and Vicia sativa. This EPS is a polymer of an octasaccharide repeating unit consisting of a backbone of two glucose and two glucuronic acid residues, a side chain of three glucose and one galactose residues. The octasaccharide is modified by two pyruvyl, two nonstoichiometric O-acetyl, and one nonstoichiometric hydroxybutanoyl groups. EPSs with a similar structure are synthesized by a number of other strains of TAX-384.

annotation: Glycosaminoglycans Glycosaminoglycans consist of hexosamine and either hexuronic acid or galactose units that are arranged in alternating unbranched sequence, and carry sulfate substituents in various positions. The common glycosaminoglycans include Galactosaminoglycans galactosaminoglycans (Chondroitin-sulfates and Dermatan-Sulfate) and Glucosaminoglycans glucosaminoglycans (Heparan-Sulfate, HEPARIN, KERATAN-6-SULFATE and Hyaluronan).

name: hemicellulose
annotation: Please note that hemicellulose is a not a precise biological term. The plant cell wall is composed of CELLULOSE and many additional complex polymers. Unlike cellulose, which is crystalline and resistant to hydrolysis, the other polymers are amorphous and are easily hydrolyzed by dilute acid or base. Hemicellulose is a heterogeneous term that is used for all the plant cell wall components that are soluble in dilute alkali solutions, or that go into solution quite readily in hot dilute mineral acids with the formation of simple sugars. The term is often used to describe all the polysaccharide components of the cell wall other than cellulose. Hemicelluloses constitute about one-fourth of perennial plants and about one-third of annual plants. Since the term does not refer to a particular chemical entity, hemicelluloses extracted from different plant sources are rarely identical. However, D-xylose is the dominant building unit of the hemicelluloses of most woods and annual plants. D-xylose occurs as the polymer Xylans, as derivatives of xylan, such as Glucuronoxylans glucuronoxylan and Arabinoxylan arabinoxylan, and as side chains on other types of backbones, such as Xyloglucan xyloglucan and Xylogalacturonan xylogalacturonan. D-Mannose is also very abundant, and occurs as the polymer 1-4-Mannan mannan or in combination with D-glucose or D-galactose as Glucomannans glucomannans, Galactomannans galactomannan, or Galactoglucomannans galactoglucomannan. Arabinose is common as well, either in the form of Arabinans "L-arabinan" or in the form of side chains on other polysaccharides. Other sugars common in hemicellulose include Galactose galactose, Rhamnose rhamnose, Galacturonate galacturonate and Mannuronate mannuronate. Hemicellulose consists of shorter chains than cellulose, t

name: a lanthanide cation
annotation: The lanthanide (or lanthanoid) series is a group of 15 elements that have atomic numbers 57 to 71. This group starts from lanthanum and it ends at lutetium. All lanthanoids except for lutetium are f-block elements, meaning that electrons fill the 4f electron shell, except for lutetium which is a d-block lanthanoid. They also belong to the rare earth elements, along with the non-lanthanoids Scandium and Yttrium. Lanthanoids are metals. Most of them slowly turn into their hydroxides when they are placed into water, like the alkali metals. When placed in the air they usually form a coating of oxide like most metals.

Medium-chain-fatty-acids "Medium-chain fatty acids" (MCFA) are fatty acids with aliphatic chain of 6-12 carbons. Long-Chain-Fatty-Acids "Long-chain fatty acids" (LCFA) are fatty acids with aliphatic chains of 13-22 carbons. Very-long-chain-fatty-acids "Very-long-chain fatty acids" (VLCFA) are fatty acids with aliphatic chains of 23 carbons or longer.

They are the principal components responsible for the pharmaceutical activities of ginseng. Based on the structure of their aglycones, ginsenosides have been categorized into three broad types; Protopanaxadiol-type-ginsenosides "protopanaxadiol-type ginsenosides" (PPD, containing hydroxyl groups at positions 3 and 20), Protopanaxatriol-type-ginsenosides "protopanaxatriol-type ginsenosides" (PPT, containing hydroxyl groups at positions 3, 6 and 20), and Oleanonic-acid-type-ginsenosides "oleanonic-acid-type saponins".

name: an oxyluciferin
annotation: Luciferin (from the Latin lucifer, "light-bringer") is a generic term for the substrates of the luciferase enzymes, which are found in organisms that generate bioluminescence. Luciferins typically undergo an enzyme-catalyzed oxidation and the resulting excited state intermediate emits light upon decaying to its ground state. This class includes the oxidized forms of the different luciferins.

annotation: In this class of glycosylinositol phosphoceramides, a long carbon chain is attached at position R1 to form a fatty amide and a long carbon chain is attached at position R2 to form a long chain base. Either or both of these chains may contain one or more double bonds and may have hydroxyl groups attached. Based on the notation GIPC(m:n)-p, each compound within this class has a total of 40 carbons (m), one double bond (n), and three hydroxyl groups (p) present in the sphingoid base and the fatty amide. However, the length, hydroxylation position, double bond position, and double bond orientation of the groups attached at R2 and R3 will differ between individual members of this group of compounds.

name: a short chain fatty acid
annotation: The term "fatty acid" was originally coined to describe aliphatic monocarboxylic acids derived from or contained in esterified form in an animal or vegetable fat, oil or wax. However, over the years the term has been expanded to refer to additional, shorter monocarboxylic acids with an aliphatic tail such as PROPIONATE and BUTYRIC_ACID (but not the shorter FORMATE and ACET). The length of a fatty acid is determined by the length of the longest carbon chain, including the carbon of the carboxy group. Common natural fatty acids usually have an even number of carbons in the longest chain and can be either saturated or unsaturated. In many organisms they are often modified by branching, hydroxylation, methylation, epoxidation and other types of modifications. Inside living cells fatty acids are rarely found in free form. They are usually bound to coenzyme A or acyl-carrier proteins, or form part of Triacylglycerides triglycerides, Phospholipids phospholipids, Lipopolysaccharides lipopolysaccharides, and Cholesterol-esters cholesterol esters. Many enzymes that act on fatty acids, as well as the corresponding Fatty-Acyl-CoAs "fatty acyl-CoAs", alcohols, and aldehydes, have evolved to recognize substrates with a limited range of chain lengths. To classify these enzymes, it is helpful to divide the substrates into smaller groups based on their chain length. The following subgroups have been defined: Short-chain-fatty-acids "Short-chain fatty acids" (SCFA) have 3-5 carbons in the longest chain. Medium-chain-fatty-acids "Medium-chain fatty acids" (MCFA) have 6-12 carbons in the longest chain. Long-Chain-Fatty-Acids "Long-chain fatty acids" (LCFA) have 13-22 carbons in the longest chain. Very-long-chain-fatty-acids "Very-long-chain fatty acids" (VLCFA) have 23-27 carbons in the lo

name: a glucomannan
annotation: Glucomannans Glucomannans (GM), along with Galactoglucomannans galactoglucomannans (GGM), are the major hemicellulosic components of the secondary cell walls of softwoods, but occur only in minor amounts in the secondary cell walls of hardwoods. Glucomannans Glucomannans consist of a backbone composed of β(1→4)-linked glucopyranose and mannopyranose residues, and are often branched. Unlike GMs, GGM chains are substituted by galactopyranose residues. Glucomannans show some variations in structural features depending on the plant species and stage of plant development . Low-molecular-weight GMs, acetylated at position 2 or 3 of some mannopyranose residues, were isolated from aspen and birch wood . Soluble, often partially acetylated GMs occur as constituents of bulbs, tubers, seeds, roots, and leaves of some non-gramineousmonocotyl plants. An example are the GMs from plants of the genus TAX-25641, which are known for their medicinal value. A GM from the potato-like plant TAX-78372 is known as konjac mannan in Japanese food applications. Studies of konjac mannan showed that is is branched (8%), with branches joined through the C-6 position of only the glucose residues. Both glucopyranose and mannopyranose residues (in the ratio of 1 : 2) were suggested to terminate the branches .

name: a [glycerolipid]-arachidonate
annotation: ARACHIDONIC_ACID "Arachidonate" (arachidonic acid) is a polyunsaturated ω-6 fatty acid with a 20-carbon chain and four cis-double bonds. It is produced at high levels by mosses (see PWY-5353), some plants, and by some marine bacteria (see PWY-7583). Mammals cannot synthesize ARACHIDONIC_ACID de novo, but most mammals are able to synthesize it from simpler unsaturated fatty acids (see PWY-7592). In mammals ARACHIDONIC_ACID is present in membrane phospholipids (especially phosphatidylethanolamine, phosphatidylcholine, and phosphatidylinositides) and is abundant in the brain, muscles, and liver. It accounts for roughly 10-20% of the phospholipid fatty acid content of skeletal muscle cells. In addition to being involved in cellular signaling as a lipid second messenger, arachidonate is also a key inflammatory intermediate and can also act as a vasodilator. Like other fatty acids, ARACHIDONIC_ACID is rarely found in its free form. It is usually found either as ARACHIDONYL-COA or CPD-17284 "incorporated into a lipid".

annotation: Polyols are compounds that contain two or more hydroxy groups.

name: Fe(III) oxide-hydroxide
annotation: Iron(III) oxide-hydroxides may occur in anhydrous [FeO(OH)] or hydrated [FeO(OH)·nH2O] forms. The most common forms of iron(III) oxide-hydroxides are the minerals goethite and lepidocrocite, but other forms exist, including akaganeite, feroxyhyte, siderogel and limonite.

name: a β-lactone
annotation: A Lactones "lactone" is a cyclic ester, the product of condensation of an alcohol group and a carboxylate group within the same molecule. It is characterized by a closed ring consisting of two or more carbon atoms and a single oxygen atom, with a ketone group in one of the carbons adjacent to the ring oxygen. There are several types of lactones - Beta-lactones contain 3 carbons in the ring, Gamma-lactones contains 4, Delta-lactones contains 5, and Epsilon-lactones contains 6 carbons. The most stable structure for lactones are γ-lactones and δ-lactones, because of the minimal angle strain in their structure. In fact, γ-lactones are so stable that, in the presence of dilute acids at room temperature, 4-hydroxy acids esterify and cyclize spontaneously to form lactones. β-lactones, on the other hand, are not very stable. The name lactone is derived from the lactone of L-LACTATE, which is called CPD-9549, although it is really a di-lactone.

name: a pyridine
annotation: A pyridine is any organonitrogen heterocyclic compound based on a PYRIDINE skeleton and its substituted derivatives.

name: a mycolipodienoate
aliases: a mycolipodienoic acid|a mycothidienoic acid|a mycothidienoate
annotation: A unique feature of mycobacterial cell wall lipids is the presence of very long chain fatty acids with multiple methyl branches toward the carboxyl end of the carbon chain. Many types of such acids exist, including Mycosanoic-Acids "mycosanoic acids", Mycolipanoic-Acids "mycolipanoic acids", Mycolipanolic-Acids "mycolipanolic acids", Mycolipenic-Acids "mycolipenic acids", Mycolipodienoic-Acids "mycolipodienoic acids", Phthioceranic-Acids "phthioceranic acids", and Mycocerosic-Acids "mycocerosic acids". Mycolipenoic acids are 2,4,6-trimethyl-alkdienoic acids.The first double bond is at position 2 .

name: a TAG(18:3/40:2)
annotation: This class of triacylglycerols contains three acyl chains formed by the esterification of glycerol with three fatty acids. The names of triacylglycerol compound classes provide information about the three acyl chains of each member of the class, using the nomenclature TAG(a:b/y:z) where "a" represents the number of carbons found in one acyl chain and "b" represents the number of desaturations present in chain "a" and where "y" represents the total number of carbons found in the other two acyl chains and "z" represents the total number of desaturations present in the two chains included in "y." Therefore, in this TAG(18:3/40:2) class of compounds, one acyl chain has 18 carbons and three double bonds. The other two acyl chains have a total of 40 carbons and two double bonds. The attachment sites of the acyl chains at the R1 (sn-1), R2 (sn-2), or R3 (sn-3) position along the glyceryl backbone may vary among class members and cannot be inferred from their order in the compound class name. Additionally, the location and orientation of the double bonds in the unsaturated acyl chain(s) may vary among members of the class and are not specified by this notation.

name: a [glycerolipid]-di-homo-γ-linolenate
annotation: CPD-8120 "Di-homo-γ-linolenate" (dihomo-γ-linolenic acid) is a 20-carbon ω-6 polyunsaturated fatty acid with a 20-carbon chain and three cis double bonds at positions 8, 11 and 14. Di-homo-γ-linolenate is formed by elongation of CPD-8117, and is usually converted rapidly to other compounds. However, it has been detected in small amounts as a component of cellular lipids in fungi , algae , protozoa , and animals . Like other fatty acids, CPD-8120 is rarely found in its free form. It is usually found either as CPD-14407 or CPD-17283 "incorporated into a lipid".

name: a short-chain primary alcohol
annotation: A primary alcohol is a compound in which a hydroxy group, -OH, is attached to a saturated carbon atom that is connected to no more than one other carbon atom. Primary alcohols are also classified based on their length. Short-Chain-Primary-Alcohols "Short-chain primary alcohols" have alkyl chains with fewer than six carbons. Medium-Chain-Primary-Alcohols "Medium-chain primary alcohols" have alkyl chains of 6-12 carbons. Long-Chain-Primary-Alcohols "Long-chain primary alcohols" (also known as fatty alcohols) have alkyl chains of 13-22 carbons. Very-Long-Chain-Primary-Alcohols "Very long-chain primary alcohols" have alkyl chains of 23 carbons or longer.

name: a coumarin
annotation: The coumarins are a major group of plant natural products derived from the phenylpropanoid pathway. They can be classified into four categories: Simple-Coumarins "simple coumarins", Pyrone-substituted-coumarins "pyrone-substituted coumarins", Furanocoumarins furanocoumarins, and Pyranocoumarins pyranocoumarins .

name: a protopanaxadiol-type ginsenoside
annotation: Ginsenosides Ginsenosides or panaxosides are a class of steroid glycosides and triterpene saponins found exclusively in the plant genus TAX-4053 (ginseng). They are the principal components responsible for the pharmaceutical activities of ginseng. Ginsenosides are composed of an aglycone and sugar moieties. Based on the structure of their aglycones, ginsenosides have been categorized into three broad types; Protopanaxadiol-type-ginsenosides "protopanaxadiol-type ginsenosides" (PPD), which contain hydroxyl groups at positions 3 and 20, Protopanaxatriol-type-ginsenosides "protopanaxatriol-type ginsenosides" (PPT), which contain hydroxyl groups at positions 3, 6 and 20, and Oleanonic-acid-type-ginsenosides "oleanonic-acid-type saponins". Although ginsenosides only have three kinds of aglycones, more than 60 kinds of ginsenosides have been identified by now due to the variability in the type of sugar moieties, their number, and the carbon positions they link to. The biological activity of ginsenoside increases with the decrease of the number of the sugar moieties. All of the ginsenosides found in large quantities in wild ginseng (known as major ginsenosides) are heavily glycosylated, while the more active less-glycosylated ginsenosides are found in much smalller quantities (and are commonly referred to as the minor ginsenosides). Following oral intake of ginseng, the large low-solubility and poor-permeability major ginsenosides cannot be absorbed by the human body directly , and are deglycosylated into smaller forms in the gastrointestinal tract by intestinal bacteria and/or digestive enzymes . Glycosidase enzymes that can convert the major ginsenosides to minor ginsenosides are not common and are of commercial interest.

name: a phenylurea-type cytokinin
annotation: Cytokinins are a class of plant hormones that promote cell division, or cytokinesis, in plant roots and shoots. While their main role is to control cell growth and differentiation, they also affect apical dominance, axillary bud growth, and leaf senescence. The cytokinins are structurally diverse, and have been divided into two main types: Adenine-Cytokinins "adenine-type cytokinins" and Phenylurea-Cytokinins "phenylurea-type cytokinins". All the natural cytokinins fall into the first type - phenylurea-type cytokinins are synthetic. The adenine cytokinins are classified further by the configuration of their N6-side chain as isoprenoid or aromatic cytokinins. The most prevalent cytokinins are by far those with an unsaturated isoprenoid side chain, and in particular those with a trans-hydroxylated N6-side chain, such as CPD-4210 and its derivatives.

name: a flavan-4-ol
annotation: A flavan-4-ol is Flavanoids with a hydroxyl substitution at carbon 4.

name: a lycopanerol
annotation: Lycopanerols are a family of tetraterpenoid ethers related to lycopadiene. These have been isolated from the L strain of TAX-38881. They contain one to three lycopanol moieties and long chain hydrocarbons linked by ether or phenoxy bonds giving rise to high molecular weight ether lipids. Some of them can exhibit up to 150 carbon atoms .

name: pinoresinol
annotation: Pinoresinol is a lignan, produced by by radical β-β' coupling of two CONIFERYL-ALCOHOL molecules. It is produced by plants as a defense molecule, and is a component of Lignins lignin. Pinoresinol is known to be liberated from lignin during the degradation of dead plant material by white rot fungi and to be degraded by bacteria . Pinoresinol is contained in cereals, particularly in whole-grain rye products, in olive oil,and in various members of the TAX-3318 family, from the TAX-3328, TAX-3337 and TAX-3319 genera.

name: ι-carrageenose
annotation: Carrageenans Carrageenans and Carrageenoses carrageenoses are sulfated polysaccharides that are extracted from sea weeds belonging to the TAX-2763 (red algae), mainly from TAX-2769, and species from the TAX-31444 and TAX-33664 genera. They are laid out in the cell wall at a high density and can constitute up to 50% of the algae dry mass. The basic units of Carrageenans carrageenans are repeats of alternating α-1,3-linked D-galactose and β-1,4-linked D-galactose units, which are often substituted by sulfate. Carrageenans are classified according to the number and the position of sulfated ester . In Carrageenoses carrageenoses the α-1,3-linked galactose units are modified by having a 3,6-anhydro bridge. These bridges, which form a double helix during aggregation, are responsible for the gelation that is typical to these compounds. Despite this distinction, carrageenoses are often referred to as carrageenans. The three most industrially exploited carrageenans, namely, kappa-Carrageenan, iota-Carrageenan and Lambda-Carrageenan, are distinguished by the presence of one, two or three ester-sulfate groups per repeating disaccharide unit respectively. iota-Carrageenan is sulfated at position 4 of the β-1,4-linked D-galactose units and position 2 of the α-1,3-linked 3,6-anhydrogalactose units.

annotation: The predominant long-chain base in animal ceramides is sphingosine, which is only present in trace amount in plant ceramides. In contrast, sphinganine, 4-hydroxysphinganine, and 4-hydroxy-8-sphingenine are the prevalent long-chain bases in plants. The acyl chain of plant ceramides contains alpha-hydroxylated very long chain fatty acids. The plant ceramide (IV) in MetaCyc represents plant ceramides with 4-hydroxy-8-sphingenine as the long-chain base.

name: (RS)-linalool
annotation: Linalools Linalool (3,7-dimethyl-1,6-octadien-3-ol) is an acyclic monoterpene alcohol with a pleasant fragrance associated with a diverse array of plants . It is found in lavender, bergamot, jasmine, basil, rosemary, sage, star-anise, cinnamon, clove, nutmeg, coriander, cardamom, ginger, black pepper and mandarin, and is one of the three terpene compounds principally responsible for Muscat aroma. Linalool is a primary compound in floral scent research. Both enantiomeric forms, CPD-8996 and CPD-8997, occur in nature. Linalool is involved as an attractant in plant interactions with pollinators, herbivores and parasitoids . It has also been shown to be induced by artificial wounding and by jasmonic acid . The de novo synthesis of linalool has been demonstrated in floral tissues , barks and roots . Junipers, dicots and monocots emit linalool along with other monoterpenoids . Even though fungi typically do not produce monoterpenes, the de novo synthesis of linalool was demonstrate in some fungi, including the wine yeast strain, Saccharomyces cerevisiae M522, in the absence of grape derived precursors .

name: a soyasaponin
annotation: Saponins are a class of secondary metabolites that have been grouped together phenomenologically by the foaming they produce when shaken in aqueous solutions, and structurally by their composition of one or more hydrophilic glycoside moieties combined with a lipophilic triterpene derivative. They are particularly abundant in various plant species, but are also found in marine organisms. Soyasaponins are saponins found in soy beans. The aglycones of these compounds are Soyasapogenols soyasapogenols. Triterpene saponins have diverse biological actions including antiinflammatory, antifungal, antimicrobial, antiparasitic, and antitumor activities.

name: an opine
annotation: Opines Opines are a large family of low molecular weight compounds. The vast majority of opines are secondary amine derivatives resulting from the reduction of the imine formed by condensation of an amino acid with either a keto acid or a sugar. Opines from the CPD-308 family are formed when 2-KETOGLUTARATE serves as the keto substrate in the condensation reaction, while those of the CPD-309 family are formed when PYRUVATE is involved in the condensation reaction. Opines of the CPD-15773 and CPD-26410 families are formed by the condensation of an amino acid with a sugar. A small, separate class of opines is the Agrocinopines agrocinopines, which are sugar-phosphodiesters (e.g., CPD-15774, which is a phosphodiester of sucrose and L-arabinose). While opines are found in marine invertebrates, fungi, higher plants and mammals, they are most commonly found in plant crown gall tumors or hairy root tumors, where they are produced by bacteria of the genera TAX-357 and TAX-379, respectively. However, the first opine to be identified (in 1927), octopine, was isolated from an octopus muscle, hence its name, which was later extended for naming the opine family. Biosynthesis of opines in crown gall tumors is performed by enzymes encoded by genes contained in T-DNA (transfer DNA), which is part of the Ti plasmid (in TAX-357) or Ri plasmid (in TAX-379). The plasmids are inserted by the bacteria into the plant genome, where they are integrated and expressed. Those opines are used by the bacteria as energy, carbon and nitrogen sources. Each strain of TAX-357 and TAX-379 induces and catabolizes a specific set of opines, determined by the specific Ti or Ri plasmid they possess, and Ti-plasmids are classified as nopaline-type, octopine-type, agropine-type, vitopine-type, succinamopine-type

name: a [protein] Nτ-methyl-histidine
annotation: About the nomenclature of histidine: In the past two different systems of numbering the atoms in the imidazole ring of histidine had been used (biochemists numbering the nitrogen atom adjacent to the side chain as 1, while organic chemists designating it as 3), leading to considerable confusion. To avoid this confusion, IUPAC recommends to denote the nitrogen atoms of the imidazole ring of histidine by pros ('near', abbreviated π) and tele ('far', abbreviated τ) to show their position relative to the side chain.

name: a brassinosteroid
annotation: Brassinosteroids Brassinosteroids are steroidal phytohormones that regulate various important physiological and developmental processes, such as cell elongation and division, pollen fertility, photomorphogenesis, differentiation of vascular elements, and stress resistance . Brassinosteroids, which have structural similarity to ECDYSONE, the molting hormone of insects, are C27, C28 and C29 steroids depending on their C-24 alkyl substituents. More than 70 free, naturally occurring brassinosteroids have been identified in plants. The C28 brassinosteroid CPD-697 (BL) is the most biologically active and together with its C28 congeners is distributed widely in the plant kingdom .

name: dermatan-[core protein]
annotation: Dermatan-Sulfate "Dermatan sulfate" is a glycosaminoglycan found mostly in skin but also in blood vessels, the heart valves, tendons, and the lungs. Dermatan sulfate is composed of repeating disaccharide units composed of a hexose sugar and a hexuronic acid, many of which sulfated. The most common disaccharide unit in dermatan sulfate is composed of L-idopyranuronate-(1→3)-CPD-12557 (many of which are sulfated) linked by β(1 -> 3) linkages. This compound describes non-sulfated CPD-12515 residues within the context of dermatan.

name: an [amino-group carrier protein]-C-terminal-L-glutamate
annotation: LysW is an amino-group-carrier protein used by many archaeal species for the synthesis of the amino acid LYS. The amino group of CPD-468, an intermediate in the PWY-3081 "archaeal L-lysine biosynthetic pathway" (which is also found in the hyperthermophilic bacterium TAX-274), is covalently linked to the γ-carboxyl group of the last C-terminal residue of LysW (Glu54 in TAX-274) by a dedicated enzyme encoded by the G-5207 gene. LysW not only protects the amino group of CPD-468, but also serves as a carrier protein, which is easily recognized by lysine biosynthesis enzymes. In this way all subsequent transformations leading to L-lysine occur when the amino acid is attached to LysW, and the CPD-468 side chain is converted to a C-terminal lysyl side chain in the LysW-conjugated form. Eventually, a dedicated enzyme encoded by the G-5222 gene releases the mature amino acid . It has been shown that in some species, such as members of the TAX-2284 genus and TAX-311400 LysW is also used as a carrier protein for GLT during the PWY-7400 "biosynthesis of L-arginine".

name: kojibiose
annotation: CPD-16569 Kojibiose is a disaccharide that was initially characterized from koji extract [an extract produced from rice by the application of TAX-5062 (koji in Japanese) spores, an early step in the production of the Japanese liquor Sho-chu] , and later from hydrol , honey and dextrans . Kojibiose is also found as a constituent of polysaccharides and the sugar chains of glycoproteins . It can be produced from glucose by caramelization . The structure, initially thought to be 3-O-α-D-glucopyranosyl-D-glucose, was shown to be 2-O-α-D-glucopyranosyl-D-glucose a few years later . Crystalization was achieved in 1961 .

name: a phenolphthiodiolenone B
annotation: Phenolphthiodiolenone Phenolphthiodiolenones are intermediates in Phenolphthiocerols phenolphthiocerol biosynthesis. Phenolphthiocerol glycolipids have been found in many Mycobacterium species, including TAX-1769, TAX-1768, TAX-1765, TAX-1781, and TAX-78331 . Two types of phenolphthiodiolenone exist. In Phenolphthiodiolenone-A "phenolphthiodiolenone A" the oxo and the methyl groups are attached at positions 3 and 4, respectively, and the hydroxyl groups are at positions 9 and 11. In Phenolphthiodiolenone-B "phenolphthiodiolenone B" a methyl group is missing from the beginning of the molecule, and thus the oxo and methyl groups are attached at positions 2 and 3, respectively, and the hydroxyl groups are at positions 8 and 10.

name: acetylated alginate
annotation: Alginate is a major component of the cell walls of TAX-2870 (brown algae), and can make up to 20 to 30% of the total algal material. It is a linear polysaccharide of (1-4)-linked CPD-15205, with variable amounts of its C-5 epimer CPD-15204 . Alginate can absorb 200-300 times its own weight in water, forming a viscous gum. Algal alginate is not acetylated. Bacteria that belong to the TAX-286 and TAX-352 genera produce an acetylated form of alginate. The biological function of alginates differs between the two genera. In TAX-286 alginate is associated with biofilms, promoting bacterial adherence to target cell walls . Alginate production is a major contributing factor in chronic pulmonary infections by TAX-287, the causative agent of cystic fibrosis . In TAX-352 alginate is a structural element in the wall of the cyst, a desiccation-resistant capsule that is formed under adverse environmental conditions .

name: an A (Lewis y) histo-blood group epitope
annotation: The Lewis y epitope (Ley) is a difucosylated oligosaccharide derived from B-Gal-14-NacGlc-R "type 2 precursor disaccharides" carried by glycoconjugates on the cell surface. Ley is expressed predominately during embryogenesis, and expression on adult tissue is restricted to granulocytes and epithelial surfaces , where it acts as a ligand for lectin-like proteins . However, Ley is overexpressed in the majority of carcinomas, including breast, ovary, pancreas, prostate, colon, and non-small cell lung cancers, and has been correlated to the pathological staging and prognosis . In these cases, Ley occurs either at the plasma membrane as a glycolipid or linked to surface receptors . Ley was shown to play an important role in promoting cell proliferation, tumorigenecity and adhesion , and several clinical trials were conducted using Ley monoclonal antibodies for targeted immunotherapy of tumors . The human pathogenic bacterium TAX-210 is known to express the Ley antigen in its lipopolysaccharide, mimicking human cell surface glycoconjugates .

name: a histo-blood group antigen precursor disaccharide
annotation: The histo-blood group antigen precursor disaccharides are found at the terminal end of oligosaccharide chains attached to proteins or sphingolipids, and serve as the actual substrates for various glycosyltransferases that terminate the chain elongation process, forming the ABH or Lewis antigens. Five types of precursor disaccharides are known to carry ABH activity - type 1 through type 4 and type 6. Type 1 precursors are widely expressed on endodermally-derived tissues such as lining epithelia and glandular epithelia. Type 2 precursors are found mainly on ecto- or mesodermally-derived tissues, including erythrocytes . Type 3 and 4 precursors are found on ecto- or endodermally-derived tissues, including the salivary glands and kidneys . Since ABH antigens are present not only on erythrocytes, but also on various organs such as the salivary glands and kidneys , they are called histo-blood group ABH antigens. Six types of precursor disaccharides have been described: beta-Gal-13-beta-GlcNac-R: β-D-galactosyl-(1→3)-N-acetyl-β-D-glucosaminyl-R B-Gal-14-NacGlc-R: β-D-galactosyl-(1→4)-N-acetyl-β-D-glucosaminyl-R beta-Gal-13-alpha-GalNac-R: β-D-galactosyl-(1→3)-N-acetyl-α-D-galactosamine-R beta-Gal-13-beta-GalNac-R: β-D-galactosyl-(1→3)-N-acetyl-β-D-galactosamine-R beta-Gal-13-beta-Gal-R: β-D-galactosyl-(1,3)-β-D-galactosyl-R beta-Gal-14-beta-Glc-R: β-D-galactosyl-(1,4)-β-D-glucosyl-R

name: an arabinoside
annotation: Includes both α and β arabinosides.

name: a glycosylinositolphosphoceramide(42:2)-3
annotation: In this class of glycosylinositol phosphoceramides, a long carbon chain is attached at position R1 to form a fatty amide and a long carbon chain is attached at position R2 to form a long chain base. Either or both of these chains may contain one or more double bonds and may have hydroxyl groups attached. Based on the notation GIPC(m:n)-p, each compound within this class has a total of 42 carbons (m), two double bonds (n), and three hydroxyl groups (p) present in the sphingoid base and the fatty amide. However, the length, hydroxylation position, double bond position, and double bond orientation of the groups attached at R2 and R3 will differ between individual members of this group of compounds.

name: a TAG(18:2/36:4)
annotation: This class of triacylglycerols contains three acyl chains formed by the esterification of glycerol with three fatty acids. The names of triacylglycerol compound classes provide information about the three acyl chains of each member of the class, using the nomenclature TAG(a:b/y:z) where "a" represents the number of carbons found in one acyl chain and "b" represents the number of desaturations present in chain "a" and where "y" represents the total number of carbons found in the other two acyl chains and "z" represents the total number of desaturations present in the two chains included in "y." Therefore, in this TAG(18:2/36:4) class of compounds, one acyl chain has 18 carbons and two double bonds. The other two acyl chains have a total of 36 carbons and four double bonds. The attachment sites of the acyl chains at the R1 (sn-1), R2 (sn-2), or R3 (sn-3) position along the glyceryl backbone may vary among class members and cannot be inferred from their order in the compound class name. Additionally, the location and orientation of the double bonds in the unsaturated acyl chain(s) may vary among members of the class and are not specified by this notation.

name: an aromatic ether
annotation: An aromatic ether is any ether in which the oxygen is attached to at least one aryl substituent.

name: a triterpenoid
annotation: Terpenoids Terpenoids make up a very large family of natural products containing more than 50,000 structurally diverse compounds. They are categorized by the number of C5 isoprene units as follows: Hemiterpenes Hemiterpenoids (one C5 unit), Monoterpenes monoterpenoids (C10; two C5 units), Sesquiterpenes sesquiterpenoids (C15; three C5 units), Diterpenes diterpenoids (C20; four C5 units), Sesterterpenes sesterterpenoids (C25; five C5 units), Triterpenes triterpenoids (C30; six C5 units), C35-Terpenes "sesquarterpenoids" (C35; seven C5 units), and Tetraterpenes tetraterpenoids (C40; eight C5 units).

name: a fusidane-type antibiotic
annotation: Fusidane-type antibiotics are a group of fungi-derived 29-nor-protostane triterpenoid antibiotics that are characterized by a Z-configured double bond between C17 and C20, a C21 carboxylic acid, and a 16-OAc in the protostadienol framework. Fusidane-type antibiotics are the only known clinically used antibiotics that target elongation factor G (EF-G) to inhibit bacterial protein synthesis. Owing to their unique mechanism of action, they have little cross-resistance with other commonly used antibiotics .

name: a transition metal cation
annotation: The transition metals are a group of metals that are found in the middle of the periodic table. They are less reactive than the alkaline earth metals, and are harder than the the alkaline earth metals and post transition metals. Most of them have more than one oxidation state.

name: a 17α-hydroxy-C21-steroid
annotation: Steroids are compounds possessing the skeleton of cyclopenta[a]phenanthrene or a skeleton derived therefrom by one or more bond scissions or ring expansions or contractions. Methyl groups are normally present at C-10 and C-13. An alkyl side chain may also be present at C-17.

name: a [TusA]-L-cysteine
annotation: TusA is a sulfur/thiosulfate-carrier protein involved in many different functions, depending on the organism. In the Gram-negative bacterium TAX-562 TusA has a well documented function as a sulfurtransferase in tRNA modification and molybdenum cofactor biosynthesis . In organisms that store sulfur globules such as TAX-1049 TusA functions in sulfur transfter during the globules degradation. It accepts a sulfur atom from a rhodanese-like protein and transfers it to DsrE-Protein . In the acidothermophilic sulfur- and tetrathionate-oxidizing archaeon TAX-1006005 TusA is also involved in thiosulfate transfer, accepting a thiosulfate group from MONOMER-19176 .

name: 3-phenyllactate
annotation: Phenyllactic acid (PLA) exists naturally in honey and fermented products . It has been used as a natural antiseptic agent with broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity .

name: an arabinoxylan
annotation: Arabinoxylan Arabinoxylan molecules contain a chain of β-1,4-linked xylose units, with many of the xylose units substituted with 2-, 3-, or 2 and 3-linked arabinofuranose residues. The ratio of arabinose to xylose residues varies from 0.2 to 0.9 depending on the plant and tissue. The arabinofuranose residues are often decorated further by acetyl, feruloyl and glucuronyl groups. The ferulate residues can form cross links between adjacent arabinoxylan molecules, forming Feraxan. Arabinoxylan Arabinoxylans are found in both the primary and secondary cell walls of plants, including woods and cereal grains, and are a component of Hemicelluloses, the second most abundant renewable polysaccharide after cellulose. Arabinoxylan polymers are the major hemicellulose component of cell walls of the starchy endosperm (flour) and outer layers (bran) of the cereal grain, and have been identified in a variety of the main commercial cereals including wheat, rye, barley, oat, rice, corn and sorghum. They are also common in other plants such as rye grass, bamboo shoots, and pangola grass .

annotation: Heparan-Sulfate "Heparan sulfate" consists of a variably sulfated repeating disaccharide unit which is composed of a hexuronic acid and a hexosamine. The most common repeating unit, which makes up around 50% of the total disaccharide units in heparan sulfate, is composed of CPD-12521 linked to CPD-12541. This compound stand for CPD-12538 sulfated at both the 3' position and the N position (CPD-12545) within the context of Heparan-Sulfate.

name: L-glucose
annotation: L-glucose does not occur naturally in higher organisms, and its production has been only rarely reported in microbes . Several publications claimed that since it cannot be phosphorylated by hexokinase or glucokinase, it cannot be used as an energy source. However, there have been a few reports of bacterial CPD-3607 degradation . Each aldopentose or aldohexose exists as a mixture of at least six compounds: the aldehyde, the hydrated aldehyde (gem-diol), the two pyranoses, and the two furanoses. The open-chain structure shown here is provided only as a schematic illustration of chirality. Consult the appropriate class instances for information on specific ring structures and their anomeric configuration.

name: a kanamycin
annotation: Kanamycins Kanamycins are a group of Aminoglycoside-Antibiotics "aminoglycoside antibiotics" produced by the bacterium TAX-1967. The compounds were discovered in 1957 by researchers in the National Institute of Health of Tokyo, Japan . Due to their wide range of activity, especially against severe staphylococcal and mycobacterial infections, these antibiotics gained almost immediate acceptance, and the most common form, CPD-4821, was produced commercially within a year of its discovery . Kanamycins interact with the 30S subunit of the prokaryotic ribosome, inducing substantial amounts of mistranslation and indirectly inhibiting translocation during protein synthesis, resulting in death . Kanamycins are currently used to treat a wide variety of infections. Kanamycins share several biosynthetic intermediates with other aminoglycosides, including the core moiety CPD-10151 and the pseudodisaccharides CPD-14126, CPD-14125 and CPD-14137. While the main product produced by TAX-1967 is CPD-4821 (R1 = NH2; R2 = OH), additional products are also produced, including CPD-4822 (R1 = R2 = NH2), CPD-4823 (R1 = OH; R2 = NH2) , CPD-14138 (similar to CPD-4821, but contains a glucose moiety instead of the kinosamine moiety) and CPD-4824 (R1 = R2 = OH) .

name: a plant arabinogalactan-[protein]
annotation: Plant arabinogalactan-proteins belong to a large family of wall glycoproteins/proteoglycans, known as the Pro/Hyp-rich glycoproteins. They are found on the plasma membrane, in the wall, in the apoplastic space, and in secretions. The glycan moiety of these glycoproteins constitutes 90% to 98% of the weight. It usually consists of branched type II arabino-3,6-galactans, primarily O-linked to Hyp residues . In general, the glycan moieties of plant arabinogalactan-proteins (AGPs) demonstrate a great structural and compositional variability. The structure shown here is based on a representative Hyp-AG polysaccharide (AHP-1) released by base hydrolysis from a synthetic AGP (Ala-Hyp)51 from tobacco BY2 cells .

name: a carboxylate
annotation: Carboxylic acids, or carboxylates in their state under physiological pH, are the most common type of organic acids. They are carbon oxoacid acids carrying at least one -C(=O)OH group and having the structure RC(=O)OH, where R is any any monovalent functional group.

name: a wax ester
annotation: Natural waxes are biosynthesized by many plants and animals and are typically esters of fatty acids and long chain alcohols. The composition depends not only on species, but also on geographic location of the organism. Common animal wax esters include CPD-7776, a major component of beeswax; CPD-14317, which occurs in the head oil of the sperm whale, and lanolin, obtained from wool. It is estimated that there may be between 8,000 and 20,000 different types of lanolin esters, resulting from combinations between 200 different lanolin acids and 100 different lanolin alcohols that have so far been identified. TAX-3039, a unicellular phytoflagellate, can accumulate a large amount of medium-chain wax esters under anaerobic growth conditions. Plants also produce many types of waxes, often as a way to control evaporation and hydration (see PWY-282).

name: a jasmonate
annotation: The Jasmonates jasmonates are a group of important phytohormones, structurally similar to animal Prostaglandins prostaglandins and ubiquitous in the plant kingdom. Many forms exist, based on the structure of Jasmonic-Acids "Jasmonate" (jasmonic acid, JA). The jasmonates act as regulatory molecules in many developmental processes that include fertility, sex determination, root elongation and fruit ripening. They are also potent signals activating plant defences against pathogens, herbivory, wounding and abiotic stress. The jasmonate regulation system includes the COI1 receptor protein and several JAZ (JAsmonate ZIM domain) repressors, which, in the absence of jasmonate, are bound to the MYC2 transcription factors, preventing transcription. In the presence of active jasmonate molecules, COI1 interacts with the JAZ repressors, causing their PWY-7511 ubiquitylation. The ubiquitylated repressors are targeted for proteasome degradation, releasing the MYC2 transcription factors and activating transcription . Only two of the four stereoisomers of jasmonate occur naturally: CPD-731 and CPD-734 . These two forms are present in equilibrium within cells and can interconvert spontaneously through epimerization . The exact ratio of these stereoisomers within a cell has not been conclusively determined, and it may vary based on species, cell type, and physiological state. But, in the systems tested so far, CPD-734 is found at higher levels than CPD-731 . The biological activity of these stereoisomers can be quite different . In addition, enzymes involved in JA metabolism may have different preferences for the different stereoisomers .

name: a homogalacturonan
annotation: 1-4-alpha-D-galacturonosyl "Homogalacturonan" is a polysaccharide consisting of α-1,4-linked D-GALACTURONATE units. It is found in a highly methyl-esterified form when exported into cell walls. 1-4-alpha-D-galacturonosyl "Homogalacturonan" forms one type of the backbones found in Pectin "pectin".

name: a carotene
annotation: Carotenes are a type of carotenoids, isoprenoid pigments occurring naturally in plants and some microorganisms, that are purely hydrocarbons (with no oxygen).

Chondroitin sulfate is composed of repeating disaccharide units composed of a hexuronic acid and a hexose sugar, specifically CPD-12521 and CPD-12557 linked by β(1 -> 3) linkages. Both units are often sulfated. This compound stand for CPD-12521 (not-sulfated) within the context of Chondroitin-sulfates.

name: a protopanaxadiol-type ginsenoside with a single glucosyl residue at position 20
annotation: Ginsenosides Ginsenosides or panaxosides are a class of steroid glycosides and triterpene saponins found exclusively in the plant genus TAX-4053 (ginseng). They are the principal components responsible for the pharmaceutical activities of ginseng. Based on the structure of their aglycones, ginsenosides have been categorized into three broad types; Protopanaxadiol-type-ginsenosides "protopanaxadiol-type ginsenosides" (PPD, containing hydroxyl groups at positions 3 and 20), Protopanaxatriol-type-ginsenosides "protopanaxatriol-type ginsenosides" (PPT, containing hydroxyl groups at positions 3, 6 and 20), and Oleanonic-acid-type-ginsenosides "oleanonic-acid-type saponins".

name: an amino group acceptor
annotation: Amine-Donors "An amino group donor" is a compound that can donate an amino group to another compound in a reaction catalyzed by enzymes that belong to the EC-2.6.1 transaminase group" (often called aminotransferases). Prior to donating the amino group the compound is Aminated-Amine-Donors and after donating it the compound is Deaminated-Amine-Donors. Since tranmination reactions are reversible, the distinction between an amino group donor and an amino group acceptor is not determined by the actual chemical entity, but by the role it plays in a particular reaction. However, some compounds, such as GLT and L-ALPHA-ALANINE, are often used as amino group donors by the aminotransferases in reactions that are physiologically unidirectional. This particular entry is used when the identity of the amino group donor (or acceptor) is not known or cannot be defined.

name: a type 3 histo-blood group antigen precursor disaccharide
annotation: The histo-blood group antigen precursor disaccharides are found at the terminal end of oligosaccharide chains attached to proteins or sphingolipids, and serve as the actual substrates for various glycosyltransferases that terminate the chain elongation process, forming the ABH or Lewis antigens. Five types of precursor disaccharides are known to carry ABH activity - type 1 through type 4 and type 6. Type 1 precursors are widely expressed on endodermally-derived tissues such as lining epithelia and glandular epithelia. Type 2 precursors are found mainly on ecto- or mesodermally-derived tissues, including erythrocytes . Type 3 and 4 precursors are found on ecto- or endodermally-derived tissues, including the salivary glands and kidneys . Since ABH antigens are present not only on erythrocytes, but also on various organs such as the salivary glands and kidneys , they are called histo-blood group ABH antigens. Note the the type 3 histo-blood group antigen precursor disaccharide corresponds to the Core1 "O-glycan core 1 " structure. Six types of precursor disaccharides have been described: beta-Gal-13-beta-GlcNac-R: β-D-galactosyl-(1→3)-N-acetyl-β-D-glucosaminyl-R B-Gal-14-NacGlc-R: β-D-galactosyl-(1→4)-N-acetyl-β-D-glucosaminyl-R beta-Gal-13-alpha-GalNac-R: β-D-galactosyl-(1→3)-N-acetyl-α-D-galactosaminyl-R beta-Gal-13-beta-GalNac-R: β-D-galactosyl-(1→3)-N-acetyl-β-D-galactosaminyl-R beta-Gal-13-beta-Gal-R: β-D-galactosyl-(1,3)-β-D-galactosyl-R beta-Gal-14-beta-Glc-R: β-D-galactosyl-(1,4)-β-D-glucosyl-R

name: a thiopurine
annotation: The thiopurine drugs CPD-20732, CPD-15916 and CPD-5721 are purine antimetabolites that are widely used in the treatment of acute lymphoblastic leukemia, autoimmune disorders (e.g., Crohn's disease, rheumatoid arthritis), and organ transplant recipients. The drugs are eventually inactivated by the action of EC-2.1.1.67 (TPMT), which catalyzes their S-methylation.

name: a hydroxamate siderophore
annotation: Siderophores are small, high-affinity iron chelating compounds usually produced and secreted by microorganisms. Once outside the cell, siderophores bind FE+3 with high affinity, and the iron-siderophore complexes are imported back into the cell by active-transport mechanisms. Siderophores are very common in pathogenic bacteria, since iron in the host organism is usually tightly bound to proteins, resulting in an extremely low concentration of free iron in the blood. Most siderophores belong to one of three major groups: the Catecholate-siderophores "catechols", Hydroxamate-siderophores "hydroxamates" and Thiazoline-siderophores "thiazolines". Some grasses are known to make siderophores as well. These Phytosiderophores "phytosiderophores" are also known as mugineic acids. Hydroxamate siderophores are named based on the presence of a Hydroxamates hydroxamate element in their structures.

name: mycosamine
annotation: CPD-16768 Mycosamine (3-amino, 3-6 dideoxy D-mannose) is the amino sugar present in polyene macrolide antibiotics, represented by Candicidins, CPD-17211, CPD-17212 and CPD-17213. The amino sugar component mycosamine plays a critical role in conferring the antifungal properties exhibited by polyene antibiotics . Mycosamine is synthesized in a CPD-17206 "GDP-activated form" from the primary metabolite GDP-MANNOSE.

name: a galactoglucomannan
annotation: Glucomannans Glucomannans (GM) and Galactoglucomannans galactoglucomannans (GGM) are the major hemicellulosic components of the secondary cell walls of softwoods, but occur only in minor amounts in the secondary cell walls of hardwoods. Both polymers consist of a bakbone composed of β(1→4)-linked glucopyranose and mannopyranose residues. GGM chains are substituted by galactopyranose residues and are often branched. Both polymers show some variations in structural features depending on the plant species and stage of plant development . The mannan backbone of GGM from spruce wood consists of galactose, glucose, and mannose in the mole ratio 1 : 8 : 33, and contains segments of more than four mannopyranose residues, interrupted with a segment composed of both mannopyranose and glucopyranose, or two glucopyranose residues. Branches terminated by single galactose residues are attached at positions 2, 3, and 6 of mannose residues and at positions 3 and 6 of glucose residues of the backbone. Many of the mannose residues are acetylated at positions 2 and 3 . The extent of GGM galactosylation governs its association tendency to the cellulose microfibrils .

name: an arabinogalactan
annotation: Arabinogalactan Arabinogalactans are biopolymers consisting of arabinose and galactose residues. Two main classes of arabinogalactans are found in nature: Plant-Arabinogalactans "plant arabinogalactan" and Mycobacterial-Arabinogalactans "microbial arabinogalactan". Plant-Arabinogalactans "Plant arabinogalactan" is a major constituent of many gums, and a major component of the Hemicelluloses of some plants. Mycobacterial-Arabinogalactans "Microbial arabinogalactan" is a major structural component of the mycobacterial cell wall.

name: a ganglioside GD1a
annotation: A ganglioside is a molecule composed of Glycosphingolipids (Ceramides and Oligosaccharides) with one or more sialic acids (e.g. N-ACETYLNEURAMINATE) linked on the sugar chain. Ganglioside are components of the cell plasma membrane that modulates cell signal transduction events, and appear to concentrate in lipid rafts. The name ganglioside is derived from ganglion cells of the brain, from which the first gangliosides were isolated by the German scientist Ernst Klenk in 1942. More than 60 gangliosides are known, which differ from each other mainly in the position and number of N-ACETYLNEURAMINATE residues. The same four structures- CPD-7188 "ganglioside GM1, Ganglioside-GD1a "ganglioside GD1a", Ganglioside-GD1b "ganglioside GD1b" and Ganglioside-GT1b "ganglioside GT1b", comprise the great majority of gangliosides in mammalian brains .
