name: a jasmonoyl-1-aminocyclopropane-1 carboxylate
aliases: a jasmonyl-1-aminocyclopropane-1 carboxylic acid|a JA-ACC|a jasmonate-ACC|a jasmonyl-1-aminocyclopropanecarboxylate|a jasmonyl-1-aminocyclopropane-1 carboxylate
annotation: Jasmonyl-1-aminocyclopropane-1 carboxylic acid (JA-ACC) has been detected in Arabidopsis thaliana . The biological significance of this compound remains unknown. High levels of JA-ACC can affect root elongation in Arabidopsis thaliana . This appears to depend on a functional ethylene signaling pathway, but it does not require the COI1 JA-signaling component . Treatment of the Japanese weed, Polygonum longisetum, with exogenous JA-ACC, increases sesquiterpene production in the leaves .

name: a sialyl-Tn antigen
annotation: O-Linked glycans are polysaccharide or oligosaccharide that are linked to a protein via the oxygen atom in the side chain of a SER or THR residue. In the structure below, R is hydrogen when the modified amino acid is SER and carbon when the modified amino acid is THR. The sialyl-Tn antigen is a well known cancer-associated antigen, the expression of which is related to the prognosis of cancer patients.

name: a rhodoquinone
annotation: Rhodoquinone (RQ) is a modified ubiquinone, where the methoxyl group in position 3 is replaced by an amino group. The compound was originally isolated from TAX-1085 , where is it used for the reduction of fumarate to succinate under anaerobic conditions. Rhodoquinone is not limited to prokaryotes. It was soon discovered that some eukaryotic parasites such as the nematodes TAX-6253 and TAX-6252 , as well as parasitic helminths also contain rhodoquinone, which is essential for fumarate reduction under anaerobic conditions . Later it was shown that rhodoquinone is present in all eukaryotes that reduce fumarate during anoxia, including freshwater snails, mussels, lugworms, and oysters . More recently, rhodoquinone has been detected in mitochondria purified from certain mouse and human tissues and it has been suggested that RQ is produced by all mammals . .

name: α-D-galacturonate-[rhamnogalacturonan I oligosaccharide]
annotation: Rhamnogalacturonans-I "Rhamnogalacturonan I " is a polysaccharide in the Pectin pectin family, consisting of repeating α-(1,2)-L-rhamnose-α-(1,4)-D-GALACTURONATE disaccharide units. About half of the Rha residues are substituted with side chains of various neutral oligosaccharides such as arabinans, galactans, and arabinogalactans. This compound describes a CPD-12523 residue at the non-reducing end of Rhamnogalacturonans-I "rhamnogalacturonan I".

name: a B (Lewis y) histo-blood group epitope
annotation: The Lewis y epitope (Ley) is a difucosylated oligosaccharide derived from B-Gal-14-NacGlc-R "type 2 precursor disaccharides" carried by glycoconjugates on the cell surface. Ley is expressed predominately during embryogenesis, and expression on adult tissue is restricted to granulocytes and epithelial surfaces , where it acts as a ligand for lectin-like proteins . However, Ley is overexpressed in the majority of carcinomas, including breast, ovary, pancreas, prostate, colon, and non-small cell lung cancers, and has been correlated to the pathological staging and prognosis . In these cases, Ley occurs either at the plasma membrane as a glycolipid or linked to surface receptors . Ley was shown to play an important role in promoting cell proliferation, tumorigenecity and adhesion , and several clinical trials were conducted using Ley monoclonal antibodies for targeted immunotherapy of tumors . The human pathogenic bacterium TAX-210 is known to express the Ley antigen in its lipopolysaccharide, mimicking human cell surface glycoconjugates .

name: a microcin
annotation: Microcins are a class of gene-encoded low-molecular-mass antibacterial peptides secreted by bacteria. Microcins produced by commensal TAX-562 strains target and eliminate enteric pathogens such as TAX-28901 by mimicking the siderophores the pathogens use for iron scavenging.

name: an acyl sugar
annotation: Acylsugars are specialized carbohydrate metabolites with a glucose- or sucrose backbone esterified with mono- or oligo aliphatic acyl groups.

name: a 24-epicathasterone sulfate
annotation: Sulfated brassinosteroids can be produced in vitro, but the single sulfate group present on these compounds may be added at one of two different positions , represented by "R" groups on this compound. Brassinosteroid sulfates have not been detected in vivo in plants to date, but, there is some indirect evidence for their existence .

name: a (4E)-4-sphinga-4,8-dienine ceramide
annotation: Ceramides are found in high concentrations within the cell membrane and are one of the component lipids that make up Sphingomyelins sphingomyelin, one of the major lipids in the lipid bilayer. This type of ceramide, containing an additional double bond at position 8 of the sphingoid base, is a common modification in plants and fungi . In plants, there is commonly a mixture of ceramides with the double bond at position 8 in the LCB in both cis and trans configurations, whereas the double bond at the 4 position is generally in the trans configuration .

name: a diol
annotation: Diols are compound that contain two hydroxy groups. Aliphatic diols in which the two hydroxyls reside on different carbons are also called glycols glycols.

name: a dimycocerosyl phenolphthiocerol
annotation: CPD-18578 Phenol-phthiocerol glycolipids have been found in TAX-1769, TAX-1768, TAX-1765, TAX-1781, and TAX-78331 . In the latter the phenol-phthiocerol is attached to a trisaccharide (2,3,4-tri-O-methyl L-fucopyranosy-(α1→3)-L-rhamnopyranosyl-(α1→3)-2-O-methyl L-rhamnopyranose) and the two secondary alcohol functions are esterified by Mycocerosic-Acids "mycocerosic acids" .

name: a secondary alcohol
annotation: A secondary alcohol is a compound in which a hydroxy group, -OH, is attached to a saturated carbon atom that has two other carbon atoms attached to it.

name: a [heparan-sulfate]-2-O-sulfo-β-D-glucuronate
annotation: Heparan-Sulfate "Heparan sulfate" consists of a variably sulfated repeating disaccharide unit which is composed of a hexuronic acid and a hexosamine. The most common repeating unit, which makes up around 50% of the total disaccharide units in heparan sulfate, is composed of CPD-12521 linked to CPD-12541. This compound stand for CPD-12527 within the context of Heparan-Sulfate.

name: a 2-hydroxyornithine lipid
annotation: An ornithine lipid is comprised of LYSO-ORNITHINE-LIPIDS with a second fatty acyl group ester-linked to the 3-hydroxy position of the first fatty acyl group (reviewed in ). The composition of the amide-linked (R1) and ester-linked (R2) fatty acids in ornithine lipids varies among bacterial species . In addition, ornithine lipids are often modified by hydroxylation and methylation at different positions .

name: a catecholate siderophore
annotation: Siderophores are small, high-affinity iron chelating compounds usually produced and secreted by microorganisms. Once outside the cell, siderophores bind FE+3 with high affinity, and the iron-siderophore complexes are imported back into the cell by active-transport mechanisms. Siderophores are very common in pathogenic bacteria, since iron in the host organism is usually tightly bound to proteins, resulting in an extremely low concentration of free iron in the blood. Most siderophores belong to one of three major groups: the Catecholate-siderophores "catechols", Hydroxamate-siderophores "hydroxamates" and Thiazoline-siderophores "thiazolines". Some grasses are known to make siderophores as well. These Phytosiderophores "phytosiderophores" are also known as mugineic acids. Catecholate siderophores are named based on a CATECHOL element present in their structure (catecholate is the name of the anion form that is the conjugate base of catechol).

name: a nonionic detergent
annotation: Nonionic or Neutral detergents don't have any ionic groups. They are used in dishwashing liquids.

name: a methylphenol
annotation: A cresol, or methylphenol, is a benzene ring carrying a single hydroxy substituent and a single methyl substituent.

name: an angucyclin
annotation: The angucyclines are a group of aromatic polyketide natural products from microbial sources of which the first member was discovered in 1966. The classification of the angucyclines is linked to the tetracyclic benz[a]anthracene system. The angucyclines display anticancer activity as well as a multitude of other activities such as antibacterial, antiviral, and enzyme inhibitory properties. Most of them are glycoconjugates containing one or more sugars attached at different positions of the angucyclinone framework .

name: a hydroxyphthioceranate
annotation: A unique feature of mycobacterial cell wall lipids is the presence of very long chain fatty acids with multiple methyl branches toward the carboxyl end of the carbon chain. Many types of such acids exist, including Mycosanoic-Acids "mycosanoic acids", Mycolipanoic-Acids "mycolipanoic acids", Mycolipanolic-Acids "mycolipanolic acids", Mycolipenic-Acids "mycolipenic acids", Mycolipodienoic-Acids "mycolipodienoic acids", Phthioceranic-Acids "phthioceranic acids", and Mycocerosic-Acids "mycocerosic acids". Hydroxyphthioceranic acids are hepta and octa-methylated alkanoic very-long chain fatty acids with a hydroxyl group on the carbon next to the last methylated carbon. They are the major acyl group found in CPD-18600, the major sulfolipid of TAX-1773 . In heptamethylated hydroxyphthioceranic acids R1=OH, R2,R3 = H. In octamethylated hydroxyphthioceranic acids, R1= H, R2=CH3, and R3=OH.

name: a sterol 3-glucoside
annotation: In this group of compounds, one glucose residue is connected to a sterol backbone via a glycosidic bond to the brassicasteryl C3 carbon. The sub-class is determined by the aryl chain, denotated by the R1 group, on the sterol backbone.

name: polyvinyl alcohol(n)
annotation: POLYVINYL-ALCOHOL "Polyvinyl alcohol" (PVA) is a water-soluble synthetic polymer that is used in paper and textile mills and in copolymers as a biodegradable segment.

name: a neomycin
annotation: Neomycins are a family of broad-spectrum highly toxic antibiotics produced by TAX-1906. They were discovered in 1949 by Waksman and Lechevalier (Waksman later received the 1951 Nobel Prize in Physiology and medicine) . Neomycin usually exists as a complex composed of two stereoisomers, CPD-14142 and CPD-14141, which are the two active components. A third component, CPD-14137 "neomycin A", which is a degradation product, is present in small quantities. Neomycin was originally used as a treatment for tuberculosis, but serious side effects soon became apparent, including ototoxicity , which sometimes developed to complete loss of hearing, and nephrotoxicity. Thus systemic use of neomycin is limited, and the most common mode of use is via topical application .

name: a vitamin B6
annotation: Vitamins-B6 Vitamin B6 is a group term for PYRIDOXAL (PL), PYRIDOXINE (PN), PYRIDOXAMINE (PM) and their 5'-phosphorylated derivatives PYRIDOXAL_PHOSPHATE (PLP), PYRIDOXINE-5P (PNP) and PYRIDOXAMINE-5P (PMP), which are also referred to as vitamers. Pyridoxal 5'-phosphate (PLP) is the biochemically active form, and is an essential cofactor in all living systems . It plays an important role in amino acid and carbohydrate metabolism and has been implicated in singlet oxygen resistance . Most bacteria, archaea, fungi, and plants synthesize PLP in a single reaction, as described in PWY-6466, although some bacteria, such as E. coli, use a longer, more complex pathway (PYRIDOXSYN-PWY) . Animals do not synthesize this compound, making it an essential nutrient in their diet. PLP is an essential cofactor of numerous metabolic enzymes, predominantly in amino acid metabolism. It is one of the most versatile cofactors and participates in transamination, decarboxylation, racemization, Cα-Cβ cleavage and α-β elimination reactions. In humans this vitamin has a role in numerous functions ranging from modulation of hormone function to potent antioxidant activity.

name: a glycosylinositolphosphoceramide(44:0)-3
annotation: In this class of glycosylinositol phosphoceramides, a long carbon chain is attached at position R1 to form a fatty amide and a long carbon chain is attached at position R2 to form a long chain base. Either or both of these chains may contain one or more double bonds and may have hydroxyl groups attached. Based on the notation GIPC(m:n)-p, each compound within this class has a total of 44 carbons (m), zero double bonds (n), and three hydroxyl groups (p) present in the sphingoid base and the fatty amide. However, the length, hydroxylation position, double bond position, and double bond orientation of the groups attached at R2 and R3 will differ between individual members of this group of compounds.

name: a cyclohexanone
annotation: Cyclohexanones are any alicyclic ketone based on a cyclohexane skeleton, including substituted derivatives thereof.

name: a glucuronan
annotation: Glucuronan is a polysaccharide composed of CPD-12521 residues connected by 1→4 linkages. The glucuronan produced by the bacterium TAX-382 is variably O-acetylated at C-3 and/or C-2 positions depending on the Mg2+ concentration in the culture medium.

name: a monoamine
annotation: A monoamine is a compound that has only a single amine group.

name: maackiain
annotation: Maackiain Maackiain is a common pterocarpan that has been detected in many legumes. Within the two subtribes Diocleinae and Erythrininae it was found in 12 species representing 4 genera . Maackiain was originally discovered in the TAX-37501 tree, which is named after its discoverer, the 19th-century Siberian explorer Karlovich Maack. Maackiain can be found in both a (-) and a (+) conformations. In chickpea, where it is a major pterocarpan phytoalexin together with medicarpin, it occurs exclusively in the (-) conformation .

name: an a-series ganglioside
annotation: |About the nomenclature of gangliosides Ganglioside naming is somewhat complex. Two ganglioside nomenclature systems are currently in use. The most commonly used is a short-hand nomenclature suggested by Svennerholm, which was initially based on the migration order of Ganglio-Series-GSLs "ganglio-series" gangliosides in chromatography . The more comprehensive system formulated by IUPAC is applied much less frequently. A typical ganglioside name using the old system looks like Ganglioside-GQ1b "ganglioside GQ1b". The first letter refers to the glycosphingolipid series. For example, the "G" stands for the Ganglio-Series-GSLs "ganglio series". The next letter describes the number of sialic acid residues: (A = 0, M =1, D = 2, T = 3, Q = 4, P = 5, H = 6, S = 7). The following number describes the number of the non-sialic residues, and is derived in a rather complex manner. It was initially assumed that the number of such residues does not exceed 5. The number in the name was calculated as (5 - the number of uncharged carbohydrates). In addition, several sub-series of gangliosides have been defined based on the number of sialic acids attached to the galactose residue of the lacosylceramide core. If no sialic residues are present at that position, the compounds belong to the 0-Series-Ganglioside "0-series". Those with one sialic acid at that position are named the A-Series-Gangliosides "a-series", those with two sialic acids belong to the B-Series-Gangliosides "b-series", and so on. C-Series-Gangliosides "C-series" or higher gangliosides are rare in adult mammals and birds, but are abundant in fish and amphibians. Confusingly, GM1b and GD1c are both 0-series gangliosides, despite having a "b" and "c" in their names. So, for example, the Ganglioside-GQ1b "ganglioside GQ1b" is n

name: a (22R,23R)-28-homocastasterone sulfate
annotation: Sulfated brassinosteroids can be produced in vitro, but the single sulfate group present on these compounds may be added at one of several different positions , represented by "R" groups on this compound. Brassinosteroid sulfates have not been detected in vivo in plants, but, there is some indirect evidence for their existence .

name: 3,6-anhydro-L-galactose
annotation: 3,6-Anhydro-L-galactose is a sugar component of the glycans found in red macroalgae, such as Agaroses agarose and Porphyrans porphyran.

name: a cytokinin
annotation: Cytokinins Cytokinins are a class of plant hormones that promote cell division, or cytokinesis, in plant roots and shoots. While their main role is to control cell growth and differentiation, they also affect apical dominance, axillary bud growth, and leaf senescence. The cytokinins are structurally diverse, and have been divided into two main types: Adenine-Cytokinins "adenine-type cytokinins" and Phenylurea-Cytokinins "phenylurea-type cytokinins". All the natural cytokinins fall into the first type - phenylurea-type cytokinins are synthetic. The adenine cytokinins are classified further by the configuration of their N6-side chain as isoprenoid or aromatic cytokinins. The most prevalent cytokinins are by far those with an unsaturated isoprenoid side chain, and in particular those with a trans-hydroxylated N6-side chain, such as CPD-4210 and its derivatives.

name: an angiotensin
annotation: Angiotensin is an oligopeptide hormone that causes vasoconstriction and a subsequent increase in blood pressure. It is part of the renin-angiotensin system, which is a major target for drugs that lower blood pressure. CPD-13004 Angiotensin-1 is formed by cleavage of Angiotensinogens angiotensinogen by EC-3.4.23.15, which cleaves a peptide bond between the leucine (Leu) and valine (Val) residues. The other angiotensins are formed by further cleavage of CPD-13004

name: a pseudomonate
annotation: Pseudomonic acids are polyketide antibiotics produced by ORG-6360 . The antibiotic mupirocin is a mixture of four pseudomonic acids: CPD0-2581 (about 90%), CPD-21448 (about 8%), CPD-18977 (Ile]"| and ultimately protein synthesis. The main ingredient of mupirocin, CPD0-2581, consists of a C17 polyketide-derived substructure (CPD-21453) esterified by CPD-21101. For the biosynthesis of pseudomonic acids, see PWY-8012.

name: a phytoestrogen
annotation: Estrogens are hormones that stimulate or control the development and maintenance of female sex characteristics in mammals by binding to estrogen receptors. They are important in both the menstrual and the estrous reproductive cycles. Estrogens are synthesized in all vertebrates as well as some insects, suggesting that estrogenic sex hormones have an ancient evolutionary history. The estrogens that occur naturally in the body are steroids, however other types of molecules acquired in the diet are known to bind to the estrogen receptors and are thus also considered estrogens. They include molecules produced by plants (phytoestrogens), molecules produced by fungi (mycoestrogens), and synthetic compounds (xenoestrogens). Phytoestrogens are a diverse group of naturally occurring nonsteroidal plant compounds that, because of its structural similarity with CPD-352, have the ability to cause estrogenic and/or antiestrogenic effects. Most phytoestrogens are mainly substituted natural phenolic compounds such as coumestans coumestans, PRENYLFLAVANONES prenylflavonoids, and Isoflavones isoflavones. The best-researched are Isoflavones isoflavones, which are commonly found in soy and red clover.

name: fucose
annotation: Fucose is a hexose deoxy sugar. It received its name from the brown algae genus TAX-3011, from which it was first decribed. The alga was named because of its dark redish color, as one of the meanings of the Latin word fucus is "red paint". Algae of the TAX-3009 order produce fucose-rich polysaccahrides known as Fucoidans fucoidans. However, fucose is a common sugar, found in N-linked glycans on the mammalian, insect and plant cell surface, in many bacterial glycans, and in other biomolecules. Most of the fucose found in biology is in the L-fucoses "L conformation", and the use of D-Fucoses is rather rare (for example in some Cardenolide-Glycosides "cardenolide glycosides").

name: a dioxindole-3-acetyl-3-O-hexose
annotation: This compound is present in the flavedo (epicarp) of oranges (TAX-2711) fed with radiolabeled INDOLE_ACETATE_AUXIN, DIOXINDOLE-3-ACETATE or 2-OXINDOLE-3-ACETIC-ACID . There is strong evidence that the hexose present in orange is glucose (see: CPD-9642) but, galactose, mannose, and lactose cannot be conclusively excluded .

name: a chondroitin sulfate
annotation: Chondroitin-sulfates "Chondroitin sulfate" is a sulfated Glycosaminoglycans "glycosaminoglycan" composed of alternating units of CPD-12557(β1,3)CPD-12521 linked to each other by a β(1,4) linkage. It is usually found attached to proteins as part of a proteoglycan. Chondroitin sulfate is a major component of connective tissue matrix (such as skin and cartilage), but is also found on cell surface and basement membranes and in intracellular granules of certain cells. Functions in matrix locations are mainly structural, while functions in membranes are mainly as receptors. The saccharide units can be sulfated in variable positions and quantities - CPD-12557 can be sulfated at positions 4 and 6 to yield CPD-12516, CPD-12565 or CPD-12526, while CPD-12521 can be sulfated at position 2 to form CPD-12527 (the sulfate group is indicated as R3 in the diagram below). The following names have been used to define different sulfation states: Chondroitin-Sulfate-A "Chondroitin sulfate A" contains CPD-12521 and CPD-12516 Chondroitin-Sulfate-C "Chondroitin sulfate C" contains CPD-12521 and CPD-12565 Chondroitin-Sulfate-D "Chondroitin sulfate D" contains CPD-12527 and CPD-12565 Chondroitin-Sulfate-E "Chondroitin sulfate E" contains CPD-12521 and CPD-12526 The term "chondroitin sulfate B" has been used in the past to refer to Dermatan-Sulfate, but is not used any more.

name: a luciferin
annotation: Luciferin (from the Latin lucifer, "light-bringer") is a generic term for the substrates of the luciferase enzymes, which are found in organisms that generate bioluminescence. Luciferins typically undergo an enzyme-catalyzed oxidation and the resulting excited state intermediate emits light upon decaying to its ground state.

name: a macrolide
annotation: A macrolide is a macrocyclic lactone with a ring of twelve or more members.

name: a (1->3)-β-D-xylan
annotation: 13-BETA-D-XYLANS "β-1,3-D-xylan" is a polysaccharide composed of β-1,3-linked BETA-D-XYLOSE residues. This polysaccharide was first found in the siphonous green seaweed TAX-148944 as a water-insoluble and alkaline-soluble component of cell walls . It was subsequently found in cell walls of some red (TAX-2784 and TAX-31346) and green (TAX-76312, TAX-3128 and TAX-170436) algae . It has not been detected in cell walls of land plants, which contain 1-4-beta-Xylan instead. Analysis of 13-BETA-D-XYLANS "β-1,3-D-xylan" from the siphonous green alga TAX-177073 showed that it is a linear heteropolysaccharide also containing glucose residues linked by β(1,3) linkages as well as some xylose residues linked by β(1,4) linkages. Both of these are thought to be located within the β-1,3-xylose chain and linked via covalent bonds .

name: a TAG(18:3/38:1)
annotation: This class of triacylglycerols contains three acyl chains formed by the esterification of glycerol with three fatty acids. The names of triacylglycerol compound classes provide information about the three acyl chains of each member of the class, using the nomenclature TAG(a:b/y:z) where "a" represents the number of carbons found in one acyl chain and "b" represents the number of desaturations present in chain "a" and where "y" represents the total number of carbons found in the other two acyl chains and "z" represents the total number of desaturations present in the two chains included in "y." Therefore, in this TAG(18:3/38:1) class of compounds, one acyl chain has 18 carbons and three double bonds. The other two acyl chains have a total of 38 carbons and one double bond. The attachment sites of the acyl chains at the R1 (sn-1), R2 (sn-2), or R3 (sn-3) position along the glyceryl backbone may vary among class members and cannot be inferred from their order in the compound class name. Additionally, the location and orientation of the double bonds in the unsaturated acyl chain(s) may vary among members of the class and are not specified by this notation.

name: heparan sulfate containing a glucuronate--N,3-O-disulfo-D-glucosamine disaccharide
annotation: Heparan sulfate is a member of the Glycosaminoglycans "glycosaminoglycan" family. It consists of a variably sulfated repeating disaccharide unit which is composed of a hexuronic acid and a hexosamine. The most common repeating unit, which makes up around 50% of the total disaccharide units in heparan sulfate, is composed of CPD-12521 linked to CPD-12541. This alternative unit, which is a substrate for heparanase, consists of CPD-12521 linked to N3-O-disulfo-D-glucosamine.

annotation: This compound class collects various aliphatic ω-aminoaldehydes.

name: an actinomycin
annotation: The ACTINOMYCIN actinomycins are a family of bicyclic chromopeptide lactones with strong antineoplastic activity that are produced by various streptomycete strains . The first actinomycin was isolated from TAX-1890 in 1940, and was the first antibiotic obtained in pure form from an actinomycete . ACTINOMYCIN-D "Actinomycin D" was the first antibiotic shown to have anti-cancer activity , and was approved as an anticancer drug in 1964, making it one of the oldest anticancer drugs. Actinomycin D inhibits transcription by binding DNA at the transcription initiation complex and preventing elongation of RNA chain by RNA polymerase . A common feature of all actinomycins is the chromophoric moiety CPD-18447, which is a unique phenoxazinone dicarboxylic acid to which two pentapeptide lactone rings are attached with amide linkages .

name: a 6-phosphogluco-amylopectin
annotation: This compound class represents the CPD-7043 component of native starch grains that are phosphorylated on the C6 hydroxyl group of one or more glucosyl residue in one or more of the α-glucan chains. This modification is likely to produce a transition state for starch where the CPD-7043 glucans are less ordered but still insoluble . Starch is a combination of two polymeric carbohydrates (polysaccharides) called Alpha-Amyloses and CPD-7043. Alpha-Amyloses contains up to several thousand α-glucosyl units linked almost exclusively in linear α(1->4) linkages, with very few branches of α(1->6) linkages. In CPD-7043, on the other hand, branching is common, and occurs approximately every 24-30 glucose monomer units. Alpha-Amyloses α-amylose accounts for 30% of starch, while CPD-7043 accounts for the other 70%.

name: a tertiary amine
annotation: Tertiary amines are derivatives of ammonia where all three hydrogen atoms in ammonia are replaced by alkyls.

annotation: A primary amide is Amides in whose molecule the nitrogen atom is bonded to only one carbon atom.

name: a retinol
annotation: Retinol is one of the animal forms of vitamin A. It is convertible to other forms of vitamin A, and the Retinyl-Esters "retinyl ester" derivative of the alcohol serves as the storage form of the vitamin in animals. Retinol is produced in the body from the hydrolysis of Retinyl-Esters "retinyl esters", and from the reduction of RETINAL.

name: a medium-chain primary alcohol
annotation: A primary alcohol is a compound in which a hydroxy group, -OH, is attached to a saturated carbon atom that is connected to no more than one other carbon atom. Primary alcohols are also classified based on their length. Short-Chain-Primary-Alcohols "Short-chain primary alcohols" have alkyl chains with fewer than six carbons. Medium-Chain-Primary-Alcohols "Medium-chain primary alcohols" have alkyl chains of 6-12 carbons. Long-Chain-Primary-Alcohols "Long-chain primary alcohols" (also known as fatty alcohols) have alkyl chains of 13-22 carbons. Very-Long-Chain-Primary-Alcohols "Very long-chain primary alcohols" have alkyl chains of 23 carbons or longer.

name: a terpenoid
annotation: Terpenoids Terpenoids make up a very large family of natural products, containing more than 50,000 structurally diverse compounds, that are derived from five-carbon CPD-9436 units. They are categorized by the number of C5 isoprene units as follows: Hemiterpenes Hemiterpenoids (one C5 unit), Monoterpenes monoterpenoids (C10; two C5 units), Sesquiterpenes sesquiterpenoids (C15; three C5 units), Diterpenes diterpenoids (C20; four C5 units), Sesterterpenes sesterterpenoids (C25; five C5 units), Triterpenes triterpenoids (C30; six C5 units), C35-Terpenes "sesquarterpenoids" (C35; seven C5 units), and Tetraterpenes tetraterpenoids (C40; eight C5 units). Larger compounds are referred to as polyterpenoids.

name: a ketoxime
annotation: Oximes "An oxime" is a class of chemical compounds with the general formula (R1)(R2)HC=NOH. Ketoximes A ketoxime is an oxime where both R1 and R2 are organic groups other than a hydrogen, and is usually generated by the reaction of HYDROXYLAMINE and LONG-CHAIN-KETONE ketones. The term oxime is derived from a condensation of the words oxygen and Imides imide.

name: an isoflavone
annotation: Isoflavones comprise a class of organic compounds, often naturally occurring. They include any Isoflavonoids isoflavonoid with a 3-aryl-1-benzopyran-4-one (3-aryl-4H-chromen-4-one) skeleton and its substituted derivatives. Isoflavones are produced via a branch of the general PWY-361 that produces Flavonoids flavonoid compounds in higher plants, but differ from Flavones flavones in the location of the aryl group. Most members of the Fabaceae family contain significant quantities of isoflavones.

name: a γ-butenolide
annotation: Butenolides are γ-lactones that consist of a CPD-27325 skeleton and its substituted derivatives.

name: a steroid
annotation: Steroids are compounds possessing the skeleton of cyclopenta[a]phenanthrene or a skeleton derived therefrom by one or more bond scissions or ring expansions or contractions. Methyl groups are normally present at C-10 and C-13. An alkyl side chain may also be present at C-17. Steroids are abundant in the environment. Large amounts of steroids and their derivatives are synthesized by eukaryotes, and these molecules play very important and diverse roles and include hormones, detergents that facilitate the absorption of lipids by the intestine, and membrane constituents. Some of them, CHOLESTEROL and related compounds, are ubiquitous as membrane constituent of eukaryotes (for instance, up to 10% of the dry mass of yeast is ERGOSTEROL) and as the precursors of all steroid hormones, vitamin D, and the bile acids. Interestingly, with exception of methane-oxidizing bacteria that contain relatively large amounts of sterols, sterols are absent from most prokaryotes. However, many bacterial membranes contain Hopanoids hopanoids, which are similar pentacyclic sterol-like molecules .

name: a glycosylinositolphosphoceramide(40:0)-2
annotation: In this class of glycosylinositol phosphoceramides, a long carbon chain is attached at position R1 to form a fatty amide and a long carbon chain is attached at position R2 to form a long chain base. Either or both of these chains may contain one or more double bonds and may have hydroxyl groups attached. Based on the notation GIPC(m:n)-p, each compound within this class has a total of 40 carbons (m), zero double bonds (n), and two hydroxyl groups (p) present in the sphingoid base and the fatty amide. However, the length, hydroxylation position, double bond position, and double bond orientation of the groups attached at R2 and R3 will differ between individual members of this group of compounds.

name: L-iduronate
annotation: L-Iduronate is a reducing acidic sugar that can form a ring structure, as shown for CPD-12515. CPD-12515 is the major uronic acid component of the glycosaminoglycans Dermatan-Sulfate and HEPARIN. It is also present in Heparan-Sulfate, but in a minor amount relative to its carbon-5 epimer GLUCURONATE. CPD-12515 is often modified by the addition of an O-sulfate group at carbon position 2 to form CPD-12518. The open-chain structure shown here is provided only as a schematic illustration of chirality. Consult the appropriate class instances for information on specific ring structures and their anomeric configuration.

annotation: Heparan-Sulfate "Heparan sulfate" consists of a variably sulfated repeating disaccharide unit which is composed of a hexuronic acid and a hexosamine. The most common repeating unit, which makes up around 50% of the total disaccharide units in heparan sulfate, is composed of CPD-12521 linked to CPD-12541. This compound stand for CPD-12538 sulfated at both the 6' position and the N position (N6-O-disulfo-D-glucosamine) within the context of Heparan-Sulfate.

name: an organic phosphate
annotation: Organophosphates are a class of organophosphorus compounds with the general structure O=P(OR)3, a central phosphate molecule with alkyl or aromatic substituents. important examples including key biomolecules such as DNA, RNA and ATP, as well as many insecticides, herbicides, nerve agents and flame retardants.

name: a ganglioside LM1
annotation: ACETYLNEURAMINYL-23-BETA-D-GALACTOSYL-ET "Ganglioside LM1" is the predominant Gangliosides ganglioside (61%) of the peripheral nerve myelin .

name: a protopanaxatriol-type ginsenoside with two glycosyl residues at position 6
annotation: Ginsenosides Ginsenosides or panaxosides are a class of steroid glycosides and triterpene saponins found exclusively in the plant genus TAX-4053 (ginseng). They are the principal components responsible for the pharmaceutical activities of ginseng. Based on the structure of their aglycones, ginsenosides have been categorized into three broad types; Protopanaxadiol-type-ginsenosides "protopanaxadiol-type ginsenosides" (PPD, containing hydroxyl groups at positions 3 and 20), Protopanaxatriol-type-ginsenosides "protopanaxatriol-type ginsenosides" (PPT, containing hydroxyl groups at positions 3, 6 and 20), and Oleanonic-acid-type-ginsenosides "oleanonic-acid-type saponins".

name: [dermatan sulfate] containing 4-O-sulfo-N-acetyl-D-galactosamine-[core protein]
annotation: Dermatan-Sulfate "Dermatan sulfate" is a glycosaminoglycan found mostly in skin but also in blood vessels, the heart valves, tendons, and the lungs. Dermatan sulfate is composed of repeating disaccharide units composed of a hexose sugar and a hexuronic acid, many of which sulfated. The most common disaccharide unit in dermatan sulfate is composed of CPD-12515 and CPD-12557 (many are sulfated) linked by β(1 -> 3) linkages. This compound describes CPD-12557 residues sulfated at the 4' position within the context of Dermatan-Sulfate.
family_key: sm:name:dermatan sulfate containing 4 o sulfo n acetyl d galactosamine core protein

name: a xyloglucan cellotetraose
annotation: Xyloglucan is the major hemicellulose in dicotyledon type I cell walls and has a backbone of β-1,4-linked cellotetraose units, which are substituted in C-6 by a-D-xylosyl residues at two or three of the first three glucosyl residues (from the reducing end) . Some of the xylosyl residues are further substituted by galactosyl, arabinosyl or fucosyls residues . The cross-linking between xyloglucan chains is a major factor in the stability of the three-dimensional matrix between adjacent cellulose microfibrils . A common terminology refers to a glucosyl unit within a xylosylglycan as G and to a glucose residues substituted by D-xylose in a (1,6)-α-linkage as X. Using this terminology, most xylosylglucans consist of repeating heptasaccharide XXXG units. Subsequently (1,2)-β-D-galactosyl residues are added at the second and/or third xylose residue and an(1,2)-α-L-fucose is added at the galactosyl unit of the third xylose residue .

name: a [glycerolipid]-vernolate
annotation: Vernolates Vernolate (vernolic acid) is a fatty acid with an 18-carbon chain that contains a cis double bond at position 9 and an epoxy group at positions 12-13. Like other fatty acids, Vernolates is rarely found in its free form. It is usually found Glycerolipid-vernolate "incorporated into a lipid".

name: a carrageenan
annotation: Carrageenans Carrageenans and Carrageenoses carrageenoses are sulfated polysaccharides that are extracted from sea weeds belonging to the TAX-2763 (red algae), mainly from TAX-2769, and species from the TAX-31444 and TAX-33664 genera. They are laid out in the cell wall at a high density and can constitute up to 50% of the algae dry mass. The basic units of Carrageenans carrageenans are repeats of alternating α-1,3-linked D-galactose and β-1,4-linked D-galactose units, which are often substituted by sulfate. Carrageenans are classified according to the number and the position of sulfated ester . In Carrageenoses carrageenoses the α-1,3-linked galactose units are modified by having a 3,6-anhydro bridge. These bridges, which form a double helix during aggregation, are responsible for the gelation that is typical to these compounds. Despite this distinction, carrageenoses are often referred to as carrageenans. The three most industrially exploited carrageenans, namely, kappa-Carrageenan, iota-Carrageenan and Lambda-Carrageenan, are distinguished by the presence of one, two or three ester-sulfate groups per repeating disaccharide unit respectively.

name: a short-chain 3-oxo-fatty acid
annotation: The term "fatty acid" was originally coined to describe aliphatic monocarboxylic acids derived from or contained in esterified form in an animal or vegetable fat, oil or wax. However, over the years the term has been expanded to refer to additional, shorter monocarboxylic acids with an aliphatic tail such as PROPIONATE and BUTYRIC_ACID (but not the shorter FORMATE and ACET). The length of a fatty acid is determined by the length of the longest carbon chain, including the carbon of the carboxy group. Common natural fatty acids usually have an even number of carbons in the longest chain and can be either saturated or unsaturated. In many organisms they are often modified by branching, hydroxylation, methylation, epoxidation and other types of modifications. Inside living cells fatty acids are rarely found in free form. They are usually bound to coenzyme A or acyl-carrier proteins, or form part of Triacylglycerides triglycerides, Phospholipids phospholipids, Lipopolysaccharides lipopolysaccharides, and Cholesterol-esters cholesterol esters. Many enzymes that act on fatty acids, as well as the corresponding Fatty-Acyl-CoAs "fatty acyl-CoAs", Fatty-Alcohols alcohols, and Fatty-Aldehydes aldehydes, have evolved to recognize substrates with a limited range of chain lengths. To classify these enzymes, it is helpful to divide the substrates into smaller groups based on their chain length. The following subgroups have been defined: Short-chain-fatty-acids "Short-chain fatty acids" (SCFA) have 3-5 carbons in the longest chain. Medium-chain-fatty-acids "Medium-chain fatty acids" (MCFA) have 6-12 carbons in the longest chain. Long-Chain-Fatty-Acids "Long-chain fatty acids" (LCFA) have 13-22 carbons in the longest chain. Very-long-chain-fatty-acids "Very-long-chain fatty acids" (VLCF

name: a TAG(18:2/38:2)
annotation: This class of triacylglycerols contains three acyl chains formed by the esterification of glycerol with three fatty acids. The names of triacylglycerol compound classes provide information about the three acyl chains of each member of the class, using the nomenclature TAG(a:b/y:z) where "a" represents the number of carbons found in one acyl chain and "b" represents the number of desaturations present in chain "a" and where "y" represents the total number of carbons found in the other two acyl chains and "z" represents the total number of desaturations present in the two chains included in "y." Therefore, in this TAG(18:2/38:2) class of compounds, one acyl chain has 18 carbons and two double bonds. The other two acyl chains have a total of 38 carbons and two double bonds. The attachment sites of the acyl chains at the R1 (sn-1), R2 (sn-2), or R3 (sn-3) position along the glyceryl backbone may vary among class members and cannot be inferred from their order in the compound class name. Additionally, the location and orientation of the double bonds in the unsaturated acyl chain(s) may vary among members of the class and are not specified by this notation.

name: [keratan]-galactose 6'-sulfate
annotation: KERATAN-6-SULFATE "Keratan sulfate" is a glycosaminoglycan found mostly in the cornea, cartilage, and bone. It is a linear polymer that consists of a repeating disaccharide unit. The basic repeating disaccharide unit consists of GALACTOSE and N-ACETYL-BETA-D-GLUCOSAMINE, either of which can be sulfated at carbon position 6. This compound stand for CPD-3604 sulfated at the 6' position within the context of KERATAN-6-SULFATE.

name: a phytocassane
annotation: The phytocassanes are rice phytoalexins that were isolated from TAX-148305 infected rice leaves or TAX-456999 infected rice stems. The first four were discovered in 1995 and named phytocassanes A-D , and were followed by a fifth member (phytocassane E) in 1997 . It should be noted that several years later it was shown that the rice phytocassanes are configured with ent-, rather than the originally suggested normal stereochemistry and, thus, are enantiomeric to the originally suggested chemical structures .

name: a [SAMP] C-terminal glycine
annotation: Archaea have a mechanism of protein post-translational modification, termed SAMPylation, that shares many biochemical features with the ubiquitination system of eukaryotes. The archaeal equivalents of ubiquitin have been named Small Archaeal Modifier Proteins (SAMPs). Like ubiquitin, these proteins possess a β-grasp fold and carboxy-terminal diglycine motif, and as in ubiquitination, the C-terminal carboxylate forms an isopeptide bond with the ε-amino group of lysine residues on target proteins . The C-terminal glycine of SAMP2 can form a bond with a lysine residue of another SAMP2 molecule, forming poly-SAMP chains, as in the case of polyubiquitination. This structure shows the C-terminal glycine residue of SAMP, which forms covalent bonds with other prtoeins during SAMPylation.

name: a [chondroitin-sulfate] containing 2-O-sulfo-β-D-glucuronate
annotation: Chondroitin-sulfates "Chondroitin sulfate" is a glycosaminoglycan found mostly attached to proteins as part of a proteoglycan. Chondroitin sulfate is composed of repeating disaccharide units composed of a hexuronic acid and a hexose sugar, specifically CPD-12521 and CPD-12557 linked by β(1 -> 3) linkages. Both units are often sulfated. This compound stand for CPD-12527 within the context of Chondroitin-sulfates.
family_key: sm:name:a chondroitin sulfate containing 2 o sulfo d glucuronate

name: a glycosaminoglycan
annotation: Glycosaminoglycans (also known as mucopolysaccharides) are important components of connective tissues. They are composed of long unbranched polysaccharides consisting of a repeating disaccharide unit. The repeating units consist of an N-acetyl-hexosamine and a hexose or hexuronic acid, either or both of which are often sulfated. Members of the glycosaminoglycan family vary in the type of hexosamine, hexose or hexuronic acid unit they contain (e.g. CPD-12521, CPD-12515, galactose, CPD-12557, CPD-12541). Glycosaminoglycans are divided into four main classes: Chondroitin-sulfates and Dermatan-Sulfate; HEPARIN and Heparan-Sulfate; Hyaluronan; and KERATAN-6-SULFATE "keratan sulfate". With the exception of hyaluronan, the polysaccharide chains are linked covalently at the reducing end to a core protein through an O-glycosidic linkage to a serine residue. The modified proteins are known as Proteoglycans proteoglycans. Most glycosaminoglycans are attached via a specific tetrasaccharide known as the GlcA-Gal-Gal-Xyl-Proteins "glycosaminoglycan-protein linkage region".

name: a siderophore-bound Fe(II)
annotation: This entry stands for siderophores-bound FE+2 cation(s). Siderophores are small, high-affinity iron chelating compounds secreted by microorganisms. Once outside the cell, siderophores bind FE+3 with high affinity, and the iron-siderophore complexes are imported back into the cell by active-transport mechanisms. Siderophores are very common in pathogenic bacteria, since iron in the host organism is usually tightly bound to proteins, resulting in an extremely low concentration of free iron in the blood. Most siderophores belong to one of three major groups: the Catecholate-siderophores "catechols", Hydroxamate-siderophores "hydroxamates" and Thiazoline-siderophores "thiazolines". Some grasses are known to make siderophores as well. These Phytosiderophores "phytosiderophores" are also known as mugineic acids.

name: a phthiodiolenone A
annotation: Phthiodiolenones Phthiodiolenones are intermediates in Phthiocerols phthiocerol biosynthesis. Mutation of the G185E-7207 gene of TAX-1773 results in accumulation of Phthiodiolenones phthiodiolenone, which contains an unsaturated bond between C-4 and C-5 . Two types of phthiodiolenone exist. In Phthiodiolenone-A "phthiodiolenone A" the oxo and the methyl groups are attached at positions 3 and 4, respectively, and the hydroxyl groups are at positions 9 and 11. In Phthiodiolenone-B "phthiodiolenone B" a methyl group is missing from the beginning of the molecule, and thus the oxo and methyl groups are attached at positions 2 and 3, respectively, and the hydroxyl groups are at positions 8 and 10.

name: a bacteriochlorophyll d
annotation: Green sulfur bacteria (TAX-191412) and green flimentous bacteria (TAX-1106) contain chlorosomes - large organelles that are believed to be the most efficient light-harvesting antennae in nature . Green sulfur bacteria can grow at light intensities that are too low to support growth of any other phototroph, up to a million times lower light than normal sunlight . Chlorosomes contain about 1,500 bacteriochlorophyll molecules per reaction center. They are filled with 10-30 rod-shaped elements which are oriented parallel to the long axis of the chlorosomes and have a diameter of 10 nm. Each rod consists of a tubular bilayer formed by self-organization of the antenna bacteriochlorophyll molecules. Unlike all other known photosynthetic antenna, in which the chlorophyll molecules are bound to proteins, chlorosomes do not contain accessory proteins. The chlorosomal bacteriochlorophyll pigments are able to self-aggregate due to their unique chemical characteristics. The bacteriochlorophylls found in chlorosomes are either CPD-9092 "bacteriochlorophyll c", CPD-9093 "bacteriochlorophyll d" or CPD-9094 "bacteriochlorophyll e", depending on the species. They all possess a hydroxyl group at the C31 position that allows them to chelate the Mg atom of a neighboring molecule and to interact with the C13-oxo carbonyl group of another neighboring pigment molecule, affording the self-aggregation . In addition, all of these bacteriochlorophylls lack C-132-methylcarboxyl side chains, which are found in all other (bacterio)chlorophylls but would interfere with the ability to form aggregates . Despite of their names, CPD-9092 "bacteriochlorophyll c", CPD-9093 "bacteriochlorophyll d" and CPD-9094 "bacteriochlorophyll e" are actually chlorins rather than bacteriochlorins. name: a phthioceranate
annotation: A unique feature of mycobacterial cell wall lipids is the presence of very long chain fatty acids with multiple methyl branches toward the carboxyl end of the carbon chain. Many types of such acids exist, including Mycosanoic-Acids "mycosanoic acids", Mycolipanoic-Acids "mycolipanoic acids", Mycolipanolic-Acids "mycolipanolic acids", Mycolipenic-Acids "mycolipenic acids", Mycolipodienoic-Acids "mycolipodienoic acids", Phthioceranic-Acids "phthioceranic acids", and Mycocerosic-Acids "mycocerosic acids". Phthioceranic acids are hepta and octa-methylated alkanoic very-long chain fatty acids found in Mycobacteria . The chiral centers bearing the methyl groups have a D (dextrorotatory) stereo configuration . In heptamethylated phthioceranic acids R1 = H. In octamethylated phthioceranic acids R1=CH3.

name: camphor
annotation: Camphor Camphor is a bicyclic monoterpene with a characteristic pungent odor and taste. Two enantiomers occur naturally - CPD-862, the predominant natural isomer, and S-CAMPHOR. Camphor is produced by several trees, including the camphor tree (TAX-13429) as well as hundreds of other plants, including TAX-38868 (garden sage), TAX-39367 (rosemary), TAX-39169 (lavender), TAX-35608 (sweet wormwood), and TAX-13328 . A study of enzymes from the camphor tree found multiple terpene synthases, two that lead to production of CPD-862 and one that leads to production of S-CAMPHOR . Camphor can be synthesized chemically, as an racemic mixture of the two enantiomers, from CPD-4892, which is obtained from oil of turpentine. Today most camphor is synthetic. It has a wide range of uses, including as a plasticizer for modified celluloses in lacquers, varnishes, and plastics; as a moth repellente; and as a preservative in cosmetics and embalming fluids. It is also used in medicine as an ingredient in topical creams and ointments to treat itching, irritation, and joint pain. Although it is found in essential oils used in perfumery (such as those of lavender), it significantly diminishes the quality of these oils and reduces their market value. Oils that contain elevated camphor content are usually used as insect repellents and for other non-perfumery uses . Even though camphor has been traditionally used in foods and medicines, it is toxic to humans and is especially dangerous to young children. Contact can lead to skin and eye irritation. Ingestion of camphor can cause irritability, seizures, cardiac arrhythmias, and cardiopulmonary arrest . Camphorated oil was removed from the US market in the 1980s due to accidental poisonings.

name: a furan-containing fatty acid
annotation: Furan-containing fatty acids are generated in large amounts in algae, but they are also produced by plants and microorganisms. Fish and other marine organisms as well as mammals consume these acids in their food and incorporate them into phospholipids and cholesterol esters. They are catabolized to dibasic urofuran acids, which are excreted in the urine. Furan-containing fatty acids react readily with peroxyl radicals to generate dioxoenes. Their radical-scavenging ability may contribute to the protective properties of fish and fish oil diets against mortality from heart disease .

name: hyaluronan
annotation: Hyaluronan Hyaluronan is an anionic, nonsulfated Glycosaminoglycans "glycosaminoglycan" distributed widely throughout connective, epithelial, and neural tissues, including the vitreous humor of the eye, the aorta, blood, brain, liver and skin . It is produced by almost all members of the animal kingdom as well as by certain members of streptococci . Hyaluronan can be very large, with its molecular weight often reaching the millions. It forms a strikingly viscous solution and functions primarily as a joint lubricant and shock absorber, although it is also thought to be involved in cell proliferation, migration and differentiation. Hyaluronan is composed of repeating disaccharide unit of [N-ACETYL-BETA-D-GLUCOSAMINE (β1,4) CPD-12521] (HYALURONATE) linked by β 1,3 linkages. Though classified as a glycosaminoglycan, it differs from other members of this family. While all other glycosaminoglycans are synthesized by resident Golgi enzymes and covalently attached to core proteins, hyaluronan is synthesized at the inner face of the plasma membrane as a free linear polymer without any protein core. In addition, it is the only glycosaminoglycan that is not sulfated .

name: an α,β dihydroxyphytoceramide
annotation: Alpha-beta-dihydroxyphytoceramides "An α,β dihydroxyphytoceramide" is Phytoceramides in which the fatty acid moiety is hydroxylated at the carbon closest to the amide bond, and the carbon next to it. These modifiecations are not visible in the structure shown here.

name: an N-terminal L-glutaminyl-[protein]
aliases: a peptide with N-terminal L-glutamine|a [protein] N-terminal L-glutamine
annotation: In eukaryotes, N-terminal-asparagine "N-terminal Asn" (N) and L-Glutaminyl-Peptides "N-terminal Gln" (Q) are tertiary destabilizing residues in that they function, through enzymatic deamidation, to yield the secondary destabilizing N-terminal residues Asp (D) and Glu (E). Asp and Glu are conjugaed by ATE1-encoded Arg-tRNA-protein transferase, to arginine, one of the primary destabilizing N-terminal residues. Arginine is recognized by the E3 Ub ligases of the N-end rule pathway, called N-recognins. A third destabilizing residue that functions through arginylation is an N-terminal-L-cysteine "N-terminal Cys" (C), which is arginylated after its nitric oxide (NO)-dependent oxidation to N-terminal-L-cysteine-sulfinate or N-terminal-L-cysteine-sulfonate (via Protein-nitrosothiol-L-alanine) .

annotation: Maltooligosaccharides are glucooligosaccharides derived from D-glucose monomers linked via α-1,4 bonds as in MALTOSE. More specifically, the term is applied to linear oligosaccharides composed of more than two and less than ten units of glucose. The corresponding α-1,6 glucooligosaccharides are called Isomaltooligosaccharides isomaltooligosaccharides.

name: palatinose
annotation: CPD-20122 Palatinose, also known as isomaltulose, is a reducing disaccharide of about one-third the sweetness of sucrose, that is very resistant to hydrolysis by acid and by EC-3.2.1.26. It has very similar physical properties to sucrose, and is found naturally in small quantities in honey .

name: an α factor
aliases: Saccharomyces cerevisiae mating pheromon α factor
annotation: Mating in TAX-4932 results from the fusion of two haploid cells of opposite mating type, a and α, to form a diploid. The mating pheromones, CPD-10692 and Alpha-factor, produced by a and α haploid cells, respectively, stimulate receptors on cells of the opposite mating type to initiate responses that lead to cell fusion. The molecular structure of the α factor has been investigated. Four different oligopeptides exhibiting α factor activity have been isolated . These peptides, designated as α1, α2, α3, and α4 are structurally closely related, being composed of thirteen (α1 and α3) or twelve (α2 and α4) amino acids, respectively. The α3 and α4 peptides are oxidation products, containing an internal L-Methionine-sulfoxides instead of MET. The peptides were found to be composed of the following amino acids: 2 glutamate or glutamine, 2 proline, 1 glycine, methionine or methionine sulfoxide, 2 leucine, 1 tyrosine, 1 lysine, 1 histidine, 1 or 2 tryptophan. TYR is the C-terminal amino acid in all four peptides. The tridekapeptides differ from the dodekapeptides by an additional N-terminal TRP residue.

name: an O4-methylthymine in DNA
annotation: This compound class stands for generic and unspecified DNA 4-O-methylthymines.

name: a porphyran
annotation: Porphyran is a sulfated carbohydrate extracted from the cell wall of red seaweeds belonging to the genus TAX-2784 . Unlike Agaroses agarose and Carrageenans carrageenan, porphyran is not utilized as ingredient for its gelling or texturizing properties. However, it is one of the most consumed algal polysaccharides because it is the main component of edible TAX-2788 and TAX-2785, both of which are extensively used in the preparation of sushi . The backbone of porphyran is composed of about 30% of Agaroses agarose repeating units (1,3-linked β-D-galactopyranose followed by 1,4-linked 3,6-anhydro-α-L-galactopyranose), with the remaining residues being 3-linked β-D-galactopyranose and 4-linked α-L-galactopyranose-6-sulfate. The 4-linked α-L-galactopyranose-6-sulfate residues can be converted to CPD-13430 by the enzyme 2.5.1.5-RXN "galactose-6-sulfurylase, EC 2.5.1.5". In addition, the galactan backboned is decorated by methyl-ether groups at the C6 position of the 3-linked β-D-galactopyranose residues. As other sulfated galactans, porphyran may have some potential pharmacological applications , based on, for example, its hypolipidemic and anti-allergic properties .

name: a triazine
annotation: Triazine compounds contain an aromatic ring in which three of the carbon atoms have been replaced by nitrogen atoms.

name: κ-carrageenose
annotation: Carrageenans Carrageenans and Carrageenoses carrageenoses are sulfated polysaccharides that are extracted from sea weeds belonging to the TAX-2763 (red algae), mainly from TAX-2769, and species from the TAX-31444 and TAX-33664 genera. They are laid out in the cell wall at a high density and can constitute up to 50% of the algae dry mass. The basic units of Carrageenans carrageenans are repeats of alternating α-1,3 linked D-galactose and β-1,4-linked D-galactose units, which are often substituted by sulfate. Carrageenans are classified according to the number and the position of sulfated ester . In Carrageenoses carrageenoses the α-1,3-linked galactose units are modified by having a 3,6-anhydro bridge. These bridges, which form a double helix during aggregation, are responsible for the gelation that is typical to these compounds. Despite this distinction, carrageenoses are often referred to as carrageenans. The three most industrially exploited carrageenans, namely, kappa-Carrageenan, iota-Carrageenan and Lambda-Carrageenan, are distinguished by the presence of one, two or three ester-sulfate groups per repeating disaccharide unit respectively. kappa-Carrageenan is sulfated only at position 4 of the β-1,4 linked D-galactose units.

name: (carboxymethyl)cellodextrin
annotation: Carboxymethylcellulose Carboxymethylcellulose (CMC) is a CELLULOSE derivative with carboxymethyl groups bound to some of the hydroxyl groups of the glucopyranose monomers that make up the cellulose backbone. All available positions, O2, O3, and O6, can be modified. It is often used as its sodium salt, sodium carboxymethyl cellulose. Unlike unmodified cellulose, carboxymethylcellulose is soluble in water. CMC is used in food science as a viscosity modifier or thickener, and to stabilize emulsions in various products including ice cream. It is also a constituent of many non-food products, such as K-Y Jelly, toothpaste, laxatives, diet pills, water-based paints, detergents, textile sizing and various paper products. Carboxymethylcellodextrins are shorter polymers of varying length (two or more monomers) resulting from the breakdown of carboxymethylcellulose.

name: lichenan
annotation: Lichenin Lichenan is a complex glucan occurring in certain species of lichens, consisting of glucosyl residues interconnected by β-1,3 and β-1,4 linkages. It is an important carbohydrate for reindeers and northern flying squirrels, which eat lichen.

name: a carbamate ester
annotation: A carbamate is an organic compound derived from carbamic acid.

name: a megalomicin
annotation: The megalomicins, which were discovered in 1968 , are a family of related macrolide antibiotics produced by the soil bacterium TAX-47868. They are therapeutically diverse compounds that possess antiparasitic, antiviral and antibacterial properties. Like other macrolide antibiotics, they are more active against Gram-positive bacteria than against Gram-negative bacteria . The first family member to have its structure reported was CPD-12966 . Acyl derivatives of CPD-12966, including CPD-14689, CPD-12967, CPD-14691, and CPD-14690, were soon discovered . The megalomicins have the same aglycone as CPD-13804 and CPD-13951, but include a unique deoxyamino sugar, CPD-14692, at the C-6 hydroxyl.

name: a xyloglucan oligosaccharide
annotation: Xyloglucan oligosaccharides are produced from Xyloglucan xyloglucan by the action of endo- or exo-(1,4)-β-glucanases. A study that analyzed the products of tamarind xyloglucan digestion by several endoglucanases from different organisms found that all enzymes produced oligosaccharides with the composition CPD-13375 XXXG, CPD-13376 XXLG/CPD-13377 XLXG and CPD-13378 XLLG as the end products of xyloglucan hydrolysis (see Xyloglucan for explanation of this terminology). Xyloglucan from TAX-3702 is comprised of repeating subunits of CPD-13418 XXFG, CPD-13375 XXXG, CPD-13376 XXLG, CPD-13417 XLFG, CPD-13378 XLLG, and CPD-13377 XLXG .

name: chondroitin sulfate C
annotation: Chondroitin-sulfates "Chondroitin sulfate" is a sulfated Glycosaminoglycans "glycosaminoglycan" composed of alternating units of CPD-12557 and CPD-12521. It is usually found attached to proteins as part of a proteoglycan. The saccharide units can be sulfated in variable positions and quantities - CPD-12557 can be sulfated at positions 4 and 6 to yield CPD-12516, CPD-12565 or CPD-12526, while CPD-12521 can be sulfated at position 2 to form CPD-12527. The following names have been used to define different sulfation states: Chondroitin-Sulfate-A "Chondroitin sulfate A" contains CPD-12521 and CPD-12516 Chondroitin-Sulfate-C "Chondroitin sulfate C" contains CPD-12521 and CPD-12565 Chondroitin-Sulfate-D "Chondroitin sulfate D" contains CPD-12527 and CPD-12565 Chondroitin-Sulfate-E "Chondroitin sulfate E" contains CPD-12521 and CPD-12526 The term "chondroitin sulfate B" has been used in the past to refer to Dermatan-Sulfate, but is not used any more.

name: a fructooligosaccharide
annotation: Fructooligosaccharides (FOS), sometimes called oligofructose or oligofructan, are a class of oligosaccharides used as an artificial or alternative sweetener. A common source of FOS is the hydrolysis of Inulin inulin by EC-3.2.1.7, which results in two types of FOS: either oligosaccharides consisting solely of fructose [(β-D-Fruf-(2→1)-β-D-Fruf)]n (e.g. INULOBIOSE, CPD-24815, and CPD-24816), or oligosaccharides that terminate with glucose [(β-D-Fruf-(2→1)-β-D-Fruf)]n-(2↔1)-α-D-Glcp (e.g. 1-KESTOTRIOSE, NYSTOSE and CPD-24817). The second mechanism that produces FOS is the transfructosylation action of EC-3.2.1.26, on SUCROSE, which results in FOS of the general formula G(Fn) that can contain linkages other than β(1→2) . FOS exhibit sweetness levels between 30 and 50 percent of sugar, and are used as alternative sweeteners. They resist hydrolysis by salivary and intestinal digestive enzymes, but are fermented in the colon by anaerobic bacteria. They are considered probiotics and may stimulate the growth of beneficial bacteria such as Bifidobacteria species .

name: an arylamine
annotation: Anilines are compounds that contain a benzene ring carrying at least one amino substituent.

name: tranylcypromine
annotation: Tranylcypromine is a drug of the substituted phenethylamine and amphetamine classes that acts as a monoamine oxidase inhibitor (MAOI). It is indicated primarily for the treatment of major depressive disorder, and can also be used in the management of various mood and anxiety disorders, typically as a last resort after conventional antidepressants have been tried without success. Indications for monoamine oxidase inhibitors are currently very limited, due to their significant potential for adverse effects, many interactions, and the availability of newer and safer (although not necessarily more efficacious) antidepressants. Tranylcypromine is a nonselective and irreversible inhibitor of the enzyme monoamine oxidase (MAO).

name: (carboxymethyl)cellulose
annotation: Carboxymethylcellulose (CMC) is a CELLULOSE derivative with carboxymethyl groups bound to some of the hydroxyl groups of the glucopyranose monomers that make up the cellulose backbone. All available positions, O2, O3, and O6, can be modified. It is often used as its sodium salt, sodium carboxymethyl cellulose. Unlike unmodified cellulose, carboxymethylcellulose is soluble in water. CMC is used in food science as a viscosity modifier or thickener, and to stabilize emulsions in various products including ice cream. It is also a constituent of many non-food products, such as K-Y Jelly, toothpaste, laxatives, diet pills, water-based paints, detergents, textile sizing and various paper products.

name: a siderophore-bound Fe(III)
aliases: an iron(III) chelate|an Fe3+-siderophore|a ferric-siderophore complex
annotation: This entry stands for siderophores-bound FE+3 cation(s). Siderophores are small, high-affinity iron chelating compounds secreted by microorganisms. Once outside the cell, siderophores bind FE+3 with high affinity, and the iron-siderophore complexes are imported back into the cell by active-transport mechanisms. Siderophores are very common in pathogenic bacteria, since iron in the host organism is usually tightly bound to proteins, resulting in an extremely low concentration of free iron in the blood. Most siderophores belong to one of three major groups: the Catecholate-siderophores "catechols", Hydroxamate-siderophores "hydroxamates" and Thiazoline-siderophores "thiazolines". Some grasses are known to make siderophores as well. These Phytosiderophores "phytosiderophores" are also known as mugineic acids.

name: a prenol
annotation: Prenols are alcohols that possess a carbon skeleton that is composed of one or more CPD-9436 units (the biogenetic precursors of the Polyisoprenoids isoprenoids).

name: D-psicose
annotation: D-Psicose, often referred to as allulose, is a low-calorie epimer of FRU, used by some major commercial food and beverage manufacturers as a low-calorie sweetener. It is naturally present in small quantities in certain foods. This compound class represents a reducing sugar that exists in solution in multiple configurations. The open-chain structure shown here is provided only as a schematic illustration of chirality. Consult the appropriate class instances for information on specific ring structures and their anomeric configuration.

name: a mycolate
annotation: Mycolic acids are the hallmark of the cell envelope of TAX-1773 and related species and genera. They comprise a homologous series of C60-C90 (2R)-alkyl-(3R)-hydroxy very long-chain fatty acids, and are found as either esters of Arabinogalactan or as free lipids in the form of trehalose dimycolate (TDM). Both forms contribute to forming the two leaflets of the mycobacterial outer membrane, also called the mycomembrane, which protects the organism from antibiotics and the host's immune system . There is a significant variability in the structure of mycolic acids among different species. Acids from TAX-1716 species contain 22-38 carbon atoms, TAX-697025 and TAX-697025 spp. contain 30-36, TAX-37914 spp. contain 34-38, TAX-1827 spp. contain 34-52 in, TAX-1817 spp. contain 46-60, TAX-2053 Gordonia spp. contain 46-66, TAX-2060 spp. contain 64-78 , TAX-1763 spp. contain 60-90, and TAX-286801 spp. contain up to 100 carbon atoms . The acids are formed by a Claisen-type condensation of two components - the meromycolic chain and a fatty acid. The meromycolic chain of TAX-1773 typically incorporates a hydroxy group at C-1, a cyclopropyl ring fused onto the C-16/C-17 bond (at either a cis or a trans configuration), a methoxyl or oxo group at C-35, and a methyl group at C-36. The modifications and their positions may change among strains and species. Several structural classes of mycolic acids exist. In TAX-1773 the most apolar ones, known as α-mycolic acids, contain 74-80 carbon atoms (out of which 22-26 carbons are located in the fatty acid moiety and the rest on the mero-chain) and generally two double bonds (that can be in either cis- or trans-configuration) or two cis-cyclopropyl groups located in the meromycolic chain. The cyclopropane rings are believed to protect the organism

name: a 1,3-diglyceride
annotation: The name glyceride describes any ester of GLYCEROL with Fatty-Acids "fatty acids". Depending on the number of fatty acids, glycerides are classified as Monoacylglycerols monoglycerides, Diacylglycerides diglycerides and Triacylglycerols triglycerides. Diglycerides are classified further into 1-2-Diglycerides "1,2-diglycerides" and 1-3-Diglycerides "1,3-diglycerides".

name: a tetrapyrrole
annotation: Tetrapyrroles are natural pigments containing four pyrrole rings joined by one-carbon units linking position 2 of one pyrrole ring to position 5 of the next.

name: a 1-alkene
annotation: Alkenes are acyclic hydrocarbons with (only) one carbon-carbon double bond. They can be either branched or non-branched. In a 1-alkene the double bond is located between carbons 1 and 2.

name: a rhamnogalacturonan type II
annotation: Rhamnogalacturonan type II (RG-II) is a relatively small polysaccharide that makes up about 10% of Pectin pectin. Despite its small size, it, is the most structurally complex pectin component. Its α-1,4-linked D-galacturonate backbone is decorated with four side branches (named A-D) consisting of 12 different types of sugars in over 20 different linkages . Some of the sugars are quite rare, including CPD-13046, CPD-20946 (3-C-carboxy-5-deoxy-L-xylose), and CPD-20949 (Dha). RG-II exists in the primary wall as a dimer that is cross linked by a 1:2 borate-diol diester . The borate diester cross link is formed between the apiosyl residue in side chain A of each monomer subunit . Despite this complexity the structure of RG-II is largely conserved across plant species. It has been reported that the structure is virtually the same in members of the Brassicacea, Cucurbitaceae, Leguminoseae, Apiaceae, Liliaceae, Araceae, Amaryllidaceae, Gramineae, and more families . However, later work reported some variation in the side chains and methylation status within and across different plant speces . Please note that the structure shown here is not entirely accurate, due to the inability of software to depict unusual monosaccharides. CPD-13046, CPD-20946 and CPD-20949 are depicted as generic pentose, hexuronate, and heptose, respectively.

name: a toluene
annotation: The toluenes include any substituted benzene in which the substituents include one (and only one) methyl group.

name: a short-chain alkane
annotation: Alkanes are saturated acyclic hydrocarbons. They consist only of hydrogen and saturated carbon atoms and are bonded exclusively by single bonds. They could be either branched or unbranched. Linear alkanes are often classified by the length of their acyl moiety. Short-Chain-Alkanes "Short-chain alkanes" contain less than 6- carbons. Medium-Chain-Alkanes "Medium-chain alkanes" contain 6-12 carbons. Long-Chain-Alkanes "Long-chain alkanes" contain 13-22 carbons. Very-Long-Chain-Alkanes "Very-long-chain alkanes" contain 23 carbons or longer.

name: a macromolecule
annotation: A macromolecule is a very large molecule important to biophysical processes, such as a protein or nucleic acid.

name: a glucuronoxylan
annotation: Glucuronoxylans Glucuronoxylans are a component of Hemicelluloses. They are linear polymers of β-D-xylopyranosyl units linked by (1→4) glycosidic bonds, with many of the xylose units substituted with (1→2)- and/or (1→3)-linked glucuronate residues, many of which are methylated at position 4. Most of the glucuronoxylans have single 4-O-methyl-α-D-glucopyranosyl uronate residues (MeGlcA) attached at position 2. This structural type is usually named as 4-O-Methyl-D-Glucurono-D-xylan "4-O-methyl-D-glucurono-D-xylan" (MGX). Angiosperm (hardwood) glucuronoxylans also have a high rate of substitution (70-80%) by acetyl groups, at position 2 and/or 3 of the β-D-xylopyranosyl, conferring on the xylan its partial solubility in water. The ratio of xylosyl residues to D-glucuronate/4-O-methylglucuronate residues in Glucuronoxylans glucuronoxylans varies greatly among species. An average ratio of 10:1 is accepted, although it can be as high as 2:1, for example, in quince (TAX-36610) seeds .

name: a binuclear copper center
annotation: A binuclear copper center is a metal-sulfur cluster comprising two copper atoms bridging sulfur ligands.

name: a prodrug
annotation: Prodrugs are compounds that, upon administration, are metabolized (converted within the body) into a pharmacologically active drug. Prodrugs usually have better absorbance and distribution than their respective active drug, and are designed to improve bioavailability or targeting.

Strain B of this species is rich in triterpenoid C30 botryococcenes, and synthesizes higher order botryococcenes upto C37, via methyl addition using S-adenosyl methionine. The compound class shown here is one of six possible isomers of C34 botryococcenes.

name: a TAG(18:2/32:1)
annotation: This class of triacylglycerols contains three acyl chains formed by the esterification of glycerol with three fatty acids. The names of triacylglycerol compound classes provide information about the three acyl chains of each member of the class, using the nomenclature TAG(a:b/y:z) where "a" represents the number of carbons found in one acyl chain and "b" represents the number of desaturations present in chain "a" and where "y" represents the total number of carbons found in the other two acyl chains and "z" represents the total number of desaturations present in the two chains included in "y." Therefore, in this TAG(18:2/32:1) class of compounds, one acyl chain has 18 carbons and two double bonds. The other two acyl chains have a total of 32 carbons and one double bond. The attachment sites of the acyl chains at the R1 (sn-1), R2 (sn-2), or R3 (sn-3) position along the glyceryl backbone may vary among class members and cannot be inferred from their order in the compound class name. Additionally, the location and orientation of the double bonds in the unsaturated acyl chain(s) may vary among members of the class and are not specified by this notation.

aliases: a [heparan sulfate]-containing N-sulfoglucosamine|a heparin containing α-D-N-sulfoglucosamine|a [heparan sulfate] containing GlcNS
annotation: Heparan-Sulfate "Heparan sulfate" consists of a variably sulfated repeating disaccharide unit which is composed of a hexuronic acid and a hexosamine. The most common repeating unit, which makes up around 50% of the total disaccharide units in heparan sulfate, is composed of CPD-12521 linked to CPD-12541. This compound stand for N-sulfo-D-glucosamine within the context of Heparan-Sulfate, and not at the terminal non-reducing end..

name: an agaran
annotation: Agarans Agarans and Agaroses agaroses are sulfated polysaccharides that are extracted from sea weeds belonging to the TAX-2763 (red algae), mainly from TAX-2769, and species from the TAX-31444 and TAX-33664 genera. The basic units of Agarans agarans are repeats of β -1,3 linked D-galactose and α-1,4- linked L-galactose units, which are substituted by sulfate. Different types of agarans have sulfate groups at different positions (depicted as R3 in the structure) ( . In Agaroses agaroses the α-1,4- linked galactose units are modified by having a 3,6-anhydro bridge. These bridges are responsible for the gelation that is typical to these compounds, by forming a double helix during the process.

name: a non-ribosomal peptide
annotation: Nonribosomal peptides (NRP) are a class of peptide secondary metabolites, usually produced by microorganisms like bacteria and fungi. NRPs are found in some higher organisms, such as nudibranchs, but are thought to be synthesized by symbiotic bacteria living inside these organisms. NRPs are not produced by ribosomes directed by mRNA. Instead, they are synthesized by a groups of enzymes called Non-ribosomal-peptide-synthetases "non-ribosomal peptide synthetases. Each nonribosomal peptide synthetase can synthesize only one type of peptide. NRPs are often highly modified, and are known to contain D-amino acids, as well as modifications such as methylation, formylation, cyclization, glycosylation, acylation, halogenation, and hydroxylation.

name: a non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug
annotation: Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAIDs) are a group of anti-inflammatory drugs that do not belong to the steroid family. In addition to anti-inflammatory actions, non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs have analgesic, antipyretic, and platelet-inhibitory actions. They act by blocking the synthesis of Prostaglandins prostaglandins by inhibiting cyclooxygenase, which converts ARACHIDONIC_ACID to cyclic endoperoxides, precursors of Prostaglandins prostaglandins.
