name: a progestogen
annotation: Progestogens are steroid hormones that interact with progesterone receptors in target tissues to bring about effects similar to those of progesterone. They are named for their function in maintaining pregnancy (pro-gestational), although they are also present at other phases of the estrous and menstrual cycles

name: a trimethylated mycocerosate
annotation: A unique feature of mycobacterial cell wall lipids is the presence of very long chain fatty acids with multiple methyl branches toward the carboxyl end of the carbon chain. Many types of such acids exist, including Mycosanoic-Acids "mycosanoic acids", Mycolipanoic-Acids "mycolipanoic acids", Mycolipanolic-Acids "mycolipanolic acids", Mycolipenic-Acids "mycolipenic acids", Mycolipodienoic-Acids "mycolipodienoic acids", Phthioceranic-Acids "phthioceranic acids", and Mycocerosic-Acids "mycocerosic acids". Mycocerosic acids are 2,4,6-trimethyl- and 2,4,6,8-tetramethyl-2-alkanoic acids present in many pathogenic TAX-1762, including TAX-1773, TAX-1765, TAX-1777, TAX-29311, TAX-1768, TAX-1769 . Unlike the other types of fatty acids found in mycobacteria, the chiral centers bearing the methyl groups have an L (levorotatory) stereo configuration. The name "mycocerosic acid" was suggested by Ginger and Anderson in 1945 . The mycocerosic acids of the mycobacteria are found mainly as CPD-18612 (DIM A) waxes or in phenolic glycolipids. In TAX-1773 the common mycoceroic acids are C30 and C32 . In TAX-1765 the common mycoceroic acids are mainly C26, C27, C29, and C30 .

name: a diester
annotation: A diester is a compound containing two ester groups.

name: a C21-steroid
annotation: Steroids are compounds possessing the skeleton of cyclopenta[a]phenanthrene or a skeleton derived therefrom by one or more bond scissions or ring expansions or contractions. Methyl groups are normally present at C-10 and C-13. An alkyl side chain may also be present at C-17.

name: acetylxylan
annotation: 1-4-beta-Xylan (1→4)-β-D-xylan is present in some trees and other plants in a partially acetylated form. Angiosperm (hardwood) xylans (e.g. from beechwood) have a high rate of substitution (70-80%) by acetyl groups at position 2 and/or 3 of the β-D-xylopyranosyl residues, conferring on the xylan its partial solubility in water .

name: an HpODE
annotation: HPODE is an acronym for HydroPeroxyOctadecaDiEnoate. It describes a family of mono-hydroperoxylated fatty acids containing two double bonds that are derived from LINOLEIC_ACID.

name: a TAG(18:2/40:4)
annotation: This class of triacylglycerols contains three acyl chains formed by the esterification of glycerol with three fatty acids. The names of triacylglycerol compound classes provide information about the three acyl chains of each member of the class, using the nomenclature TAG(a:b/y:z) where "a" represents the number of carbons found in one acyl chain and "b" represents the number of desaturations present in chain "a" and where "y" represents the total number of carbons found in the other two acyl chains and "z" represents the total number of desaturations present in the two chains included in "y." Therefore, in this TAG(18:2/40:4) class of compounds, one acyl chain has 18 carbons and two double bonds. The other two acyl chains have a total of 40 carbons and four double bonds. The attachment sites of the acyl chains at the R1 (sn-1), R2 (sn-2), or R3 (sn-3) position along the glyceryl backbone may vary among class members and cannot be inferred from their order in the compound class name. Additionally, the location and orientation of the double bonds in the unsaturated acyl chain(s) may vary among members of the class and are not specified by this notation.

name: amylopectin
annotation: Starch Starch is a mixture of the two polymeric carbohydrates (polysaccharides) Alpha-Amyloses and CPD-7043. Alpha-Amyloses "α-Amylose" contains up to several thousand glucosyl units linked almost exclusively in linear α(1->4) linkages, with very few branches of α(1->6) linkages. In CPD-7043, on the other hand, branching is common, and occurs approximately every 24-30 glucose units. Alpha-Amyloses "α-Amylose" accounts for 30% of starch, while CPD-7043 accounts for the other 70%.

name: an ε-lactone
annotation: A Lactones "lactone" is a cyclic ester, the product of condensation of an alcohol group and a carboxylate group within the same molecule. It is characterized by a closed ring consisting of two or more carbon atoms and a single oxygen atom, with a ketone group in one of the carbons adjacent to the ring oxygen. There are several types of lactones - Beta-lactones contain 3 carbons in the ring, Gamma-lactones contains 4, Delta-lactones contains 5, and Epsilon-lactones contains 6 carbons.

name: an N-acyl-neuraminate
annotation: Sialic acids are an important family of related 9-carbon sugars acids, present on the surface of many different cells and functioning in a wide range of different biological processes. Among the sialic acids, N-ACETYLNEURAMINATE is the most frequently found member in humans, and CPD-273 is abundant in many other species. A total of more than 50 different sialic acids have been described, including the deaminated compound CPD-21259 "3-deoxy-D-glycero-D-galacto-nononate" or KDN . They can be O-acetylated in positions 4, 7, or 9 , but also N-deacetylated, O-methylated, sulfated, or modified by lactonization .

name: a lactam
annotation: A Lactams lactam (the name is derived from a combination of the words Lactones lactone + Amides amide) is a cyclic amide of amino carboxylic acids. Prefixes made of Greek letters indicate how many carbon atoms (apart from the carbonyl moiety) are present in the ring.

name: a TAG(18:3/36:5)
annotation: This class of triacylglycerols contains three acyl chains formed by the esterification of glycerol with three fatty acids. The names of triacylglycerol compound classes provide information about the three acyl chains of each member of the class, using the nomenclature TAG(a:b/y:z) where "a" represents the number of carbons found in one acyl chain and "b" represents the number of desaturations present in chain "a" and where "y" represents the total number of carbons found in the other two acyl chains and "z" represents the total number of desaturations present in the two chains included in "y." Therefore, in this TAG(18:3/36:5) class of compounds, one acyl chain has 18 carbons and three double bonds. The other two acyl chains have a total of 36 carbons and five double bonds. The attachment sites of the acyl chains at the R1 (sn-1), R2 (sn-2), or R3 (sn-3) position along the glyceryl backbone may vary among class members and cannot be inferred from their order in the compound class name. Additionally, the location and orientation of the double bonds in the unsaturated acyl chain(s) may vary among members of the class and are not specified by this notation.

name: a retinoate
annotation: Retinoic acids comprise a biologically active form of retinoids (vitamin A and its derivatives). The spatiotemporal gradient of RA is essential for the regulation of cell proliferation and the differentiation and organ development. There are three stereoisomeric forms of retinoic acid: RETINOATE, CPD-13549, and CPD-13548, which show different binding affinities to the retinoic acid receptors. RETINOATE is known to bind exclusively to the nuclear retinoic acid receptors (RARs), whereas CPD-13549 binds to both RARs and retinoid x-receptors (RXRs). CPD-13548 is naturally generated in the body, yet it has a 100-fold lower affinity to RARs than RETINOATE or CPD-13549, and it does not bind to RXRs.

name: a short-chain 2,3,4-saturated fatty acid
annotation: Fatty-Acids "A fatty acid" is a carboxylic acid with a long unbranched aliphatic tail. This class describes Fatty-Acids "fatty acids" that do not contain any double bonds in the 2, 3, and 4 positions. The compounds in this class are often classified by the length of their aliphatic chains. Short-Chain-234-Saturated-Fatty-Acids "Short-chain 2,3,4-saturated fatty acids" are 2,3,4-saturated fatty acids with aliphatic chains of less than 6 carbons. Medium-Chain-234-Saturated-Fatty-Acids "Medium-chain 2,3,4-saturated fatty acids" are 2,3,4-saturated fatty acids with aliphatic chain of 6-12 carbons. Long-Chain-234-Saturated-Fatty-Acids "Long-chain 2,3,4-saturated fatty acids" are 2,3,4-saturated fatty acids with aliphatic chains of 13-22 carbons. Very-Long-Chain-234-Saturated-FAs "Very-long-chain 2,3,4-saturated fatty acids" are 2,3,4-saturated fatty acids with aliphatic chains of 23 carbons or longer.

name: a lignin
annotation: Lignins Lignin is a complex phenolic biopolymer and an integral part of the secondary cell walls of plants and some algae. It is one of the most abundant organic polymers on Earth, exceeded only by CELLULOSE, and constitutes from a quarter to a third of the dry mass of wood. During the formation of the secondary cell wall, lignin fills the spaces in the cell wall between the CELLULOSE, Hemicelluloses, and Pectin pectin components, driving out water and strengthening the wall. Lignin is heterogeneous, and lacks a defined primary structure. The illustration below is meant to provide a general concept rather than describe an exact structure. The main basic monomers (known as monolignols) are the phenylpropanoids COUMARYL-ALCOHOL, CONIFERYL-ALCOHOL, and SINAPYL-ALCOHOL (see PWY-361). The composition and relative abundance of the lignols differ among different organisms. The monolignols are glycosylated for the purpose of transport to the lignification zone, but the glucose moiety is removed prior to polymerization (see PWY-83). The biosynthesis of lignin is not well understood. It consists of radical-radical coupling, and is catalyzed by oxidative enzymes (peroxidases and/or laccases). Lignin is covalently linked to the hemicellulose fibers, and thus crosslinks different components of the cell wall, conferring mechanical strength. Lignin can be degraded by certain fungi and bacteria that produce the enzymes MANGANESE-PEROXIDASE-RXN "manganese peroxidase", RXN-17136 "lignin peroxidase" and CELLOBIOSE-DEHYDROGENASE-ACCEPTOR-RXN "cellobiose dehydrogenase".

name: an arabinan
annotation: The arabinans are branched polysaccharides with a backbone of α-(1,5)-linked arabinofuranosyl residues with α-(1,2)- and α-(1,3)-linked arabinofuranosyl side chains. Pectin Pectins with attached arabinans can be isolated from many fruits and vegetables such as apples, sugar beet, apricots, carrots, cabbage, onion and pears.

name: dermatan sulfate containing 4-O-sulfo-N-acetyl-D-galactosamine and L-iduronate 2-O-sulfate-[core protein]
annotation: Dermatan-Sulfate "Dermatan sulfate" is a sulfated Glycosaminoglycans "glycosaminoglycan" composed of repeating disaccharide units of variable composition connected by a β-(1→4) linkage. The dermatan polymer is attached to proteins via a linker region. Dermatan sulfate is expressed in many mammalian tissues and is the predominant glycan present in skin. Dermatan and dermatan sulfate proteoglycans have also been implicated in cardiovascular disease, tumorigenesis, infection, wound repair, and fibrosis . The most common disaccharide unit in dermatan sulfate is composed of CPD-12515 and CPD-12557, connected by a β-(1→3) linkage. The two residues are often sulfated to CPD-12518, CPD-12516, CPD-12565 or CPD-12526. Despite the fact that dermatan sulfate has been referred to as "chondroitin sulfate B", it differs from Chondroitin-sulfates by the presence of CPD-12515 (although some of the disaccharide units may contain CPD-12521 as in chondroitin sulfate) and is not classified as such any more.

name: short heparan sulfate fragments
annotation: Heparan sulfate is a member of the Glycosaminoglycans "glycosaminoglycan" family. It consists of a variably sulfated repeating disaccharide unit which is composed of a hexuronic acid and a hexosamine. The most common repeating unit, which makes up around 50% of the total disaccharide units in heparan sulfate, is composed of CPD-12521 linked to CPD-12541. Heparan sulfate can be cleaved by the enzyme heparanase to short fragments .

name: a cyclitol
annotation: Cyclitols are cyclic polyols - cycloalkanes containing at least one hydroxyl group on three or more ring atoms.

name: a neoagaro-oligosaccharide
annotation: In the field of oligosaccharides derived from Agaroses agarose, Carrageenans carrageenans, etc., in which alternate residues are 3,6-anhydro sugars, the prefix neo designates an oligosaccharide whose non-reducing end is the anhydro sugar, and the absence of this prefix means that it is not.

name: a [glycerolipid]-α-parinarate
annotation: CPD-17408 α-Parinarate is a conjugated polyunsaturated fatty acid with 18 carbon atoms and 4 conjugated double bonds. It occurs naturally in the seeds of the makita tree (ORG-6271), which is found in Fiji and other Pacific islands. Makita seeds contain about 46% of CPD-17408, and 34% of another conjugated fatty acid, CPD-8231. CPD-17408 α-Parinarate is also found in the seed oil of TAX-63779, where it makes about 30% of the seed oil. Because of its fluorescent properties, α-parinarate is commonly used as a molecular probe in the study of biomembranes. Like other fatty acids, CPD-17408 is rarely found in the cell in its free form. It is usually CPD-17410 "incorporated into glycerolipids".

name: a protopanaxadiol-type ginsenoside with two glucosyl residues at position 20
annotation: Ginsenosides Ginsenosides or panaxosides are a class of steroid glycosides and triterpene saponins found exclusively in the plant genus TAX-4053 (ginseng). They are the principal components responsible for the pharmaceutical activities of ginseng. Based on the structure of their aglycones, ginsenosides have been categorized into three broad types; Protopanaxadiol-type-ginsenosides "protopanaxadiol-type ginsenosides" (PPD, containing hydroxyl groups at positions 3 and 20), Protopanaxatriol-type-ginsenosides "protopanaxatriol-type ginsenosides" (PPT, containing hydroxyl groups at positions 3, 6 and 20), and Oleanonic-acid-type-ginsenosides "oleanonic-acid-type saponins".

name: a semiquinone
annotation: A semiquinone is a free radical resulting from the removal of one hydrogen atom with its electron during the process of dehydrogenation of Reduced-Quinones to Quinones or, alternatively, the addition of a single hydrogen atom to Quinones. Quinones are organic compounds derived from aromatic compounds, with two carbonyl groups incorporated into the ring structure with rearrangement of the double bonds where necessary. Quinones are conjugated but not aromatic. A classic example of a quinone is P-BENZOQUINONE. Quinones are commonly named with a prefix that indicates the parent aromatic hydrocarbon (for example, "benzo-" for benzene, "naphtho-" for naphthalene, and "anthra-" for anthracene) with a "-quinone" suffix. Several types of quinones serve as electron acceptors in electron transport chains. Quinones can carry two electrons, and when fully reduced they are referred to as Reduced-Quinones quinols. The addition of a single H atom to a quinone results in formation of a free radical called Semiquinones. Many quinone derivatives often have other biological activities, such as CPD-11501 "vitamin K".

name: a castasterone-sulfate
annotation: Sulfated brassinosteroids, including castasterone-sulfate can be produced in vitro, but the single sulfate group present on these compounds may be added at one of several different positions , represented by "R" groups on this compound. Brassinosteroid sulfates have not been detected in vivo in plants to date, but, there is some indirect evidence for their existence .

name: a monolactam
annotation: This compound class stands for Lactams lactams in which the amide bond is contained within a four-membered ring, which includes the amide nitrogen and the carbonyl carbon. β-Lactam compounds imitate the naturally occurring D-ALA-D-ALA substrate for the group of enzymes known as penicillin binding proteins (PBPs), which cross-link the peptidoglycan in bacterial cell wall, and thus act as powerful antibiotics . Naturally occurring Beta-Lactams "β-lactam antibiotics" are classified into several groups according to their chemical structure: In Monolactams monolactams the β-lactam ring is not fused to another ring. This class includes compounds such as the Nocardicins nocardicins and the Monobactams monobactams . In Penams penams the β-lactam ring is fused to a saturated five atom ring (a 4:5 structure). Penams contain a sulfur atom at position 1 of the five atom ring (for example, the PENICILLIN penicillins) . Related classes include the Carbapenams carbapenams, which contain a carbon atom in that position , and the Clavams oxopenams (also known as clavams), which contain an oxygen atom at that position (e.g. CPD-9261) . Penems Penems are molecues similar to penams in which the five atom ring is unsaturated at positions 2-3. Similarly, penems contain a sulfur atom at position 1 of the five atom ring, while Carbapenems carbapenems contains a carbon atom in that position . In Cephams cephams and Cephems cephems the β-lactam ring is fused to a six-atom ring (a 4:6 strcuture) that contains a sulfur atom at position 1. In cephams the ring is saturated, while cephems have an unsaturated bond at positions 3-4 . 1-Oxocephams/1-oxacephems conain an oxygen atom at position 1 .

name: an O-acyl-L-carnitine
annotation: This class contains DL-CARNITINE molecules esterified at the C3 hydroxyl to a wide range of acyl groups, including acetyl, or short, medium, or long chain fatty acyl groups . The general role of acylcarnitines is to transport acyl-groups (organic acids and fatty acids) from the cytoplasm into the mitochondria so that they can be broken down to produce energy.

name: a 6-phosphogluco-3-phosphogluco-amylopectin
annotation: This compound class represents the CPD-7043 component of native starch grains that are phosphorylated on the C6 hydroxyl group of one or more glucosyl residue and on the C3 hydroxyl group of one or more glucosyl residue in one or more of the α-glucan chains. This modification is likely to lead to solubilization of the starch . Starch is a combination of two polymeric carbohydrates (polysaccharides) called Alpha-Amyloses and CPD-7043. Alpha-Amyloses contains up to several thousand α-glucosyl units linked almost exclusively in linear α(1->4) linkages, with very few branches of α(1->6) linkages. In CPD-7043, on the other hand, branching is common, and occurs approximately every 24-30 glucose monomer units. Alpha-Amyloses accounts for 30% of starch, while CPD-7043 accounts for the other 70%.

name: a jasmonate or derivative
annotation: The Jasmonates jasmonates are a group of important phytohormones, structurally similar to animal Prostaglandins prostaglandins and ubiquitous in the plant kingdom. Many forms exist, based on the structure of Jasmonic-Acids "Jasmonate" (jasmonic acid, JA). The jasmonates act as regulatory molecules in many developmental processes that include fertility, sex determination, root elongation and fruit ripening. They are also potent signals activating plant defences against pathogens, herbivory, wounding and abiotic stress. The jasmonate regulation system includes the COI1 receptor protein and several JAZ (JAsmonate ZIM domain) repressors, which, in the absence of jasmonate, are bound to the MYC2 transcription factors, preventing transcription. In the presence of active jasmonate molecules, COI1 interacts with the JAZ repressors, causing their PWY-7511 ubiquitylation. The ubiquitylated repressors are targeted for proteasome degradation, releasing the MYC2 transcription factors and activating transcription . Only two of the four stereoisomers of jasmonate occur naturally: CPD-731 and CPD-734 . These two forms are present in equilibrium within cells and can interconvert spontaneously through epimerization . The exact ratio of these stereoisomers within a cell has not been conclusively determined, and it may vary based on species, cell type, and physiological state. But, in the systems tested so far, CPD-734 is found at higher levels than CPD-731 . The biological activity of these stereoisomers can be quite different . In addition, enzymes involved in JA metabolism may have different preferences for the different stereoisomers . This master class includes a large set of cyclopentanone derivatives of Jasmonic-Acids "jasmonate". Confusingly, these compounds are often referred to c

annotation: This compound is an intermediate in the biosynthesis of the CPD-13839. In nodule bacteria, the production of Exopolysaccharides exopolysacchrides (EPS) is necessary for the nodulation of legumes such as Medicago, Pisum, Trifolium, Leucaena, and Vicia spp., which form indeterminate-type nodules. TAX-1041145 synthesizes an acidic EPS, which is necessary to establish nitrogen-fixing symbiosis with Pisum sativum, Vicia faba, and Vicia sativa. This EPS is a polymer of an octasaccharide repeating unit consisting of a backbone of two glucose and two glucuronic acid residues, a side chain of three glucose and one galactose residues. The octasaccharide is modified by two pyruvyl, two nonstoichiometric O-acetyl, and one nonstoichiometric hydroxybutanoyl groups. EPSs with a similar structure are synthesized by a number of other strains of TAX-384.

name: a mycolipanolate
annotation: A unique feature of mycobacterial cell wall lipids is the presence of very long chain fatty acids with multiple methyl branches toward the carboxyl end of the carbon chain. Many types of such acids exist, including Mycosanoic-Acids "mycosanoic acids", Mycolipanoic-Acids "mycolipanoic acids", Mycolipanolic-Acids "mycolipanolic acids", Mycolipenic-Acids "mycolipenic acids", Mycolipodienoic-Acids "mycolipodienoic acids", Phthioceranic-Acids "phthioceranic acids", and Mycocerosic-Acids "mycocerosic acids". Mycolipanolic acids are mono-hydroxylated, 2,4,6-tri-methylated alkanoic long chain fatty acids. They differ from Mycolipanoic-Acids "mycolipanic acids" only by the presence of the hydroxyl group .

name: a phthiocerol B
annotation: Phthiocerols Phthiocerol-based lipids and glycolipids have been found in many species of TAX-1762, including TAX-1773, TAX-1765, TAX-33894, TAX-1781, TAX-1768, TAX-1777, TAX-1769 and TAX-1809 . Phthiocerols comprise a linear carbohydrate molecule to which one methoxyl group, one methyl group, and two secondary hydroxyl groups are attached. Two types of phthiocerols have been described. In Phthiocerol-A "phthiocerol A" the methoxyl and methyl groups are attached at positions 3 and 4, respectively, and the hydroxyl groups are at positions 9 and 11. In Phthiocerol-B "phthiocerol B" a methyl group is missing from the beginning of the molecule, and thus the methoxyl and methyl groups are attached at positions 2 and 3, respectively, and the hydroxyl groups are at positions 8 and 10. The two hydroxyl groups serve as attachment points for fatty acids, in a manner analogous to the much more ubiquitous sn-glycerol. The phthiocerol molecules have a range of 28 to 34 carbon atoms in the main chain, indicating a promiscuity of the enzymes involved in their biosynthesis. Longer molecules have additional carbon atoms added at the ω end of the molecule. In most organisms the two hydroxyl groups are esterified by Mycocerosic-Acids "mycocerosic acids". In TAX-1781 and TAX-1809, however, they are esterified by Phthioceranic-Acids "phthioceranic acids" .

name: a [cullin]-L-lysine
annotation: Cullins Cullins are a family of proteins that provide a scaffold for some RING-type E3 ubiquitin transferases (EC-2.3.2.27 "EC 2.3.2.27"), which are not able to bind a substrate protein directly. These E3 proteins form a complex with a cullin protein and a substrate recognition subunit (SRS). Some complexes also include an adaptor that links the SRS to the complex. The complexes are known as CRLs for Cullin-RING ubiquitin Ligases. CRLs comprise the largest known category of ubiquitin ligases, and regulate an extensive number of dynamic cellular processes, including multiple aspects of the cell cycle, transcription, signal transduction, and development . There are five major types of cullins in metazoa, each of which forms a distinct class of CRL complex, with distinct adaptors and/or substrate-recognition subunits. CRLs are activated by the covalent attachment of the ubiquitin-like protein NEDD8 NEDD8 to the cullin, and are inhibited by binding to the CAND1 inhibitor . The human genome contains eight cullin genes.

name: a mycosanoate
annotation: A unique feature of mycobacterial cell wall lipids is the presence of very long chain fatty acids with multiple methyl branches toward the carboxyl end of the carbon chain. Many types of such acids exist, including Mycosanoic-Acids "mycosanoic acids", Mycolipanoic-Acids "mycolipanoic acids", Mycolipanolic-Acids "mycolipanolic acids", Mycolipenic-Acids "mycolipenic acids", Mycolipodienoic-Acids "mycolipodienoic acids", Phthioceranic-Acids "phthioceranic acids", and Mycocerosic-Acids "mycocerosic acids". Mycosanoic acids are 2,4-dimethylated long-chain alkanoic acids with a size range of C21-C25 . The name has been originally proposed in .

name: a B (Lewis b) histo-blood group epitope
annotation: The Lewis blood group antigens, discovered in 1946, are biosynthetically and structurally related carbohydrate structures used as markers of cell differentiation and embryonic development, based on fucosylation carried by a number of fucosyltransferases. The system is named after Mrs. H. D. G. Lewis, in whose serum the first member of the family was discovered . However, the presence of the Lewis antigens is not limited to erytrocytes, and they are found in different tissues and organs. One of these fucosyltransferases, encoded by the HS10249 gene, is bifunctional and can fucosylate both the 3 and 4 positions of the penultimate sugar in the precursor disaccharide. The gene, also known as the Lewis gene, has a dominant allele (which was named Le) and a recessive non-functional allele (known as le). The α(1,4)-fucosyltransferase activity is only relevant when the substrates are derived from the beta-Gal-13-beta-GlcNac-R "type 1 precursor disaccharide", as that position is already occupied in the B-Gal-14-NacGlc-R "type 2 disaccharide". The first Lewis antigens to be discovered were the Lewis-a-epitope "Lewis a" and Antigen-Leb "Lewis b" antigens, which are produced from beta-Gal-13-beta-GlcNac-R "type 1 precursor disaccharides" (see PWY-7832). However, the α(1,3) fucosylation of the N-acetylglucosaminyl residue of B-Gal-14-NacGlc-R leads to formation of several related antigens known as the CPD-8594 "Lewis x epitope" (also known as SSEA-1, for stage-specific embryonic antigen 1), Antigen-Ley "Lewis y epitope", and the sLea-Antigen "sialyl-Lewis x epitope" (see PWY-7831).

name: a mycobacterial arabinogalactan
annotation: Arabinogalactan Arabinogalactans are biopolymers consisting mostly of arabinose and galactose residues. Two main classes of arabinogalactans are found in nature: Plant-Arabinogalactans "plant arabinogalactan" and Mycobacterial-Arabinogalactans "microbial arabinogalactan". The Mycobacterial-Arabinogalactans "microbial arabinogalactan" consists of arabinose and galactose monosaccharides in their furanose configurations and is a major structural component of the mycobacterial cell wall, where it is covalently attached to the peptidoglycan. The galactan portion of the polymer is linear, consisting of approximately 30 units with alternating β-(1-5) and β-(1-6) glycosidic linkages. Arabinans Arabinan chains are attached at three branch points within the galactan chain (believed to be at residues 8, 10 and 12) . These chains consist of about 30 residues . The arabinan portion of the polymer is a complex branched structure with glycosidic linkages of α-(1-3), β-(1-5). The chains are capped by a D-arabinofuranose residue attached by a β-(1-2) linkage. In the Mycobacterial cell wall, the arabinan portion is usually capped with mycolic acids that are attached to the β-(1-2) linked residue. Arabinogalactan molecules in slow-growing mycobacterial strains possess a single galactosamine (GalN) residue attached via an α-linkage to a highly branched arabinofuranose residue in the arabinan core .

Mutation of the G185E-7207 gene of TAX-1773 results in accumulation of Phthiodiolenones phthiodiolenone, which contains an unsaturated bond between C-4 and C-5 . Two types of phthiodiolenone exist. In Phthiodiolenone-A "phthiodiolenone A" the oxo and the methyl groups are attached at positions 3 and 4, respectively, and the hydroxyl groups are at positions 9 and 11. In Phthiodiolenone-B "phthiodiolenone B" a methyl group is missing from the beginning of the molecule, and thus the oxo and methyl groups are attached at positions 2 and 3, respectively, and the hydroxyl groups are at positions 8 and 10.

annotation: This class of triacylglycerols contains three acyl chains formed by the esterification of glycerol with three fatty acids. The names of triacylglycerol compound classes provide information about the three acyl chains of each member of the class, using the nomenclature TAG(a:b/y:z) where "a" represents the number of carbons found in one acyl chain and "b" represents the number of desaturations present in chain "a" and where "y" represents the total number of carbons found in the other two acyl chains and "z" represents the total number of desaturations present in the two chains included in "y." Therefore, in this TAG(18:3/34:2) class of compounds, one acyl chain has 18 carbons and three double bonds. The other two acyl chains have a total of 34 carbons and two double bonds. The attachment sites of the acyl chains at the R1 (sn-1), R2 (sn-2), or R3 (sn-3) position along the glyceryl backbone may vary among class members and cannot be inferred from their order in the compound class name. Additionally, the location and orientation of the double bonds in the unsaturated acyl chain(s) may vary among members of the class and are not specified by this notation.

name: a mucin-type O-glycan
annotation: O-Linked glycans are polysaccharide or oligosaccharide that are linked to a protein via the oxygen atom in the side chain of an SER or THR residue. In the structure below, R1 is hydrogen when the modified amino acid is SER and carbon when the amino acid is THR. In eukaryotes, O-linked glycans are assembled in the Golgi apparatus one sugar at a time, while attached to the core protein. Examples for O-linked glycoproteins include glycophorin, Mucin mucin, notch, thrombospondin, Factor-VII "factor VII" and Factor-IX "factor IX". O-Linked glycans are polysaccharide or oligosaccharide that are linked to a protein via the oxygen atom in the side chain of a SER or THR residue. The most common O-glycosylation in eukaryotic organisms is mucin-type O-glycosylation, consisting of glycans attached via O-linked CPD-3604, although O-linkages to fucose, glucose, mannose, xylose and other sugars are known. Eight core-type structures of mucin-type O-glycans have been proposed, including Core1 "core 1", Core2 "core 2", GLUCOSAMINYL-13-N-ETCETERA-GALACTOSAMINY "core 3", GLUCOSAMINYL-16-ETCETERA-GALACTOSAMINYL- "core 4", Core5 "core 5", Core6 "core 6", Core7 "core 7" and Core8 "core 8" .

name: a sesquarterpenoid
annotation: Terpenoids Terpenoids make up a very large family of natural products containing more than 50,000 structurally diverse compounds. They are categorized by the number of C5 isoprene units as follows: Hemiterpenes Hemiterpenoids (one C5 unit), Monoterpenes monoterpenoids (C10; two C5 units), Sesquiterpenes sesquiterpenoids (C15; three C5 units), Diterpenes diterpenoids (C20; four C5 units), Sesterterpenes sesterterpenoids (C25; five C5 units), Triterpenes triterpenoids (C30; six C5 units), C35-Terpenes "sesquarterpenoids" (C35; seven C5 units), and Tetraterpenes tetraterpenoids (C40; eight C5 units).

name: [heparin]-α-N-sulfo-D-glucosamine
annotation: HEPARIN "Heparin" is a polymer mainly composed of a repeating trisulfated disaccharide unit composed of CPD-12518 and CPD-12533 linked by a (1->4) bond (often referred to as IdoA(2S)-(1->4)-GlcNS(6S)). Consecutive glycosidic linkages formed between C1 of CPD-12533 in one base unit and C4 of CPD-12518 of another unit result in the formation of the heparin polymer. This compound stand for N-sulfo-D-glucosamine within the context of HEPARIN, and not at the terminal non-reducing end.

name: [heparin]-α-N-acetyl-D-glucosamine 3-O-sulfate
annotation: HEPARIN "Heparin" is a polymer mainly composed of a repeating trisulfated disaccharide unit composed of CPD-12518 and CPD-12533 linked by a (1->4) bond (often referred to as IdoA(2S)-(1->4)-GlcNS(6S)). Consecutive glycosidic linkages formed between C1 of CPD-12533 in one base unit and C4 of CPD-12518 of another unit result in the formation of the heparin polymer. This compound stand for CPD-12567 within the context of HEPARIN, and not at the terminal non-reducing end..

name: a carbapenem
annotation: This compound class stands for Lactams lactams in which the amide bond is contained within a four-membered ring, which includes the amide nitrogen and the carbonyl carbon. β-Lactam compounds imitate the naturally occurring D-ALA-D-ALA substrate for the group of enzymes known as penicillin binding proteins (PBPs), which cross-link the peptidoglycan in bacterial cell wall, and thus act as powerful antibiotics . Naturally occurring Beta-Lactams "β-lactam antibiotics" are classified into several groups according to their chemical structure: In Monolactams monolactams the β-lactam ring is not fused to another ring. This class includes compounds such as the Nocardicins nocardicins and the Monobactams monobactams . In Penams penams the β-lactam ring is fused to a saturated five atom ring (a 4:5 structure). Penams contain a sulfur atom at position 1 of the five atom ring (for example, the PENICILLIN penicillins) . Related classes include the Carbapenams carbapenams, which contain a carbon atom in that position , and the Clavams oxopenams (also known as clavams), which contain an oxygen atom at that position (e.g. CPD-9261) . Penems Penems are molecues similar to penams in which the five atom ring is unsaturated at positions 2-3. Similarly, penems contain a sulfur atom at position 1 of the five atom ring, while Carbapenems carbapenems contains a carbon atom in that position . In Cephams cephams and Cephems cephems the β-lactam ring is fused to a six-atom ring (a 4:6 strcuture) that contains a sulfur atom at position 1. In cephams the ring is saturated, while cephems have an unsaturated bond at positions 3-4 . 1-Oxocephams/1-oxacephems conain an oxygen atom at position 1 .

name: cellulose polysulfate
annotation: Cellulose polysulfate, reported from the large marine gastropod TAX-500097, contains glucose residues sulfated at positions 6 and 2 and/or 3 .

name: a plant soluble heteroglycan
annotation: This class of soluble heteroglycans has been identified in Arabidopsis plants and is involved in the cytosolic metabolism of maltose released from the chloroplast during starch degradation . Various subfractions of this broad class of water-soluble heteoglycans have been identified in various plant species including Arabidopsis and Pisum sativum (peas) and their sugar composition, sugar linkage profile and degree of branching have been characterized in various organs at different times of day .

name: an alkylated nucleobase
annotation: Nucleobases are the parts of nucleic acids (DNA and RNA) that are involved in pairing of the two strands. The primary nucleobases are CYTOSINE, GUANINE, ADENINE (found in both DNA and RNA), THYMINE (found only in DNA) and URACIL (found only in RNA), abbreviated as C, G, A, T, and U, respectively. There are many additional nucleobases, usually derived from the primary ones. Based on their structure, nucleobases are divided into Purines purines and Pyrimidine-Bases pyrimidines.

name: a long-chain alkane
annotation: Alkanes are saturated acyclic hydrocarbons. They consist only of hydrogen and saturated carbon atoms and are bonded exclusively by single bonds. They could be either branched or unbranched. Linear alkanes are often classified by the length of their acyl moiety. Short-Chain-Alkanes "Short-chain alkanes" contain less than 6- carbons. Medium-Chain-Alkanes "Medium-chain alkanes" contain 6-12 carbons. Long-Chain-Alkanes "Long-chain alkanes" contain 13-22 carbons. Very-Long-Chain-Alkanes "Very-long-chain alkanes" contain 23 carbons or longer.

annotation: This compound class stands for generic and unspecified DNA 6-O-methylguanines.

name: a coelenterazine dioxetanone
annotation: OPLOPHORUS-LUCIFERIN Coelenterazine and its analogues are the luciferase substrate (=luciferin) in the light-emitting process of bioluminescent coelenterates as well as various, distantly related, types of organisms such as squids, shrimps, fishes, and some chaetognaths . It is also found in many nonluminous organisms . In bioluminescent systems, OPLOPHORUS-LUCIFERIN coelenterazine is bound by either Coelenterazine-binding-proteins or by an aequorin protein, depending on the species. In the first system, upon binding of Ca2+ ions the coelenterazine-binding protein releases coelenterazine, which is then oxidized and decarboxylated by the luciferase enzyme, resulting in CPD-20235. In the second system, aequorin binds and oxidizes coelenterazine, retainning it in the form of bound CPD-20222. Upon binding of Ca2+ ions, the enzyme catalyzes the decarboxylation and formation of CPD-20235. In vitro the excited coelenteramide relaxes to the ground state by emission of blue light. However, in vivo the excitation energy is transferred to GFP by Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) . Coelenterazine is a derivative of imidazopyrazinone. Its biosynthetic origin is not known. However, it has been shown that the hydromedusa TAX-6100 is not able to synthesize it, and is dependent on a dietary supply of this luciferin for bioluminescence . The only report of de novo biosynthesis of coelenterazine is from the bioluminescent shrimp TAX-576189 .

annotation: This compound class stands for all D-amino acids.

name: a keratan sulfate
annotation: Keratan sulfate is the name given to any of several sulfated Glycosaminoglycans "glycosaminoglycans" that have been found, especially in the cornea, cartilage, and bone. It is a linear polymer that consists of a repeating disaccharide unit. The basic repeating disaccharide unit is [3)-β-Gal-(1→4)-β-GlcNAc-(1→]. Either of the GALACTOSE and N-ACETYL-BETA-D-GLUCOSAMINE residues may be sulfated at carbon position 6.

name: an organometalloidal compound
annotation: Organometalloidal compounds are compounds that have bonds between one or more metalloid atoms and one or more carbon atoms of an organyl group.

name: a [chondroitin-sulfate] containing 4-O-sulfo-N-acetylgalactosamine
annotation: Chondroitin-sulfates "Chondroitin sulfate" is a glycosaminoglycan found mostly attached to proteins as part of a proteoglycan. Chondroitin sulfate is composed of repeating disaccharide units composed of a hexuronic acid and a hexose sugar, specifically CPD-12521 and CPD-12557 linked by β(1 -> 3) linkages. Both units are often sulfated. This compound stand for CPD-12557 sulfated at the 4' position within the context of Chondroitin-sulfates.

name: an aminated amino group donor
annotation: Amine-Donors "An amino group donor" is a compound that can donate an amino group to another compound in a reaction catalyzed by enzymes that belong to the EC-2.6.1 transaminase group" (often called aminotransferases). Prior to donating the amino group the compound is Aminated-Amine-Donors and after donating it the compound is Deaminated-Amine-Donors. Since tranmination reactions are reversible, the distinction between an amino group donor and an amino group acceptor is not determined by the actual chemical entity, but by the role it plays in a particular reaction. However, some compounds, such as GLT and L-ALPHA-ALANINE, are often used as amino group donors by the aminotransferases in reactions that are physiologically unidirectional. This particular entry is used when the identity of the amino group donor (or acceptor) is not known or cannot be defined.

name: a flavanone
annotation: Flavanones are Flavanoids flavans with a keto group at position 4. They are similar to Flavones but lack the double bond between carbons 2 and 3. Thus, while CPD-8485 is 2-phenylchromen-4-one, CPD-2043 flavanone is 2,3-dihydro-2-phenylchromen-4-one.

name: a K vitamin
annotation: Vitamin K is a name given to a group of derivatives of 2-methyl-1,4-naphthoquinone that are required by mammals for the posttranslational modification of certain proteins, mostly involved in blood coagulation. These compounds, while essential for mammals, are not synthesized by them, and are thus considered vitamins. The natural forms of the vitamin are vitamin K1 (2-METHYL-3-PHYTYL-14-NAPHTHOQUINONE) and vitamin K2 (Menaquinones). Since menaquinones produced by different organisms have different tail lenghts, there are many variations of vitamin K2, usually specified based on the number of carbons in their tails, such as in Vitamin K2(45). Menaquinones are synthesized by gut bacteria, and are absorbed into the blood. Thus, dietary deficiency is extremely rare in healthy individuals. There are also several synthetic forms- vitamin K3 through K9. Vitamin K3 (CPD-3766) is the most active of the synthetic forms.

name: a plant arabinogalactan
annotation: Arabinogalactans are biopolymers consisting of arabinose and galactose residues. Two main classes of arabinogalactans are found in nature: Plant-Arabinogalactans "plant arabinogalactan" and Mycobacterial-Arabinogalactans "microbial arabinogalactan". Plant-Arabinogalactans "Plant arabinogalactans" (AGs) are widely spread throughout the plant kingdom. Many edible and inedible plants are rich sources of these polysaccharides. AGs are associated with the pectin cell-wall component by physical bonds, and some of them are covalently linked to the complex pectin molecule as neutral side chains . AGs occur in two structurally different forms described as Plant-Arabinogalactans-I "type I" and Plant-Arabinogalactans-II "type II". AG of type I has a linear (1→4)-β-D-Galp backbone, bearing 20-40% of α-L-Araf residues (1→5)-linked in short chains, in general at position 3. It is commonly found in pectins from citrus, apple and potato. Type I AG has been isolated from the skin of Opuntia ficus indica pear fruits . AG of type II, known as arabino-3,6-galactan, has a (1→3)-β-D-Galp backbone heavily substituted at position 6 by mono- and oligosaccharide side chains composed of arabinosyl and galactosyl units. It is more widespread than the AG type I and occurs in cell walls of dicots and cereals often linked to proteins (known as arabinogalactan proteins). AG type II, along with galactomannans and cellulose, comprises the predominating polysaccharides of green arabica coffee beans. In some case type II AGt contains a small amount of β-glucuronate. AG type II occurs as minor, water-soluble components in softwoods. It is very abundant in the wood of larch trees (members of the genus TAX-3325) - certain parts of the western larch (TAX-3327) contain up to 35% AG . However, larch AG type II

name: an aryl-lactate
annotation: Aryl-lactates are metabolites derived from gut microbes that impact human physiology, including growth, development, and immune function. The aryl-lactates CPD-5921, CPD-2222, and CPD-21492 are derived from PHE, TYR and TRP, respectively, via the enzymes EC-2.6.1.57, and microbial EC-1.1.1.110 . Aryl-lactates are present in nearly all fermented foods . Evidence suggests that they can bind immunomodulatory receptors, including the human hydroxycarboxylic acid receptor 3 (HCAR3), and aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AhR) .

name: a 5-methyl-CpG site
annotation: The CpG sites or CG sites are regions of DNA where a cytosine nucleotide occurs 5' to a guanine nucleotide. "CpG" is shorthand for "-C-phosphate-G-", that is, cytosine and guanine separated by a single phosphate group. The "CpG" notation is used to distinguish this linear sequence from the CG base-pairing of cytosine and guanine. Cytosines in CpG dinucleotides are often methylated to form 5-methylcytosine. In mammals, 70% to 80% of CpG cytosines are methylated .

name: SHGLI
aliases: a soluble heteroglycan fraction LI (plants)|a soluble heteroglycan LI
annotation: This class of soluble heteroglycans has been identified in Arabidopsis plants and is involved in the cytosolic metabolism of maltose released from the chloroplast during starch degradation . In the "L" fraction obtained from Arabidopsis leaves, the SHG is composed of fucose, arabinose, rhamnose, galactose, glucose, xylose, and mannose with galactose and arabinose making up the bulk of the glycan . Compared to subfraction LII, LI heteroglycans are less branched but have more complex linkages, such as various glucosyl, mannosyl and fucosyl linkages .

name: a chondroitin sulfate disaccharide
annotation: The structure depicted here is that of the most common repeating disaccharide unit found in Chondroitin-sulfates, with both the hexose and the hexuronic acid in their sulfated form.

name: heparosan-[core protein]
annotation: This compound class describes a Heparosan polymer, attached to a serine residue in a glycoprotein, prior to any subsequent modifications. In animal tissues heparosan is synthesized attached to cell surface or extracellular matrix proteins, known as "core proteins". Heparosan consists of a repeating disaccharide unit composed of CPD-12521-(1,4)-CPD-12541. The disaccharide units are linked by a (1-4) linkage to each other. The polymer chain is attached to the core proteins via a tetrasaccharide linkage region that differs in composition from the rest of the polymer. The heparosan skeleton serves as a starting point in the biosynthesis of several other glycosaminoglycan such as Heparan-Sulfate and HEPARIN, and bacterial heparosan is sometimes used for the synthesis of non-animal sourced heparin-like products.

name: a phthiocerol
annotation: Phthiocerols Phthiocerol-based lipids and glycolipids have been found in many species of TAX-1762, including TAX-1773, TAX-1765, TAX-33894, TAX-1781, TAX-1768, TAX-1777, TAX-1769 and TAX-1809 . The name "phthiocerol" was given to the compound in 1936 . Phthiocerols comprise a linear carbohydrate molecule to which one methoxyl group, one methyl group, and two secondary hydroxyl groups are attached. Two types of phthiocerols have been described. In Phthiocerol-A "phthiocerol A" the methoxyl and methyl groups are attached at positions 3 and 4, respectively, and the hydroxyl groups are at positions 9 and 11. In Phthiocerol-B "phthiocerol B" a methyl group is missing from the beginning of the molecule, and thus the methoxyl and methyl groups are attached at positions 2 and 3, respectively, and the hydroxyl groups are at positions 8 and 10. The two hydroxyl groups serve as attachment points for fatty acids, in a manner analogous to the much more ubiquitous sn-glycerol. The phthiocerol molecules have a range of 28 to 34 carbon atoms in the main chain, indicating a promiscuity of the enzymes involved in their biosynthesis. Longer molecules have additional carbon atoms added at the ω end of the molecule. In most organisms the two hydroxyl groups are esterified by Mycocerosic-Acids "mycocerosic acids". In TAX-1781 and TAX-1809, however, they are esterified by Phthioceranic-Acids "phthioceranic acids" .

name: a rifamycin
annotation: The rifamycins are a group of antibiotics that are particularly effective against mycobacteria. They are used to treat tuberculosis, leprosy, and mycobacterium avium complex (MAC) infections. The rifamycins include both natural products such as RIFAMYCIN-B and synthetic derivatives such as CPD-18727.

name: a TAG(18:3/32:1)
annotation: This class of triacylglycerols contains three acyl chains formed by the esterification of glycerol with three fatty acids. The names of triacylglycerol compound classes provide information about the three acyl chains of each member of the class, using the nomenclature TAG(a:b/y:z) where "a" represents the number of carbons found in one acyl chain and "b" represents the number of desaturations present in chain "a" and where "y" represents the total number of carbons found in the other two acyl chains and "z" represents the total number of desaturations present in the two chains included in "y." Therefore, in this TAG(18:3/32:1) class of compounds, one acyl chain has 18 carbons and three double bonds. The other two acyl chains have a total of 32 carbons and one double bond. The attachment sites of the acyl chains at the R1 (sn-1), R2 (sn-2), or R3 (sn-3) position along the glyceryl backbone may vary among class members and cannot be inferred from their order in the compound class name. Additionally, the location and orientation of the double bonds in the unsaturated acyl chain(s) may vary among members of the class and are not specified by this notation.

name: a tetracycline
annotation: Tetracyclines are a subclass of polyketides that have an octahydrotetracene-2-carboxamide skeleton, substituted with hydroxy and other groups. Many of them are potent antibiotics.

name: an O-acyl-carnitine
annotation: This class contains DL-CARNITINE molecules esterified at the C3 hydroxyl to a wide range of acyl groups, including acetyl, or short, medium, or long chain fatty acyl groups . The general role of acylcarnitines is to transport acyl-groups (organic acids and fatty acids) from the cytoplasm into the mitochondria so that they can be broken down to produce energy.

name: an N1-methyladenine in RNA
annotation: Represents an N1-methyladenine lesion in a generic RNA species

name: an azobenzene
annotation: Azobenzenes share the core azobenzene structure, comprising two phenyl rings linked by a N=N double bond. The rings may have different functional groups.

name: tuberonic acid
annotation: 12-Hydroxyjasmonates "Tuberonic acid" (12-hydroxyjasmonate) was first described from potato and Solanaceous species for its tuber-inducing activity, hence its trivial name . However, it was later detected in many plant species, including TAX-3702, and is often associated with would response . At least two different stereoisomers, CPD-11260 and CPD-11253, exist in TAX-3702, where the former appears to be more abundant . The biological activity of tuberonic acid may differ between species. For example, it can induce tuber formation in potatoes, but prompt distinct sets of transcriptional changes in tomato .

name: a β-glucosyl-hydroxymethylcytosine in DNA
annotation: Phage T4 DNA differs from typical DNA molecules in that it contains no CYTOSINE. Instead, in contains the modified base CPD-15531 which pairs with GUANINE. The hydroxy group is often glucosylated, as in Alpha-Glycosylated-Hydroxymethyl-DNAs "α-glucosyl-hydroxymethylcytosine", Beta-Glycosylated-Hydroxymethylated-DNAs "β-glucosyl-hydroxymethylcytosine" and Gluc-Glycosyl-Hydroxymethylcytosine-DNAs "β-glucosyl-(1→4)-β-glucosyl-hydroxymethylcytosine", so that T4 DNA is typically covered with sugar chains.

name: a glycosylinositolphosphoceramide(40:0)-3
annotation: In this class of glycosylinositol phosphoceramides, a long carbon chain is attached at position R1 to form a fatty amide and a long carbon chain is attached at position R2 to form a long chain base. Either or both of these chains may contain one or more double bonds and may have hydroxyl groups attached. Based on the notation GIPC(m:n)-p, each compound within this class has a total of 40 carbons (m), zero double bonds (n), and three hydroxyl groups (p) present in the sphingoid base and the fatty amide. However, the length, hydroxylation position, double bond position, and double bond orientation of the groups attached at R2 and R3 will differ between individual members of this group of compounds.

annotation: Amino acid opines are any opines whose structure is based on the condensation of an amino acid with a carbohydrate or a keto-acid.

name: a single-stranded oligodeoxyribonucleotide
annotation: An oligodeoxyribonucleotide is a short nucleic acid polymer with 50 or fewer deoxyribonucleotides. Oligodeoxyribonucleotide can be either single- or double-stranded.

name: xanthan
annotation: Xanthan Xanthan is an exopolysaccharide produced by the bacterium TAX-339 and related species. Because of its rheological properties, including high viscosity and pseudo-plasticity, and stability in extreme conditions, xanthan gum has been used in food, agriculture and industry as an emulsifying, thickening or suspending agent. It was approved for use in foods in 1968. Xanthan gum has been implicated as the pathogenic substance causing black rot in crucifers . The polymer is composed of repeating pentasaccharide units with the structure of a β-1,4-linked D-glucose backbone with trisaccharide side chains composed of mannose-(β-1,4)-glucuronic acid-(β-1,2)-mannose attached to alternate glucose residues in the backbone by α-1,3 linkages . The terminal repeat unit is anchored to the cytoplasmic membrane by an undecaprenyldiphosphate lipid carrier, and assembley of the polymer occurs while anchored. Acetyl and pyruvyl residues are often added to one of the mannose units as non-carbohydrate decorations.

name: a [phospholipid]-sterculate
annotation: Like other fatty acids, STERCULATE is rarely found in its free form. It is usually found Lipid-sterculate "incorporated into a lipid".

name: an antimicrobial peptide
annotation: Antimicrobial peptides (AMPs) are a class of small peptides that widely exist in nature and form an important part of the innate immune system of different organisms. AMPs have a wide range of inhibitory effects against bacteria, fungi, parasites and viruses. While almost all AMPS are cationic, some anionic AMPs also exist. The AMPs have been classified based on several criteria, including their source, activity, structural characteristics, and amino acid composition. When classified based on sources, AMPs have been classidfied as originating from mammals, amphibians, microorganisms, and insects. When classified based on activity, AMPS are divided into antibacterial, antiviral, antifungal, antiparasitic, anti-human immunodeficiency virus (HIV), and anti-tumor peptides. When classified based on structure, AMPs have been divided into linear α-helical peptides, β-sheet peptides, linear extension structure, and both α-helix and β-sheet peptides. When classidied based on their composition, AMPs have been divided into proline-rich peptides, tryptophan- and arginine-rich antimicrobial peptides, histidine-rich peptides, and glycine-rich antimicrobial peptides. AMPs act by different mechanisms. The most common one is membrane-targeting, during which the AMPs generate a pore in the bacterial membrane. However, some AMPs enter the cell. where they can inhibit biosynthesis of proteins or nucleic acids, inhibit essential proteases, or interfere with cell division .

name: a macrocycle
annotation: A macrocycle is a cyclic macromolecule or a macromolecular cyclic portion of a molecule.

name: a steroidal estrogen
annotation: Estrogens are hormones that stimulate or control the development and maintenance of female sex characteristics in mammals by binding to estrogen receptors. They are important in both the menstrual and the estrous reproductive cycles. Estrogens are synthesized in all vertebrates as well as some insects, suggesting that estrogenic sex hormones have an ancient evolutionary history. The estrogens that occur naturally in the body are steroids, however other types of molecules acquired in the diet are known to bind to the estrogen receptors and are thus also considered estrogens. They include molecules produced by plants (phytoestrogens), molecules produced by fungi (mycoestrogens), and synthetic compounds (xenoestrogens). The main steroidal Estrogens estrogens in humans are ESTRONE, CPD-352 and ESTRIOL, which differ in the number of hydroxyl groups (one for ESTRONE, two for CPD-352 and three for ESTRIOL). CPD-352 is about 10 times as potent as ESTRONE and about 80 times as potent as ESTRIOL in its estrogenic effect. CPD-352 is the predominant estrogen in non-pregenant women during their reproductive years in terms of serum levels. ESTRONE Estrone is the predominant circulating estrogen in women during menopause and ESTRIOL is the predominant circulating estrogen during pregnancy.

name: a xylogalacturonan
annotation: Xylogalacturonan "Xylogalacturonan" is a substituted 1-4-alpha-D-galacturonosyl "homogalacturonan" in which the α-1,4-linked D-GALACTURONATE backbone is substituted at position O-3 with a BETA-D-XYLOSE. Occasionally the xylose is further substituted at position O-4 with an additional β linked xylose. The homogalacturonan backbone is partially methylesterified at the C-6 carboxyl, and may be O-acetylated at positions O-2 or O-3. Xylogalacturonan "Xylogalacturonan" has been reported to be abundant in the reproductive tissues of many plants. Recently it was reported to be present in vegetative tissues of Arabidopsis .

name: a [glycerolipid]-dicranin
annotation: CPD-12654 Dicranin is an unusual fatty acid containing a Δ6-triple bond. It is found in many moss species including TAX-3225 and TAX-3222.

name: a (1,4)-β-D-mannan
annotation: β-1,4-Mannans are major constitutents of hemicelluloses in plant tissue and are polymers composed of β(1→4)-linked mannose residues. Some contain galactopyranosyl side chains (see Galactomannans). Slightly galactosylated mannans (4% galactose), considered as linear β(1→4)-D-mannans, have been isolated from the seed endosperm of vegetable ivory nut (TAX-115507) and date (TAX-42345) . Note that there are several types of mannans. The proteins in yeast cell walls contain particularly complex N-glycans referred to as α-mannans in which the core N-glycan is extended by ~200 α-1,6-mannose units that are also decorated with α-linked mannose side chains . In some yeasts, such as the human pathogen TAX-5476, β-1,2-mannose residues cap the α-mannans side chains, and extended β-1,2-mannan chains are attached to the α-mannan via phosphate bridges . β-1,2-Mannans also fulfill an important storage role in TAX-5658 Leishmania parasites .

name: a TAG(18:2/40:1)
annotation: This class of triacylglycerols contains three acyl chains formed by the esterification of glycerol with three fatty acids. The names of triacylglycerol compound classes provide information about the three acyl chains of each member of the class, using the nomenclature TAG(a:b/y:z) where "a" represents the number of carbons found in one acyl chain and "b" represents the number of desaturations present in chain "a" and where "y" represents the total number of carbons found in the other two acyl chains and "z" represents the total number of desaturations present in the two chains included in "y." Therefore, in this TAG(18:2/40:1) class of compounds, one acyl chain has 18 carbons and two double bonds. The other two acyl chains have a total of 40 carbons and one double bond. The attachment sites of the acyl chains at the R1 (sn-1), R2 (sn-2), or R3 (sn-3) position along the glyceryl backbone may vary among class members and cannot be inferred from their order in the compound class name. Additionally, the location and orientation of the double bonds in the unsaturated acyl chain(s) may vary among members of the class and are not specified by this notation.

name: a retinal
annotation: RETINAL "All-trans-retinal" is a form of vitamin A. Retinal is a polyene chromophore, and bound to proteins called opsins, is the chemical basis of animal vision. Bound to proteins called Bacteriorhodopsins bacteriorhodopsins, retinal allows certain microorganisms to convert light into metabolic energy. Carnivorous vertebrate animals obtain retinal directly from dietary meat. Vegetarian animals produce retinal from one of four carotenoids: CPD1F-129, CPD1F-118, CPD1F-126, and CPD-7409, which they must obtain from plants or other photosynthetic organisms. Invertebrates such as insects and squid use hydroxylated forms of retinal in their visual systems, which they derive from from other xanthophylls.

name: a muraymycin
annotation: The structure shown here is the muraymycin core structure; R1 and R2 represent the variable fatty acid and amino sugar components respectively .

name: a pentose
annotation: A pentose sugar is a five-carbon monosaccharide thatin its linear form contains either an aldehyde group at position 1 (Aldopentoses) or a ketone group at position 2 (Ketopentoses).

name: a corrin
annotation: Corrins Corrin is a macrocycle related to the porphyrin ring. The 4 pyrrole subunits in a corrin are joined on opposite sides by a C-CH3 methylene link and on one side by a C-H methylene link, and two of the pyrroles are joined directly. Compared to a porphyrin, the corrin is missing a bridging methylene group between a pair of pyrroles. Corrinoids usually complex a cobalt atom, as seen in cobalamin. Compound that contain a corrin ring are called Corrinoids corrinoids.

name: a [glycerolipid] olefinic fatty acid
annotation: The structures shown here depicts a dec-9-enoate within a glyceroplipid. This compound was randomly selected to represent a member of this compound class.

name: a [myosin light-chain]-O-phospho-L-serine
family_key: sm:name:a myosin light chain o phospho l serine

name: an agarose
annotation: Agarans Agarans and Agaroses agaroses are sulfated polysaccharides that are extracted from sea weeds belonging to the TAX-2763 (red algae), mainly from TAX-2769, and species from the TAX-31444 and TAX-33664 genera. The basic units of Agarans agarans are repeats of β -1,3 linked D-galactose and α-1,4- linked L-galactose units, which are substituted by sulfate. Different types of agarans have sulfate groups at different positions . In Agaroses agaroses the α-1,4- linked galactose units are modified by having a 3,6-anhydro bridge. These bridges are responsible for the gelation that is typical to these compounds, by forming a double helix during the process.

name: a phthiodiolenone
annotation: Phthiodiolenones Phthiodiolenones are intermediates in Phthiocerols phthiocerol biosynthesis. Mutation of the G185E-7207 gene of TAX-1773 results in accumulation of Phthiodiolenones phthiodiolenone, which contains an unsaturated bond between C-4 and C-5 . Two types of phthiodiolenone exist. In Phthiodiolenone-A "phthiodiolenone A" the oxo and the methyl groups are attached at positions 3 and 4, respectively, and the hydroxyl groups are at positions 9 and 11. In Phthiodiolenone-B "phthiodiolenone B" a methyl group is missing from the beginning of the molecule, and thus the oxo and methyl groups are attached at positions 2 and 3, respectively, and the hydroxyl groups are at positions 8 and 10.

name: a galactolipid
annotation: Galactolipids are the main lipids of the membranes of chloroplasts and photosynthetic microorganisms. The main galactolipids in these membranes are D-Galactosyl-12-diacyl-glycerols (MGDG) and Galactosyl-galactosyl-diacyl-glycerols (DGDG). These compounds have been found in the X-ray structures of photosynthetic complexes, and are believed to assume a direct role in photosynthesis . The galactolipids D-galactosylceramide galactocerebrosides and Cerebroside-3-sulfates "galactocerebrosides 3-sulfate" are abundant components in myelin, the membrane around the axons in the nervous system of vertebrates . In addition, some galactolipids act as secondary metabolites and herbivore deterrents in plants and algae .

name: a homocyclic compound
annotation: Homocyclic compounds are cyclic compounds in which the ring members atoms are all of the same element.

name: a quinone
annotation: Quinones are organic compounds derived from aromatic compounds, with two carbonyl groups incorporated into the ring structure with rearrangement of the double bonds where necessary. Quinones are conjugated but not aromatic. A classic example of a quinone is P-BENZOQUINONE. Quinones are commonly named with a prefix that indicates the parent aromatic hydrocarbon (for example, "benzo-" for benzene, "naphtho-" for naphthalene, and "anthra-" for anthracene) with a "-quinone" suffix. Several types of quinones serve as electron acceptors in electron transport chains. Those quinones are usually prenylated with a lipid-soluble isoprenyl tail that anchors them in the membrane lipid bilayer. Quinones can carry two electrons, and when fully reduced they are referred to as Reduced-Quinones quinols. The addition of a single H atom to a quinone results in formation of a free radical called Semiquinones. Many quinone derivatives often have other biological activities, such as CPD-11501 "vitamin K".

name: an XLLG xylogulcan
aliases: a xyloglucan with galactose side chain at positions 2 and 3
annotation: Xyloglucan Xyloglucan is the major hemicellulose in dicotyledon type I cell walls. The cross-linking between xyloglucan chains is a major factor in the stability of the three-dimensional matrix between adjacent cellulose microfibrils . Xyloglucan has a backbone of β-1,4-linked cellotetraose units, which are substituted in C-6 by α-D-xylosyl residues at two or three of the first three glucosyl residues, but not in the position closest to the reducing end . Some of the xylosyl residues are subsequently further substituted by (1,2)-β-D-galactosyl residues at the second and/or third xylose residue and by (1,2)-α-L-fucose at the galactosyl unit of the third xylose residue . A common terminology uses G for basic non-substituted glucosyl units within a xylosylglycan, X for glucose residues substituted by α-D-xylose, L for glucose residues substituted by α-D-xylose and β-D-galactose, and F for glucose residues substituted by α-D-xylose, β-D-galactose and α-L-fucose . Most xylosylglucans consist of repeating heptasaccharide Xyloglucans-Xylose XXXG units.

name: an (S)-secondary alcohol
annotation: A secondary alcohol is a compound in which a hydroxy group, -OH, is attached to a saturated carbon atom which has two other carbon atoms attached to it.

name: a (4E,8E)-sphinga-4,8-dienine ceramide
annotation: Ceramides are found in high concentrations within the cell membrane and are one of the component lipids that make up Sphingomyelins sphingomyelin, one of the major lipids in the lipid bilayer. This type of ceramide, containing an additional double bond at position 8 of the sphingoid base, is a common modification in plants and fungi . In plants, there is commonly a mixture of ceramides with the double bond at position 8 in both cis and trans configurations, whereas the double bond at the 4 position is generally in the trans configuration . In the diatom TAX-35128 both bonds are in the trans configuration .

name: an anthracycline
annotation: Anthracyclines are a class of antibiotics produced by TAX-1883 bacteria that are used in cancer as chemotherapy drugs. This group of antibiotics is defined by the presence of a four fused-ring aromatic system, based on a 7,8,9,10-tetrahydro-5,12-naphthacenequinone framework, which usually occurs as a glycoside with a deoxy(amino)sugar. The framework is synthesized by a polyketide-synthase from an acetyl-CoA or propanoyl-CoA starter unit and malonyl-CoA extender units, and subsequently modified by other enzymes . The anthracyclines intercalate between base pairs of the DNA/RNA strand, inhibit topoisomerase II enzymes, and prevent DNA replication. They are among the most effective anticancer treatments ever developed and are effective against more types of cancer than any other class of chemotherapeutic agents.

name: an androgen
annotation: Androgens are steroid hormones that stimulate or control the development and maintenance of male characteristics in vertebrates, including the activity of the accessory male sex organs and development of male secondary sex characteristics, by binding to androgen receptors. Androgens are also produced by females, and are the precursors to estrogens. Present in lower concentrations than in males, androgens contribute to libido and sexual arousal in females. Important androgens include TESTOSTERONE, 17-BETA-HYDROXY-5ALPHA-ANDROSTAN-3-O (DHT) and ANDROST4ENE androstenedione. TESTOSTERONE Testosterone is the major androgen in adult males, while DHT plays a crucial role in utero, causing differentiation of the penis, scrotum and prostate. In adulthood DHT contributes to balding, prostate growth, and sebaceous gland activity.

name: heparan sulfate
annotation: Heparan-Sulfate "Heparan sulfate" (also known as heparitin sulfate) is a member of the Glycosaminoglycans "glycosaminoglycan" family. It consists of a variably sulfated repeating disaccharide unit composed of a hexuronic acid and a hexosamine. The most common repeating unit, which makes up around 50% of the total disaccharide units in heparan sulfate, is composed of CPD-12521 linked to CPD-12541. In other units CPD-12521 may be substituted by CPD-12515 or its sulfated form CPD-12518, and N-ACETYL-BETA-D-GLUCOSAMINE may be sulfated to CPD-12565, or may be substituted by N-sulfo-D-glucosamine or CPD-12533. Heparan sulfate binds to specific proteins such as antithrombin and several growth factors, thereby regulating various biological processes including anticoagulation and angiogenesis . The unique sulfation pattern of a monosaccharide sequence within HS is believed to be critical for binding to the target protein. The structure of heparan sulfate is very similar to that of HEPARIN. Heparan sulfate contains lower amounts of sulfate and less iduronic acid than heparin . In addition, heparan sulfates disaccharide units are organized into distinct sulfated and non-sulfated domains, while heparin disaccharides are sulfated randomly . Heparan-Sulfate "Heparan sulfate" chains comprise three different types of domains: S-domains that contain contiguous N-sulfated residues, unmodified N-acetyl-rich regions, and mixed transition zones between the S-domains and the unmodified segments. Only the S-domains have binding capacities for growth factors and other proteins .

name: a protopanaxadiol-type ginsenoside with one glucosyl residue at position 3
annotation: Ginsenosides Ginsenosides or panaxosides are a class of steroid glycosides and triterpene saponins found exclusively in the plant genus TAX-4053 (ginseng). They are the principal components responsible for the pharmaceutical activities of ginseng. Based on the structure of their aglycones, ginsenosides have been categorized into three broad types; Protopanaxadiol-type-ginsenosides "protopanaxadiol-type ginsenosides" (PPD, containing hydroxyl groups at positions 3 and 20), Protopanaxatriol-type-ginsenosides "protopanaxatriol-type ginsenosides" (PPT, containing hydroxyl groups at positions 3, 6 and 20), and Oleanonic-acid-type-ginsenosides "oleanonic-acid-type saponins".
family_key: sm:name:a protopanaxadiol type ginsenoside with one glucosyl residue at position 3

name: a galactomannan (Aspergillus)
annotation: Mannans are polymers composed of MANNOSE residues. Pure mannans are rarely found, and most contain α(1,6)-linked galactopyranosyl side chains. However, in certain filamentous fungi galactofuranose (Galf) is found in galactomannan instead of galactopyranose. The galactomannan attached to N-glycans in TAX-746128 is composed of a linear mannan core comprising CPD-20116 "α-(1-2)-linked mannotetraose" repeating units attached to each other via α-(1-6) linkages and decorated by side chains of β-(1-5)-galactofuranose units, which are linked to the C2 position of the non-reducing mannose residue in each mannotetraose unit . The length of the side chains depends on growth conditions. Cells grown in YPD medium produced galactomannan with side chain consisting an average of 3-4 units of galactofuranose , while cells grown in YNB medium incorporated on average 10 Galf units that may contain one β-(1,6) linkage midchain . It should be noted that a different form of galactomannan containing galactofuranose resides is found in O-glycans produced by this organism, in which the mannan core is much shorter, consisting of only one to two mannosyl residues .

name: a phytosiderophore
annotation: Siderophore Siderophores are small, high-affinity iron chelating compounds usually produced and secreted by microorganisms. Once outside the cell, siderophores bind FE+3 with high affinity, and the iron-siderophore complexes are imported back into the cell by active-transport mechanisms. Some grasses are known to make siderophores as well. These Phytosiderophores "phytosiderophores" are also known as mugineic acids.

name: a diazaborine
annotation: Diazaboraines are hetrocyclic compounds containing one boron and two nitrogen atoms.

name: an aromatic 2-oxo-acid
annotation: The R group in this structure contains an aromatic ring.

name: a phenolphthiocerol A
annotation: Phthiocerols Phthiocerol-based lipids and glycolipids have been found in many species of TAX-1762, including TAX-1773, TAX-1765, TAX-33894, TAX-1781, TAX-1768, TAX-1777, TAX-1769 and TAX-1809 . Many organisms, including TAX-1769, TAX-1768, TAX-1765, TAX-1781, and TAX-78331, also produce glycolipids based on the related compound Phenolphthiocerols phenolphthiocerol, which contains a phenol group at the ω end of the molecule . The phenol is usually glycosylated by a type or species-specific mono-, tri-, or a tetrasaccharide. For example, in TAX-78331 the trisaccharide (2,3,4-tri-O-methyl L-fucopyranosyl-(α1→3)-rhamnopyranosyl-(α1→3)-2-O-methyl L-rhamnopyranose) is attached. In addition, the two secondary alcohol functions are usually esterified by Mycocerosic-Acids "mycocerosic acids" or Phthioceranic-Acids "phthioceranic acids" (see CPD-18579) .

name: all-trans-3-hydroxyretinal
annotation: 3-Hydroxy-11-cis-retinal is a retinoid used as the visual pigment chromophore in some insects . When the receptor absorbs light photons the retinal undergoes a conformation change to All-trans-3-hydroxyretinal .

name: cysteate
annotation: Cysteate serves as a sole source of carbon and energy for the aerobic growth of bacteria , as an electron acceptor for several sulfate-reducing bacteria , as an electron donor for some nitrate-reducing bacteria , and as the substrate for a fermentation in a sulfate-reducing bacterium .
