name: a 9-methyl-(4E,8E)-sphinga-4,8-dienine ceramide
annotation: Ceramides are found in high concentrations within the cell membrane and are one of the component lipids that make up Sphingomyelins sphingomyelin, one of the major lipids in the lipid bilayer. This type of ceramide, containing an additional double bond at position 8 of the sphingoid base and a methyl group at position 9, is found in some fungi .

name: a 1,4-naphthoquinones
annotation: 1,4-Naphthoquinones are naphthoquinones in which the oxo groups of the quinone moiety are at positions 1 and 4 of the parent naphthalene ring.

name: an estrogen
annotation: Estrogens are hormones that stimulate or control the development and maintenance of female sex characteristics in mammals by binding to estrogen receptors. They are important in both the menstrual and the estrous reproductive cycles. Estrogens are synthesized in all vertebrates as well as some insects, suggesting that estrogenic sex hormones have an ancient evolutionary history. The estrogens that occur naturally in the body are steroids, however other types of molecules acquired in the diet are known to bind to the estrogen receptors and are thus also considered estrogens. They include molecules produced by plants (phytoestrogens), molecules produced by fungi (mycoestrogens), and synthetic compounds (xenoestrogens). The main steroidal Estrogens estrogens in humans are ESTRONE, CPD-352 and ESTRIOL, which differ in the number of hydroxyl groups (one for ESTRONE, two for CPD-352 and three for ESTRIOL). CPD-352 is about 10 times as potent as ESTRONE and about 80 times as potent as ESTRIOL in its estrogenic effect. CPD-352 is the predominant estrogen in non-pregenant women during their reproductive years in terms of serum levels. ESTRONE Estrone is the predominant circulating estrogen in women during menopause and ESTRIOL is the predominant circulating estrogen during pregnancy.

name: a lividomycin
annotation: The lividomycins are a family of Aminoglycoside-Antibiotics "aminoglycosidic antibiotics" produced by the bacterium TAX-282216. The lividomycins were discovered In Japan in the early 70s . Like other aminoglucoside antibiotics, it inhibits protein synthesis by binding to 16S ribosomal RNA . Despite promising initial results against urinary tract infections , tuberculosis and other respiratory tract infections , lividomycin never became widely used.

name: a [glycerolipid]-(9Z,12E)-octadecadienoate
annotation: CPD-8203 "(9Z,12E)-Octadecadienoate" is an unusual unsaturated fatty acid that contains a trans double bond. It is usuall found CPD-17285 "attached to a lipid".

name: an oligomycin
annotation: Oligomycins are 26-membered ring macrolides produced by Streptomyces species that can be toxic to other organisms through their ability to inhibit mitochondrial membrane-bound ATP synthases by blocking its proton channel (the Fo subunit). That subunit is named using the letter "o" for "oligomycin" (and not using the nunber zero, a common error).

name: a bacteriochlorophyll c
annotation: Green sulfur bacteria (TAX-191412) and green flimentous bacteria (TAX-1106) contain chlorosomes - large organelles that are believed to be the most efficient light-harvesting antennae in nature . Green sulfur bacteria can grow at light intensities that are too low to support growth of any other phototroph, up to a million times lower light than normal sunlight . Chlorosomes contain about 1,500 bacteriochlorophyll molecules per reaction center. They are filled with 10-30 rod-shaped elements which are oriented parallel to the long axis of the chlorosomes and have a diameter of 10 nm. Each rod consists of a tubular bilayer formed by self-organization of the antenna bacteriochlorophyll molecules. Unlike all other known photosynthetic antenna, in which the chlorophyll molecules are bound to proteins, chlorosomes do not contain accessory proteins. The chlorosomal bacteriochlorophyll pigments are able to self-aggregate due to their unique chemical characteristics. The bacteriochlorophylls found in chlorosomes are either CPD-9092 "bacteriochlorophyll c", CPD-9093 "bacteriochlorophyll d" or CPD-9094 "bacteriochlorophyll e", depending on the species. They all possess a hydroxyl group at the C31 position that allows them to chelate the Mg atom of a neighboring molecule and to interact with the C13-oxo carbonyl group of another neighboring pigment molecule, affording the self-aggregation . In addition, all of these bacteriochlorophylls lack C-132-methylcarboxyl side chains, which are found in all other (bacterio)chlorophylls but would interfere with the ability to form aggregates . Despite of their names, CPD-9092 "bacteriochlorophyll c", CPD-9093 "bacteriochlorophyll d" and CPD-9094 "bacteriochlorophyll e" are actually chlorins rather than bacteriochlorins. All three are usually f

name: a Lewis a epitope
annotation: The Lewis-a-epitope "Lewis a epitope" is formed by α(1,4) fucosylation of the N-acetylglucosaminyl residue of Core1, the disaccharide precursor that can also lead to formation of the Type-1-H-Antigen by α(1,2) fucosylation at its terminal galactosyl residue . The related CPD-8594 "Lewis x epitope" is formed by α(1,3) fucosylation of the N-acetylglucosaminyl residue of B-Gal-14-NacGlc-R. Both the Lewis-a-epitope and the CPD-8594 cannot be further transformed to ABH-type antigens .

name: anacardate
annotation: Anacardic acids are chemical compounds found It is primarily found in cashew nuts (TAX-171929) but also in mangos and Pelargonium geraniums. They are closely related to Urushiol urushiol, and also cause an allergic skin rash on contact. Anacardic acids also have antibacterial activity against Gram-positive bacteria. The term anacardic acid is used for a mixture of several closely related compounds, consisting of a CPD-110 core substituted with an alkyl chain that has 15 or 17 carbon atoms and may be saturated or unsaturated. The exact mixture depends on the species of the plant. The major component of the cashew nut is a C15:3, all-Z form (CPD-14640). Anacardic acid consisting of a 15 carbon unsaturated side chain, which is found in the cashew plant, is the most active against Gram-positive bacteria.

name: an anthraquinone
annotation: Anthraquinone derivatives are found in microorganisms, fungi, plants, marine organisms, and insects. In higher plants, most anthraquinones occur as mono- or diglycosides. The yellow or red colored compounds have some commercial relevance as natural dyes. 1,8-dihydroxyanthraquinones serve as laxatives. In fungi and some higher plants (Leguminosae, Rhamnaceae, Polygonaceae, and Caesalpiniaceae), anthraquinones are biosynthesized via a polyketide pathway which comprises the successive condensation of acetyl CoA with 7 molecules of malonyl CoA to form an octa-α-keto-acyl chain, which is converted to the anthraquinone system via decarboxylation, cyclization, and aromatization.

name: an azole
annotation: An azole is any monocyclic heteroarene consisting of a five-membered ring containing nitrogen. Azoles can also contain one or more other non-carbon atoms, such as nitrogen, sulfur or oxygen.

name: an octose
annotation: An octose is an eight-carbon monosaccharide which in its linear form contains either an aldehyde group at position 1 (aldooctose) or a ketone group at position 2 (ketooctose).

name: a dimycocerosyl glycosyl phenolphthiocerol
annotation: CPD-18578 Phenolphthiocerol-based glycolipids, known as PGLs, have been found in TAX-1769, TAX-1768, TAX-1765, TAX-1781, and TAX-78331 . The phenolphthiocerol moiety of PGLs is attached to mono-, tri-, or tetrasaccharide, depending on the mycobacterial species. Most sugar residues are O-methylated deoxysugars. The two secondary alcohol functions are esterified by Mycocerosic-Acids "mycocerosic acids" or Phthioceranic-Acids "phthioceranic acids", depending on the species .

name: a TAG(18:2/36:1)
annotation: This class of triacylglycerols contains three acyl chains formed by the esterification of glycerol with three fatty acids. The names of triacylglycerol compound classes provide information about the three acyl chains of each member of the class, using the nomenclature TAG(a:b/y:z) where "a" represents the number of carbons found in one acyl chain and "b" represents the number of desaturations present in chain "a" and where "y" represents the total number of carbons found in the other two acyl chains and "z" represents the total number of desaturations present in the two chains included in "y." Therefore, in this TAG(18:2/36:1) class of compounds, one acyl chain has 18 carbons and two double bonds. The other two acyl chains have a total of 36 carbons and one double bond. The attachment sites of the acyl chains at the R1 (sn-1), R2 (sn-2), or R3 (sn-3) position along the glyceryl backbone may vary among class members and cannot be inferred from their order in the compound class name. Additionally, the location and orientation of the double bonds in the unsaturated acyl chain(s) may vary among members of the class and are not specified by this notation.

name: a plastoquinol
annotation: Plastoquinones are found in the chloroplasts of higher plants, red, brown and green algae, and in cyanobacteria, and are important members of the photosynthetic electron transfer chain, where they carry electrons from CPLX490-3506 to the CPLX-9022. In fact, it has been shown that in cyanobacteria essentially all electron transport through unbound quinones proceeds via PQ in cyanobacterial thylakoids. Apart from PQ the only quinone found in thylakoids is vitamin K1, which is associated with PS I . Plastoquinones also serve as cofactors involved in the desaturation of phytoene in the synthesis of carotenoids (see PWY-6475). They are analogs of Ubiquinones "ubiquinones" (Coenzyme Q), with two methyl groups substituting the methoxy groups found in Q. Plastoquinone can function as acceptor/donor for one or two electrons. The fully reduced form is called Plastoquinols. Plastoquinone was isolated originally in 1946 from alfalfa, but was not identified at the time. It was rediscovered in 1959 and its structure was described in the same year (see ). Please note that the structure shown here is that of CPD-12829 and is not accurate for this generic class of compounds, which contains plastoquinols with different side-chain lengths.

name: a phenolphthiotriol
annotation: Phenolphthiotriol Phenolphthiotriols are intermediates in Phenolphthiocerols phenolphthiocerol biosynthesis. Phenolphthiocerol glycolipids have been found in many Mycobacterium species, including TAX-1769, TAX-1768, TAX-1765, TAX-1781, and TAX-78331 . Two types of phenolphthiotriol exist. In Phenolphthiotriol-A "phenolphthiotriol A" the first hydroxyl and the methyl groups are attached at positions 3 and 4, respectively, and the other hydroxyl groups are at positions 9 and 11. In Phenolphthiotriol-B "phenolphthiotriol B" a methyl group is missing from the beginning of the molecule, and thus the first hydroxyl and the methyl groups are attached at positions 2 and 3, respectively, and the other hydroxyl groups are at positions 8 and 10.

name: a glycosphingolipid
annotation: Glycosphingolipids Glycosphingolipids are a family of complex lipids composed of Ceramides and mono- or oligosaccharide moieties that play an important role in various cellular functions including recognition, cell adhesion, proliferation and differentiation . The lipid portion is embedded in the outer plasma membrane leaflet and the sugar chains extended to the outer environment, acting as an antigen. Together with Glycoproteins glycoproteins and Glycosaminoglycans glycosaminoglycans, the glycosphingolipids contribute to the glycocalyx that covers eukaryotic cell surfaces. Glycoshphingolipids have been classified into the following 12 series based on their structure: Arthro-Series-GSLs arthro, Gala-Series-GSLs gala, Ganglio-Series-GSLs ganglio, Globo-Series-GSLs globo, Isoglobo-Series-GSLs isoglobo, Lacto-Series-GSLs lacto, Muco-Series-GSLs muco, Mollu-Series-GSLs mollu, Neogala-Series-GSLs neogala, Neolacto-Series-GSLs neolacto, Schisto-Series-GSLs schisto and Spirometo-Series-GSLs spirometo . Glycosphingolipids are synthesized by the sequential addition of sugar residues to ceramide by glycosyltransferases that are specific to each glycosidic linkage. Lactosyl-Ceramides Lactosylceramide, a product of the second glycosylation step in the biosynthesis of many glycosphingolipids, is an important branching point, being a common precursor for the synthesis of the four major classes of glycosphingolipids: the Ganglio-Series-GSLs "ganglio series", the Globo-Series-GSLs "globo series", the Lacto-Series-GSLs "lacto series", and the Neolacto-Series-GSLs "neolacto series" . In the diagram below, R1 describes the variable part of the sphingoid base, R2 describes the fatty acid, and R3 describes the glycan moiety.

name: an ecdysteroid
annotation: Ecdysteroid is a general name for multifunctional, polyhydroxylated steroid hormones that are found in arthropods, plants and fungi. In arthropods they act as molting hormones. Ecdysteroids have potential practical uses in humans and other mammals (reviewed in ).

name: an organic hydroperoxide
annotation: This compound class stands for generic and unspecified alkylhydroperoxides.

name: a deaminated amino group donor
annotation: Amine-Donors "An amino group donor" is a compound that can donate an amino group to another compound in a reaction catalyzed by enzymes that belong to the EC-2.6.1 transaminase group" (often called aminotransferases). Prior to donating the amino group the compound is Aminated-Amine-Donors and after donating it the compound is Deaminated-Amine-Donors. Since tranmination reactions are reversible, the distinction between an amino group donor and an amino group acceptor is not determined by the actual chemical entity, but by the role it plays in a particular reaction. However, some compounds, such as GLT and L-ALPHA-ALANINE, are often used as amino group donors by the aminotransferases in reactions that are physiologically unidirectional. This particular entry is used when the identity of the amino group donor (or acceptor) is not known or cannot be defined.

name: a butirosin
annotation: CPD-14140 "Butirosins A" and CPD-14193 are naturally occurring derivatives of the Aminoglycoside-Antibiotics "2-aminoglycoside antibiotics" CPD-14196 and CPD-14143, respectively, produced by the bacterium TAX-1397 and discovered in the early 1970s . The butirosins are unique among naturally occurring aminoglycosides since they contain an (S)-4-amino-2-hydroxybutyrate (AHBA) side-chain at the C-1 amine of the central 2-deoxystreptamine ring. This modification has been shown to confer resistance against several aminoglycoside-modifying enzymes (the C-1 amine is a frequent target of aminoglycoside N-acyltransferases). As a result, the butirosins are active toward many aminoglycoside-resistant strains. Although the butirosins are not themselves clinically useful drugs, the properties of their side-chain have led to successful synthetic alteration of CPD-4822 and CPD-14161 to produce CPD-14197 and CPD-14162, respectively .

name: a bacteriochlorophyllide d
annotation: Green sulfur bacteria (TAX-191412) and green flimentous bacteria (TAX-1106) contain chlorosomes - large organelles that are believed to be the most efficient light-harvesting antennae in nature . Green sulfur bacteria can grow at light intensities that are too low to support growth of any other phototroph, up to a million times lower light than normal sunlight . Chlorosomes contain about 1,500 bacteriochlorophyll molecules per reaction center. They are filled with 10-30 rod-shaped elements which are oriented parallel to the long axis of the chlorosomes and have a diameter of 10 nm. Each rod consists of a tubular bilayer formed by self-organization of the antenna bacteriochlorophyll molecules. Unlike all other known photosynthetic antenna, in which the chlorophyll molecules are bound to proteins, chlorosomes do not contain accessory proteins. The chlorosomal bacteriochlorophyll pigments are able to self-aggregate due to their unique chemical characteristics. The bacteriochlorophylls found in chlorosomes are either CPD-9092 "bacteriochlorophyll c", CPD-9093 "bacteriochlorophyll d" or CPD-9094 "bacteriochlorophyll e", depending on the species. They all possess a hydroxyl group at the C31 position that allows them to chelate the Mg atom of a neighboring molecule and to interact with the C13-oxo carbonyl group of another neighboring pigment molecule, affording the self-aggregation . In addition, all of these bacteriochlorophylls lack C-132-methylcarboxyl side chains, which are found in all other (bacterio)chlorophylls but would interfere with the ability to form aggregates . Despite of their names, CPD-9092 "bacteriochlorophyll c", CPD-9093 "bacteriochlorophyll d" and CPD-9094 "bacteriochlorophyll e" are actually chlorins rather than bacteriochlorins. All three are usually f

name: a persulfate
annotation: The term persulfate refers to ions or compounds with more oxygen than normal sulfates. These do not have sulfur in a different oxidation state; rather, they contain peroxide units, where two oxygens take the place of one in a normal sulfate; the oxygen atoms are in oxidation state 1. The main forms are peroxomonosulfates and peroxodisulfates.

name: a phenylpropanoid
annotation: The phenylpropanoids are a large family of compounds, mostly found in plants, that are synthesized from PHE and, to a lesser extent, TYR. The pathway that starts from PHE forms COUMARATE as a main intermediate. The name "phenylpropanoid" is derived from this compound, which consists of an aromatic phenyl group and a propene tail. Further processing results in the biosynthesis of many types of compounds, including Lignols monolignols (precursors to Lignins lignin and lignocellulose), Flavonoids flavonoids, Coumarins coumarins, Stilbenoids stilbenoids, and phenolic acids. Phenylpropanoids are found throughout the plant kingdom, where they serve as essential components of a number of structural polymers, provide protection from ultraviolet light, defend against herbivores and pathogens, and also mediate plant-pollinator interactions as floral pigments and scent compounds.

name: an excited coelenteramide monoanion
annotation: OPLOPHORUS-LUCIFERIN Coelenterazine and its analogues are the luciferase substrate (=luciferin) in the light-emitting process of bioluminescent coelenterates as well as various, distantly related, types of organisms such as squids, shrimps, fishes, and some chaetognaths . It is also found in many nonluminous organisms . In bioluminescent systems, OPLOPHORUS-LUCIFERIN coelenterazine is bound by either Coelenterazine-binding-proteins or by an aequorin protein, depending on the species. In the first system, upon binding of Ca2+ ions the coelenterazine-binding protein releases coelenterazine, which is then oxidized and decarboxylated by the luciferase enzyme, resulting in CPD-20235. In the second system, aequorin binds and oxidizes coelenterazine, retainning it in the form of bound CPD-20222. Upon binding of Ca2+ ions, the enzyme catalyzes the decarboxylation and formation of CPD-20235. In vitro the excited coelenteramide relaxes to the ground state by emission of blue light. However, in vivo the excitation energy is transferred to GFP by Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) . Coelenterazine is a derivative of imidazopyrazinone. Its biosynthetic origin is not known. However, it has been shown that the hydromedusa TAX-6100 is not able to synthesize it, and is dependent on a dietary supply of this luciferin for bioluminescence . The only report of de novo biosynthesis of coelenterazine is from the bioluminescent shrimp TAX-576189 . Studies of the excited state of coelenteramide have shown that emission occurs exclusively from the electronic singlet excited state of the monoanion form .

name: an N-terminal L-alanine-[cypemycin]
family_key: sm:name:an n terminal l alanine cypemycin

name: a mannosylerythritol lipid
annotation: CPD-25639 "Mannosylerythritol lipids" (MELs) are amphiphilic glycolipids that display high surface activity and act as biodetergents (biosurfactants). The compounds are produced by yeast strains that belong to the TAX-5269 and TAX-63261 genera. They show excellent surface-active properties and versatile biochemical actions. The compounds were first described in 1956 from an TAX-5269 species . However, the most common MEL producing species are members of the TAX-63261 genus, such as TAX-84753, TAX-84754, TAX-84752, and TAX-203536 . The lipids are acylated at positions O-2' and O-3' by a range of fatty acyl residues. In TAX-5270 the fatty acid at O-2' ranges from C2 to C6, while that at O-3' ranges from C12 to C16. In addition, MELs differ in their acetylation status. Four general types are produced differing in the acetylation of positions O-4' and O-6'. Mel-D has no acetylation, MEL-B is acetylated only at the O-6' position, MEL-C is acetylated only at the O-4' position, and MEL-A is acetylated at both positions . The biological function of MELs is still unknown, but they are of growing biotechnological interest as a sustainable alternative for chemical surfactants .

name: a (4E,8Z)-4-sphinga-4,8-dienine ceramide
annotation: Ceramides are found in high concentrations within the cell membrane and are one of the component lipids that make up Sphingomyelins sphingomyelin, one of the major lipids in the lipid bilayer. This type of ceramide, containing an additional double bond at position 8 of the sphingoid base, is a common modification in plants and fungi . In plants, there is commonly a mixture of ceramides with the double bond at position 8 in both cis and trans configurations, whereas the double bond at the 4 position is generally in the trans configuration . In the diatom TAX-35128 both bonds are in the trans configuration .

name: a γ-lactone
annotation: A Lactones "lactone" is a cyclic ester, the product of condensation of an alcohol group and a carboxylate group within the same molecule. It is characterized by a closed ring consisting of two or more carbon atoms and a single oxygen atom, with a ketone group in one of the carbons adjacent to the ring oxygen. There are several types of lactones - Beta-lactones contain 3 carbons in the ring, Gamma-lactones contains 4, Delta-lactones contains 5, and Epsilon-lactones contains 6 carbons.

name: a terminal olefin
annotation: Terminal olefins are hydrocarbons that contain one carbon-carbon double bond at the very end of the molecule. They may contain additional double bonds throughout the molecule, and can be either branched or non-branched.

name: a pteridine
annotation: CPD-15307 Pteridine is a chemical compound composed of fused PYRIMIDINE-RING and CPD-26401 rings. The name is also used for a group of heterocyclic compounds containing a wide variety of substitutions on this structure. Examples for important pteridine-derived compound classess include Pterins pterins and FLAVIN flavins.

name: a short-chain fatty aldehyde
annotation: Fatty-Aldehydes "Fatty aldehydes" are aldehydes that are formed by the reduction of the carboxylic acid group of Fatty-Acids fatty acids. Many enzymes that act on fatty acids, as well as the corresponding Fatty-Acyl-CoAs "fatty acyl-CoAs", Fatty-Alcohols alcohols, and Fatty-Aldehydes aldehydes, have evolved to recognize substrates with a limited range of chain lengths. To classify these enzymes, it is helpful to divide the substrates into smaller groups based on their chain length. The following subgroups have been defined: Short-Chain-Aldehydes "Short-chain fatty aldehydes" are derived from Short-chain-fatty-acids "fatty acids that have 3-5 carbons in the longest chain". Medium-Chain-Aldehydes "Medium-chain fatty aldehydes" are derived from Medium-chain-fatty-acids "fatty acids that have 6-12 carbons in the longest chain". Long-Chain-Aldehydes "Long-chain fatty aldehydes" are derived from Long-Chain-Fatty-Acids "fatty acids that have 13-22 carbons in the longest chain". Very-Long-Chain-Aldehydes "Very-long-chain fatty aldehydes" are derived from Very-long-chain-fatty-acids "fatty acids that have 23-27 carbons in the longest chain". Ultra-Long-Chain-Aldehydes "Ultra-long-chain fatty aldehydes" are derived from Ultra-long-chain-fatty-acids "fatty acids that have more than 27 carbons in the longest chain".

name: an icosadienoate
aliases: an eicosadienoic acid|an eicosadienoate|an icosadienoic acid
annotation: This compound class stands for any 20-carbon, straight-chain, polyunsaturated fatty acid having two double bonds at any positions. The structure shown here is not generic (it is the structure of CPD-17188), and is only provided as a general guidance.

name: a diazine
annotation: Diazines are organic heterocyclic compounds containing a benzene ring in which two of the C-H fragments have been replaced by isolobal nitrogens.

name: an isomaltooligosaccharide
annotation: Isoaltooligosaccharides are glucooligosaccharide derived from D-glucose monomers linked via α-1,6 bonds as in Isomaltose. More specifically, the term is applied to linear oligosaccharides composed of more than two and less than ten units of glucose. The corresponding α-1,4 glucooligosaccharides are called Malto-Oligosaccharides maltooligosaccharides.

name: a zealexin
annotation: Zealexins Zealexins are a class of nonvolatile sesquiterpenoid defense compounds found in maize. They are produced in response to a wide variety of biotic threats, including TAX-5518 and other fungi as well as TAX-29057. Jasmonic acid and ethylene can also work synergistically to promote zealexin production.

name: a phenoxyl radical of a phenolic donor
family_key: sm:name:a phenoxyl radical of a phenolic donor
display_name: a phenoxyl radical of a phenolic donor

name: a benzylacyclouridine
annotation: The benzylacyclouridines are potent and specific inhibitors of mammalian and bacterial EC-2.4.2.3. In this diagram, R1 = OH or OCOCH2CH2COONa; R2 = H or CH2OH, R3 = H or CH3 and R4 = H, OCH2-benzene, or OCH2-benzene-OCH2-benzene .

name: acetan
annotation: Acetan Acetan is an acidic heteropolysaccharide produced by the bacterium TAX-28448 (previously known as Acetobacter xylinum) that consists of a cellulose backbone with side-chains attached to alternate glucose units. The polymer is composed of repeating units of a heptasaccharide with the following structure: α-L-rhamnose-(1->6)-β-D-glucose-(1->6)-α-D-glucose-(1->4)-β-D-glucuronate-(1->2)-α-D-mannose-(1->3)-β-D-glucose-(1->4)-β-D-glucose. These repeating units are attached to each other via β(1->4) links between the terminal two glucose units, forming a cellulose backbone with alternating pentasaccharide branches . Acetan is structurally related to Xanthan.

name: a Lewis epitope
annotation: The Lewis blood group antigens, discovered in 1946, are biosynthetically and structurally related carbohydrate structures used as markers of cell differentiation and embryonic development, based on fucosylation carried by a number of fucosyltransferases. The system is named after Mrs. H. D. G. Lewis, in whose serum the first member of the family was discovered . However, the presence of the Lewis antigens is not limited to erytrocytes, and they are found in different tissues and organs. One of these fucosyltransferases, encoded by the HS10249 gene, is bifunctional and can fucosylate both the 3 and 4 positions of the penultimate sugar in the precursor disaccharide. The gene, also known as the Lewis gene, has a dominant allele (which was named Le) and a recessive non-functional allele (known as le). The α(1,4)-fucosyltransferase activity is only relevant when the substrates are derived from the beta-Gal-13-beta-GlcNac-R "type 1 precursor disaccharide", as that position is already occupied in the B-Gal-14-NacGlc-R "type 2 disaccharide". The first Lewis antigens to be discovered were the Lewis-a-epitope "Lewis a" and Antigen-Leb "Lewis b" antigens, which are produced from beta-Gal-13-beta-GlcNac-R "type 1 precursor disaccharides" (see PWY-7832). However, the α(1,3) fucosylation of the N-acetylglucosaminyl residue of B-Gal-14-NacGlc-R leads to formation of several related antigens known as the CPD-8594 "Lewis x epitope" (also known as SSEA-1, for stage-specific embryonic antigen 1), Antigen-Ley "Lewis y epitope", and the sLea-Antigen "sialyl-Lewis x epitope" (see PWY-7831).

name: a protopanaxatriol-type ginsenoside
annotation: Ginsenosides Ginsenosides or panaxosides are a class of steroid glycosides and triterpene saponins found exclusively in the plant genus TAX-4053 (ginseng). They are the principal components responsible for the pharmaceutical activities of ginseng. Ginsenosides are composed of an aglycone and sugar moieties. Based on the structure of their aglycones, ginsenosides have been categorized into three broad types; Protopanaxadiol-type-ginsenosides "protopanaxadiol-type ginsenosides" (PPD), which contain hydroxyl groups at positions 3 and 20, Protopanaxatriol-type-ginsenosides "protopanaxatriol-type ginsenosides" (PPT), which contain hydroxyl groups at positions 3, 6 and 20, and Oleanonic-acid-type-ginsenosides "oleanonic-acid-type saponins". Although ginsenosides only have three kinds of aglycones, more than 60 kinds of ginsenosides have been identified by now due to the variability in the type of sugar moieties, their number, and the carbon positions they link to. The biological activity of ginsenoside increases with the decrease of the number of the sugar moieties. All of the ginsenosides found in large quantities in wild ginseng (known as major ginsenosides) are heavily glycosylated, while the more active less-glycosylated ginsenosides are found in much smalller quantities (and are commonly referred to as the minor ginsenosides). Following oral intake of ginseng, the large low-solubility and poor-permeability major ginsenosides cannot be absorbed by the human body directly , and are deglycosylated into smaller forms in the gastrointestinal tract by intestinal bacteria and/or digestive enzymes . Glycosidase enzymes that can convert the major ginsenosides to minor ginsenosides are not common and are of commercial interest.

name: jadomycin A
annotation: The jadomycins are a unique family of angucycline-derived antibiotics. Jadomycin A is produced by the organism TAX-953739 under stress conditions such as heat shock, phage infection, and particularly ethanol treatment. This compound is bioactive against Gram-positive (including the methicillin-resistant TAX-1280) and Gram-negative bacteria, but unlike Jadomycin-B, it is not active against yeast . The difference between the two compounds is that Jadomycin-B includes an CPD-11914 moiety . Jadomycin A exists as a diastereomeric mixture of CPD-12938 and CPD-21699 , with the former being the predominant form at a ratio of 10:1. The two forms can interconvert via an aldehyde intermediate .

name: a sapogenin
annotation: A sapogenin is the aglycon moiety of Saponins. Sapogenins may be steroids or triterpenoids.

name: a carbobicyclic compound
annotation: Carbobicyclic compounds are bicyclic compound in which all the ring atoms are carbon.

name: heparosan
annotation: Heparosan "Heparosan" (also known as heparan) is a member of the Glycosaminoglycans "glycosaminoglycan" family. It consists of a repeating disaccharide unit composed of CPD-12521-(1,4)-CPD-12541. The disaccharide units are linked by a (1-4) linkage to each other. Heparosan is a bacterial glycosaminoglycan produced and found in the capsule of some pathogenic bacteria such as Escherichia coli K5 and Pasteurella multocida. The heparosan skeleton serves as a starting point in the biosynthesis of several other glycosaminoglycan such as Heparan-Sulfate and HEPARIN, and bacterial heparosan is sometimes used for the synthesis of non-animal sourced heparin-like products.

name: a [elongation-Factor-P]-L-lysine
family_key: sm:name:a elongation factor p l lysine

name: a sulfone
annotation: Sulfones are a class of organosulfur compounds occurring widely in nature. They contain two carbon-sulfur bonds, with a hexavalent sulfur atom that is also linked to two oxygen atoms. All sulfones have the general structure: S(=O)2R2, where R2 represents linkage to two carbon atoms either within one (e.g. the heterocyclic ring in CPD-944) or two (e.g. the two methyl groups of CPD-10437) substituents. The *S(=O)2* group is called a sulfonyl group or a sulfuryl group.

name: an alkene
annotation: Alkenes are acyclic hydrocarbons with (only) one carbon-carbon double bond. They can be either branched or non-branched.

name: a sphing-8(E)-enine-ceramide
annotation: The predominant long-chain base in animal ceramides is sphingosine, which is only present in trace amount in plant ceramides. In contrast, sphinganine, 4-hydroxysphinganine, and 4-hydroxy-8-sphingenine are the prevalent long-chain bases in plants. The acyl chain of plant ceramides contains alpha-hydroxylated very long chain fatty acids. The plant ceramide (IV) in MetaCyc represents plant ceramides with 4-hydroxy-8-sphingenine as the long-chain base.

name: a ceramide phosphorylcholine
annotation: Sphingomyelins Sphingomyelins are a type of SPHINGOLIPIDS sphingolipids found in animal cell membranes, especially in the membranous myelin sheath that surrounds some nerve cell axons. It usually consists of PHOSPHORYL-CHOLINE and Ceramides, but PHOSPHORYL-ETHANOLAMINE can replace PHOSPHORYL-CHOLINE. Sphingomyelins are found in all tissues and lipoproteins; for example, they account for ~5% of the lipid phosphate in liver and 25% in pancreas . Sphingomyelins have a function in signal transduction . Sphingomyelins are highly enriched in the plasma membrane of cells, and were considered to be found largely in the exoplasmic leaflet of the cell membrane. However, there is some evidence that there may also be a sphingomyelin pool in the inner leaflet of the membrane . Moreover, neutral sphingomyelinase-2, an enzyme that breaks down sphingomyelins into ceramides, has been found to localize exclusively to the inner leaflet, further suggesting that there may be sphingomyelin present there. Sphingomyelin can accumulate in a rare hereditary disease called Niemann-Pick Disease. The disease is caused by a deficiency in the enzyme sphingomyelinase, rsulting in accumulation of sphingomyelin in the spleen, liver, lungs, bone marrow, and the brain, causing irreversible neurological damage.

name: a campesteryl ester
annotation: This class of compounds includes campesteryl esters that have one acyl chain, denoted by R, connected to the campesteryl backbone via an ester linkage on the C3 carbon. For unsaturated acyl chains, the number, location, and orientation of the double bonds will vary between individual members of this group of compounds.

name: a sulfanylalkanol
annotation: Volatile sulfanylalkanols are a component of the odorous compounds produced by bacteria acting on axillary (underarm) secretions .

name: a modified nucleobase
annotation: Nucleobases are the parts of nucleic acids (DNA and RNA) that are involved in pairing of the two strands. The primary nucleobases are CYTOSINE, GUANINE, ADENINE (found in both DNA and RNA), THYMINE (found only in DNA) and URACIL (found only in RNA), abbreviated as C, G, A, T, and U, respectively. There are many additional nucleobases, usually derived from the primary ones. Based on their structure, nucleobases are divided into Purines purines and Pyrimidine-Bases pyrimidines.

name: a mycobactin
annotation: The important pathogen TAX-1773 relies on 2-hydroxyphenyloxazoline-containing siderophore molecules called mycobactins for the acquisition of iron in the restrictive environment of the mammalian macrophage. These compounds are synthesized through the action of a cluster of genes that include both nonribosomal peptide synthase and polyketide synthase components . Mycobactins are hexadentate ligands with a tripodal architecture. Their basic scaffold consists of an o-hydroxyphenyloxazoline system attached to an acylated hydroxylysine residue esterified with a 3-hydroxybutyric acid unit. This linker is, in turn, tethered to a terminal cyclized hydroxylysine moiety forming a seven-membered lactam ring. Mycobactins exhibit a very high affinity toward the ferric ion Fe(III) as they form a stable binary complex, with a stability constant (Ks) of ~1025 .

name: medicarpin
annotation: Pterocarpan phytoalexins are antimicrobial compounds in leguminous plants formed upon pathogenic attacks and similar biotic stress-related events. Medicarpin Medicarpin, first isolated from heartwood of the tropical tree TAX-149635 , is a major pterocarpan phytoalexin. It has been reported from many legumes, including alfalfa (TAX-3879) , red clover (TAX-57577) , broad bean (TAX-3906) , all members of the TAX-78532 genus , and the model legume TAX-3880 . Medicarpin Medicarpin is found in both a (-) and a (+) conformations. While CPD-3402 appears to be the most common form, CPD-20031 has been reported from several plants such as TAX-3897 and TAX-77268 .

name: 2,3-dihydroxypropane 1-sulfonate
annotation: 2-3-dihydroxypropane-1-sulfonate "2,3-Dihydroxypropane-1-sulfonate" (DHPS) is a widespread intermediate in plant and algal transformations of CPD-10247 from the plant sulfolipid SULFOQUINOVOSYLDIACYLGLYCEROL . It is also recovered quantitatively during bacterial degradation of CPD-10247 , and is secreted by all diatoms . DHPS is also a putative precursor of CPD-11797 in some organisms, such as TAX-246200 .

name: a α-limit dextrin with short branches
annotation: Truncated-Limit-Dextrins "An α-limit dextrin with short branches" is formed when CPD0-971 is subjected to the ENZRXN-14616 activity of the YPR184W-MONOMER. The enzyme shortens the branching chains, leaving only single glucose residues connected by an α-1,6 bond to the main (α-1,4)-glucan chain.

name: D-phenylalanyl-[gramicidin-S synthetase]
annotation: This class contains an acyl carrier protein holoenzyme domain of a nonribosomal peptide synthetase bound to CPD-216. This domain is also known as a thiolation domain, or a peptidyl carrier protein domain. This chemical structure is modified from . The holo-acyl carrier protein (holo-[acp]) is represented here as a superatom. The synonym for this structure was named using the IUPAC name generator (Preferred IUPAC Name) in MarvinSketch version 5.0 from ChemAxon. Note that these names do not take into account the thioester linkage between the phosphopantetheinyl arm of the holo-acyl carrier protein and the amino acid.

name: a dihydroflavonol
annotation: A dihydroflavonol is Flavones hydroxylated at C3 and lacking the double bond between carbons 2 and 3. The name is misleading, since the absence of the double bond makes it Flavanols rather than Flavonols.

name: an S-alkylsulfenate
aliases: an alkyl sulfenate|a sulfenic acid|a sulfenate
annotation: A sulfenic acid is an organosulfur compound and oxoacid with the general formula RSOH. In contrast to Alkyl-Sulfinates sulfinic and SULFONATES sulfonic acids, simple sulfenic acids, such as CPD-17426, are highly reactive and cannot be isolated in solution. Sulfenic acids are fromed in plants of the TAX-4678 genus from S-Alkyl-L-Cysteine-S-Oxides "S-alkyl-L-cysteine S-oxide" by the action of EC-4.4.1.4 (alliinase). They are acted upon quickly by a second enzyme, the lachrymatory-factor synthase, giving a stable S-oxide product. In the absence of lachrymatory-factor synthase they tend to condense with other sulfenic acid molecules quickly to form CPD-17425 thiosulfinates.

name: a chelator
annotation: A chelate is a chemical compound composed of a metal ion and a chelating agent. A chelating agent is a substance whose molecules can form several bonds to a single metal ion. An example of a simple chelating agent is ethylenediamine.

name: a jasmonoyl-L-leucine
aliases: a JA-Leu|a jasmonate-leucine|a jasmonic acid-leucine|a jasmonic acid-leucine conjugate|a jasmonyl-leucine
annotation: Jasmonoyl-leucine (JA-Leu) has been detected in Arabidopsis thaliana and a number of other plant species . Two stereoisomers of this compound may exist in plants. CPD-11234 is one naturally occurring form of the compound . JA-Leu may not be a biologically active form of the hormone in all species. It does not appear to function in herbivory defense responses in TAX-49451 . The CPD-11234 isomer also does not affect seedling root growth in Arabidopsis thaliana nor does it promote JAZ/COI1 interactions in vitro . However, JA-Leu can elicit phytoalexin production in rice .

name: an olefinic compound
annotation: Olefinic compounds are any organic molecular entity that contains at least one C=C bond.

name: a di-trans, poly-cis-polyprenol
annotation: This compound class stands for di-trans, poly-cis-polyprenols of any length.
family_key: sm:name:a di trans poly cis polyprenol

name: a primary amine
annotation: Primary amines are derivatives of ammonia where one of the three hydrogen atoms in ammonia is replaced by an alkyl group.

name: a [glycerolipid]-linoleate
annotation: This compound class describes a linoleoyl group that is incorporated within a lipid molecule. LINOLEIC_ACID Linoleate (linoleic acid) is an ω-6 polyunsaturated fatty acid with an 18-carbon chain and two cis double bonds at positions 9 and 12. Linoleate is produced by many organisms, including bacteria, plants, fungi and some insects. It is abundant in many vegetable oils, especially safflower and sunflower oils. While mammals require linoleate, which is found in their membranes and is used as the precursor for production of CPD-8117, ARACHIDONIC_ACID, Prostaglandins prostaglandins, Leukotrienes leukotrienes, and Thromboxanes thromboxanes, they are not able to produce it since they lack EC-1.14.19.6. Thus linoleate is an essential fatty acid, and must be obtained through the diet. Like other fatty acids, LINOLEIC_ACID is rarely found in its free form. It is usually found either as CPD-18 or Linoleoyl-groups "incorporated into a lipid".

name: a sphingoid base
annotation: Sphingoids (sphingoid bases, or long chain bases) are building blocks of SPHINGOLIPIDS sphingolipids. They are amino alcohols, composed predominantly (but not exclusively) of 18 carbon atoms. They are characterized by the presence of a hydroxyl group at C1 (and often also at C3), and an amine group at C2. The commonly known sphingoids include SPHINGOSINE, D-erythro-Shinganines sphinganine (dihydrosphingosine), and Phytosphingosines phytosphingosine (4-hydroxysphinganine).

name: a chlorophyll
annotation: Chlorophyll is a green pigment found in most plants, algae, and cyanobacteria. The name is derived from Greek (chloros "green" and phyllon "leaf". Chlorophyll absorbs light strongly in the blue and red spectra and has a strong green color. Chlorophylls play a crucial role in photosynthesis, both in the antennae and in the reaction centers. By far most of the chlorophylls are found in the antennae, where they absorb light and transfer that light energy by resonance energy transfer to the reaction center. Some of the cholorophylls (known as P700 and P680) are part of the photosystems reaction centers, where they perform the charge separation reaction, sending electrons to a transfer chain that ultimately results in the reduction of NADP to NADPH.

name: a cyclohexanedione
annotation: Cyclohexanediones are cyclohexanones carrying two oxo substituents.

name: an α-glucosyl-hydroxymethylcytosine in DNA
annotation: Phage T4 DNA differs from typical DNA molecules in that it contains no CYTOSINE. Instead, in contains the modified base CPD-15531 which pairs with GUANINE. The hydroxy group is often glucosylated, as in Alpha-Glycosylated-Hydroxymethyl-DNAs "α-glucosyl-hydroxymethylcytosine", Beta-Glycosylated-Hydroxymethylated-DNAs "β-glucosyl-hydroxymethylcytosine" and Gluc-Glycosyl-Hydroxymethylcytosine-DNAs "β-glucosyl-(1→4)-β-glucosyl-hydroxymethylcytosine", so that T4 DNA is typically covered with sugar chains.

annotation: The R group in the structure contains an aromatic ring.

name: a phenolphthiotriol B
annotation: Phenolphthiotriol Phenolphthiotriols are intermediates in Phenolphthiocerols phenolphthiocerol biosynthesis. Phenolphthiocerol glycolipids have been found in many Mycobacterium species, including TAX-1769, TAX-1768, TAX-1765, TAX-1781, and TAX-78331 . Two types of phenolphthiotriol exist. In Phenolphthiotriol-A "phenolphthiotriol A" the first hydroxyl and the methyl groups are attached at positions 3 and 4, respectively, and the other hydroxyl groups are at positions 9 and 11. In Phenolphthiotriol-B "phenolphthiotriol B" a methyl group is missing from the beginning of the molecule, and thus the first hydroxyl and the methyl groups are attached at positions 2 and 3, respectively, and the other hydroxyl groups are at positions 8 and 10.

name: a naphthoquinone
annotation: Naphthoquinones are polycyclic aromatic ketone metabolite of NAPHTHALENE.

name: a mono-rhamnolipid
annotation: This class contains glycosides composed of a single rhamnose moiety joined in an O-glycosidic linkage to a non-glycero lipid moiety. The lipid moiety is mainly two (sometimes one or three) β-hydroxy fatty acid chains (represented here in part by R1 and R2) linked to each other through an ester bond between the β-hydroxyl group of the chain distal to the glycosidic bond with the carboxyl group of the chain proximal to the glycosidic bond. The carboxyl group of the distal chain is usually free, but in a few cases it is esterified with a short alkyl group. The fatty acid chains may be saturated, mono-unsaturated, or poly-unsaturated and the chain length may vary from C8 to C16. In rare cases the rhamnose 2-hydroxyl group distal to the glycosidic bond may be acylated with a long chain alkenoate. . Also see Di-Rhamnolipids and 3-3-HAAs.

name: a [glycerolipid]-ricinoleate
annotation: CPD-12193 Ricinoleate is an unsaturated ω-9 fatty acid (indicating the double bond is located 9 carbons from the methyl end of the molecule). It is a major component of the seed oil obtained from TAX-3988 (Castor bean) seeds. CPD-12193 Ricinoleate is also a major component of he sclerotium (a compact mass of hardened fungal mycelium containing food reserves) produced by the ergot fungus (TAX-5111). Like other fatty acids, CPD-12193 is rarely found in its free form. It is usually found either as CPD-15364 or CPD-17396 "incorporated into a lipid".

name: a phenolphthiocerol B
annotation: Phthiocerols Phthiocerol-based lipids and glycolipids have been found in many species of TAX-1762, including TAX-1773, TAX-1765, TAX-33894, TAX-1781, TAX-1768, TAX-1777, TAX-1769 and TAX-1809 . Phthiocerols comprise a linear carbohydrate molecule to which one methoxyl group, one methyl group, and two secondary hydroxyl groups are attached. Two types of phthiocerols have been described. In Phthiocerol-A "phthiocerol A" the methoxyl and methyl groups are attached at positions 3 and 4, respectively, and the hydroxyl groups are at positions 9 and 11. In Phthiocerol-B "phthiocerol B" a methyl group is missing from the beginning of the molecule, and thus the methoxyl and methyl groups are attached at positions 2 and 3, respectively, and the hydroxyl groups are at positions 8 and 10. The two hydroxyl groups serve as attachment points for fatty acids, in a manner analogous to the much more ubiquitous sn-glycerol. The phthiocerol molecules have a range of 28 to 34 carbon atoms in the main chain, indicating a promiscuity of the enzymes involved in their biosynthesis. Longer molecules have additional carbon atoms added at the ω end of the molecule. Many organisms, including TAX-1769, TAX-1768, TAX-1765, TAX-1781, and TAX-78331, produce the related compound Phenolphthiocerols phenolphthiocerol, which contains a phenol group at the ω end of the molecule . The phenol is usually glycosylated by a type or species-specific mono-, tri-, or a tetrasaccharide. For example, in TAX-78331 the trisaccharide (2,3,4-tri-O-methyl L-fucopyranosyl-(α1→3)-rhamnopyranosyl-(α1→3)-2-O-methyl L-rhamnopyranose) is attached. In addition, the two secondary alcohol functions are often esterified by Mycocerosic-Acids "mycocerosic acids" or Phthioceranic-Acids "phthioceranic acids" (see CPD-18579) .

name: a [mitogen-activated protein kinase]-L-tyrosine
annotation: Mitogen-Activated-Protein-Kinase "Mitogen-activated protein kinases" (MAP kinase or MAPK) are eukaryotic protein kinases that are specific to the amino acids serine, threonine, and tyrosine. They are found in all eukaryotes including unicellular eukaryotes, fungi, plants, and animals. MAP kinases are involved in directing cellular responses to a diverse array of stimuli, such as mitogens, osmotic stress, heat shock and proinflammatory cytokines. They regulate cell functions including proliferation, gene expression, differentiation, mitosis, cell survival, and apoptosis . MAP kinases are catalytically inactive in their base form. In order to be active, they need to be phosphorylated. Most of the MAP kinases belong to the "classical MAP kinases" family and need to be phosphorylated on two amino acids, a threonine and a tyrosine, which are found in a TxY motif at the activation loop. Phosphorylation of the tyrosine precedes phosphorylation of the threonine. Some MAP kinases are atypical and require only a single residue in their activation loops to be phosphorylated.

annotation: Hydrazide are compounds derived from oxoacids by replacing the -OH group with -NRNR2 (R groups are commonly H).

name: an α 2,8-linked polysialic acid
aliases: α-2,8-Linked polymer of sialic acid|an α 2,8-linked polymer of sialate
annotation: Polysialic acids are polymers of N-ACETYLNEURAMINATE "sialic acid" and its derivatives. The degree of polymerization is 8 to 200 Sia residues. Polysialic acid is an unusual posttranslational modification that occurs on the neural cell adhesion molecules (NCAMs). In the synapse, the strong negative charge of the polysialic acid prevents NCAM cross-linking of cells.

name: a sesquiterpenoid
annotation: Terpenoids Terpenoids make up a very large family of natural products containing more than 50,000 structurally diverse compounds. They are categorized by the number of C5 isoprene units as follows: Hemiterpenes Hemiterpenoids (one C5 unit), Monoterpenes monoterpenoids (C10; two C5 units), Sesquiterpenes sesquiterpenoids (C15; three C5 units), Diterpenes diterpenoids (C20; four C5 units), Sesterterpenes sesterterpenoids (C25; five C5 units), Triterpenes triterpenoids (C30; six C5 units), C35-Terpenes "sesquarterpenoids" (C35; seven C5 units), and Tetraterpenes tetraterpenoids (C40; eight C5 units).

name: a ribosomally synthesized and post-translationally modified peptide
annotation: Ribosomally synthesized and post-translationally modified peptides (RiPPs), also known as ribosomal natural products, are a diverse class of natural products of ribosomal origin. RiPPs are produced by a variety of organisms, including prokaryotes, eukaryotes, and archaea, and possess a wide range of biological functions.

name: a phenolphthiodiolone
annotation: Phenolphthiodiolone Phenolphthiodiolones are intermediates in Phenolphthiocerols phenolphthiocerol biosynthesis. Phenolphthiocerol glycolipids have been found in many Mycobacterium species, including TAX-1769, TAX-1768, TAX-1765, TAX-1781, and TAX-78331 . Two types of phenolphthiodiolone exist. In Phenolphthiodiolone-A "phenolphthiodiolone A" the oxo and the methyl groups are attached at positions 3 and 4, respectively, and the hydroxyl groups are at positions 9 and 11. In Phenolphthiodiolone-B "phenolphthiodiolone B" a methyl group is missing from the beginning of the molecule, and thus the oxo and methyl groups are attached at positions 2 and 3, respectively, and the hydroxyl groups are at positions 8 and 10.

name: a cis,trans-isoprenoid phosphate
annotation: For isoprenoid and isoprenoid phosphates nomenclature, see Polyisoprenoids.

name: a hexose
annotation: Hexose is the name given to any six-carbon monosaccharide which in its linear form contains either an aldehyde group at position 1 (aldohexose) or a ketone group at position 2 (ketohexose).

name: a glycosylinositolphosphoceramide(44:0)-2
annotation: In this class of glycosylinositol phosphoceramides, a long carbon chain is attached at position R1 to form a fatty amide and a long carbon chain is attached at position R2 to form a long chain base. Either or both of these chains may contain one or more double bonds and may have hydroxyl groups attached. Based on the notation GIPC(m:n)-p, each compound within this class has a total of 44 carbons (m), zero double bonds (n), and two hydroxyl groups (p) present in the sphingoid base and the fatty amide. However, the length, hydroxylation position, double bond position, and double bond orientation of the groups attached at R2 and R3 will differ between individual members of this group of compounds.

name: an asperlicin
annotation: The peptidyl alkaloids of the Asperlicins asperlicin family are valued as therapeutics with promising potential due to their ability to act as potent competitive inhibitors of the gallbladder peptide hormone cholycystokinin (CKK), which is involved in fat and protein digestion .

name: a phytochelatin
annotation: Phytochelatins are the principal heavy-metal-detoxifying components in plant, fungi, and nematodes . They are formed from GLUTATHIONE by EC-2.3.2.15 (better known as phytochelatin synthase) and consist of repeating units (2-11) of γ-glutamylcysteine followed by a C-terminal Gly, Ser, or beta-Ala residue (although CPD-24346 and CPD-24347 are the most common). Phytochelatins play a pivotal role in heavy metal, primarily Cd2+, tolerance by chelating these substances and decreasing their free concentrations.

name: nigeran
annotation: Nigeran (mycodextran) is a water insoluble D-glucan found in the hypal wall of fungi such as TAX-5061 and TAX-36656. This unbranced D-Glucan consists of alternating α-1,3 and α-1,4-glucosidic linkages.

name: an aurachin
annotation: Aurachin alkaloids were originally isolated from the myxobacterium TAX-675526 in the 1980s . They are potent inhibitors of mitochondrial respiration through blockage of NADH oxidation, presumably because of their significant structural similarity to Menaquinones menaquinone . CPD-7733 "Aurachin C" is a particularly effective inhibitor of the two terminal oxidases of TAX-562, CYT-O-UBIOX-CPLX "cytochromes bo" and CYT-D-UBIOX-CPLX "cytochrome bd", whereas CPD-15909 displays a selectivity for inhibition of cytochrome bd . Beside TAX-40, only one other producer species has been discovered - the Gram-positive bacterium TAX-1833, which produces CPD-15906 . Despite the fact that these two organisms are phylogenetically unrelated, the genes that encode the biosyntheric enzymes share some similarity .

name: a [PqsD protein]-S-anthraniloyl-L-cysteine
annotation: PqsD from TAX-287 is a member of the FabH family of condensing enzymes that participates in the synthesis of the signal molecule CPD-12838 (see PWY-6660) and of 4-hydroxy-2-1H-quinolone (DHQ) , a molecule whose function is not yet known (see PWY-6661). PqsD catalyzes the condensation of 2-AMINOBENZOYL-COA (anthraniloyl-CoA) with MALONYL-COA. The enzyme forms an acyl-enzyme intermediate with 2-AMINOBENZOYL-COA, which then reacts with MALONYL-COA to produce the short-lived thioester CPD-19113. This compound forms a branch - in the absence of PqsE it is susceptible to spontaneous cyclization to form 4-hydroxy-2-1H-quinolone. However, when PqsE is present, it removes the CoA moiety, forming CPD-19114 . The enzyme activity was analyzed by TLC analysis of reaction mixtures containing MALONYL-COA and [14C]2-AMINOBENZOYL-COA. Products were quantitated by LC-MS . The crystal structure of recombinant wild-type and mutant enzymes has been determined. The protein was found to be a homodimer and has structural similarities to a variety of condensing enzymes including EG10277 from TAX-562 (see CPLX0-252) .

name: a nitrosamine
annotation: Nitrosamines have a nitroso group bonded to a deprotonated amine.They are formed at low pH (optimal pH of 3.5) by the electrophilic substitution reaction between a nitrozonium cation (NO+) and an amine nitrogen. Nitrosamines are associated with carcinogenic, mutagenic and teratogenic properties . Nitrosamines are traditionally divided into volatile and non-volatile groups. Volatile nitrosamines found in foods include CPD-28107 (NDEA), CPD-18996 (NDMA), CPD-28108 (NPIP), N-nitrozopyrrolidine (NPYR), N-nitrosodibutylamine (NDBA), N-nitrosomorpholine (NMOR), N-nitrozodipropylamine (NDPA), and N-nitrosodibenzylamine (NDBzA), while common non-volatile nitrosamines invclude N-nitrosodiphenylamine (NDPhA), N-nitrosomethylaniline (NMA), N-nitroso-2-methyl-thiazolidine-4-carboxylic acid (NMTCA), N-nitrosoproline, N-nitrosohydroxyproline, N-nitrososarcosine, N-nitrosothiazolidine, N-nitrosothiazolidine-4-carboxylate, N-nitrozooxazolidine-4-carboxylate, N-nitroso-5-methyloxazoldine-4-carboxylate, and N-nitroso-2-(hydroxymethyl) thiazolidine-4-carboxylate

name: an N-acyl-sphingosylphosphorylcholine
annotation: Sphingomyelins Sphingomyelins are a type of SPHINGOLIPIDS sphingolipids found in animal cell membranes, especially in the membranous myelin sheath that surrounds some nerve cell axons. It usually consists of PHOSPHORYL-CHOLINE and Ceramides, but PHOSPHORYL-ETHANOLAMINE can replace PHOSPHORYL-CHOLINE. Sphingomyelins are found in all tissues and lipoproteins; for example, they account for ~5% of the lipid phosphate in liver and 25% in pancreas . Sphingomyelins have a function in signal transduction . Sphingomyelins are highly enriched in the plasma membrane of cells, and were considered to be found largely in the exoplasmic leaflet of the cell membrane. However, there is some evidence that there may also be a sphingomyelin pool in the inner leaflet of the membrane . Moreover, neutral sphingomyelinase-2, an enzyme that breaks down sphingomyelins into ceramides, has been found to localize exclusively to the inner leaflet, further suggesting that there may be sphingomyelin present there. Sphingomyelin can accumulate in a rare hereditary disease called Niemann-Pick Disease. The disease is caused by a deficiency in the enzyme sphingomyelinase, rsulting in accumulation of sphingomyelin in the spleen, liver, lungs, bone marrow, and the brain, causing irreversible neurological damage.

name: an ansatrienin
annotation: The redox pair CPD-21971/CPD-21972 were discovered in 1981 from TAX-68264 under the names mycotrienin I and mycotrienin II . The same compounds were reported one year later from TAX-42684 under their currently used names . The unique structures of this family of compounds are characterized by three conjugated double bonds in the 21-membered macrolactam ring, and a cyclohexanoyl-alanyl side chain attached to the C-11 hydroxyl group of the ansa ring . They exhibit potent activity against fungi and yeasts, as well as cytotoxicity and limited antibacterial activity.

name: a flavan
annotation: A flavan is a type of Flavonoids with a 3,4-dihydro-2-aryl-2H-1-benzopyran skeleton, including its substituted derivatives. It is similar to Flavones, but lacks the 2,3 double bond.

name: α-L-rhamnose-[rhamnogalacturonan I oligosaccharide]
annotation: Rhamnogalacturonans-I "Rhamnogalacturonan I " is a polysaccharide in the Pectin pectin family, consisting of repeating α-(1,2)-L-rhamnose-α-(1,4)-D-GALACTURONATE disaccharide units. About half of the Rha residues are substituted with side chains of various neutral oligosaccharides such as arabinans, galactans, and arabinogalactans. This compound describes an L-rhamnose residue at the non-reducing end of rhamnogalacturonan I.

name: a factor 420
annotation: One of the distinguishing features of methanogenic bacteria is their blue-green fluorescence when irradiated with UV light. This fluorescence is due to F420, a flavin-related redox-active compound that is used as a cosubstrate by many enzymes in these organisms. While it is well-known for its role in methanogenesis, factor 420 has also been found in various eubacteria of the actinobacteria class, such as Streptomyces, Rhodococcus, Nocardioides, and Mycobacterium, where it assists in the synthesis of antibiotics like tetracycline and lincomycin. In addition, a modified form of the factor is also used by cyanobacteria in the repair of DNA damage. Factor 420 (F420), named for its intense absorbance at 420 nm, was originally isolated by Cheeseman et al , and later identified by Eirich et al as the N-(N-L-lactyl-γ-L-glutamyl)-L-glutamic acid phosphodiester of 7,8-didemethyl-8-hydroxy-5-deazariboflavin 5'-phosphate . The PWY-8112 "F420 biosynthetic pathway" produces the intermediate CPD-17242 "F420-0", which contains no glutamate side chains. Glutamates are then added by several enzymes. F420 molecules isolated from different organisms differ in the number of the glutamate molecules in the side chains, as well as the nature of the bond (which could be either α or γ) .

name: a brassinolide sulfate
annotation: Sulfated brassinosteroids, including brassinolide-sulfate, can be produced in vitro, but the single sulfate group present on these compounds may be added at one of several different positions , represented by "R" groups on this compound. Brassinosteroid sulfates have not been detected in vivo in plants to date, but, there is some indirect evidence for their existence .

name: a [glycerolipid]-(5Z,8Z,11Z,14Z,17Z)-icosapentaenoate
annotation: 5Z8Z11Z14Z17Z-EICOSAPENTAENOATE Icosapentaenoate (also spelled eicosapentaenoate, or EPA) is a polyunsaturated fatty acid that contains five cis double bonds, one of which is located at the third carbon from the omega end, which makes it an omega-3 fatty acid. EPA is produced by microalgae and is common in fish who eat the algae, although the fish can not produce it. It is also produced by some marine bacteria, mostly those adapted to low temperatures and high pressures . It is not usually found in higher plants, although trace amounts have been identified in TAX-46147. Humans require either LINOLENIC_ACID or EPA to produce CPD-10244, a primary structural component of the human brain cerebral cortex, sperm, testicles and retina. While humans can not synthesize EPA de novo, they are able to convert the essential fatty acid LINOLENIC_ACID to EPA. Like other fatty acids, 5Z8Z11Z14Z17Z-EICOSAPENTAENOATE is rarely found in its free form. It is usually found either as CPD-14018 or CPD-17282 "incorporated into a lipid".

name: a benzoxazinoid
annotation: Benzoxazinoids were identified in the early 1960s as secondary metabolites of the grasses (including the major agricultural crops maize (TAX-4577), wheat (TAX-4565), and rye (TAX-4550)) that function as natural pesticides and exhibit allelopathic properties . Benzoxazinoids are synthesized in two subfamilies of the TAX-4479 and sporadically in single species of the dicots . They play an important role in defense against pests such as insects, pathogenic fungi and bacteria . The most common benzoxazinoids are CPD-6365 and CPD-6366. Since the aglycone benzoxazinoids are autotoxic, they are stored in glucosylated forms that have reduced chemical reactivity . The glucosides are stored in the vacuoles . This allows the plants to store the toxic compounds in anticipation of pathogen attacks, prompting the suggestion to name these compounds "phytoanticipins".

name: N-acetyl-α-D-glucosaminyl-[hyaluronan]
annotation: This compounds describes an CPD-12541 residue located at the non-reducing end of Hyaluronan.

name: a β-(1,4)-mannan oligosaccharide
annotation: Mannan is a plant polysaccharide that is a polymer of the sugar mannose. This compound class represents short mannan saccharides that are produced by the action of mannanase enzymes. The structure that is shown here represnts a tetrasaccharide and is provided only as an example.

name: dermatan sulfate containing 4,6-di-O-sulfo-N-acetyl-D-galactosamine and L-iduronate 2-O-sulfate
annotation: Dermatan-Sulfate "Dermatan sulfate" is a glycosaminoglycan found mostly in skin but also in blood vessels, the heart valves, tendons, and the lungs. Dermatan sulfate is composed of repeating disaccharide units composed of a hexose sugar and a hexuronic acid, many of which sulfated. The most common disaccharide unit in dermatan sulfate is composed of CPD-12515 and CPD-12557 (many are sulfated) linked by β(1 -> 3) linkages. This compound describes CPD-12557 residues sulfated at both the 4' and 6' positions within the context of Dermatan-Sulfate.

name: an N-(1-deoxy-β-D-fructopyranos-1-yl)amine
annotation: CPD-25244 "Fructosyl-amines" are formed by the non-enzymatic reaction of the aldehyde group of CPD-15374 with an amino group (the Maillard reaction), followed by a spontaneous isomerization of the sugar, referred to as an Amadori rearrangement . An analysis of the form of Amadori products at room temperature has found that they exist at approximately 61% β-pyranose, 6% α-pyranose, 15% β-furanose, 16% α-furanose, and 2% keto forms . The β-pyranose form is displayed here.

name: a [glycerolipid]-stearidonate
annotation: CPD-12653 Stearidonate (SDA) is an ω-3 fatty acid, sometimes called moroctic acid. In mammals it is biosynthesized (in its CPD-14392 form) from LINOLENOYL-COA by the enzyme EC-1.14.19.3. CPD-12653 Stearidonate is found in the seed oils of several plants, including hemp, blackcurrant, corn gromwel and echium, where it is produced from CPD-8117 by EC-1.14.19.25. CPD-12653 Stearidonate is also produced by Group 4 cyanobacteria (such as TAX-1148), where it is esterified at the sn-1 position of glyecrolipids by EC-1.14.19.36. Like other fatty acids, CPD-12653 is rarely found in its free form. It is usually found either as CPD-14392 or CPD-17278 "incorporated into a lipid".

family_key: sm:name:a dietary all trans retinyl ester

name: a chlorophyll a
annotation: Chlorophylls Chlorophyll is a green photosynthetic pigment found in photosynthetic plants, algae, and bacteria. Its name is derived from ancient Greek: chloros = green and phyllon = leaf. Chlorophyll functions as the main light harvesting pigment in photosynthesis, capturing photons and transfering them to the reaction centers. Chlorophyll absorbs mostly in the blue and, to a lesser extent, red portions of the electromagnetic spectrum, hence its intense green color. There are several variants of chlorophyll (CHLOROPHYLL-A, CPD-10334, CHLOROPHYLL-B, CPD-18895, CPD-10336, CPD-10337, CPD-10335, CPD-18896, CPD-18897 and CPD-18898). While the chlorophyl a found in most organisms is esterified with a PHYTOL side-chain, some organisms, such as the green sulfur bacterium TAX-1097, produce a chlorophyll a that is esterified with a CPD-18893 side-chain.

name: a 2-deoxystreptamine antibiotic
annotation: The aminoglycosides are a class of clinical antibiotics that interact with bacterial ribosomal RNA and inhibit protein synthesis. The majority of the aminoglycosides consist of a unique aminocyclitol CPD-10151 core, which is decorated with various aminosugars, leading to diverse structural molecules,including Kanamycins kanamycin, CPD-14154 tobramycin, Gentamycins gentamycin, CPD-14143 ribostamycin, CPD-14140 butirosin, Neomycins neomycin, CPD-14177, Lividomycins lividomycin, CPD-14157 apramycin, and HYGROMYCIN-B.

name: a mycocerosate
annotation: A unique feature of mycobacterial cell wall lipids is the presence of very long chain fatty acids with multiple methyl branches toward the carboxyl end of the carbon chain. Many types of such acids exist, including Mycosanoic-Acids "mycosanoic acids", Mycolipanoic-Acids "mycolipanoic acids", Mycolipanolic-Acids "mycolipanolic acids", Mycolipenic-Acids "mycolipenic acids", Mycolipodienoic-Acids "mycolipodienoic acids", Phthioceranic-Acids "phthioceranic acids", and Mycocerosic-Acids "mycocerosic acids". Mycocerosic acids are 2,4,6-trimethyl- and 2,4,6,8-tetramethyl-2-alkanoic acids present in many pathogenic TAX-1762, including TAX-1773, TAX-1765, TAX-1777, TAX-29311, TAX-1768, TAX-1769 . Unlike the other types of fatty acids found in mycobacteria, the chiral centers bearing the methyl groups have an L (levorotatory) stereo configuration. The name "mycocerosic acid" was suggested by Ginger and Anderson in 1945 . The mycocerosic acids of the mycobacteria are found mainly as CPD-18612 (DIM A) waxes or in phenolic glycolipids. In TAX-1773 the common mycoceroic acids are C30 and C32 . In TAX-1765 the common mycoceroic acids are mainly C26, C27, C29, and C30 .

name: an EpOME
annotation: EpOME is an acronym for EpoxyOctaMonoEnoate. It describes a family of compounds obtained by epoxidation of one of the double bonds of LINOLEIC_ACID, which is catalyzed by a number of cytochrome P450 epoxygenases.

name: aggrecan
annotation: Aggrecan is a proteoglycan, a core protein modified with carbohydrates. The human core protein, encoded by the gene ACAN, is very large (over 250 kDa). Along with Type-II collagen, aggrecan forms a major structural component of cartilage, particularly articular cartilage. Aggrecan consists of two globular structural domains at the N-terminal end and one globular domain at the C-terminal end, separated by a large domain heavily modified with glycosaminoglycans. The two main modifier moieties are themselves arranged into distinct regions, a Chondroitin-sulfates region and a KERATAN-6-SULFATE region.

name: a GlgE-glycogen
annotation: TAX-1773 and other pathogenic mycobacterial species produce a capsular layer outside the bacterial wall and plasma membrane that is thought to be involved in immune evasion and virulence . The capsular layer is composed of α-glucan (80-90%) and arabinomannan (10-20%) polysaccharides, proteins of the ESX-1 secretion system, and small amounts of lipid . The glucan structure resembles Glycogens glycogen, consisting of relatively short chains of α (1->4)-linked glucose molecules, which are connected to each other in a branching pattern by α (1->6) bonds . However, the polymer differs from classical glycogen in seceral aspects. The chains are shorter, consisting of 7 sugars vs 11. In addition, the branching pattern is different - while there are more α-1,6 branches than in glycogen (14% vs 9%), less branches are coming off each branch (1.2-1.6 vs 1.8-1.9 in glycogen) .

name: a benzopyran
annotation: Benzopyran is an polycyclic organic compound that results from the fusion of a benzene ring to a heterocyclic pyran ring.

name: an azo compound
annotation: An azo compound is any compound with the general structure R-N=N-R'.

name: a jasmonoyl-L-phenylalanine
aliases: a phenylalanine-jasmonate conjugate|a JA-Phe|a jasmonate-phenylalanine|a jasmonic acid-phenylalanine|a jasmonic acid-phenylalanine conjugate|a jasmonyl-phenylalanine
annotation: Jasmonoyl-phenylalanine (JA-Phe) has been detected in TAX-3702 . Two stereoisomers of this compound exist in plants - CPD-11235 and CPD-19817 . It has been reported that, unlike JA-Ile, JA-Phe does not promote interaction between the JAZ1/JAZ3 and COI1 proteins from tomato in vitro. However, the compound used was mixture of synthetic (+)-JA-Phe and (-)-JA-Phe diastereomers, and did not include the naturally-occuring CPD-19817 . CPD-11235 [(-)-JA-L-Phe] was tested for its activity in TAX-3702 using three different assays, and did not show any biological activity (supplementary materials). On the other hand, (-)-JA-L-Phe was reported to elicit strongly the biosynthesis of the phytoalexin CPD-7079 in rice . It is possible that the other stereoisomer, CPD-19817, might have some biological activity (as is the case for CPD-11259 vs. CPD-11232) . JA-Phe and its hydroxylated derivatives have been shown to accumulate in TAX-3702 following leaf wounding or fungal infection, although the stereochemistry has not been taken into account in this study as well .

name: N,6-O-disulfo-D-glucosamine
annotation: The sulfated form of CPD-12538 us usually found in Heparan-Sulfate in the form of HEPARIN-GLUCOSAMINE-3-O-SULFATE.

name: a lacto-series glycosphingolipid
annotation: Glycosphingolipids Glycosphingolipids are a family of complex lipids composed of Ceramides and mono- or oligosaccharide moieties that play an important role in various cellular functions including recognition, cell adhesion, proliferation and differentiation . The lipid portion is embedded in the outer plasma membrane leaflet and the sugar chains extended to the outer environment, acting as an antigen. Together with Glycoproteins glycoproteins and Glycosaminoglycans glycosaminoglycans, the glycosphingolipids contribute to the glycocalyx that covers eukaryotic cell surfaces. Glycosphingolipids are synthesized by the sequential addition of sugar residues to ceramide by glycosyltransferases that are specific to each glycosidic linkage. Glycoshphingolipids have been classified into the following 12 series based on their structure: Arthro-Series-GSLs arthro, Gala-Series-GSLs gala, Ganglio-Series-GSLs ganglio, Globo-Series-GSLs globo, Isoglobo-Series-GSLs isoglobo, Lacto-Series-GSLs lacto, Muco-Series-GSLs muco, Mollu-Series-GSLs mollu, Neogala-Series-GSLs neogala, Neolacto-Series-GSLs neolacto, Schisto-Series-GSLs schisto and Spirometo-Series-GSLs spirometo . Lactosyl-Ceramides Lactosylceramide is an important branching point, being a common precursor for the synthesis of the four major classes of glycosphingolipids: the Ganglio-Series-GSLs "ganglio series", the Lacto-Series-GSLs "lacto series", the Neolacto-Series-GSLs "neolacto series", and the Globo-Series-GSLs "globo series" . The core structure that defines the Lacto-Series-GSLs "lacto series" is Galβ(1,3)GlcNAcβ(1,3)Galβ(1,4)Glcβ-Cer. In the diagram below, R1 describes the variable part of the sphingoid base, and R2 describes the fatty acid.

name: feraxan
annotation: Feraxan is a complex polysaccharide consisting of Arabinoxylan arabinoxylan molecules decorated by feruloyl groups that are ester-linked to the arabinose residues. The ferulate residues form cross links between adjacent arabinoxylan molecules. Feraxan is one of the major components of soluble fibre from cereals such as rice and ragi .

name: a methoxylated aromatic compound
annotation: This class includes any aromatic ether that consists of a benzene skeleton substituted with one or more methoxy groups.

name: a γ-lactam
annotation: This compound class stands for Lactams lactams in which the amide bond is contained within a five-membered ring, which includes the amide nitrogen and the carbonyl carbon.

name: a melleolide
annotation: The Melleolides melleolides are a large family of aryl ester secondary metabolites produced by species of the basidiomycete genus TAX-47424, which are composed of an CPD-47 moiety esterified to a tricyclic sesquiterpene (CPD-14796) alcohol . Both CPD-47 and protoilludane derivatives have been described from numerous other organisms , but these two elements are combined into a single entity only in the melleolides, which are not found outside the TAX-47424. Biosynthesis of the sesquiterpene protoilludene moiety is achieved by cyclization of FARNESYL-PP, catalyzed by EC-4.2.3.135, and results in the tricyclic CPD-14796 backbone . In TAX-47429, CPD-47 is biosynthesized by a seven-domain polyketide synthase whose terminal thioesterase domain also catalyzes the coupling of CPD-47 to protoilludene-based alcohols . Modifications of the basic structure include hydroxylation at various positions of the sesquiterpene, oxidation of the primary alcohol at C-1 to an aldehyde or carboxy group, shift or reduction of the cyclohexene double bond, methyl ether formation at O-5', and chlorination at C-6' , resulting in excess of 60 melleolide analogs and making the melleolides one of the most diverse groups of fungal natural products . Various melleolides have been shown to inhibit microbial growth and display phytotoxicity . The antifungal activity has been linked to the position of the double bond within the sesquiterpene moiety . Melleolides have also been reported to have cytotoxic effects on human cancer cell lines by decreasing DNA synthesis .
