annotation: This compound stands for a mixture of CPD-25024 and CPD-25025. In most cases, each of these compounds mutarotates quickly to form a mixture of the two, and thus it is the general rule to use this non-specific form in reactions unless it is specifically known that an enzyme can accept only one of the forms.

name: a [protein] α-D-galactosamine
annotation: O-Linked glycans are polysaccharide or oligosaccharide that are linked to a protein via the oxygen atom in the side chain of a SER or THR residue. In the structure below, R is hydrogen when the modified amino acid is SER and carbon when the modified amino acid is THR.

name: an oxo-ETE
annotation: This class contains C20 polyunsaturated fatty acids that have four double bonds and at least one oxo group. oxoETE stands for oxoEicosaTetraEnoate.

name: a tirandamycin
annotation: The tirandamycins belong to a small group of natural products based on tetramic acid, containing a 2,4-pyrrolidinedione ring system. Compounds on this gamily demonstrate extensive biological activities such as antibacterial, antiviral, antiulcerative, cytotoxic, mycotoxic, and antitumor . In addition to dienoyltetramic acid moiety, tirandamycins possess a bicyclic ketal skeleton that is also found in some other antimicrobial tetramic acid antibiotics, such as CPD-18222, CPD-18221, CPD-18224, and CPD-18223 . Tirandamycins inhibit the bacterial RNA polymerase (RNAP), and inhibit both chain initiation and chain elongation . CPD-18214 "Tirandamycin A" was first isolated from the terrestrial bacterium ORG-6282 , and its absolute stereochemistry was determined by X-ray diffraction shortly after . Tirandamycin A and its other congeners CPD-18216, CPD-18217, and CPD-18218 were later reisolated or discovered from TAX-67297 , the marine-derived TAX-529089 , and TAX-861528, which was isolated from South China Sea marine sediment.

name: L-xylose
annotation: This compound class represents a reducing sugar that exists in solution in multiple configurations. The open-chain structure shown here is provided only as a schematic illustration of chirality. Consult the appropriate class instances for information on specific ring structures and their anomeric configuration.

name: a radical
annotation: Radicals are atoms, molecules, or ions that have unpaired valence electrons. In most cases, these unpaired electrons make radicals highly reactive.

name: a cephalosporin
annotation: A class of β-lactam antibiotics differing from the penicillins in having a 6-membered, rather than a 5-membered, S-containing side ring.

name: callose
annotation: Cell walls of higher plants contain callose, which is a linear homopolysaccharide made up 1-3-beta-D-Glucans with β-1,6 branches. Callose plays an important role in many processes associated with plant development and in response to environmental stimuli in the form of abiotic and biotic stresses. Callose is also a transitory component of cell plate involved in the formation of new walls during cell division. It is a structural component of plasmodesmatal canals, and is also found in developing cell walls of fibre cells and cotton seed hairs . The main enzyme in callose biosynthesis is callose synthase. The molecular mechanism underlying callose biosynthesis is still poorly understood. Molecular studies have revealed a set of genes in Arabidopsis genome that catalyze and degrade callose . 12 glucan synthase like (GSL) genes appear to be involved in male gametogenesis in Arabidopsis . Two genes, GSL1 and GSL5, are also known to play a role in pollen development and fertility . Callose is degraded by β-1,3-glucanases . See for a review.

name: α-amylose
annotation: Starch Starch is a mixture of the two polymeric carbohydrates (polysaccharides) Alpha-Amyloses and CPD-7043. Alpha-Amyloses "α-Amylose" contains up to several thousand glucosyl units linked almost exclusively in linear α(1->4) linkages, with very few branches of α(1->6) linkages. In CPD-7043, on the other hand, branching is common, and occurs approximately every 24-30 glucose units. Alpha-Amyloses "α-Amylose" accounts for 30% of starch, while CPD-7043 accounts for the other 70%.

name: [heparan sulfate]-α-N-acetyl-D-glucosamine 6-O-sulfate
annotation: Heparan-Sulfate "Heparan sulfate" consists of a variably sulfated repeating disaccharide unit which is composed of a hexuronic acid and a hexosamine. The most common repeating unit, which makes up around 50% of the total disaccharide units in heparan sulfate, is composed of CPD-12521 linked to CPD-12541. This compound stand for CPD-12541 sulfated at the C6 position within the context of Heparan-Sulfate, and not at the terminal non-reducing end..

name: a mycolipanoyl-CoA
annotation: This is the acyl-CoA form of Mycolipanoic-Acids "mycolipanoic acids".

name: a diphthamide-[translation elongation factor 2]
aliases: a {3-[4-(2-amino-2-carboxy-ethyl)-1H-imidazol-2-YL]-1-carbomyl-propyl}-trimethyl-ammonium|a 2-[(3R)-3-carbamoyl-3-(trimethylammonio)propyl]-L-histidyl|a 2'-[3-carboxamido-3-(trimethylammonio)propyl]-L-histidine|a peptide diphthamide|a diphthamide in eEF-2|a [translation elongation factor 2] diphthamide
annotation: CPD-17005 Diphthamide is a unique posttranslationally modified histidine residue found only in translation elongation factor 2 (eEF-2). This factor is found in archaebacteria and all eukaryotes, but not in eubacteria, and is conserved from archaebacteria to humans. Diphthamide serves as the target for the CPLX-8048 and for Pseudomonas MONOMER-15589 "endotoxin A". Both toxins catalyze the transfer of ADP-ribose from NAD+ to diphthamide on eEF-2, inactivating it and halting cellular protein synthesis, causing cell death .

name: a [glycerolipid]-crepenynate
annotation: CREPENYNATE Crepenynate (crepenynic acid) is a fatty acid with an 18-carbon chain containing a cis double bond at position 9 and a triple bond at position 12. Like other fatty acids, CREPENYNATE is rarely found in its free form. It is usually found Glycerolipid-crepenynate "incorporated into a lipid". CREPENYNATE Crepenynate makes up to 70% of the fatty acids in triacylglycerol from the seed oil of the plant TAX-72610 .

name: a steryl-ester
annotation: In steryl esters, one acyl chain, denoted by R1, is connected to the sterol backbone via an ester linkage on the C3 carbon. For unsaturated acyl chains, the number, location, and orientation of the double bonds will vary between individual members of this group of compounds. The subclass is determined by the aryl chain on the backbone denotated by the R2 group.

name: an (S)-2-halocarboxylate
annotation: The X in this structure represents a halogen.

name: heparan sulfate containing a glucuronate--N,6-O-disulfo-D-glucosamine disaccharide
annotation: Heparan sulfate is a member of the Glycosaminoglycans "glycosaminoglycan" family. It consists of a variably sulfated repeating disaccharide unit which is composed of a hexuronic acid and a hexosamine. The most common repeating unit, which makes up around 50% of the total disaccharide units in heparan sulfate, is composed of CPD-12521 linked to CPD-12541. This alternative unit, which is a substrate for heparanase, consists of CPD-12521 linked to N6-O-disulfo-D-glucosamine .

name: a hemiterpene
annotation: Terpenoids Terpenoids make up a very large family of natural products containing more than 50,000 structurally diverse compounds. They are categorized by the number of C5 isoprene units as follows: Hemiterpenes Hemiterpenoids (one C5 unit), Monoterpenes monoterpenoids (C10; two C5 units), Sesquiterpenes sesquiterpenoids (C15; three C5 units), Diterpenes diterpenoids (C20; four C5 units), Sesterterpenes sesterterpenoids (C25; five C5 units), Triterpenes triterpenoids (C30; six C5 units), C35-Terpenes "sesquarterpenoids" (C35; seven C5 units), and Tetraterpenes tetraterpenoids (C40; eight C5 units).

name: a prostaglandin
annotation: The term prostanoid is used to describe a subclass of Icosanoids icosanoids that includes the Prostaglandins prostaglandins and the Thromboxanes thromboxanes. These lipid compounds are derived from the ω-6 essential fatty acid ARACHIDONIC_ACID. Every prostanoid contains 20 carbon atoms with an internal carbon ring structure. Prostaglandins are messenger molecules produced de novo by most tissues and organs that act in an autocrine or paracrine fashion to mediate both homeostatic and inflammatory reactions. The name prostaglandin derives from the fact that initially it was thought to be present mainly in prostate secretions. See also PWY66-374.

name: glycerophosphoinositol
annotation: CPD-26208 Glycerophosphoinositol (GPI) is an ionic compatible solute only found thus far in hyperthermophilic bacteria and archaea of the genera TAX-2713 and TAX-2233, respectively . Different stereoisomers of this compound accumulate in archaea and bacteria, respectively. In bacteria, the first enzyme of the pathway, EC-2.7.7.39, has a storng or absolute preference for GLYCEROL-3P, leading to production of CPD-541. In archaea, the comparable enzyme has a preference for SN-GLYCEROL-1-PHOSPHATE, resulting in formation of CPD-26207 .

name: melanochrome
annotation: Spectroscopic studies of the biosynthesis of CPD-12379 showed that it takes place in three chromophoric phases. The first phase corresponds to the formation of a red pigment (λmax 475 nm), L-DOPACHROME. The second phase corresponds to a purple intermediate, which was designated CPD-12378, with a broad absorption maximum at 540 nm, and the third phase is characterized by a general absorption due to CPD-12379 . Chemical analysis of CPD-12378 suggests that it is composed of different oligomers (mostly dimers and trimers) of DIHYDROXYINDOLE (DHI) and 56-DIHYDROXYINDOLE-2-CARBOXYLATE linked by 2-4' and 2-7' linkages .

name: L-rhamnopyranose
annotation: This compound stands for a mixture of RHAMNOSE and CPD0-1112. In most cases, each of these compounds mutarotates quickly to form a mixture of the two, and thus it is the general rule to use this non-specific form in reactions unless it is specifically known that an enzyme can accept only one of the forms.

name: an [H. volcanii protein]-L-asparagine-[tetrasaccharide]
annotation: The pentasaccharide decorating at least four ASN residues of the TAX-2246 S-layer glycoprotein (namely Asn-13, Asn-83, Asn-274 and Asn-279) is composed of β-D-Man-(1→2)-[methyl-O-4]-β-D-GlcA-(1→4)-α-D-GalA-(1→4)-β-D-GlcA-(1→4)-β-D-Glc-Asn-[protein] .

name: a monobactam
annotation: A class of β-lactam antibiotics that lack the double ring construction of the traditional β-lactam antibiotics. Monolactams are not sensitive to metallo-β-lactamases . CPD-9235 Aztreonam is a U.S. Food and Drug Administration-approved monobactam in clinical use, and has proved efficacious in the clinical treatment of many multidrug-resistant infections, eradicating those caused by bacteria resistant to the most broadly active antibiotics, including meropenem, vancomycin, and imipenem .

name: a phenolphthiodiolone B
annotation: Phenolphthiodiolone Phenolphthiodiolones are intermediates in Phenolphthiocerols phenolphthiocerol biosynthesis. Phenolphthiocerol glycolipids have been found in many Mycobacterium species, including TAX-1769, TAX-1768, TAX-1765, TAX-1781, and TAX-78331 . Two types of phenolphthiodiolone exist. In Phenolphthiodiolone-A "phenolphthiodiolone A" the oxo and the methyl groups are attached at positions 3 and 4, respectively, and the hydroxyl groups are at positions 9 and 11. In Phenolphthiodiolone-B "phenolphthiodiolone B" a methyl group is missing from the beginning of the molecule, and thus the oxo and methyl groups are attached at positions 2 and 3, respectively, and the hydroxyl groups are at positions 8 and 10.

name: a carboxyadenylated [SAMP] C-terminal glycine
annotation: Archaea have a mechanism of protein post-translational modification, termed SAMPylation, that shares many biochemical features with the ubiquitination system of eukaryotes. The archaeal equivalents of ubiquitin have been named Small Archaeal Modifier Proteins (SAMPs). Like ubiquitin, these proteins possess a β-grasp fold and carboxy-terminal diglycine motif, and as in ubiquitination, the C-terminal carboxylate forms an isopeptide bond with the ε-amino group of lysine residues on target proteins . The C-terminal glycine of SAMP2 can form a bond with a lysine residue of another SAMP2 molecule, forming poly-SAMP chains, as in the case of polyubiquitination. This structure shows the C-terminal glycine residue of SAMP forming a covalent bond with adenylate, an intermediate in the reaction catalyzed by EC-6.2.1.55.
family_key: sm:name:a carboxyadenylated samp c terminal glycine

name: an ansamycin
annotation: Ansamycins Ansamycins are a class of bacterial secondary metabolites that includes several important antibiotics such as the Streptovaricins streptovaricins and Rifamycins rifamycins. They are named ansamycins (from the Latin ansa, handle) because of their unique structure, which consists of an aromatic moiety bridged at nonadjacent positions by a polyketide-derived aliphatic chain through an amide linkage . The different members of the ansamycin family differ mainly in their aromatic moiety, which can include a naphthalene ring, a naphthoquinone ring, a benzene ring or or a benzoquinone ring. Beyond their interesting structures, the ansamycins display a number of significant bioactivities including RNA polymerase inhibition , heat shock protein HSP90 inhibition , and antiproliferation activities .

name: a bile acid
annotation: Bile (or gall) is a dark green to yellowish brown fluid, produced by the liver of most vertebrates, that aids the digestion of lipids in the small intestine . In humans bile is produced continuously by the liver and stored and concentrated in the gall bladder. After eating, the bile is discharged into the the first section of the small intestine (the duodenum). The main active ingredient (about 0.7%) of the bile is bile acids. Bile acids are classified into primary and secondary. The primary bile acids are produced in the liver from CHOLESTEROL, while the secondary bile acids are produced by gut bacteria that modify the primary acids. About half of the cholesterol produced in the body is used for bile acid synthesis. The primary bile acids are conjugated in the liver with GLY or TAURINE before secretion into the bile. The structures and proportions of the different bile acids vary among different species . Bile acids also act as steroid hormones through their interaction with receptors encoded by the NR1H4 and GPBAR1 genes .

name: L-xylopyranose
annotation: This compound stands for a mixture of CPD-21258 and CPD-21257. In most cases, each of these compounds mutarotates quickly to form a mixture of the two, and thus it is the general rule to use this non-specific form in reactions unless it is specifically known that an enzyme can accept only one of the forms.

name: a cardanol
annotation: Cardanols are a group of related phenols obtained from Anacardic-Acid "anacardic acids", a common component of cashew nutshells with anitibacterial activity, that consists of a CPD-110 core substituted by saturated or unsaturated alkyl chains of 15-17 carbons. Heating anacardic acids results in decarboxylation and their conversion to cardanols without destroying their activity. The most common form of cardanol is CPD-14641.

name: ascarylose
annotation: Ascarylose is a reducing sugar that exists in solution in multiple configurations. The open-chain structure shown here is provided only as a schematic illustration of chirality. Consult the appropriate class instances for information on specific ring structures and their anomeric configuration.

name: a sphing-8(Z)-enine-ceramide
annotation: The predominant long-chain base in animal ceramides is sphingosine, which is only present in trace amount in plant ceramides. In contrast, sphinganine, 4-hydroxysphinganine, and 4-hydroxy-8-sphingenine are the prevalent long-chain bases in plants. The acyl chain of plant ceramides contains alpha-hydroxylated very long chain fatty acids. The plant ceramide (IV) in MetaCyc represents plant ceramides with 4-hydroxy-8-sphingenine as the long-chain base.

name: a cannabinoid
annotation: Cannabinoid are a diverse group of pharmacologically active secondary metabolites. While they are named after the Cannabis plant, which forms many of these compounds, some cannabinoids are produced natively by humans and animals.

name: an isoflavonoid
annotation: An isoflavanoid is a type of Flavonoids with an aryl substituent at position 3. They are biologically active compounds, such as the phytoestrogens produced by pea family plants.

name: a bulgecin
annotation: Bulgecins are O-sulphonated glycopeptides produced by Pseudomonas acidophila and Pseudomonas mesoacidophila that potentiate the antibacterial activity of -lactam antibiotics and produce characteristic bulges when added to bacteria such as Escherichia coli in association with β-lactams.

name: an alternan
annotation: Alternan is a branched glucan with a unique backbone structure of alternating α-(1→6) and α-(1→3) linkages, produced from sucrose by rare strains of the bacterium TAX-1245 Leuconostoc mesenteroides.

name: an α-limit dextrin
aliases: a glycogen phosphorylase-limit dextrin|a glycogen phosphorylase-limited dextrin
annotation: CPD0-971 "A limit dextrin" is the name given to the highly-branched core of the polysaccharide fragments remaining at the end (limit) of exhaustive hydrolysis of CPD-7043 amylopectin or Glycogens glycogen by α-1,4-glucan hydrolase enzymes such as GLYCOPHOSPHORYL-CPLX and MALDEXPHOSPHORYL-CPLX. These enzymes shorten the chains, but are not able to hydrolyze the α-1,6 bonds at branch points. Since the branching residues block the enzymes from getting close, the enzymes leave a tail of 4 α-1,4-linked residues next to the branch points.

name: an amide
annotation: Amides are derivatives of oxoacids in which an acidic hydroxy group has been replaced by an amino or substituted amino group. Most amide compounds fall under Carboxamides carboxamides, where the nitrogen atom is bonded with a carbon atom. However, the term amide also refers to compounds where the nitrogen is bonded to a non-carbon atom (see for example Sulfonamides).
notes: audit_collides_with=molecule.ecocyc.Amides

name: a purine nucleoside
annotation: Nucleosides are glycosylamines consisting of Bases bound to a CPD0-1110 ribose or 2-DEOXYRIBOSE sugar via a β-glycosidic linkage. The nucleosides are called Ribonucleosides ribonucleosides when the sugar is CPD0-1110 ribose, and Deoxy-Ribonucleosides deoxyribonucleosides when it is 2-DEOXYRIBOSE. Common Ribonucleosides ribonucleosides include CYTIDINE, URIDINE, ADENOSINE, GUANOSINE and INOSINE. Examples of Deoxy-Ribonucleosides deoxyribonucleosides include THYMIDINE and DEOXYADENOSINE. When nucleosides are phosphorylated on the sugar's primary alcohol group, the resulting phosphonucleosides are called Nucleotides nucleotides. Purines are heterocyclic aromatic compounds that contain a PYRIMIDINE-RING ring fused to an IMIDAZOLE-RING ring. Two of the ribonucleosides in nucleic acids, ADENOSINE and GUANOSINE, are purines.

name: a 12-hydroxyjasmonate sulfate
aliases: a 12-OHJA sulfate|a 12-hydroxyjasmonic acid sulfate|a SO4-hydroxy-JA|a 12-hydroxy-JA sulfate
annotation: This compound has been detected in Arabidopsis thaliana and many other plant species . This compound may not be biologically active. Unlike methyl JA, it does not inhibit germination or root growth in tomato seedlings . It is possible that at least two stereoisomers of this compound, with different different bond configurations on the cyclopentanone ring, exist in plants.

name: a thiazoline siderophore
annotation: Siderophores are small, high-affinity iron chelating compounds usually produced and secreted by microorganisms. Once outside the cell, siderophores bind FE+3 with high affinity, and the iron-siderophore complexes are imported back into the cell by active-transport mechanisms. Siderophores are very common in pathogenic bacteria, since iron in the host organism is usually tightly bound to proteins, resulting in an extremely low concentration of free iron in the blood. Most siderophores belong to one of three major groups: the Catecholate-siderophores "catechols", Hydroxamate-siderophores "hydroxamates" and Thiazoline-siderophores "thiazolines". Some grasses are known to make siderophores as well. These Phytosiderophores "phytosiderophores" are also known as mugineic acids. Thiazoline siderophores are named based on the presence of a thiazoline (5-membered heterocyclic compound containing both sulfur and nitrogen in the ring) element in their structures.

name: an erythromycin
annotation: This class of macrolide antibiotics includes several wide-spectrum macrolide antibiotics obtained from the actinomycete TAX-1836 (formerly known as Streptomyces erythraeus). The antibiotic Erythromycins erythramycin binds to the 50S ribosomal subunit precursor, as well as the mature form of the 50S subunit of the bacterial 70S rRNA complex, inhibiting its synthesis and mRNA translation, and thus stopping structural and functional processes that are critical for life or replication. See PWY-6977.

name: chrysolaminarin
annotation: Chrysolaminarin is a linear polymer of (1→3) and (1→6) β-linked D-glucose units in a ratio of 11:1 . Chrysolaminarin is a storage polysaccharide typically found in photosynthetic TAX-33634 (heterokonts). It is used as a carbohydrate food reserve by phytoplankton such as TAX-2836 (diatoms), and is one of the most common biopolymers in the world.

name: a tetramethylated mycocerosate
annotation: A unique feature of mycobacterial cell wall lipids is the presence of very long chain fatty acids with multiple methyl branches toward the carboxyl end of the carbon chain. Many types of such acids exist, including Mycosanoic-Acids "mycosanoic acids", Mycolipanoic-Acids "mycolipanoic acids", Mycolipanolic-Acids "mycolipanolic acids", Mycolipenic-Acids "mycolipenic acids", Mycolipodienoic-Acids "mycolipodienoic acids", Phthioceranic-Acids "phthioceranic acids", and Mycocerosic-Acids "mycocerosic acids". Mycocerosic acids are 2,4,6-trimethyl- and 2,4,6,8-tetramethyl-2-alkanoic acids present in many pathogenic TAX-1762, including TAX-1773, TAX-1765, TAX-1777, TAX-29311, TAX-1768, TAX-1769 . Unlike the other types of fatty acids found in mycobacteria, the chiral centers bearing the methyl groups have an L (levorotatory) stereo configuration. The name "mycocerosic acid" was suggested by Ginger and Anderson in 1945 . The mycocerosic acids of the mycobacteria are found mainly as CPD-18612 (DIM A) waxes or in phenolic glycolipids. In TAX-1773 the common mycoceroic acids are C30 and C32 . In TAX-1765 the common mycoceroic acids are mainly C26, C27, C29, and C30 .

name: an S-alkylsulfinate
aliases: an alkyl sulfinate|a sulfinic acid|a sulfinate
annotation: A sulfinate is an organosulfur compound and oxoacid with the general formula RSO(O-).

name: a D-glucuronide
aliases: a glucosiduronic acid|a glucosiduronate|a D-glucuronoside|a D-glucosiduronate
annotation: Glucosiduronic acids are compounds produced by linking glucuronic acid to another substance via a glycosidic bond.

name: an [E2 NEDD8-conjugating enzyme]-L-cysteine
annotation: The NEDD8-carrier-proteins-E2 "E2 NEDD8-conjugating enzymes" perform the second step in the neddylation process. The activity of the enzyme starts with the transfer of NEDD8 from an NEDD8-activating-proteins-E1 "[E1 NEDD8-activating enzyme]"-NEDD8 NEDD8 complex to a catalytic L-cysteine residue at the active site by transesterification. In the presence of the appropriate E3 ligases, the NEDD8 protein is then transferred to a lysine residue of specific substrates.
family_key: sm:name:an e2 nedd8 conjugating enzyme l cysteine

name: a serine phosphoethanolamine
annotation: CPD-538 "Serine ethanolamine phosphate" is found in the animal world in both of its stereoisomeric forms. The CPD-480 "L isomer", first isolated from muscle extracts of the river turtle (TAX-31138) , is present in and is believed to be restricted to the tissues of fish, amphibia, reptiles, and birds . its function is as yet unknown . The CPD-19125 "D isomer" was first detected in and subsequently isolated from the tissues of the earthworm , where it has been shown to be the precursor of the guanidino base, LOMBRICINE .

name: a double-stranded oligodeoxyribonucleotide
annotation: An oligodeoxyribonucleotide is a short nucleic acid polymer with 50 or fewer deoxyribonucleotides. Oligodeoxyribonucleotide can be either single- or double-stranded.

name: a deacetylated heparosan-[core protein]
annotation: Heparan-Sulfate "Heparan sulfate" consists of a variably sulfated repeating disaccharide unit which is composed of a hexuronic acid and a hexosamine. The most common repeating unit, which makes up around 50% of the total disaccharide units in heparan sulfate, is composed of CPD-12521 linked to CPD-12541. This compound stand for CPD-12538 (not-sulfated) within the context of Heparan-Sulfate, and not at the terminal non-reducing end..

name: an oligosaccharide phosphate
annotation: This structure is one example of many oligosaccharide-phosphates.

name: a β-D-glucosyl-(1→4)-β-D-glucosyl-hydroxymethylcytosine in DNA
annotation: Phage T4 DNA differs from typical DNA molecules in that it contains no CYTOSINE. Instead, in contains the modified base CPD-15531 which pairs with GUANINE. The hydroxy group is often glucosylated, as in Alpha-Glycosylated-Hydroxymethyl-DNAs "α-glucosyl-hydroxymethylcytosine", Beta-Glycosylated-Hydroxymethylated-DNAs "β-glucosyl-hydroxymethylcytosine" and Gluc-Glycosyl-Hydroxymethylcytosine-DNAs "β-glucosyl-(1→4)-β-glucosyl-hydroxymethylcytosine", so that T4 DNA is typically covered with sugar chains.
family_key: sm:name:a d glucosyl 1 4 d glucosyl hydroxymethylcytosine in dna

name: a glycosylinositolphosphoceramide(40:1)-2
annotation: In this class of glycosylinositol phosphoceramides, a long carbon chain is attached at position R1 to form a fatty amide and a long carbon chain is attached at position R2 to form a long chain base. Either or both of these chains may contain one or more double bonds and may have hydroxyl groups attached. Based on the notation GIPC(m:n)-p, each compound within this class has a total of 40 carbons (m), one double bond (n), and two hydroxyl groups (p) present in the sphingoid base and the fatty amide. However, the length, hydroxylation position, double bond position, and double bond orientation of the groups attached at R2 and R3 will differ between individual members of this group of compounds.

name: a (22R,23R)-28-homobrassinolide sulfate
annotation: Sulfated brassinosteroids can be produced in vitro, but the single sulfate group present on these compounds may be added at one of several different positions , represented by "R" groups on this compound. Brassinosteroid sulfates have not been detected in vivo in plants, but, there is some indirect evidence for their existence .

name: an anthocyanin
annotation: Anthocyanins are plant pigments that may appear red, purple, or blue depending on the pH. Their name is derived from Greek: anthos = flower kyanos = blue. The anthocyanins are glycosylated forms of Anthocyanidins anthocyanidins.

name: an XLFG xylogulcan
aliases: a xyloglucan modified with galactose and fucose side chains
annotation: Xyloglucan Xyloglucan is the major hemicellulose in dicotyledon type I cell walls. The cross-linking between xyloglucan chains is a major factor in the stability of the three-dimensional matrix between adjacent cellulose microfibrils . Xyloglucan has a backbone of β-1,4-linked cellotetraose units, which are substituted in C-6 by α-D-xylosyl residues at two or three of the first three glucosyl residues, but not in the position closest to the reducing end . Some of the xylosyl residues are subsequently further substituted by (1,2)-β-D-galactosyl residues at the second and/or third xylose residue and by (1,2)-α-L-fucose at the galactosyl unit of the third xylose residue . A common terminology uses G for basic non-substituted glucosyl units within a xylosylglycan, X for glucose residues substituted by α-D-xylose, L for glucose residues substituted by α-D-xylose and β-D-galactose, and F for glucose residues substituted by α-D-xylose, β-D-galactose and α-L-fucose . Most xylosylglucans consist of repeating heptasaccharide Xyloglucans-Xylose XXXG units.

name: a capsaicinoid
annotation: The pungency of chili or hot peppers comes from phenolic alkaloids called Capsaicinoids capsaicinoids. These compounds are exclusively produced in fruits of plants belonging to the TAX-4071 genus. A quarter of the world population consumes hot peppers in some form daily . Structurally capsaicinoids consist of a common aromatic moiety, CPD-9327, modified by a short chain fatty acid that may have variable length and different degrees of saturation . The accumulation of Capsaicinoids capsaicinoids is strictly correlated with developmental stages of the fruit and usually spans the ripening stages after fruit size increase has ceased . The capsaicinoids are localized in the epidermal cells of the fruit placenta . Pungency differs considerably in species ranging from very hot to sweet peppers and has a positive correlation with the level of capsaicinoids biosynthetic gene expression . CAPSAICIN Capsaicin and CPD-9185 are the most common types. Other naturally occurring capsaicinoids include CPD-9182, CPD-9333, CPD-9331, CPD-9181, and CPD-9330, which differ in the fatty acid chain length (n=7-10) and the presence and/or position of a double bond .

name: a β-D-galactosyl-N-acylsphingosine
annotation: In the galactosylceramides (GalCer) found in humans (also called cerebrosides), the hexosyl residues are present in β-anomeric configuration. GalCers with α-configuration occur only in lower organisms and are highly immunogenic for mammals .

name: an HpETE
annotation: This class of hydroperoxy-fatty acids are derived from arachidonate.

name: Forssman globoside
annotation: Haptens are small molecules that elicit an immune response only when attached to a large carrier such as a protein. The FORSSMAN-HAPTEN "Forssman hapten" (Fs), which was described in 1911 , is one of the most potent haptenic sphingoglycolipids . Primates, including humans, lack Fs synthase activity, and have naturally occurring Fs antibodies in plasma. However, some individuals with the enigmatic ABO subgroup Apae were shown to expressed Fs glycolipids on their erythrocites . The compound was also reported from human tumors . Primates and humans have a gene that encodes the Fs synthase, but it encodes an inactive protein . In individuals that do express the Fs antigen, the HS07505 gene contains a mutation that results in an arginine to glutamine change at residue 296. Transfection experiments and molecular modeling showed that the Agr296Gln mutation reactivates the human Fs synthase. Based on these findings, the authors suggested to name a new histo-blood group system in man, FORS .

name: an N6-SAMP-[protein]-L-lysine
annotation: Archaea have a mechanism of protein post-translational modification, termed SAMPylation, that shares many biochemical features with the ubiquitination system of eukaryotes. The archaeal equivalents of ubiquitin have been named Small Archaeal Modifier Proteins (SAMPs). Like ubiquitin, these proteins possess a β-grasp fold and carboxy-terminal diglycine motif, and as in ubiquitination, the C-terminal carboxylate forms an isopeptide bond with the ε-amino group of lysine residues on target proteins . The C-terminal glycine of SAMP2 can form a bond with a lysine residue of another SAMP2 molecule, forming poly-SAMP chains, as in the case of polyubiquitination.

name: an aralkylamino compound
annotation: Aralkylamino compounds are organic amino compound in which an aminoalkyl group is linked to an arene.

name: a TAG(18:2/40:3)
annotation: This class of triacylglycerols contains three acyl chains formed by the esterification of glycerol with three fatty acids. The names of triacylglycerol compound classes provide information about the three acyl chains of each member of the class, using the nomenclature TAG(a:b/y:z) where "a" represents the number of carbons found in one acyl chain and "b" represents the number of desaturations present in chain "a" and where "y" represents the total number of carbons found in the other two acyl chains and "z" represents the total number of desaturations present in the two chains included in "y." Therefore, in this TAG(18:2/40:3) class of compounds, one acyl chain has 18 carbons and two double bonds. The other two acyl chains have a total of 40 carbons and three double bonds. The attachment sites of the acyl chains at the R1 (sn-1), R2 (sn-2), or R3 (sn-3) position along the glyceryl backbone may vary among class members and cannot be inferred from their order in the compound class name. Additionally, the location and orientation of the double bonds in the unsaturated acyl chain(s) may vary among members of the class and are not specified by this notation.

name: factor 430
annotation: CPD-19766 "Factor 430" (often referred to as coenzyme F430) is a nickel containing tetrapyrrole cofactor that is present in all methanogenic bacteria. It was initially observed in 1977 by Jean LeGall in extracts of TAX-145262. The name factor 430 (F430) was coined by Gunsalus and Wolfe who reported the absorbance maximumat 430 nm . The compound belongs to the corphins, a class of compounds that combine structural elements of both Corrins corrins and Porphyrins porphyrins . In 1982 Ellefson et al showed that CPD-19766 is a cofactor of the enzyme EC-2.8.4.1 (methyl-coenzyme M reductase) . This enzyme catalyzes the final step of methane production in all methanogenic bacteria: the reduction of the methyl group in Me-CoM to CH4. F430 exists in two distinct intracellular pools: either bound to EC 2.8.4.1 or free of protein in the cytosol. Early literature on F430 was confusing due to reports of heterogeneity in F430 samples that depended on the isolation and purification procedures used in different laboratories. Later work showed that F430 exists primarily in two isomeric forms. These forms differ in the relative stereochemical disposition of acid side chains at positions 12 and 13 . The biosynthetic route leading to PWY-5196 was discovered only in 2016 .

name: an ε amino acid
annotation: An ε amino acid is a non-proteinogenic amino-acid in which the amino group is located on the carbon atom at the ε position from the carboxy group.

name: β-D-galactosyl-(1→3)-[β-D-galactosyl-(1→4)-N-acetyl-β-D-glucosaminyl-(1→6)]-N-acetyl-α-D-galactosaminyl-R

name: an aminoglycoside
annotation: An aminoglycoside is a molecule composed of a sugar group and an amino group. Several aminoglycoside function as antibiotics that are used to treat certain bacterial infections. Anthracyclines are another group of aminoglycosides, which are used in chemotherapy.

name: a gibberellin
annotation: Gibberellins (GAs) are plant hormones that regulate growth and influence various developmental processes, including stem elongation, germination, dormancy, flowering, sex expression, enzyme induction, and leaf and fruit senescence. All known gibberellins are diterpenoid acids that are synthesized by the terpenoid pathway in plastids and then modified in the endoplasmic reticulum and cytosol until they reach their biologically-active form (see PWY-5052). The gibberellins are named GA1 through GAn in order of discovery. All gibberellins have either 19 or 20 carbon units grouped into either four or five ring systems (see CPD1F-95 and CPD1F-134, respectively). The fifth ring is a lactone ring. GAs containing a tetracyclic ent-gibberellane structure (CPD1F-95) are called C20-GAs, whereas GAs containing a pentacyclic 20-nor-ent-gibberellane structure (CPD1F-134) are called C19-GAs. C20-GAs do not usually have biological activity but can be metabolized to active C19-GAs (note that not all C19-GAs are bioactive).

name: a hydroxyphthioceranyl-CoA
annotation: This class stands for the acyl-CoA form of Hydroxyphthioceranic-Acids "hydroxyphthioceranic acids". In heptamethylated hydroxyphthioceranic acids R1=OH, R2,R3 = H. In octamethylated hydroxyphthioceranic acids, R1= H, R2=CH3, and R3=OH.

name: a keratan
annotation: Keratan is a linear polymer that consists of the repeating disaccharide unit [3)-β-Gal-(1→4)-β-GlcNAc-(1→].. The sugar residues are often sulfated at carbon position 6, in which case the polymer is referred to as KERATAN-6-SULFATE.

annotation: Heparan-Sulfate "Heparan sulfate" consists of a variably sulfated repeating disaccharide unit which is composed of a hexuronic acid and a hexosamine. The most common repeating unit, which makes up around 50% of the total disaccharide units in heparan sulfate, is composed of CPD-12521 linked to CPD-12541. This compound stand for CPD-12544 within the context of Heparan-Sulfate, and not at the terminal non-reducing end..

name: a dibenzooxazepine
annotation: Dibenzooxazepines are organic heterotricyclic compounds consisting of two benzene rings fused to a seven-membered ring containing one oxygen and one nitrogen atom.

name: a cholesteryl ester(18:2)
annotation: In this group of cholesteryl esters, one 18-carbon acyl chain, denoted by R, is connected to the cholesteryl backbone via an ester linkage on the C3 carbon. There are two double bonds present in the acyl chain but the location and orientation of the double bonds will vary between individual members of this group of compounds.

name: an iso-fatty acid
annotation: Iso-fatty acids are a class of branched-chain fatty acids (BCFA) that are characterized by a methyl-substituent located on the penultimate carbon of the carboxyalkyl chain.

name: a [glycerolipid]-calendate
annotation: Like other fatty acids, CPD-8228 is rarely found in its free form. It is usually found either as a CoA-derivative, or Calendoyl-Lipid "incorporated into a lipid".

name: N-(ADP-D-ribosyl)diphthamide-[translation elongation factor 2]
annotation: Diphthamide is a unique posttranslationally modified histidine residue found only in translation elongation factor 2 (eEF-2). It is found in archaebacteria and all eukaryotes, but not in eubacteria, and is conserved from archaebacteria to humans. Diphthamide serves as the target for the diphtheria toxin and for Pseudomonas exotoxin A. Both toxins catalyze the transfer of ADP-ribose from NAD+ to diphthamide on eEF-2, inactivating it and halting cellular protein synthesis, causing cell death .

annotation: The compounds in this class are galactinol-type disaccharide sugar alcohols. One example of the compounds in this class is CPD-458 galactinol.

name: an imine
annotation: Imines are compounds with the structure RN=CR'R''. Imine is used as a suffix in systematic nomenclature to denote the C=NH group excluding the carbon atom.

annotation: This compounds describes an CPD-12541 residue located at the non-reducing end of Heparan-Sulfate.

name: a proanthocyanidin
annotation: Proanthocyanidins are a class of polyphenols found in a variety of plants. They are flavonoid oligomers obtained by the condensation of two or more units of Flavanols flavanols. Many proanthocyanidins are oligomers of CPD-1961 and CPD-7630 and their gallic acid esters. More complex polyphenols, having the same polymeric building block, form the group of tannins. The structure shown here is for illustration only, and shows a generic dimeric proanthocyanidin. Natural procyanidins are highly diverse in size and composition, and the monomers are usually lined up by C-4/C-8 or C-4/C-6 linkages .

name: D-lyxopyranose
annotation: This compound stands for a mixture of CPD-227 and CPD-11896. In most cases, each of these compounds mutarotates quickly to form a mixture of the two, and thus it is the general rule to use this non-specific form in reactions unless it is specifically known that an enzyme can accept only one of the forms.

name: heparin
annotation: HEPARIN "Heparin" is a glycosaminoglycan polymer with a molecular weight ranging from 3 to 30 kDa. It is composed of a variable sulfated repeating disaccharide units composed of hexuronic (either iduronic or glucuronic) acid and glucosamine residues, in which the 2 position of the hexuronic acid and the 2, 3, or 6 positions of glucosamine may be sulfated. The two residues within a disaccharide are linked by a (1->4) bond, as are the disaccharides to each other. The main disaccharide unit consists of CPD-12518 and CPD-12533 [it is often referred to as IdoA(2S)-(1->4)-GlcNS(6S)]. However, CPD-12518 can be substituted by CPD-12521, and CPD-12533 can be replaced by N-ACETYL-BETA-D-GLUCOSAMINE, N-sulfo-D-glucosamine, CPD-12545 or CPD-12565. The basic IdoA(2S)-(1->4)-GlcNS(6S) disaccharide accounts for at least 85% of heparin isolated from beef lung and about 75% of heparin from porcine intestinal mucosa . Variations in the extent and location of sulfation, as well as the conformation of the alternating hexuronic acid residues, lead to a high degree of heterogeneity. By convention, heparin refers to molecules which possess a high sulfate content and large amounts of iduronic acid. The structure of HEPARIN is very similar to that of Heparan-Sulfate (also known as heparitin sulfate). Heparan sulfate contains lower amounts of sulfate and less iduronic acid than heparin . In addition, heparan sulfates disaccharide units are organized into distinct sulfated and non-sulfated domains, while heparin disaccharides are sulfated more uniformly .

name: a branched (1,6)-α-D-mannan with truncated side chain
aliases: a branched α-(1,6)-D-mannan with truncated side chain
annotation: The mannan found in the cell walls of TAX-4932 is composed of a linear chain of Unbranched-1-6-Mannan "α-(1,6)-D-mannan" decorated by side chains and attached to ASN residues of proteins by a linkage unit consisting of two β-(1,4)-N-acetylglucosamine residues. The composition of the side chains varies, but largely consists of α-(1,3)-mannosyl-α-(1,2)-mannosyl-α-(1,2)-mannosyl chains. The side chain can be shorter, and can be branched as well. This structure results from the action of EC-3.2.1.198, which cleaves CPD-12789 off the side chains, leaving single α(1,2)-mannose residues attached to the backbone.

name: a very-long-chain fatty alcohol
annotation: Fatty alcohols are aliphatic Alcohols alcohols consisting of a chain of 3 or more carbon atoms. They may be saturated or unsaturated and may be branched or unbranched. Many enzymes that act on Fatty-Acids "fatty acids", as well as the corresponding Fatty-Acyl-CoAs "fatty acyl-CoAs", Fatty-Alcohols alcohols, and Fatty-Aldehydes aldehydes, have evolved to recognize substrates with a limited range of chain lengths. To classify these enzymes, it is helpful to divide the substrates into smaller groups based on their chain length. The following subgroups have been defined: Short-Chain-Alcohols "Short-chain alcohols" have 3-5 carbons in the longest chain. Medium-Chain-Alcohols "Medium-chain alcohols" have 6-12 carbons in the longest chain. Long-chain-alcohols "Long-chain alcohols" have 13-22 carbons in the longest chain. Very-Long-Chain-Alcohols "Very-long-chain alcohols" have 23-27 carbons in the longest chain. Ultra-Long-Chain-Alcohols "Ultra-long-chain alcohols" have more than 27 carbons in the longest chain.

annotation: This class of triacylglycerols contains three acyl chains formed by the esterification of glycerol with three fatty acids. The names of triacylglycerol compound classes provide information about the three acyl chains of each member of the class, using the nomenclature TAG(a:b/y:z) where "a" represents the number of carbons found in one acyl chain and "b" represents the number of desaturations present in chain "a" and where "y" represents the total number of carbons found in the other two acyl chains and "z" represents the total number of desaturations present in the two chains included in "y." Therefore, in this TAG(18:2/38:0) class of compounds, one acyl chain has 18 carbons and two double bonds. The other two acyl chains have a total of 38 carbons and zero double bonds. The attachment sites of the acyl chains at the R1 (sn-1), R2 (sn-2), or R3 (sn-3) position along the glyceryl backbone may vary among class members and cannot be inferred from their order in the compound class name. name: a 1,3,5-triazine
annotation: The s-triazine compounds have numerous applications throughout industry and agriculture. Those containing N-alkyl substituents have been applied successfully as herbicides.

name: a phenolphthiocerol
annotation: Phthiocerols Phthiocerol-based lipids and glycolipids have been found in many species of TAX-1762, including TAX-1773, TAX-1765, TAX-33894, TAX-1781, TAX-1768, TAX-1777, TAX-1769 and TAX-1809 . Phthiocerols comprise a linear carbohydrate molecule to which one methoxyl group, one methyl group, and two secondary hydroxyl groups are attached. Two types of phthiocerols have been described. In Phthiocerol-A "phthiocerol A" the methoxyl and methyl groups are attached at positions 3 and 4, respectively, and the hydroxyl groups are at positions 9 and 11. In Phthiocerol-B "phthiocerol B" a methyl group is missing from the beginning of the molecule, and thus the methoxyl and methyl groups are attached at positions 2 and 3, respectively, and the hydroxyl groups are at positions 8 and 10. The two hydroxyl groups serve as attachment points for fatty acids, in a manner analogous to the much more ubiquitous sn-glycerol. The phthiocerol molecules have a range of 28 to 34 carbon atoms in the main chain, indicating a promiscuity of the enzymes involved in their biosynthesis. Longer molecules have additional carbon atoms added at the ω end of the molecule. Many organisms, including TAX-1769, TAX-1768, TAX-1765, TAX-1781, and TAX-78331, produce the related compound Phenolphthiocerols phenolphthiocerol, which contains a phenol group at the ω end of the molecule . The phenol is usually glycosylated by a type or species-specific mono-, tri-, or a tetrasaccharide. For example, in TAX-78331 the trisaccharide (2,3,4-tri-O-methyl L-fucopyranosyl-(α1→3)-rhamnopyranosyl-(α1→3)-2-O-methyl L-rhamnopyranose) is attached. In addition, the two secondary alcohol functions are often esterified by Mycocerosic-Acids "mycocerosic acids" or Phthioceranic-Acids "phthioceranic acids" (see CPD-18579) .

name: an alicyclic ketone
annotation: Alicyclic ketones are cyclic ketone in which the carbocyclic ring structure may be saturated or unsaturated, but may not be a benzenoid or other aromatic system.

name: α-D-glucosaminide-[heparan sulfate]
annotation: This compounds describes an CPD-12538 residue located at the non-reducing end of Heparan-Sulfate.

name: a myxoxanthophyll
annotation: Myxoxanthophylls are a class of glycosylated carotenoids found in cyanobacteria. The carotenoid portion of these molecules ( = aglicone) is myxol, and the sugar moiety differs among the different species .

name: a plastoquinone
annotation: PLASTOQUINONE Plastoquinones (PQ) are benzoquinone electron carriers that can function as acceptor/donor for either one or two electrons. They are analogs of Ubiquinones "ubiquinones" (Coenzyme Q), with two methyl groups substituting the methoxy groups found in Q. The fully reduced form is called Plastoquinols. Plastoquinones are found in the chloroplasts of higher plants, red, brown and green algae, and in cyanobacteria, and are important members of the photosynthetic electron transfer chain, where they carry electrons from CPLX490-3506 to the CPLX-9022. In fact, it has been shown that in cyanobacteria essentially all electron transport through unbound quinones proceeds via PQ in cyanobacterial thylakoids. Apart from PQ the only quinone found in thylakoids is vitamin K1, which is associated with PS I . Plastoquinones also serve as cofactors involved in the desaturation of phytoene in the synthesis of carotenoids (see PWY-6475). Plastoquinone was isolated originally in 1946 from alfalfa, but was not identified at the time. It was rediscovered in 1959 and its structure was described in the same year (see ). Please note that tyhe structure shown here is that of PLASTOQUINONE-9 and is not accurate for this generic class of compounds, which contains plastoquinones with different side-chain lengths.

name: λ-carrageenan
annotation: Carrageenans Carrageenans and Carrageenoses carrageenoses are sulfated polysaccharides that are extracted from sea weeds belonging to the TAX-2763 (red algae), mainly from TAX-2769, and species from the TAX-31444 and TAX-33664 genera. They are laid out in the cell wall at a high density and can constitute up to 50% of the algae dry mass. The basic units of Carrageenans carrageenans are repeats of alternating α-1,3-linked D-galactose and β-1,4-linked D-galactose units, which are often substituted by sulfate. Carrageenans are classified according to the number and the position of sulfated ester . In Carrageenoses carrageenoses the α-1,3-linked galactose units are modified by having a 3,6-anhydro bridge. These bridges, which form a double helix during aggregation, are responsible for the gelation that is typical to these compounds. Despite this distinction, carrageenoses are often referred to as carrageenans. The three most industrially exploited carrageenans, namely, kappa-Carrageenan, iota-Carrageenan and Lambda-Carrageenan, are distinguished by the presence of one, two or three ester-sulfate groups per repeating disaccharide unit respectively. Lambda-Carrageenan is sulfated at position 2 of the β-1,4 linked D-galactose units and positions 2 and 6 of the α-1,3-linked galactose units. Some carrabiose units of the λ-carrageenan from TAX-2765380 are substituted with four sulfate groups (D2S,6S-G2S,4S) . Since λ-carrageenans do not feature 3,6-anhydro-bridges, they do not make physical gels.

name: a glycosylinositol phosphoceramide
annotation: Glycosylinositol phosphoceramides (GIPCs) are found only in plants, fungi, and some protozoa. For GIPC biosynthesis, Ceramides is trafficked to the Golgi, where a phosphorylinositol headgroup is added to form Inositol-Phosphoceramides (IPC) by EC-2.7.1.227 (IPC synthase) . The IPC core can then be glycosylated by various glycosyltransferases to produce mature GIPCs . The biosynthesis of IPCs is well conserved between plants and fungi . However, plant GIPCs are more highly glycosylated than those in S. cerevisiae, usually containing α-glucuronate linked to the IPC core structure, to which additional sugar units such as glucosamine (GlcN), N-acetyl-glucosamine (GlcNAc), mannose (Man), and arabinose (Ara) may be attached . In this class of glycosylinositol phosphoceramides, a long carbon chain is attached at position R1 to form a fatty amide and a long carbon chain is attached at position R2 to form a long chain base. Either or both of these chains may contain one or more double bonds and may have hydroxyl groups attached. To describe subclasses of this class, the following notation is used: GIPC(m:n)-p. The total number of carbons present in the sphingoid base and the fatty amide is "m" while the total number of double bonds is "n." The total number of hydroxyl groups (not including the hydroxyl group used as the site of the head group attachment) is "p."

name: a cycloalkene
annotation: Cycloalkenes are cyclic hydrocarbons with one carbon-carbon double bond.

name: a pheophytin
annotation: Pheophytins are degradation products of chlorophylls, which lack the center Mg atom.

name: a purine base
annotation: Nucleobases are the parts of nucleic acids (DNA and RNA) that are involved in pairing of the two strands. The primary nucleobases are CYTOSINE, GUANINE, ADENINE (found in both DNA and RNA), THYMINE (found only in DNA) and URACIL (found only in RNA), abbreviated as C, G, A, T, and U, respectively. There are many additional nucleobases, usually derived from the primary ones. Based on their structure, nucleobases are divided into Purines purines and Pyrimidine-Bases pyrimidines. Purines are heterocyclic aromatic compounds that contain a PYRIMIDINE-RING ring fused to an IMIDAZOLE-RING ring. Two of the bases in nucleic acids, ADENINE and GUANINE, are purines.

name: a benzosemiquinone
annotation: There are three isomers of Benzosemiquinones benzosemiquinones: CPD-12560, CPD-12561, and BENZOSEMIQUINONE.
notes: audit_collides_with=molecule.ecocyc.Benzosemiquinone

name: a phenolphthiodiolone A
annotation: Phenolphthiodiolone Phenolphthiodiolones are intermediates in Phenolphthiocerols phenolphthiocerol biosynthesis. Phenolphthiocerol glycolipids have been found in many Mycobacterium species, including TAX-1769, TAX-1768, TAX-1765, TAX-1781, and TAX-78331 . Two types of phenolphthiodiolone exist. In Phenolphthiodiolone-A "phenolphthiodiolone A" the oxo and the methyl groups are attached at positions 3 and 4, respectively, and the hydroxyl groups are at positions 9 and 11. In Phenolphthiodiolone-B "phenolphthiodiolone B" a methyl group is missing from the beginning of the molecule, and thus the oxo and methyl groups are attached at positions 2 and 3, respectively, and the hydroxyl groups are at positions 8 and 10.

name: a 4-hydroxysphing-8-enine-22:0 ceramide
annotation: In plants, there will often be a racemic mixture of molecules with cis and trans configurations of the double bond at position 8 in the sphingenine .

name: a ceramide phosphorylethanolamine
annotation: Sphingomyelins Sphingomyelins are a type of SPHINGOLIPIDS sphingolipids found in animal cell membranes, especially in the membranous myelin sheath that surrounds some nerve cell axons. It usually consists of PHOSPHORYL-CHOLINE and Ceramides, but PHOSPHORYL-ETHANOLAMINE can replace PHOSPHORYL-CHOLINE. Sphingomyelins are found in all tissues and lipoproteins; for example, they account for ~5% of the lipid phosphate in liver and 25% in pancreas . Sphingomyelins have a function in signal transduction . Sphingomyelins are highly enriched in the plasma membrane of cells, and were considered to be found largely in the exoplasmic leaflet of the cell membrane. However, there is some evidence that there may also be a sphingomyelin pool in the inner leaflet of the membrane . Moreover, neutral sphingomyelinase-2, an enzyme that breaks down sphingomyelins into ceramides, has been found to localize exclusively to the inner leaflet, further suggesting that there may be sphingomyelin present there. Sphingomyelin can accumulate in a rare hereditary disease called Niemann-Pick Disease. The disease is caused by a deficiency in the enzyme sphingomyelinase, rsulting in accumulation of sphingomyelin in the spleen, liver, lungs, bone marrow, and the brain, causing irreversible neurological damage. Ceramide phosphorylethanolamine lipids were also reported from the anaerobic bacterium TAX-837 .

name: [keratan sulfate]-α-N-acetyl-D-glucosamine
annotation: Keratan sulfate is the name given to any of several sulfated Glycosaminoglycans "glycosaminoglycans" that have been found, especially in the cornea, cartilage, and bone. It is a linear polymer that consists of a repeating disaccharide unit. The basic repeating disaccharide unit consists of GALACTOSE and N-ACETYL-BETA-D-GLUCOSAMINE, either of which can be sulfated at carbon position 6. This compound stand for a non-sulfated N-ACETYL-BETA-D-GLUCOSAMINE within the context of KERATAN-6-SULFATE, and not at the terminal non-reducing end..

name: an aldoxime
annotation: Oximes "An oxime" is a class of chemical compounds with the general formula (R1)(R2)HC=NOH. Aldoxime An aldoxime is an oxime where R1 is a hydrogen, and is is usually generated by the reaction of HYDROXYLAMINE and Aldehydes aldehydes. The term oxime is derived from a condensation of the words oxygen and Imides imide.

name: [rhamnogalacturonan I oligosaccharide]-α-L-rhamnose
annotation: Rhamnogalacturonans-I "Rhamnogalacturonan I " is a polysaccharide in the Pectin pectin family, consisting of repeating α-(1,2)-L-rhamnose-α-(1,4)-D-GALACTURONATE disaccharide units. About half of the Rha residues are substituted with side chains of various neutral oligosaccharides such as arabinans, galactans, and arabinogalactans. This compound describes an L-rhamnose residue at the reducing end of rhamnogalacturonan I.

name: a dimycocerosyl phthiocerol
annotation: CPD-18580 Phthiocerol glycolipids have been found in many species of TAX-1762. The two secondary alcohol functions are often esterified by Mycocerosic-Acids "mycocerosic acids" . CPD-18612 "Dimycocerosyl phthiocerol" (DIM) and CPD-18579 (PGL) are important virulence factors .

name: a chitodextrin
annotation: CHITIN Chitin is a polymer of repeating units of β-(1,4)-linked N-ACETYL-BETA-D-GLUCOSAMINE (β-GlcNAcn). It may be the second most abundant biopolymer in the world, after cellulose. It is the main component of the cell walls of fungi, the exoskeletons of arthropods such as crustaceans and the insects, the radula of mollusks, and the beaks of cephalopods. In nature, chitin polymers are likely to have a high degree of polymerization and are insoluble in water . Chitodextrins Chitodextrins are polymers that are still purely composed of β-(1,4)-linked N-ACETYL-BETA-D-GLUCOSAMINE residues, but have smaller degrees of polymerization than native CHITIN chitin. Though the specific upper limit for defining a chitodextrin is hard to define, a class of related compounds referred to as chitooligosaccharides, composed of both N-ACETYL-BETA-D-GLUCOSAMINE and GLUCOSAMINE typically have a degree of polymerization of 20 or fewer residues . In the structure shown for this compound, R1 will be a hydrogen if the N-ACETYL-BETA-D-GLUCOSAMINE GlcNAc residue that it is attached to is the terminal residue on the non-reducing end of the compound. R2 will be a hydrogen if the N-ACETYL-BETA-D-GLUCOSAMINE GlcNAc residue that it is attached to is the terminal residue on the reducing end of the compound. However, R1 and/or R2 may also be a single N-ACETYL-BETA-D-GLUCOSAMINE GlcNAc residue or a chain of n N-ACETYL-BETA-D-GLUCOSAMINE GlcNAc residues.

name: [heparin]-α-N-sulfo-D-glucosamine 3-O-sulfate
annotation: HEPARIN "Heparin" is a polymer mainly composed of a repeating trisulfated disaccharide unit composed of CPD-12518 and CPD-12533 linked by a (1->4) bond (often referred to as IdoA(2S)-(1->4)-GlcNS(6S)). Consecutive glycosidic linkages formed between C1 of CPD-12533 in one base unit and C4 of CPD-12518 of another unit result in the formation of the heparin polymer. This compound stand for CPD-12545 within the context of HEPARIN, and not at the terminal non-reducing end..

name: D-apiose
annotation: Apiose is a unique branched-chain pentose found principally in plants. It is a key component of structurally complex cell wall polysaccharides such as CPD-14581 "rhamnogalacturonan-II" (RG-II) in the cell walls of higher plants and in apigalacturonan in the cell walls of aquatic monocots, and is a building block of a large number of naturally occurring secondary metabolites. The name "apiose" is derived from the flavonoid glycoside 7-O-BETA-D-APIOFURANOSYL-12-BETA-D-GLU apiin, from which it was first obtained . Apiin itself is named after parsley (TAX-4043), which at the time was known as Apium petroselinum .

name: a ketocarotenoid
annotation: Ketocarotenoid are a subclass of carotenoids. While carotenes are purely hydrocarbons with no oxygen, and xanthophylls contain oxygen atoms in the form of hydroxyl groups and/or as epoxids, ketocarotenoids contain an oxo group.

name: a TAG(18:3/34:0)
annotation: This class of triacylglycerols contains three acyl chains formed by the esterification of glycerol with three fatty acids. The names of triacylglycerol compound classes provide information about the three acyl chains of each member of the class, using the nomenclature TAG(a:b/y:z) where "a" represents the number of carbons found in one acyl chain and "b" represents the number of desaturations present in chain "a" and where "y" represents the total number of carbons found in the other two acyl chains and "z" represents the total number of desaturations present in the two chains included in "y." Therefore, in this TAG(18:3/34:0) class of compounds, one acyl chain has 18 carbons and three double bonds. The other two acyl chains have a total of 34 carbons and zero double bonds. The attachment sites of the acyl chains at the R1 (sn-1), R2 (sn-2), or R3 (sn-3) position along the glyceryl backbone may vary among class members and cannot be inferred from their order in the compound class name. Additionally, the location and orientation of the double bonds in the unsaturated acyl chain(s) may vary among members of the class and are not specified by this notation.

name: a GXGG xylogulcan
annotation: Xyloglucan Xyloglucan is the major hemicellulose in dicotyledon type I cell walls. The cross-linking between xyloglucan chains is a major factor in the stability of the three-dimensional matrix between adjacent cellulose microfibrils . Xyloglucan has a backbone of β-1,4-linked cellotetraose units, which are substituted in C-6 by α-D-xylosyl residues at two or three of the first three glucosyl residues, but not in the position closest to the reducing end . Some of the xylosyl residues are subsequently further substituted by (1,2)-β-D-galactosyl residues at the second and/or third xylose residue and by (1,2)-α-L-fucose at the galactosyl unit of the third xylose residue . A common terminology uses G for basic non-substituted glucosyl units within a xylosylglycan, X for glucose residues substituted by α-D-xylose, L for glucose residues substituted by α-D-xylose and β-D-galactose, and F for glucose residues substituted by α-D-xylose, β-D-galactose and α-L-fucose . Most xylosylglucans consist of repeating heptasaccharide Xyloglucans-Xylose XXXG units.

name: a jasmonoyl-L-isoleucine
aliases: a JA-Ile|a jasmonate-isoleucine|a jasmonic acid-isoleucine|a jasmonic acid-isoleucine conjugate|a jasmonyl isoleucine
annotation: Jasmonoyl-isoleucine (JA-Ile) has been detected in Arabidopsis thaliana and a number of other plant species . There are two different naturally occurring stereoisomers of this compound, CPD-11232 and CPD-11259, that can spontaneously interconvert through epimerization . In Arabidopsis thaliana, JA-Ile promotes binding between the COI1 F-box protein and the JAZ1 repressor involved in jasmonic acid-mediated signaling . Therefore, JA-Ile promotes the degradation of JAZ1. Initial studies suggested that CPD-11232 "(-)-JA-Ile" was a biologically active phytohormone . However, later work shows that CPD-11259 is the biologically active form of the compound . This compound exists at low levels in most commercial preparations of CPD-11232 and may explain the observed biological activity originally associated with CPD-11232 "(-)-JA-Ile". A pure preparation of that stereoisomer does not promote JAZ/COI1 binding in vitro . JA-Ile has also been detected in TAX-49451 and plays a role in defense responses but it is unclear which form of JA-Ile is active in this process.

name: a sphingoid base 1-phosphate
annotation: Sphingoids (sphingoid bases, or long chain bases) are building blocks of sphingolipids. They are amino alcohols, composed predominantly of 18 carbon atoms. They are characterized by the presence of a hydroxyl group at C1 and C3, and an amine group at C2. The commonly known sphingoids include sphingosine, sphinganine (dihydrosphingosine), and phytosphingosine (4-hydroxysphinganine). Sphingoid bases can be phosphorylated at C1. Phosphorylated shingoids, such as sphingosine 1-phosphate, can be important signalling intermediates.

name: a metalloid cation
annotation: Metalloids are elements whose properties are intermediate between those of a typical metal and a typical nonmetal. The term is somewhat vague, since there is no single property that can be used to unambiguously identify an element as a metalloid. The term is normally applied to a group of between six and nine elements (boron, silicon, germanium, arsenic, antimony, tellurium, and possibly bismuth, polonium, astatine) found near the center of the P-block or main block of the periodic table.

name: a galacturonan
annotation: Galacturonan is a polysaccharide consisting of α-1,4-linked D-GALACTURONATE units. It is the main constituent of the backbone of pectin. Many substituted forms exist.

name: an α-1,6-D-mannan
annotation: There are several types of α-1,6-D-mannan. The proteins in yeast cell walls contain particularly complex N-glycans referred to as α-mannans in which the core N-glycan is extended by ~200 α-1,6-mannose units that are also decorated with α-linked mannose side chains . In some yeasts, such as the human pathogen TAX-5476, β-1,2-mannose residues cap the α-mannans side chains, and extended β-1,2-mannan chains are attached to the α-mannan via phosphate bridges . α-1,6-D-mannan also makes up the backbone of the mycobacterial lipoarabinomannan (LAM), which is attached to the plasma membrane .

name: an 11,13-icosadienoate
annotation: The chemical formula for this long dienoic fatty acid is C20H36O2 and it has a molecular weight of 307.5 daltons, but there are 4 possible structures with different cis and trans configuations within this class.

name: a carboxamidine
annotation: Carboxamidines are compounds having the structure RC(=NR)NR2.

name: a 1,2-β-D-glucan
annotation: β-1,2-Glucan is a homopolymer composed of glucose residues connected by β(1,2) linkages. This type of glucan has been reported from some Gram-negative bacteria, such as TAX-357, TAX-379, TAX-28105, and TAX-234, where it exists mainly in cyclic form . Cyclic β-1,2-glucans have been reported to play a role in osmotic regulation , but were also reported to act as commensal factors in TAX-379 and as infectious factors in TAX-235 . Cyclic β-1,2-glucans are usually found with a degree of polymerization (DPs) around 20, although a much longer glucan (DPs of around 40) has been reported from TAX-382 . Linear β-1,2-glucans have been found in TAX-434 and TAX-338 , and linear glucans with β-1,6-glucosyl branches was reported in TAX-562 and TAX-317 (see CPD-16398) .

name: a TAG(18:3/38:3)
annotation: This class of triacylglycerols contains three acyl chains formed by the esterification of glycerol with three fatty acids. The names of triacylglycerol compound classes provide information about the three acyl chains of each member of the class, using the nomenclature TAG(a:b/y:z) where "a" represents the number of carbons found in one acyl chain and "b" represents the number of desaturations present in chain "a" and where "y" represents the total number of carbons found in the other two acyl chains and "z" represents the total number of desaturations present in the two chains included in "y." Therefore, in this TAG(18:3/38:3) class of compounds, one acyl chain has 18 carbons and three double bonds. The other two acyl chains have a total of 38 carbons and three double bonds. The attachment sites of the acyl chains at the R1 (sn-1), R2 (sn-2), or R3 (sn-3) position along the glyceryl backbone may vary among class members and cannot be inferred from their order in the compound class name. Additionally, the location and orientation of the double bonds in the unsaturated acyl chain(s) may vary among members of the class and are not specified by this notation.

name: a jasmonoyl-L-valine
aliases: a jasmonate-valine|a jasmonic acid-valine|a jasmonic acid-valine conjugate|a jasmonyl-valine|a JA-Val
annotation: Jasmonoyl-valine (JA-Val) has been detected in Arabidopsis thaliana and a number of other plant species . Two stereoisomers of this compound may exist in plants. CPD-11233 is one naturally occurring form of the compound . JA-Val may not be a biologically active form of the hormone in some species. It does not appear to function in herbivory defense responses in TAX-49451 . The CPD-11233 isomer also does not affect seedling root growth in Arabidopsis thaliana nor does it promote JAZ/COI1 interactions in vitro.

name: a heteroarene
annotation: An aromatic compound contains one or more aromatic rings. The pi orbitals of the ring's atoms form a conjugated system and are in resonance, giving increased stability compared to saturated compounds having a similar structure. As a result aromatic rings are very stable and do not break apart easily. Similar structures in which at least one of the ring's carbon atoms is replaced by one of the heteroatoms oxygen, nitrogen, or sulfur, are called heteroarenes.

name: a [glycerolipid]-(4Z,7Z,10Z,13Z,16Z,19Z)-docosahexaenoate
annotation: CPD-10244 Docosahexaenoate (DHA) is a 22-carbon long-chain polyunsaturated fatty acid (LCPUFA) with 6 methylene interrupted cis-double bonds . It is prevalent in the membrane lipids of some human tissues such as brain, testis and retina, and plays a critical role in the development and function of these tissues. It is also a precursor of different classes of icosanoid effectors involved in the regulation of many important functions in mammals . Many of the compounds drived from DHA are listed under DHA-Derived-Metabolites. DHA is an n-3 (ω-3) fatty acid which is defined according to the location of the last double bond at the methyl end. It is synthesized de novo in several microalgae as well as in some fungi. It is also produced by some marine bacteria, mostly those adapted to low temperatures and high pressures . Mammals are not able to synthesis DHA de novo, and instead synthesize it from the essential fatty acid LINOLENIC_ACID (which they obtain in their diet) via alternating steps of microsomal aerobic desaturation and elongation followed by a peroxisomal β-oxidation step . Like other fatty acids, CPD-10244 is rarely found in its free form. It is usually found either as CPD-17312 or CPD-17355 "incorporated into a lipid".

name: [dermatan-sulfate] containing 2-O-sulfo-α-L-iduronate
annotation: Dermatan-Sulfate "Dermatan sulfate" is a glycosaminoglycan found mostly in skin but also in blood vessels, the heart valves, tendons, and the lungs. Dermatan sulfate is composed of repeating disaccharide units composed of a hexose sugar and a hexuronic acid, many of which sulfated. The most common disaccharide unit in dermatan sulfate is composed of CPD-12515 and CPD-12557 (many are sulfated) linked by β(1 -> 3) linkages. This compound stand for CPD-12518 within the context of Dermatan-Sulfate.
family_key: sm:name:dermatan sulfate containing 2 o sulfo l iduronate

name: mycoside B
annotation: CPD-18578 Phenolphthiocerol-based glycolipids have been found in TAX-1769, TAX-1768, TAX-1765, TAX-1781, and TAX-78331 . The phenolic glycolipid CPD-18679 is the major PGL of TAX-1765, and is found in small amounts in hypervirulent strains of TAX-1773 that produce CPD-18579. The phenolphthiocerol backbone in this compound is attached to the monosaccharide (2-O-methyl-α-L-rhamnoside) and the two secondary alcohol functions are esterified by Mycocerosic-Acids "mycocerosic acids" .

name: asolectin
annotation: Asolectin is a mixture of phospholipids obtained commercially from soybeans. It comprises roughly equal proportions of PHOSPHATIDYLCHOLINE phosphatidylcholine, L-1-PHOSPHATIDYL-ETHANOLAMINE phosphatidylethanolamine and Phosphatidylinositols phosphatidylinositol, along with minor amounts of other phospholipids and polar lipids .

name: a [glycerolipid]-dimorphecolate
annotation: CPD-8204 Dimorphecolate, an unusual fatty acid derived from OLEATE-CPD by the concerted activity of two divergent desaturase enzymes, is the major fatty acid (more than 60%) found in seeds of TAX-13490 species . It has three unusual structural features: 1) a C-9 hydroxyl group 2) conjugated Δ10,Δ12 double bonds, and 3) two trans double bonds, rather than the much more common cis bonds. CPD-8204 Dimorphecolate has potential value in industrial applications such as the manufacture of paints, inks, lubricants, plastics, and nylon. Like other polyunsaturated fatty acids, dimorphecolate is not found in the seeds in a free form, but as a CPD-17286 "component of phospholipids".

name: an ecdysteroid 22-phosphate
annotation: This class holds ecdysteroids that are phosphorylated at the C22 position.

name: a flavonoid
annotation: Flavonoids are a class of plant secondary metabolites. Their name is derived from the Latin word flavus meaning yellow, their colour in nature. In higher plants, flavonoids are involved in UV filtration, symbiotic nitrogen fixation and floral pigmentation. They are the most important plant pigments for flower coloration producing yellow or red/blue pigmentation. The term flavonoids is very generic, and is used to describe any organic molecular entity whose stucture is based on derivatives of a phenyl-substituted CPD-20037 1-phenylpropane possessing a C15 or C16 skeleton, or such a structure condensed with a C6-C3 lignan precursors. The following are three of the main classes of flavonoids, although many more exist: Flavones Flavones, which are based on the backbone of CPD-8485 (2-phenylchromen-4-one) Isoflavonoids Isoflavonoids, which are based on the backbone of CPD-15821 Neoflavonoids Neoflavonoids, derived from CPD-15822.

name: a [LuxE]-L-cysteine
annotation: EC-6.2.1.19, encoded by the luxE gene, is part of the fatty acid reductase multienzyme complex found in bioluminescent bacteria. The complex catalyzes the activation, transfer, and reduction of fatty acids to generate fatty aldehydes, which serve as the substrate for the luminescence reaction. The enzyme activates long-chain fatty acids to fatty acyl-AMP, followed by their attachment to a cysteine residue located very close to the carboxyl terminus of the protein. The acyl-protein thioester serves as a substrate for EC-1.2.1.50 which releases the acyl moiety in the form of an aldehyde .

name: a poly-6-phosphogluco-maltodextrin
annotation: Maltodextrins are linear polymers of glucose molecules joined by an O-glycosidic (α-1->4) bond. The term is usally used to describe a mixture of chains that vary from 3-17 glucose units long. Maltodextrin is commonly used as a food additive, and is commercially produced from corn and wheat Starch by partial hydrolysis. This poly-6-phosphogluco-maltodextrin describes a maltodextrin that contains more than one α-linked glucosyl residue that is phosphorylated at the O6 position . While the phophorylation may occur at positions other than those shown in the attached structure, at least two phosphorylated residues exist in the molecule.

name: an 11-hydroxyjasmonate sulfate
annotation: This compound has not been detected in plants, but, its precursor, 11-Hydroxyjasmonates does exist in Arabidopsis thaliana and many other plant species . If this compound does exist, at least two different stereoisomeric forms may be found in plants. If it is functionally similar to 12-Hydroxyjasmonate-Sulfates, then this compound may not be biologically active. Unlike methyl JA, 12-Hydroxyjasmonate-Sulfates "12-hydroxyjasmonate" does not inhibit germination or root growth in tomato seedlings .

name: a TAG(18:3/40:4)
annotation: This class of triacylglycerols contains three acyl chains formed by the esterification of glycerol with three fatty acids. The names of triacylglycerol compound classes provide information about the three acyl chains of each member of the class, using the nomenclature TAG(a:b/y:z) where "a" represents the number of carbons found in one acyl chain and "b" represents the number of desaturations present in chain "a" and where "y" represents the total number of carbons found in the other two acyl chains and "z" represents the total number of desaturations present in the two chains included in "y." Therefore, in this TAG(18:3/40:4) class of compounds, one acyl chain has 18 carbons and three double bonds. The other two acyl chains have a total of 40 carbons and four double bonds. The attachment sites of the acyl chains at the R1 (sn-1), R2 (sn-2), or R3 (sn-3) position along the glyceryl backbone may vary among class members and cannot be inferred from their order in the compound class name. Additionally, the location and orientation of the double bonds in the unsaturated acyl chain(s) may vary among members of the class and are not specified by this notation.

name: a (2R,3R)-dihydroflavonol
annotation: A dihydroflavonol is Flavones hydroxylated at C3 and lacking the double bond between carbons 2 and 3. The name is misleading, since the absence of the double bond makes it Flavanols rather than Flavonols.

name: a cyanohydrin
annotation: A cyanohydrin is an α-hydroxynitrile in which the α carbon is further substituted.

annotation: This compound stands for a mixture of CPD-12045 and CPD-12046. In most cases, each of these compounds maturotates quickly to form a mixture of the two, and thus it is the general rule to use this non-specific form in reactions unless it is specifically known that an enzyme can accept only one of the forms.

name: a cucurbitacin
annotation: The cucurbitacins are a class of bitter-tasting oxygenated tetracyclic triterpenes produced by members of the TAX-3650 and some other plants as a means of defense against herbivores. They are based on the CPD-26233 skeleton and often occur as glycosides. Many cucurbitacins induce a cytotoxic activity that inhibits cancer cell proliferation, actin polymerization, capillary permeability, and anti-inflammatory activity .
