name: (2S,3E)-2-amino-4-(2-aminoethoxy)but-3-enoate
annotation: Oxyvinylglycines are non-proteinogenic amino acids that are produced by certain bacteria. They irreversibly inhibit enzymes that require PYRIDOXAL_PHOSPHATE as a co-factor and thus have multiple targets in bacteria, animals, and plants . One such plant target is the ethylene biosynthesis enzyme EC-4.4.1.14, which is inhibited by CPD-13353 (AVG) . AVG is commercially available under the name of ReTain and is widely used for the regulation of fruit set in orchard crops

name: 4H-imidazole
annotation: The imidazole ring is aromatic due to the presence of a sextet of π-electrons, consisting of a pair of electrons from the protonated nitrogen atom and one from each of the remaining four atoms of the ring. Thus the compound exists in several resonance structures. Imidazole also exists in two equivalent tautomeric forms, IMIDAZOLE-RING and CPD-19703. Different numbering systems have been used leading to confusion in the naming of imidazole compounds. When a side chain exists (as in L-histidine), the nitrogen atoms of the ring are denoted by pros ('near', abbreviated π) and tele ('far', abbreviated T) to show their position relative to the side chain. When a substituent is introduced at positions 4 or 5, the tautomeric character of the molecule precludes a definite numbering of these positions, resulting in names in the form "4(or 5)-substituted imidazole", as in CPD-54 "4(or 5)-aminoimidazole". However, when the imino hydrogen is substituted, tautomerism is no longer possible, and the numbering of positions 4 and 5 becomes absolute, as in CPD-19704.

name: all-trans-retinal
annotation: RETINAL "All-trans-retinal" is a form of vitamin A. Retinal is a polyene chromophore, and bound to proteins called opsins, is the chemical basis of animal vision. Bound to proteins called type 1 rhodopsins, retinal allows certain microorganisms to convert light into metabolic energy. Carnivorous vertebrate animals obtain retinal directly from dietary meat. Vegetarian animals produce retinal from one of four carotenoids: CPD1F-129, CPD1F-118, CPD1F-126, and CPD-7409, which they must obtain from plants or other photosynthetic organisms. Invertebrates such as insects and squid use hydroxylated forms of retinal in their visual systems, which they derive from from other xanthophylls.

name: cisplatin
annotation: CPD0-1392 Cisplatin is a chemotherapy medication used to treat a number of cancers including testicular cancer, ovarian cancer, cervical cancer, breast cancer, bladder cancer, head and neck cancer, esophageal cancer, lung cancer, mesothelioma, brain tumors and neuroblastoma. Following administration, one of the two of the chlorine atoms are slowly displaced by water to give the aquo complex CPD-21200. In the presence of DNA, the water molecule in CPD-21200 is easily displaced by the N-heterocyclic bases, particularly GUANINE. Subsequent displacement of the other chloride, typically by another guanine, results in crsslinking of the DNA. The damaged DNA elicits DNA repair mechanisms, and when repair proves impossible, leads to apoptosis. Cisplatin tends to preferentially kill the fastest proliferating cells, which presumably are cancer cells. CPD0-1392 Cisplatin is degraded via the PWY-4061 "glutathione-mediated detoxification" pathway .

name: neomycin B
annotation: Neomycins are a family of broad-spectrum highly toxic antibiotics produced by TAX-1906. They were discovered in 1949 by Waksman and Lechevalier (Waksman later received the 1951 Nobel Prize in Physiology and medicine) . Neomycin usually exists as a complex composed of two stereoisomers, CPD-14142 and CPD-14141, which are the two active components. A third component, CPD-14137 "neomycin A", which is a degradation product, is present in small quantities. Neomycin was originally used as a treatment for tuberculosis, but serious side effects soon became apparent, including ototoxicity , which sometimes developed to complete loss of hearing, and nephrotoxicity. Thus systemic use of neomycin is limited, and the most common mode of use is via topical application .

name: daunorubicin
annotation: CPD-13962 Daunorubicin is Anthracyclines antibiotic produced by some strains of TAX-1950 and other streptomycetes. It was discovered in the 1960s , and was widely used as as antitumor drug. However, by 1967 it was recognized that it could produce fatal cardiac toxicity. Today analogs of the natural antibiotic are used as drugs. Over 2,000 analogs have been developed. The polyketide derived framework of daunorubicin is made from propanoyl-CoA as the starter unit and malonyl-CoA for the extender units. It contains the unusual deoxyaminosugar CPD-13971. CPD-13962 Daunorubicin was shown to be hydroxylated to CPD-13973 in vivo in TAX-1950 .

name: 17β-estradiol
annotation: 17β-estradiol is a sex hormon that belongs to the Estrogens estrogen group. It is similar to ESTRONE and ESTRIOL, which differ in the number of hydroxyl groups (one for ESTRONE, two for CPD-352 and three for ESTRIOL), but is about 10 times as potent as estrone and about 80 times as potent as estriol in its estrogenic effect. CPD-352 is the predominant estrogen in non-pregenant women during their reproductive years in terms of serum levels. ESTRONE Estrone is the predominant circulating estrogen in women during menopause and ESTRIOL is the predominant circulating estrogen during pregnancy. 17β-estradiol is also present in males at roughly the same concentration as in postmenopausal women. It is produced as an active metabolic product of testosterone. In premenapausal women 17β-estradiol is produced by the granulosa cells of the ovaries by the aromatization of ANDROST4ENE (via ESTRONE). The same occurs in men in the testes. In addition, 17β-estradiol is produced from TESTOSTERONE.

name: arsenate
annotation: Arsenic is the most prevalent environmental toxic substance. It ranks first on the priority list of Hazardous Substances published by the Agency for Toxic Substances and Disease Registry (ATSDR), based on a combination of frequency, toxicity, and potential for human exposure. It is also a Group 1 human carcinogen associated with skin, bladder and lung cancer. While arsenic exists in two oxidation states, As(III) and As(V), in aerobic environments most inorganic arsenic is found as As(V), such as arsenic acid. The prevalent form of arsenic acid under neutral pH is ARSENATE arsenate2-. At neutral pH values arsenate exists mostly in the ARSENATE "AsO42-" form (pKa1= 2:3; pKa2 = 6:8; and pKa3 = 11:6) . ARSENATE Arsenate is chemically similar to phosphate, and enters most cells via phosphate transporters. Once inside cells, arsenate is removed by assorted detoxification mechanisms, described in pathways under Arsenate-Detoxification.

name: carmofur
annotation: Carmofur is derivative of CPD0-1327 and an approved antineoplastic drug used to treat colorectal cancer. It is a potent inhibitor of human acid ceramidase , of human fatty acid amide hydrolase (FAAH), and of bacterial N-acylethanolamine acid amidase . It has bee demonstrated in a structural study to inhibit the SARS-CoV-2 main protease and consequently inhibit viral replication in cells , and was reported to be a weak allosteric inhibitor of SAH nucleosidase .

name: aphidicolin
annotation: CPD-11426 is a tetracyclic diterpene that shows a variety of biological activity such as specific inhibition of DNA polymerase α. It is used as an antiviral agent against Herpes simplex type 1 and is a commercially available agent for studying cell cycles

name: darobactin
annotation: Isolated from the nematode symbiont, TAX-1004150, darobactin is active against Gram-negative pathogens including strains of Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Klebsiella pneumoniae, and Escherichia coli .

annotation: Polymyxin B3 is an antibiotic produced by TAX-1406 that is used in the treatment of Gram-negative bacterial infections. It differs from other polymyxin B compounds by having an CPD-195 fatty acid at the end of the tail .

name: 1,5-anhydro-D-glucitol
annotation: 15-ANHYDRO-D-GLUCITOL is a six-carbon monosaccharide that is the 1-deoxy form of D-glucopyranose. It is generally found in animals and plants, and is inert in these organisms (some bacteria and fungi are able to metabolize it. ). Its physiological role is unknown. Most of the 15-ANHYDRO-D-GLUCITOL found in animal blood is derived from food. The level of 15-ANHYDRO-D-GLUCITOL in human body fluid is used as a sensitive marker for glycemic control in the diagnosis of diabetes, since the plasma level of this compound decreases dramatically in diabetes patients. The lower levels in diabetic patients are thought to be due to competitive inhibition of the reabsorption of the compound at the renal proximal tubuli by a glucose transporter in the presence of excess glucose in the glomerular filtrate . 15-ANHYDRO-D-GLUCITOL can also act as a marker for coronary artery disease .

name: plumbagin
annotation: CPD-4461 Plumbagin (5-hydroxy-2-methyl-1,4-naphthoquinone) is a yellow colored pigment that accumulates in the roots of TAX-122308. It was first isolated from TAX-122308, and subsequently identified in a number of microbes and other plants. CPD-4461 Plumbagin has several pharmacological uses as an antimicrobial, antimalarial and anticancer agent. Its action on non-small lung cells has been demonstrated . Plumbagin's role as an antiproliferative agent shows that it disrupts the microtubule network by binding tubulin . It is considered a promising chemotherapeutic candidate for breast cancer .

name: 2-O-(α-D-glucopyranosyl)-D-glycerate
annotation: CPD-9190 (GG) is a rather rare compatible solute that was first identified in the cyanobacterium TAX-32049 "Synechococcus sp. PCC 7002 (referred to as Agmenellum quadruplicatum in the text)" . Following that initial discovery, GG was observed in the archaeon TAX-39664 strain FDF-1 , and in several bacteria, including a salt-sensitive mutant of the γ-proteobacterium TAX-2746 , in the γ-proteobacterium TAX-556 , and in the thermophilic and slightly halophilic bacterium TAX-309805, that belongs to the Aquificae class .

name: 2,4-dinitrophenol
annotation: 2,4-Dinitrophenol has been used during the first world war by the French in the manufacture of munitions. Later it was also used as a dye, wood preserver, herbicide and photographic developer. 2,4-Dinitrophenol is a well known protonophore (an uncoupler of oxidative phosphorylation) by preventing the uptake of inorganic phosphate molecules into the mitochondria . At lower concentrations this property causes a significant increase in the basal metabolic rate, and as aresult, a rapid weight loss, which prompted its use as weight loss drug. However, severe side effects have resulted in its labelling as "extremely dangerous and not fit for human consumption" by the Federal Food, Drug and Cosmetic Act of 1938. The UK Food Standard Agency issued a warning in 2003, labelling it as "not fit for human consumption" as well. Despite this, CPD-8179 is still marketed as a weight loss drug, mainly over the internet . It has been reported that at low concentrations CPD-8179 protects neurons against β-amyloid toxicity and attenuates nitroprusside-induced toxicity . A number of organisms that utilize CPD-8179 as the sole source of carbon and nitrogen have been described .

name: D-cycloserine
annotation: CPD-2482 is an antibiotic substance produced by several TAX-1883 species . The compound, which is a cyclic analog of D-alanine, prevents the catalytic activities of both EC-5.1.1.1 and EC-6.3.2.4 , which are necessary for the biosynthesis of the bacterial cell wall. It is clinically used as a second-line of defense against TAX-1773, especially when the bacterium is resistant to other antituberculosis antibiotics . CPD-2482 is also a candidate for the treatment of various psychological dysfunctions, since it has been shown to be a partial agonist for the CPD-10705 receptor .

name: hydroxyl radical
annotation: The hydroxyl radical is the neutral form of the hydroxide ion (OH-). Hydroxyl radicals are highly reactive and thus short-lived (half-life of approximately 10-9 seconds). However, they form an important part of radical chemistry and are very dangerous compounds to a living organism, as they can damage virtually all types of macromolecules. In biology, hydroxyl radicals are produced most often from the decomposition of hydroperoxides. Unlike SUPER-OXIDE, the hydroxyl radical cannot be eliminated by an enzymatic reaction.

name: reduced factor 430
aliases: nickel (I) porphinoid|reduced coenzyme F430|reduced cofactor F430
annotation: CPD-19766 "Factor 430" (often referred to as coenzyme F430) is a nickel containing tetrapyrrole cofactor that is present in all methanogenic bacteria. It was initially observed in 1977 by Jean LeGall in extracts of TAX-145262. The name factor 430 (F430) was coined by Gunsalus and Wolfe who reported the absorbance maximumat 430 nm . The compound belongs to the corphins, a class of compounds that combine structural elements of both Corrins corrins and Porphyrins porphyrins . In 1982 Ellefson et al showed that CPD-19766 is a cofactor of the enzyme EC-2.8.4.1 (methyl-coenzyme M reductase) . This enzyme catalyzes the final step of methane production in all methanogenic bacteria: the reduction of the methyl group in Me-CoM to CH4. F430 exists in two distinct intracellular pools: either bound to EC 2.8.4.1 or free of protein in the cytosol. Early literature on F430 was confusing due to reports of heterogeneity in F430 samples that depended on the isolation and purification procedures used in different laboratories. Later work showed that F430 exists primarily in two isomeric forms. These forms differ in the relative stereochemical disposition of acid side chains at positions 12 and 13 . The biosynthetic route leading to PWY-5196 was discovered only in 2016 .

name: 4-deoxypyridoxine
annotation: Despite its similarity to PYRIDOXAL_PHOSPHATE (PLP), the active form of vitamin B6, CPD0-1605 cannot be used as a source of vitamin B6 biosynthesis. Rather, it interferes with vitamin B6 homeostasis by acting as an extremely strong competitive inhibitor of EC-2.7.1.35. In addition, the phosphorylated form CPD0-1221 inhibits PLP-dependent enzymes .

name: arsenite3-
annotation: Arsenic is the most prevalent environmental toxic substance. It ranks first on the priority list of Hazardous Substances published by the Agency for Toxic Substances and Disease Registry (ATSDR), based on a combination of frequency, toxicity, and potential for human exposure. It is also a Group 1 human carcinogen associated with skin, bladder and lung cancer. While arsenic exists in two oxidation states, AsIII and AsV, in aerobic environments most inorganic arsenic is found as As(V), such as ARSENATE. In anaerobic environments the prevalent form is As(III), such as CPD0-2040. While most of the literature refers to the As(III) form found in the environment as CPD-763, at natural pH values most of the AsIII molecules exist in solution as CPD0-2040, an inorganic polyol similar to glycerol, since the pKa values are high: pKa1 = 9:2 and pKa2 = 12:7 . This form of arsenite can enter cells via glycerol channels such as EG10396 GlpF of TAX-562 or aquaglyceroporins, bidirectional channels that allow water and small uncharged molecules such as glycerol to pass through. Charged forms of arsenite, formed at higher pH, include CPD-763 "arsenite1-", CPD-24319 and CPD-24318.

name: L-azaserine
annotation: L-AZASERINE is the most widely studied naturally occurring diazo compound (with over 1100 publications as of 2011). It was isolated from TAX-67301 in 1954 as part of a screening program for new antitumour and antibiotic agents . L-AZASERINE acts as an analog of GLN, and is a competitive inhibitor of enzymes using glutamine. A large number of studies on the antitumour effects of azaserine have been conducted and included a number of clinical trials. Although azaserine showed some useful activity in leukaemia, it was less effective than other agents, and as a result, clinical studies on the compound have been abandoned .

name: NADPH-dependent curcumin/dihydrocurcumin reductase
annotation: The purified product of Escherichia coli gene curA was shown to catalyze the NADPH-dependent, sequential reduction of curcumin first to the stable intermediate dihydrocurcumin, and then to the final end product tetrahydrocurcumin. Curcumin is a plant secondary metabolite and curcumin metabolizing microorganisms were found in samples of human feces. The organism with the highest curcumin converting activity was isolated, cultured and identified as Escherichia coli. High curcumin converting activity was also identified in Escherichia coli K-12 substr. DH10B whose genome sequence has been determined. The curcumin metabolizing enzyme was purified from extracts of this organism and used to identify its encoding gene. Following gene cloning and overexpression, the recombinant enzyme was purified and characterized. The results showed that E. coli gene yncB encoded the curcumin-converting enzyme and it was renamed curA . CurA protein showed amino acid sequence similarity with some members of the medium-chain dehydrogenase/reductase (MDR) superfamily. Due to its non-requirement for Zn2+ the enzyme is a member of a zinc-independent family of the MDR superfamily. Creation of a C247A mutant in a putative NADPH binding site by site-directed mutagenesis and purification and assay of the resulting enzyme showed an approximately 50% reduction in activity in this mutant . The apparent molecular mass of the complex was determined by gel filtration chromatography and the apparent molecular mass of the subunit was determined by SDS-PAGE. The absorption spectrum of the enzyme suggested that the enzyme contained no bound cofactor. Qualitative and quantitative analysis for a variety of metals showed no metal content. name: peptide antibiotic transporter
annotation: Deletion, mutation and complementation studies indicate that sbmA encodes an inner-membrane transport protein which is responsible for the import of Microcin 25 (Mcc25) - a plasmid-encoded peptide antibiotic produced by Escherichia coli AY25 . SbmA transport is energised by the electrochemical gradient . SbmA belongs to the peptide uptake permease family (PUP) . SbmA is proposed to function subsequent to EG10302-MONOMER "FhuA", a multifunctional TonB dependent outer membrane receptor protein for Mcc25. After FhuA-dependent uptake across the outer membrane, SbmA facilitates the translocation of Mcc25 from the periplasmic space across the cytoplasmic membrane . Transposon insertion into sbmA results in resistance to Microcin B17 . sbmA is poorly expressed and overexpression of sbmA from a multicopy plasmid may be lethal . ΔsbmA strains show increased resistance to the antibiotic bleomycin and it has been suggested that thiazole rings constitute the key structural feature that is recognised by SbmA . SbmA transports antisense peptide nucleic acid (PNA) oligomers (antimicrobial compounds designed to target essential bacterial genes ). An E. coli K-12 ΔsbmA strain is resistant to antisense PNA oligomers targeting acpP or ftsZ . sbmA null mutants are resistant to proline-rich antimicrobial peptides of eukaryotic origin . Purified SbmA binds the eukaryotic proline-rich antimicrobial peptide Bac7 in vitro . SbmA transports the insect-derived proline-rich antimicrobial peptide pyrrhocoricin . SbmA is implicated in transport of the antimicrobial peptide, arasin . SbmA is a homodimeric inner membrane protein . Homology based protein modeling suggests that SbmA contains eight transmembrane domains with both the N terminus and C terminus located in the cytoplasm . Related reviews:

name: microcin C
annotation: Microcin C, originally named microcin 7 and often referred to as microcin C7, is a heptapeptide produced by TAX-562 with an N-formylated methionine and a C-terminal phosphoramidate linkage to adenosine, modified with an aminopropyl moiety . Microcin C is exported from producing cells through a major facilitator superfamily efflux pump encoded by the G-124166 gene. It is imported by target cells via the ABC-41-CPLX "inner membrane transporter YejABEF", which recognizes the peptide part of the microcin . Following import, the peptide is cleaved by non-specific peptidases, releasing CPD-25930, a toxic CPD0-915 mimic that targets EC-6.1.1.12, inhibiting protein synthesis .
notes: microcin C

name: MONOMER0-2799
annotation: E. coli is able to import pyridoxal, which is then utilized in the pyridoxal 5'-phosphate salvage pathway, but the transporter responsible for this has not been identified .

name: microcin B17
annotation: Microcin B17 is a linear azol(in)e-containing peptide (LAP) that functions as a narrow-spectrum bactericidal peptide. The peptide targets DNA gyrase . It is active mostly against Enterobacteriaceae and some Pseudomonas species, due to its limited ability to enter bacterial cells. The precursor peptide is encoded by the mcbA gene. All four CYS and four of the six SER residues are modified by EC-6.2.2.2 to azoline residues, which are further oxidized to azole rings. These modifications are catalyzed by a trimeric synthetase comprising McbB, McbC, and McbD (substrate recognition module, dehydrogenase, and YcaO proteins, respectively). Following generation of the azole rings the leader region is removed by a protease (tldD/tldE) encoded on the chromosome of E. coli. After complete processing, a two-component ABC transporter (McbE/McbF) exports the mature microcin B17 while also providing a level of self-protection. Microcin B17 is imported into target cells by two membrane proteins - the outer membrane protein OmpF and the plasma membrane protein SmbA. Once inside the cells, the peptide inhibits DNA gyrase and triggers double strand breaks in DNA.
